The actual scan diameter (t) was calculated as t = p × q × s, where p is the device-specific magnification factor, q is the ocular magnification factor [q = 0.01306 × (AL − 1.82)], and s is the raw OCT measurement. 2.5 Statistical analysis Data analysis was performed with Statistical Package for the Social Sciences (SPSS) version 23.0 (SPSS, Inc., Chicago, IL, United States). The distributions of the data were assessed for normality using the Shapiro–Wilk test. Normally distributed variables are presented as means and standard deviations, whereas non-normally distributed variables are expressed as medians and interquartile ranges (IQRs). Categorical data are presented as counts and percentages. For normally distributed data, group differences were analyzed using independent samples t -tests (for unpaired groups) or paired t -tests (matched groups). Non-normal data were analyzed with the Mann–Whitney U (independent) or Wilcoxon signed-rank tests (paired). Furthermore, the chi-squared test was used to compare sex ratios between the two anisometropia groups. Spearman’s rank correlation analysis was used to assess the associations between biometric parameters (i.e., SER, AL, and AL/K) and other choroidal parameters. A p -value of <0.05 indicated a statistically significant difference.

[results] 3 Results 3.1 General characteristics A total of 60 subjects were enrolled in this study, including 26 patients with unilateral hyperopic anisometropia (female/male = 12/14, age 10.53 ± 2.51 years) and 34 patients with unilateral myopic anisometropia (female/male = 17/17, age 10.11 ± 1.72 years; Table 1 ). Eyes of the patients were categorized into four groups based on spherical equivalent refraction (SER): hyperopic eyes (SER ≥ +1.50D, group A), normal eyes in hyperopic anisometropia (−0.50D ≤ SER ≤ +1.00D, group B), normal eyes in myopic anisometropia (−0.50D ≤ SER ≤ +1.00D, group C), and myopic eyes (SER ≤ −1.00D, group D). The mean anisometropia value was 2.49 ± 0.61 (1.500–3.625) D in hyperopic anisometropia participants and 2.21 ± 0.98 (1.000–4.250) D in myopic anisometropia participants ( p = 0.186, non-significant). There was no significant difference in the SER or AL between normal eyes of hyperopic and myopic anisometropia patients ( p = 0.052 and p = 0.073, respectively). Table 1 Demographic and clinical characteristics of the enrolled participants ( n = 60). Characteristics N Age (years) Sex (male, %) SER (D) AL (mm) Ave-K (D) AL/K (mm/D) CCT (mm) ACD (mm) Hyperopic anisometropia 26 10.53 ± 2.51 14 (53.85) Group A 2.63 [0.75] 22.24 ± 0.49 43.24 ± 0.89 0.51 ± 0.02 565.27 ± 28.59 3.59 ± 0.17 Group B 0.31 [0.56] 23.13 ± 0.50 43.44 ± 0.85 0.53 ± 0.02 563.35 ± 27.99 3.64 ± 0.17 p -value <0.001 <0.001 <0.001 <0.001 0.072 0.005 Myopic anisometropia 34 10.11 ± 1.72 17 (50) Group C 0 [0.59] 23.42 ± 0.70 43.46 ± 1.43 0.54 ± 0.03 557.32 ± 27.51 3.74 ± 0.21 Group D −1.88 [1.38] 24.26 ± 0.71 43.46 ± 1.46 0.56 ± 0.03 558.47 ± 27.76 3.79 ± 0.20 p -value <0.001 <0.001 0.984 <0.001 0.398 <0.001 p -value 0.46 a 0.77 b ACD, anterior chamber depth; AL, axial length; Ave-K, average K-reading; CCT, central corneal thickness; SER, spherical equivalent refraction. The results are presented as the means ± SDs, medians [interquartile ranges (IQRs)], or n (%). a Statistical significance tested with the independent-samples t -test. b Statistical significance tested with the χ 2 test. The Wilcoxon signed-rank test was used for interocular comparisons of SER. Paired-samples t -test was used for interocular comparisons of AL, Ave-K, AL/K, CCT, and ACD. 3.2 Ocular characteristics Ocular characteristics, including AL, Ave-K, AL/K ratio, CCT, and ACD, are presented in Table 1 . As expected, the AL of the hyperopic eyes (group A) was significantly shorter, and the AL of the myopic eyes (group D) was significantly longer (both p <0.001; Table 1 ). The AL/K ratio of hyperopic eyes was significantly lower than that of normal eyes, and the AL/K ratio of myopic eyes was significantly greater than that of normal eyes (both p <0.001). The Ave-K of the hyperopic eyes was lower than that of the normal eyes (p <0.001). Compared to normal eyes, myopic eyes presented a greater ACD, whereas hyperopic eyes presented a shallower ACD (both p <0.001). 3.3 Choroidal parameters measured by OCT Table 2 shows that the SFCT was significantly greater in hyperopic eyes (group A vs. group B, t = 6.55, p <0.001) and significantly lower in myopic eyes (group C vs. group D, t = 8.70, p <0.001). The horizontal choroidal thickness in hyperopic eyes was significantly greater than that in normal eyes at 0.75 mm and 1.5 mm temporal and 0.75 mm and 1.5 mm nasal locations (all p <0.01; Figure 3a ). The horizontal choroidal thickness in myopic eyes was significantly thinner than that in normal eyes at 0.75 mm and 1.5 mm temporal and 0.75 mm and 1.5 mm nasal locations (all p <0.001, Figure 3b ). In the vertical direction, the choroidal thickness was significantly greater in hyperopic eyes at all points (all p <0.01; Figure 3c ) and significantly thinner in myopic eyes at all points (all p ≤ 0.001; Figure 3d ). Table 2 Comparison of CT and choroidal vascular parameters ( n = 60). Parameters Group A ( n = 26) Group B ( n = 26) p -value Group C ( n = 34) Group D ( n = 34) p -value p -value (GA vs. GD) CT (μm, mean ± SD) SFCT 354.51 ± 45.46 295.89 ± 48.47 <0.001 318.18 ± 51.31 263.89 ± 53.97 <0.001 <0.001 N0.75 341.02 ± 50.75 285.09 ± 44.78 <0.001 301.86 ± 56.29 246.15 ± 54.77 <0.001 <0.001 N1.5 325.26 ± 53.41 263.46 ± 41.46 <0.001 267.73 ± 58.97 222.57 ± 52.23 <0.001 <0.001 T0.75 355.58 ± 41.33 309.80 ± 46.34 <0.001 333.79 ± 49.93 278.09 ± 51.57 <0.001 <0.001 T1.5 358.60 ± 41.17 324.34 ± 51.94 0.006 335.60 ± 48.44 285.93 ± 49.85 <0.001 <0.001 I0.75 336.58 ± 40.31 297.02 ± 39.28 <0.001 310.73 ± 53.02 277.08 ± 60.46 <0.001 <0.001 I1.5 333.79 ± 44.04 298.31 ± 37.23 0.001 311.00 ± 60.08 285.09 ± 56.25 0.001 <0.001 S0.75 347.64 ± 36.05 296.24 ± 39.72 <0.001 313.66 ± 52.84 276.13 ± 56.47 <0.001 <0.001 S1.5 351.46 ± 45.53 314.67 ± 39.60 0.002 324.84 ± 52.15 285.66 ± 56.29 <0.001 <0.001 Choroid vascular TCA, (mm 2 ) 0.47 ± 0.06 0.43 ± 0.07 0.003 0.46 ± 0.07 0.41 ± 0.08 <0.001 0.002 LA, (mm 2 ) 0.32 ± 0.05 0.29 ± 0.05 <0.001 0.31 ± 0.05 0.26 ± 0.05 <0.001 <0.001 SA, (mm 2 ) 0.15 ± 0.03 0.14 ± 0.03 0.181 0.15 ± 0.03 0.14 ± 0.03 0.006 0.215 LA/SA 2.11 ± 0.37 2.00 ± 0.25 0.072 2.05 ± 0.35 1.83 ± 0.21 0.002 0.002 CVI (LA/TCA, %) 67.68 ± 3.72 66.35 ± 2.62 0.050 66.82 ± 3.28 64.51 ± 2.61 <0.001 0.001 CT, choroidal thickness; CVI, choroidal vascularity index; LA, luminal area; LA/SA, luminal area/stromal area ratio; SA, stromal area; SFCT, subfoveal choroidal thickness; TCA, total choroidal area; GA, Group A; GD, Group D; SD, standard deviation. N0.75, N1.5, T0.75, T1.5, I0.75, I1.5, S0.75, and S1.5 represent the choroidal thicknesses measured at 0.75 and 1.5 mm nasally, temporally, inferiorly, and superiorly, respectively, to the fovea. P value columns (for Group A vs. Group B, Group C vs. Group D) represent intra-group comparisons (paired-samples t-test) for each parameter. P value (GA vs. GD) column reflects the inter-group comparison (independent-samples t-test) between Group A (hyperopic anisometropia) and Group D (myopic anisometropia), as indicated by the footnote GA vs GD. Figure 3 CT at different locations. CT of a horizontal B-scan at T1.5, T0.75, fovea, N0.75, and N1.5 in (a) hyperopic anisometropia and (c) myopic anisometropia patients. CT of a vertical B-scan at I1.5, I0.75, fovea, S0.75, and S1.5 in (b) hyperopic anisometropia and in (d) myopic anisometropia patients. The data are expressed as the means and 95% confidence intervals (CIs). Graphs depicting choroidal thickness at various choroidal points. Panel (a) and (b) compare hyperopic and normal eyes with circles and squares, respectively. Panels (c) and (d) compare myopic and normal eyes. The y-axis ranges from 150 to 450 micrometers. Each graph shows error bars indicating variability. Significant differences were observed between hyperopic and myopic eyes in choroidal parameters, including CVI ( p = 0.001), LA/SA ratio ( p = 0.002), TCA ( p = 0.002), and LA ( p <0.001), while SA did not show a significant difference ( p = 0.215). In children with hyperopic anisometropia, the TCA and LA in hyperopic eyes were significantly greater than those in normal eyes ( p = 0.003 and p <0.001). The LA/SA ratio and CVI were slightly greater in hyperopic eyes than in normal eyes, but the difference was not significant ( p = 0.072, p = 0.050; Table 2 ). In children with myopic anisometropia, the LA/SA ratio and CVI were significantly lower in myopic eyes ( p = 0.002, p <0.001). The TCA and LA were also significantly lower in myopic eyes ( p <0.001 for both), while SA showed a smaller but still significant difference ( p = 0.006). 3.4 Relationships between choroidal parameters and ocular factors The relationships between the choroidal parameters and the interocular differences in the SER, AL, and AL/K are presented in Table 3 . In the hyperopic anisometropia group, the interocular difference in AL showed significant negative correlations with SFCT ( p = 0.005) and LA ( p = 0.047). In contrast, no significant correlations were observed in the myopic anisometropia group (see Supplementary Figure S1 for scatterplots). Structural patterns were visualized via EDI-OCT images ( Supplementary Figure S2 ). Additionally, in the hyperopic group, the interocular difference in AL/K showed significant negative correlations with SFCT ( p = 0.001) and LA ( p = 0.039). In contrast, no significant correlations were found between SER, AL, or AL/K and any choroidal parameters in the myopic anisometropia group (all p > 0.05). Table 3 Relationships between the interocular differences in the SFCT, TCA, LA, SA, and CVI and the interocular differences in the SER, AL, and AL/K ratio. Parameters SER p -value AL p -value AL/K p -value Hyperopic anisometropia SFCT 0.366 0.066 −0.529 0.005 * −0.612 0.001 * TCA 0.321 0.110 −0.366 0.066 −0.386 0.052 LA 0.357 0.074 −0.393 0.047 * −0.407 0.039 * SA 0.229 0.261 −0.329 0.101 −0.357 0.073 LA/SA −0.081 0.693 0.182 0.373 0.244 0.230 CVI −0.014 0.947 0.127 0.536 0.236 0.246 Myopic anisometropia SFCT 0.196 0.267 −0.253 0.149 −0.167 0.345 TCA 0.088 0.621 −0.242 0.168 −0.216 0.219 LA 0.077 0.664 −0.207 0.240 −0.193 0.273 SA 0.052 0.769 0.040 0.832 0.089 0.617 LA/SA −0.025 0.888 −0.198 0.262 −0.250 0.153 CVI −0.031 0.864 −0.189 0.284 −0.240 0.171 AL: axial length; CVI: choroidal vascularity index; LA: luminal area; SA: stromal area; SER: spherical equivalent refraction; SFCT: subfoveal choroidal thickness; TCA: total choroidal area. *Correlations were significant at a p -value of <0.05.

[discussion] 4 Discussion Children with anisometropia constitute a useful population for studies on ocular structure in different refractive states because these studies explore differences between an individual’s two eyes. Our study investigated the biological parameters and choroidal microstructure of the eyes of children with unilateral anisometropia. We also analyzed the relationships between biological parameters and choroidal parameters. The results revealed significant differences in the AL and AL/K ratio between myopic and hyperopic anisometropia patients. Patients with myopia have greater ALs and AL/K ratios, whereas those with hyperopia have lower ALs and AL/K ratios. In patients with myopic anisometropia, the LA/SA ratio and CVI are lower in myopic eyes than in emmetropic eyes. In patients with hyperopic anisometropia, the LA/SA ratio and CVI are slightly greater in hyperopic eyes than in emmetropic eyes, but the differences are not statistically significant. Numerous previous studies ( 8 , 16–18 ) have shown that the development of myopia is accompanied by changes in choroidal vascularity, which are associated with increased AL. The LA/SA ratio and CVI are indicators of choroidal vascularity and provide information on the relative proportion of the choroid occupied by vascular and interstitial components. In children with myopia, the CVI ( 19 ) and choriocapillaris blood perfusion ( 20 ) are reduced. Similar results were observed in our study, with the LA/SA ratio and CVI significantly lower in myopic eyes than in normal eyes. These findings suggest that reduced choroidal vascularity may play a more significant role in the development of myopia compared to emmetropia in children. Among the children with myopic anisometropia in this study, there was a significant reduction in the TCA and LA in myopic eyes compared to emmetropic eyes. This finding is consistent with those of multiple previous clinical studies ( 6 , 7 , 21 ), which described a continuous decrease in the TCA and LA in individuals with myopia. However, there may be differences in the rates of choroidal vasculopathy and matrix reduction at different stages of myopia development and progression. Several studies ( 6 , 7 , 19 , 20 ) have reported a greater reduction in choroidal vascular components as the CVI and choroidal vascularity volume (CVV) decrease among patients with myopia. Some studies ( 22 , 23 ) reported no change in the CVI among patients with myopia. Gupta et al. ( 24 ) reported a greater reduction in the SA than in the LA among patients with high myopia, resulting in a greater CVI among these individuals. The myopic anisometropic eyes in our study had low-to-moderate myopia, and the reduction in choroidal vascular components was obvious compared with that in the emmetropic eyes. These findings suggest that a decrease in the vascular luminal area and a change in the choroidal blood circulation may occur in the early stage of myopia development in children. This study revealed an increase in the choroidal area and luminal area in hyperopic eyes. However, the difference in choroidal microstructure between hyperopic and emmetropic eyes varies greatly across studies. In a previous study of adults ( 25 ), emmetropic eyes had the highest CVI, whereas eyes with high hypermetropia and myopia had lower CVIs than emmetropic eyes. Chang et al. ( 26 ) reported that the TCA, LA, and SA were not significantly different between hyperopic and emmetropic eyes in children; however, choroidal vascular indices were not measured in this study. In previous studies on high hyperopia ( 27 ), the TCA, LA, and LA/SA ratios were significantly greater than those of normal eyes, while the CVI was lower. Our study involved children aged 6–14 years with monocular low-to-moderate hyperopic anisometropia, differing from the populations examined in the earlier studies. This inconsistency may be due to the differences in age, SE, or scanning patterns of the choroidal area across studies. In the present study, the TCA and LA of hyperopic anisometropic eyes were higher than those of emmetropic eyes. Moreover, interocular differences in LA showed significant negative correlations with the differences in AL and AL/K ratio, suggesting that eyes with shorter axial lengths and lower AL/K ratios tended to have larger luminal areas. The findings of the present study align with those of a previous study on children with different refractive states ( 28 ), which showed that the CVI and LA/SA ratio were highest in hyperopia, followed by emmetropic eyes, and lowest in myopic eyes. This may be explained by the thickening of the choroid in hyperopic anisometropic eyes, which is primarily due to the increase in the area of the choroidal luminal area. Further research is needed to elucidate this topic. Choroidal thickness (CT) is an important parameter for obtaining information about the choroidal layer. The present study revealed that the CT of hyperopic anisometropic eyes was greater than that of emmetropic eyes. In contrast, the CT of myopic anisometropic eyes was lower than that of emmetropic eyes, which is consistent with previous reports ( 8 , 9 , 29 , 30 ). Jin et al. ( 31 ) reported that the central foveal CT was positively correlated with the SER and negatively correlated with the AL. Additionally, our study demonstrated a nasally to temporally decreasing trend in the difference in choroidal thickness (CT) in hyperopic anisometropic eyes, whereas no such trend was observed in myopic anisometropic eyes. This difference may be due to the inconsistency in the extent of changes in vascular and stromal components across different quadrants during CT changes. Although the thickness of the choroid is mainly determined by the luminal components, previous studies have reported that there are differences in the distributions of luminal and stromal components in different quadrants of the foveal and macular areas ( 32 , 33 ). Our study has the following limitations. First, we adopted a cross-sectional design. With the current sample size used in this study, which included 26 hyperopic and 34 myopic anisometropia participants, the power was 87% for detecting significant correlations (e.g., r = −0.529 for AL-SFCT in the hyperopic group). While our study was adequately powered to detect large effects (e.g., AL differences and strong correlations), the subgroup analysis may have been underpowered for subtle choroidal vascular changes. Specifically, the power to detect CVI differences in hyperopic anisometropia was only 46%, suggesting the possibility of type II errors in these analyses. Future longitudinal studies with larger populations are needed for more prospective and robust results. Another limitation is that we analyzed only the central 3-mm-wide subfoveal choroidal regions. The choroidal microstructure of a wide-field region outside this area might differ because eye growth is known to be asymmetrical. Finally, this study encountered technical limitations in choroidal imaging and binarization ( 34 , 35 ). For example, images with low signal–to-noise ratios or measurement errors in manual segmentation may have led to decreased accuracy, which could have affected the reliability of the CVI as a reflection of choroidal vascularity. Future studies using a high-speed SS-OCT system ( 35 ) can improve choroidal vasculature imaging and choroidal angiography.

[conclusion] 5 Conclusion To our knowledge, this is the first study to focus on the differences in biometric parameters and choroidal vascular microstructure between unilateral hyperopic anisometropia and unilateral myopic anisometropia. Compared with normal eyes, myopic anisometropic eyes have greater axial lengths and AL/K ratios, whereas hyperopic anisometric eyes have lower axial lengths and AL/K ratios. We identified significant negative correlations between axial length and choroidal vascular parameters (SFCT and LA) in hyperopic anisometropia, suggesting a potential mechanistic link between axial elongation and choroidal thinning. The LA/SA ratio and CVI are highest in hyperopic eyes, followed by emmetropic eyes and myopic eyes. Choroidal blood flow may decrease as hyperopia progresses to emmetropia and then to myopia. In future studies, the association between refractive development and choroidal vascular structural changes (particularly CVI and luminal area variations) requires further longitudinal investigation using both OCT-based vascular indices and, where feasible, complementary time-resolved blood flow measurements such as OCT angiography (OCTA).

retrieved_papers.title: Evaluation of retinal nerve fibre layer, ganglion cell layer and choroidal thickness with optical coherence tomography in migraine patients: a case-control study.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2018]
[Abstract] Evaluation of retinal nerve fibre layer (RNFL), ganglion cell layer (GCL) and choroidal thickness (CT) with optical coherence tomography (OCT) in chronic migraine patients, to compare with healthy controls.
Ninety-four eyes of 47 chronic migraine patients (Group 1) and 68 eyes of 34 healthy individuals (Group 2) were included in this prospective case-control study. The right and left eyes were separately evaluated. Mean peripapillary RNFL thicknesses, mean GCL measured from superior and inferior quadrants, and mean CT were measured at three different regions (central, 500 μm nasal and temporal region of the fovea).
There was no statistically significant differences in RNFL between the two groups (p > 0.05), while CT values were significantly higher and GCL values were significantly lower in chronic migraine groups (p < 0.05). There were no statistically significant differences between migraine duration, frequency and length of attacks, presence of aura, relation to menstrual cycle, white matter lesions in cranial magnetic resonance imaging and RNFL, GCL and CT (p > 0.05).
In this study, we observed chronic migraine disease does not have any effect on peripapillary RNFL thickness; however, increases in CT and decreases in GCL thickness were observed in migraine patients.

[methods] Methods This study is a hospital-based comparative, cross-sectional study conducted in the departments of ophthalmology and neurology of a tertiary care hospital in eastern India from June 2023 to July 2024. Institutional ethical committee clearance was obtained before starting the study (IEC/IMS.SH/SOA/2023/553 dated June 27, 2023). The study adhered to the basic tenets of the Helsinki Declaration. Written informed consent was obtained from each patient before enrolling them in the study. All patients aged between 18 and 50 years diagnosed with migraine as per the International Classification of Headache Disorders, third edition (ICHD-3) classification were included in the study.[ 6 7 ] Healthy age- and sex-matched individuals without any ocular and systemic abnormalities constituted the control group. Patients with the following were excluded from the study: best corrected visual acuity <20/20, N6, spherical or cylindrical errors >±2 D, history of systemic disorders like diabetes, hypertension, anemia, neurologic diseases like multiple sclerosis, Parkinsonism, Alzheimer’s, and ocular diseases like glaucoma, uveitis, significant cataract, retinal and optic nerve disorders. Patients with a past or present history of smoking, history of any previous ocular trauma or surgery, and intake of any systemic medications like isotretinoin, hydroxychloroquine, thioridazine, etc., which could affect the study parameters, were also excluded from the study. After obtaining informed written consent, a detailed history including the patient’s name, age, sex, occupation, address, presenting symptom, progression, and associated symptoms was recorded. A detailed history of the type of headache, duration of the headache, duration of each episode, and laterality was obtained from each patient. Detailed information about the severity of migraine was obtained using the headache impact test-6 (HIT-6) questionnaire.[ 14 ] Best corrected visual acuity was measured using Snellen’s chart. Ishihara’s chart was used to assess color vision. A thorough evaluation of the anterior segment was done in each case using the slit lamp. Intraocular pressure was measured using Goldmann’s applanation tonometer. Pupils of both eyes were dilated using a combination of 0.8% tropicamide and 5% phenylephrine. Posterior segment evaluation was done using an indirect ophthalmoscope and a 20-D lens. Measurement of pRNFL thickness, ONH, and macular parameters was done using the 3D disc, 3D macula, and 3D macula (V) modes of the spectral domain OCT machine (3D OCT-1 Topcon Maestro version 8.3; Topcon, Tokyo, Japan). RNFL and ONH details were obtained using the 3D disc mode having a scan length of 6.0 × 6.0 mm and a resolution of 512 × 128. Macular parameters were studied using the 3D macula (V) and 3D macula modes having a scan length of 7.0 × 7.0 and 6.0 × 6.0 mm, respectively. The resolution of both the above macular scans was 512 × 128. The RNFL parameters studied were average, superior, inferior, nasal, and temporal thicknesses. The ONH parameters included were disc area, cup area, rim area, vertical cup disc ratio, linear cup disc ratio, and rim volume. Central macular thickness (CMT), superior macular RNFL, inferior macular RNFL, superior GCL, and inferior GCL thicknesses were the various macular parameters studied. The thickness calculated in each cell was compared with a normative database that had already been fed into the machine. Values within the normal range were represented as green, values in the borderline as yellow, and values outside the normal limits were shown in red. In our study, migraine patients were divided into episodic and chronic as per the ICHD-3 criteria.[ 6 7 ] According to the HIT-6 score,[ 14 ] the severity of migraine is classified as mild (little-to-no) impact: HIT-6 score: 36–49; moderate impact: HIT-6 score: 50–59; and severe impact: HIT-6 score: 60–78. Statistical analysis All analyses were done using Statistical Package for the Social Sciences software, version 24.0. The analysis included profiling patients using different demographic and clinical parameters. For all statistical purposes, values of the right eye of both study and control groups were used. Descriptive analysis of quantitative parameters was expressed as means and standard deviation. Categorical data was expressed as absolute numbers and percentages. Independent Student’s t -test was used for testing the mean between independent groups, whereas paired Student’s t -test and analysis of variance were used to calculate statistical significance between two or more groups. Cross tables were generated, and the Chi-squared test was used to test associations. P value <0.05 was considered statistically significant.

[results] Results The present study enrolled 200 subjects (100 migraine patients and 100 healthy subjects). The mean age in migraine and control groups was 31.01 ± 8.56 and 30.80 ± 8.65 years, respectively. Most subjects were females (84% in migraine group and 82% in the control group). Of the 100 migraine patients, only three patients had migraine with aura. In 43%, 32%, and 25% of cases, mild, moderate, and severe migraine were seen, respectively. Episodic migraine was seen in 71% of cases, with chronic migraine making up the rest. The mean duration of migraine in study subjects was 6.56 ± 5.08 years. The mean duration of individual attacks was 9.33 ± 13.41 h, and the mean number of migraine attacks each month was 9.31 ± 6.57. Salient demographic details are presented in Table 1 . Table 1 Salient demographic characteristics Variable Migraine ( n =100) Control ( n =100) Age (years) 31.01±8.56 30.80±8.65 Sex Male (16), female (84) Male (18), female (82) Intraocular pressure (mmHg) 14.10±2.61 14.14±2.49 Aura Present (3), absent (97) - Severity of migraine Mild- 43 Moderate- 32 Severe- 25 - Type of migraine Episodic- 71 Chronic- 29 - Headache duration (years) 6.56±5.08 - Individual attack duration (h) 9.33±13.41 - Attack frequency (months) 9.31±6.57 - Analysis of RNFL thickness The average pRNFL thickness in migraine patients and controls was 91.62 ± 15.70 and 106.19 ± 10.46 µm, respectively [ Table 2 ]. Migraine patients had statistically significant ( P < 0.05) thinner pRNFL (average and all quadrants) compared to age- and gender-matched healthy controls. Compared to patients with episodic migraine, chronic migraine patients exhibited statistically significant ( P < 0.05) thinning in pRNFL (average and all quadrants). pRNFL (average and all quadrants) was found to be statistically thinner ( P < 0.05) in severe migraine patients compared to those with mild and moderate migraine. Compared to migraine patients with a disease duration of less than 5 years, patients with a disease duration of more than 5 years had statistically significant thinning in pRNFL (average and all quadrants). Table 2 Comparison of various study parameters between migraine patients and healthy controls Parameter Migraine Controls P Average RNFL thickness (μm) 91.62±15.70 106.19±10.46 <0.001 Superior RNFL thickness (μm) 112.22±28.08 134.30±14.82 <0.001 Inferior RNFL thickness (μm) 114.26±23.34 135.45±16.97 <0.001 Nasal RNFL thickness (μm) 73.66±19.05 81.98±15.43 0.001 Temporal RNFL thickness (μm) 65.96±11.26 73.10±10.61 <0.001 Disc area (mm 2 ) 2.45±0.98 2.14±0.46 0.004 Cup area (mm 2 ) 0.86±0.61 0.70±0.45 0.029 Rim area (mm 2 ) 1.59±0.81 1.44±0.46 0.103 Linear cup disc ratio 0.56±0.15 0.54±0.17 0.258 Vertical cup disc ratio 0.57±0.15 0.52±0.16 0.033 Rim volume (mm 3 ) 0.45±0.30 0.27±0.16 <0.001 Central macular thickness (μm) 207.67±16.92 228.09±13.58 <0.001 Macular superior RNFL (μm) 31.25±3.56 35.85±2.94 <0.001 Macular inferior RNFL (μm) 32.53±4.06 37.82±3.95 <0.001 GCL superior (μm) 67.88±5.08 68.81±3.93 0.149 GCL inferior (μm) 66.95±5.25 68.87±3.88 0.004 GCL=ganglion cell layer, RNFL=retinal nerve fiber layer Analysis of ONH parameters Disc area was 2.45 ± 0.98 mm 2 in migraine patients and 2.14 ± 0.46 mm 2 in controls. Optic disc area, cup area, vertical cup disc ratio, and rim volume were more in migraine patients compared to healthy controls, and this difference was statistically significant ( P < 0.05). All the ONH parameters, that is, optic disc area, cup area, rim area, linear and vertical cup disc ratio, and rim volume, were more in chronic migraine patients than in episodic migraine patients. However, this difference was statistically insignificant ( P > 0.05). Optic disc area, cup area, rim area, linear and vertical cup disc ratio, and rim volume were more in severe migraine patients than in mild and moderate migraine patients. However, this difference was statistically significant ( P < 0.05) only in optic disc area and rim volume. In patients with migraine for more than 5 years, there was an increase in optic disc area, cup area, rim area, linear and vertical cup disc ratio, and rim volume. However, this difference was found to be statistically insignificant ( P > 0.05). Analysis of macular parameters Mean CMT was 207.67 ± 16.92 µm min migraine patients and 228.09 ± 13.58 µm in healthy controls. This difference was found to be statistically significant ( P < 0.05). Migraine patients had statistically significant ( P < 0.05) thinner macular parameters, including superior and inferior macular RNFL thicknesses, CMT, and inferior GCL thickness, compared to age- and gender-matched healthy controls. Chronic migraine patients had statistically significant ( P < 0.05) thinning in all macular parameters compared to episodic migraine patients. Compared to patients with mild and moderate migraine, those with severe migraine exhibited statistically significant ( P < 0.05) thinner macular parameters. Similarly, all macular parameters were found to be significantly ( P < 0.05) thinner in migraine patients with a longer duration of the disease (>5 years). Tables 3 - 5 compare the various study parameters based on type, severity, and duration of migraine, respectively. [discussion] Discussion The present study was a hospital-based comparative, cross-sectional study that included 100 migraine patients and 100 healthy subjects. In our study, the mean age in the migraine group was 31.0 ± 8.56 years and in the control group was 30.80 ± 8.65 years ( P > 0.05), which was comparable to the findings reported by Simsek[ 15 ] (study group: 35.7 ± 9.5, control group: 40.9 ± 12.7 years), Gunes et al .[ 13 ] (mean age: 33.05 ± 8.83 years), and Tunç et al .[ 16 ] (migraine group: 30.1 ± 7.36 years). Female preponderance was seen in both groups. Female preponderance has been reported in most of the studies.[ 15 17 ] Females suffer from migraines at about twice the rate of men. Although both biological and psychosocial factors contribute to the sex differences in migraine, it seems that sex hormones (estrogen) are the most important. Migraine attacks are associated with fluctuating hormonal levels. High estrogen levels, high estrogen fluctuations, and hormonal replacement therapy are associated with the worst migraine outcomes.[ 18 ] Compared to the studies performed by Simsek[ 15 ] on 40 migraine patients (18- migraine with aura and 22- migraine without aura) and Kanar et al .[ 19 ] on 77 migraine patients (37- migraine with aura and 40- migraine without aura), our study had a very contrasting feature: only three patients out of 100 had migraine with aura and the rest had migraine without aura. There was no explanation for the discrepancy in the results between our study and other studies, which is a limitation of our study. Due to its simplicity and ease of use, the HIT-6 questionnaire was used to determine the severity of migraine in our study, in contrast to the Migraine disability assessment test (MIDAS) score used in the studies of Simsek[ 15 ] and Kanar et al .[ 19 ] Out of 100 migraine cases, 43% had mild, 32% had moderate, and 25% had severe migraine. Moreover, 71% of the study subjects had episodic migraine. In comparison, 29% had chronic migraine, in contrast to Reggio et al .,[ 12 ] who reported episodic and chronic migraine in 43% and 57% of their cases, respectively. Certain factors like stress, menstrual cycle changes, sleep disturbances, and alcohol trigger migraine attacks. Most likely, most of our patients had an intermittent presence of these predisposing factors, leading to a higher incidence of episodic migraine compared to chronic migraine. The mean duration of migraine in study subjects was comparable to the values reported in the studies of Simsek[ 15 ] (6.2 ± 5.6 years) and Kanar et al .[ 19 ] (8.05 ± 4.7 years). Our study’s mean duration of individual migraine attacks was 9.33 ± 13.41 h. Studies by Reggio et al .[ 12 ] (28.1 ± 22.8 h) and Sorkhabi et al .[ 20 ] (25.2 ± 31.2 h) have reported considerably higher durations of individual migraine attacks. Probable reasons for the shorter duration of the individual migraine attacks in our study can be patients presenting earlier to the neurologist, a correct diagnosis, and early institution of treatment. The higher number of patients with episodic migraine in the present study could be the reason for the lesser mean number of migraine attacks per month (9.31 ± 6.57/month). This is similar to that reported in the study of Demircan et al .[ 21 ] (8.05 ± 3.16/month), but differs from that reported by Sorkhabi et al .[ 20 ] (13.6 ± 10.2/month). Migraine is a neurovascular and progressive disorder. During a migraine attack, there is vasoconstriction of the retrobulbar artery, leading to optic nerve hypoperfusion and, eventually, ganglionic cell death. Kara et al .[ 22 ] used color Doppler imaging to demonstrate that migraine patients had greater vascular resistance in the central retinal and posterior ciliary arteries during both headache and headache-free periods. Supporting this hypothesis, our study showed statistically significant ( P < 0.05) thinner pRNFL (average and all quadrants) in migraine patients compared to age- and sex-matched healthy controls. Martinez et al .[ 11 ] found thinning in the temporal quadrant, Simsek[ 15 ] and Sorkhabi et al .[ 20 ] in the nasal quadrant, Reggio et al .[ 12 ] in the nasal, temporal, and inferior quadrants, while Gunes et al .[ 13 ] and Yülek et al .[ 23 ] found no differences. This discrepancy could be attributed to the use of different OCT machines, racial variations, and the absence of standardization in the inclusion and exclusion criteria of the participants. This study examined ONH parameters since recurring constriction of the retinal and ciliary arteries can cause ischemic damage and changes to the optic disc. We found that optic disc area, cup area, vertical cup disc ratio, and rim volume were higher in migraine patients compared to healthy controls. This difference was statistically significant ( P < 0.05). The results of our study contradicted the findings of Michael et al .,[ 17 ] Martinez et al .,[ 11 ] and Yener and Korucu.[ 24 ] They reported no difference in ONH parameters. This disparity might have arisen from including individuals with supranormal values in the migraine group, which would have affected intergroup comparisons. It can also be attributed to differences in the OCT machines used in different studies. Changes in central retinal artery circulation can alter the inner retinal layers, which may be reflected in macular thickness. In the macular region, ganglionic cells form stratified multicellular layers. Thus, loss of axons due to ischemia will lead to GCL thinning. Macular parameters like superior and inferior RNFL thicknesses, CMT, and inferior GCL thickness were lesser in migraine patients ( P < 0.05) compared to healthy controls. In contrast to the present study, Demircan et al .[ 21 ] and Hamurcu et al .[ 25 ] reported no difference in the mean macular thickness between the migraine and healthy groups. Kanar et al .[ 19 ] found superior and inferior GCL thickness reduction, while Tunç et al .[ 16 ] reported no difference in GCL measurements. CMT in our study group was comparable to that reported in the study by Cankaya and Tecellioglu[ 26 ] (211.07 ± 7.36 µm). Hamurcu et al .[ 25 ] reported no difference in RNFL thickness, CMT, or optic disc area, although the optic disc rim area was larger in the migraine group. In chronic migraine, due to more frequent attacks and resultant episodes of vasospasm and hypoperfusion, there are ischemic changes in the retina and optic nerve. These changes correlate with the number of headache episodes.[ 27 28 ] Our research findings showed that individuals with chronic migraine had a significant ( P < 0.05) decrease in pRNFL thickness (average and all quadrants) and macular parameters, including CMT, superior and inferior RNFL thicknesses, as well as superior and inferior GCL thicknesses, when compared to individuals with episodic migraine. Our findings differ from those of Gunes et al .,[ 13 ] who found no differences in RNFL values, but thinner GCL values. Like the present study, Reggio et al .[ 12 ] have reported thinner RNFL, especially in the superior and inferior sectors, and GCL in chronic migraine patients compared to patients with episodic migraine. In contrast to our findings, a study by Kirbas et al .[ 29 ] reported thinning in superior quadrant RNFL thickness only (no difference in other quadrants RNFL and macular parameters) in patients with chronic migraine (CM). Since migraine is a chronic condition with varying severity, the duration and intensity of the disease may have a degrading effect on RNFL and GCL. The study’s findings revealed that migraine patients with a duration of disease >5 years exhibited thinning in pRNFL and macular parameters. Specifically, all quadrants and average values of pRNFL, as well as superior and inferior GCL thicknesses, superior and inferior macular RNFL thicknesses, and CMT showed statistically significant differences between patients with duration <5 years and >5 years ( P < 0.05). However, the ONH parameters did not show significant changes. The study suggests that as the duration of migraine increases, there is a greater extent of vasoconstriction, impacting blood flow to the choroid and retina. Several studies have reported similar results. Gunes et al .[ 13 ] found an inverse correlation between headache duration and superior and temporal RNFL, Yülek et al .[ 23 ] found an inverse correlation between duration and average RNFL thickness, and Abdellatif and Fouad[ 30 ] found an inverse correlation of GCL thickness with duration. However, the findings of Gipponi et al .,[ 31 ] Kirbas et al .[ 29 ] (no correlation with RNFL), and Cankaya and Tecellioglu[ 26 ] (no correlation with CMT) contradicted our study. Our study found an inverse correlation of severity with pRNFL thickness (average and all quadrants) and macular parameters (CMT, superior and inferior GCL, and superior and inferior RNFL). Similar to our study, Kanar et al .[ 19 ] and Abdellatif and Fouad[ 30 ] found that severity (MIDAS score) was inversely correlated with superior and inferior GCL; the former found a significant inverse correlation with average, superior, and nasal RNFL thicknesses and the latter found a significant inverse correlation with inferior and temporal RNFL thicknesses only. The present study has a few limitations. The study was limited to a specific geographic area, and the results cannot be generalized to migraine patients from other areas. Since the study was conducted in an urban setting at a tertiary institute, the study population might not reflect true representatives of the general population of rural areas. OCT angiography could have helped in supporting our hypothesis of chronic ischemia in migraine being responsible for changes in retinal and ONH parameters. However, choroidal thickness and assessment of retinal vascular plexuses could not be done because of the absence of the requisite technology in our center. We also did not perform visual field analysis in our subjects, the addition of which could have further increased the value of the study. The study is cross sectional in nature; the effect of disease progression on the retinal parameters could not be assessed.

[conclusion] Conclusion The present study reports a significant variation in pRNFL, ONH, and macular parameters in migraine patients compared to age- and gender-matched healthy individuals. Patients with a history of longer duration (>5 years) of migraine had significantly thinner pRNFL. Based on our findings, we recommend careful examination of retinal and ONH parameters in migraine patients at regular intervals to avoid any future complications. Conflicts of interest There are no conflicts of interest.

retrieved_papers.title: Choroidal Control Technology: New Horizons in Maculopathy and Presbyopia.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] To evaluate changes in choroidal thickness in presbyopes, when reading with regular glasses versus choroidal control glasses, in patients with or without Age-Related Macular Degeneration (AMD).
This was a pilot study on short-term axial length (AL) in 33 eyes of 24 presbyopic patients aged 60 to 80 years, assigned to two age-matched groups, with or without AMD. About them, changes in choroidal thickness were evaluated with ocular biometry through indirect measurements of axial length at baseline, after 20' of reading with conventional lenses, and after another 20' of reading with peripheral hyperopic defocus glasses. The differences in axial length between the three different times were analyzed.
In presbyopes without AMD there was a significant axial length shortening of -13.44 microns in the first conventional reading period, which was reversed by 90% with hyperopic defocus lenses, recovering + 12.11 microns by axial lengthening (choroidal thinning, p = 0.03). In patients with AMD, axial shortening was significantly greater than controls, -23.86 microns with conventional lenses (p < 0.001) and they, also increased their axial length with defocus, although this response was smaller in proportion (+ 15.52 microns).
Reading with positive lenses produces myopic defocus and choroidal thickening in presbyopes with and without AMD but was significantly greater in the latter. Glasses with Choroidal Control Technology reduced thickening during reading.
What is known • Presbyopia spectacles for near produce myopic defocus and choroidal thickening. What is new • There are differences in choroidal thickening during reading between normal subjects and those with age related macular degeneration. • Spectacles with Defocus Choroidal Control Technology reduce choroidal thickening during reading in presbyopes.

retrieved_papers.title: Ocular Microvascular Changes in Offspring Born to Mothers With Gestational Diabetes Mellitus: The Nanjing Eye Study.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] To evaluate the microvascular differences in macular retina and choriocapillaris (CC) between offspring born to mothers with gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) and unexposed controls.
This population-based cross-sectional study was based on the Nanjing Eye Study. Children 7 years old completed detailed ocular examinations in 2019. Quantitative parameters of ocular microvasculature in the macular region were assessed using optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA) and information about pregnancy and birth history was obtained from a comprehensive questionnaire. Generalized linear models were performed to assess associations between maternal GDM and ocular OCTA parameters adjusted for confounding factors.
A total of 681 children (mean age ± SD, 7.44 ± 0.27 years) had complete eye examination and questionnaire data, among whom 41 (6.0%) were born to mothers with GDM. In generalized linear models, offspring exposed to maternal GDM had significantly increased vessel density (VD) of the deep vascular complex (DVC) in the parafoveal temporal quadrant (difference = 1.238, P = 0.025), larger size of CC flow voids (difference = 0.134, P = 0.040), and larger area of CC flow voids (difference = 0.558, P = 0.018) compared with unexposed offspring. No significant differences were observed between the two groups in VD of superficial vascular complex, foveal avascular zone, macular thickness, or subfoveal choroidal thickness (all P > 0.05).
Our data showed that GDM offspring have decreased CC perfusion and increased VD of DVC compared with normal peers. These findings suggest that GDM has a potential effect on ocular microvasculature in offspring.
This study indicates that greater attention should be paid to eye growth and development in GDM offspring.

[Introduction] Introduction Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is defined as glucose intolerance that occurs during pregnancy, and it has become one of the most common pregnancy disorders. 1 A recent meta-analysis showed that the total prevalence of GDM in mainland China is 14.8%. 2 Women with GDM have a higher risk of developing diabetes later in life, and their offspring also have increased risks of developing obesity, metabolic syndrome, impaired insulin sensitivity and secretion, diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. 3 – 5 In addition, previous studies have found that children born to mothers with GDM are more likely to have refractive errors and various ophthalmic conditions, including uveitis, congenital cataract, and strabismic amblyopia. 1 , 6 , 7 With the development of ophthalmic examination technology allowing imaging of the retinal structure, maternal diabetes during pregnancy has been found to be associated with thinning of the pericentral macular thickness in offspring. 8 Diabetic patients with or without retinopathy have been consistently identified to have flow impairment in the capillaries of retina and choroid for the past few decades. 9 – 12 Offspring of mothers with GDM are eight times as likely to develop prediabetes or diabetes than offspring of mothers without GDM. 5 Therefore, there is curiosity about the changes in ocular microvasculature in GDM offspring, which has not yet been evaluated in any studies as far as we know. Thus, the purpose of this study was to report microvascular alterations in the macular retina and choriocapillaris (CC) in GDM offspring using optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA)—a novel, non-invasive technology that can rapidly visualize retinal and choroidal vascular flow.

[introduction] Introduction Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) is defined as glucose intolerance that occurs during pregnancy, and it has become one of the most common pregnancy disorders. 1 A recent meta-analysis showed that the total prevalence of GDM in mainland China is 14.8%. 2 Women with GDM have a higher risk of developing diabetes later in life, and their offspring also have increased risks of developing obesity, metabolic syndrome, impaired insulin sensitivity and secretion, diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. 3 – 5 In addition, previous studies have found that children born to mothers with GDM are more likely to have refractive errors and various ophthalmic conditions, including uveitis, congenital cataract, and strabismic amblyopia. 1 , 6 , 7 With the development of ophthalmic examination technology allowing imaging of the retinal structure, maternal diabetes during pregnancy has been found to be associated with thinning of the pericentral macular thickness in offspring. 8 Diabetic patients with or without retinopathy have been consistently identified to have flow impairment in the capillaries of retina and choroid for the past few decades. 9 – 12 Offspring of mothers with GDM are eight times as likely to develop prediabetes or diabetes than offspring of mothers without GDM. 5 Therefore, there is curiosity about the changes in ocular microvasculature in GDM offspring, which has not yet been evaluated in any studies as far as we know. Thus, the purpose of this study was to report microvascular alterations in the macular retina and choriocapillaris (CC) in GDM offspring using optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA)—a novel, non-invasive technology that can rapidly visualize retinal and choroidal vascular flow.

[methods] Methods Study Population The study participants are from the population-based Nanjing Eye Study (NES). Details of the NES have been previously reported. 13 – 15 In brief, all children born in Yuhuatai District, Nanjing, China, between September 2011 and August 2012 and entering kindergartens in Yuhuatai District were invited to participate in the NES for annual comprehensive eye examinations beginning in 2015. The data presented in this paper were obtained in 2019, when these children were 7 years old and had entered primary school. The study was approved by the Ethics Committee of The First Affiliated Hospital with Nanjing Medical University and followed the tenets of the Declaration of Helsinki. Written informed consent was obtained from parents or legal guardians of all participants. Oral assent was obtained from all children right before the examination. Ocular and Anthropometric Examinations Comprehensive eye examinations for all participants were performed by a team composed of six trained ophthalmologists and four optometrists, and the exams included visual acuity, ocular surface, anterior segment, posterior segment, refraction, stereoacuity test, ocular alignment and motility, ocular biometric parameters, intraocular pressure and OCTA. Axial length (AL) and corneal radius of curvature (CR) were measured by an IOLMaster 500 (Carl Zeiss Meditec, Jena, Germany). Noncycloplegic refractive status was measured using retinoscopy. The spherical equivalent (SE) was calculated as the sphere power plus half of the cylinder power. Anthropometric parameters included height and weight, which were measured without shoes and heavy clothing. Body mass index (BMI) was defined as body weight divided by the square of body height and was expressed in units of kg/m 2 . The scanning for microvasculature in macular retina and CC was performed using OCTA (Optovue RTVue XR Avanti; Optovue, Fremont, CA). It has an A-scan rate of 70,000 scans per second, uses 840-nm wavelength with a bandwidth of 50 nm, and adopts the split-spectrum amplitude decorrelation angiography (SSADA) algorithm to improve the signal-to-noise ratio. In this study, the OCTA images were obtained by scanning an area of 6 × 6 mm for macula, centered on the fovea. Retinal layers were automatically segmented by the AngioVue software. The superficial vascular complex (SVC) extends from the internal limiting membrane to 10 µm above the lower boundary of the inner plexiform layer (IPL). The deep vascular complex (DVC) extends from 10 µm above the lower boundary of the IPL to 10 µm below the lower boundary of the outer plexiform layer. The vessel density (VD) was defined as the proportion of vessel area showing blood flow relative to the total area measured. The images were then divided into three concentric circles with diameters of 1 mm, 3 mm, and 6 mm into fovea, parafovea, and perifovea regions, which were further divided into temporal, superior, nasal, and inferior quadrants, as described by the Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS). 16 The VDs of the SVC and DVC and the retinal thickness in each quadrant were calculated automatically by the AngioVue software. The Optovue three-dimensional (3D) projection artifacts removal algorithms were used in the AngioVue software to remove “false” blood flow signals caused by the SVC in the DVC. The area (mm 2 ), perimeter (mm), and acircularity index of the foveal avascular zone (FAZ) were automatically calculated. The acircularity index was defined as the ratio of the measured perimeter to the circumference of a circle with equal area, representing deviation from circularity. Bennett's formula was used to determine a scaling factor for adjustment for all vascular and structural data in macular retina (scaling factor = 3.46 × 0.013062 × [AL − 1.82]). 17 All OCTA images were reviewed independently by two investigators to ensure sufficient imaging quality and correct segmentation. Images with decentration, motion artifacts, defocus, projection artifacts, segmentation errors, or an overall quality index < 6 were excluded from analyses. Disagreements between the two investigators were adjudicated by a senior ophthalmologist. As described previously, 18 , 19 we imported the OCTA images into MATLAB (MathWorks, Natick, MA) and segmented the CC slabs (31–59 µm below the retinal pigment epithelium). The SVC image was thresholded to create a binary mask, which was then overlaid on the CC image ( Fig. 1 C) to remove the vessel shadow artifacts from the SVC. A Gaussian smoothing filter (σ = 20 µm) was applied to reduce the speckle noise in the CC image. After that, the average pixel intensity of the CC image was calculated, and pixels below the average intensity by 1 standard deviation (SD) were considered to be flow voids in the CC image ( Fig. 1 D). The area of CC flow voids was defined as a percentage between the region that was absent from flow and the total scanned region. Finally, the image of the CC flow voids was binarized and imported into ImageJ (National Institutes of Health, Bethesda, MD) for calculating the size and number of flow voids. The “Distribution” command with the parameter set to “area” was used to calculate the average size of the flow voids. The “Analyze Particles” command was used to calculate the number of flow voids. The subfoveal choroidal thickness (SFCT) was defined as the distance from the outer border of the retinal pigment epithelium to the inner border of the sclera running through the center of the fovea, and it was measured by a trained examiner using a manual caliper of the AngioVue software. Intra-grader reliability for the SFCT was high, with an intraclass correlation of 0.988 based on 300 eyes with SFCT measured twice. Figure 1. Method for quantifying CC flow voids. ( A ) En face OCTA images of the SVC. ( B ) En face OCTA images of CC. ( C ) Flow voids ( green ) overlaid onto the CC image, with projection artifacts ( red ) from the SVC removed. ( D ) Binarized CC image after artifact removal. Questionnaire The history of pregnancy and birth was obtained from a comprehensive questionnaire. Parents of each participant were asked, “Did you or your wife have any disease during pregnancy?” The possible answers were none, GDM, gestational hypertension, and other. Exclusion Criteria Children were excluded if they had a history of maternal pregnancy disease (other than GDM), fundus disease, ocular surgery, or systemic disease, or if they could not complete the examinations or had missing questionnaire data. Statistical Analyses Data analyses were performed using the SPSS Statistics for Windows 13.0 (IBM, Chicago, IL). Ocular examination data obtained from the right eye of each participant were used for analyses. Continuous variables were presented as mean ± SD, and categorical variables were summarized as frequency and percentage. Independent two-sample t -tests were used to compare means, and the χ 2 test was used to compare proportions. Generalized linear models were performed to assess associations between maternal GDM and ocular OCTA parameters in offspring, adjusted for age, gender, BMI, birth weight, premature history, and the AL/CR ratio. Adjusted means of OCTA parameters in the GDM and control groups were also calculated. Two-sided P values < 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

[results] Results The analyses included 681 children (mean age ± SD, 7.44 ± 0.27 years), among whom 41 (6.0%) had a history of maternal GDM. Table 1 compares the characteristics of the GDM and control groups. SE in the GDM group was more hyperopic than that in the control group (−0.03 D vs. −0.37 D; P = 0.002). The average VD of the DVC in the GDM group was larger than that in the control group (53.57% vs. 51.92%; P = 0.005); this trend was also observed in parafoveal (temporal, nasal, and inferior quadrants) and perifoveal (temporal, superior, nasal, and inferior quadrants) regions. The area of CC flow voids in the GDM group was also larger than that in the control group (17.92% vs. 17.39%; P = 0.018). Figure 2 shows DVC and CC images from an offspring exposed to GDM and a healthy control. There were no significant differences in the VD of the SVC, macular thickness, FAZ, or subfoveal choroidal thickness between the two groups. Table 1. Associations of maternal GDM and ocular microvascular parameters in offspring are shown in Table 2 . After adjustment for age, gender, BMI, birth weight, premature history, and AL/CR ratio in generalized linear models, offspring exposed to maternal GDM had significantly increased VD of DVC in the parafoveal temporal quadrant (difference = 1.238, P = 0.025), larger size of CC flow voids (difference = 0.134, P = 0.040), and larger area of CC flow voids (difference = 0.558, P = 0.018) compared with unexposed offspring.
