[discussion] Discussion In this study, we found that offspring born to mothers with GDM had larger area and size of CC flow voids at 7 years of age, indicating that GDM might influence the choroidal microvasculature in offspring from early life on. Furthermore, offspring born to mothers with GDM had increased VD of the DVC in the parafoveal temporal quadrant compared with those born to mothers without GDM, which might be a compensation for the defected blood flow in the CC. Diabetic retinopathy (DR) is a common microvascular complication of diabetes. Previous studies using OCTA have found that diabetes will affect both retinal and choroidal vessels, resulting in significant changes in retinal and choroidal blood flow. 9 – 12 , 20 Because the choroid is the vascular layer that supplies the outer retina, choroidal blood flow has attracted more and more attention. Recent studies have found that the CC blood flow of DR patients is generally reduced compared with healthy people. In diabetic patients without DR, the decrease in CC blood flow is also obvious. 9 , 21 Therefore, these studies suggest that CC microvascular changes occur early in diabetes and may play a role in the pathogenesis of DR. It has been reported that the microvascular ischemia originated in the choroidal layer and extended inward, affecting the deep and superficial layers of retina. 11 CC flow voids have been used to assess CC blood perfusion, reflecting areas in the CC with low perfusion or without perfusion. The quantitative analysis of CC flow voids used in our study has shown good repeatability in previous studies. 19 , 22 – 24 Offspring born to mothers with GDM have been found to have a high risk of developing diabetes. 5 Our study found that the area and size of CC flow voids both increased in offspring exposed to maternal GDM, suggesting that these offspring might have already had reduced CC blood perfusion before they developed diabetes or DR. The reduced CC perfusion might serve as an early screening biomarker of diabetes or DR in GDM offspring. Moreover, maternal diabetes during pregnancy has been reported to be associated with an increased risk of myopia in offspring. 7 This association might be partially mediated by reduced CC perfusion in these offspring, which might impair scleral oxygen supply and subsequently trigger hypoxia-induced remodeling of the scleral extracellular matrix. Therefore, the reduced CC perfusion might also be a sign of developing myopia in GDM offspring. However, compared with previous studies, 9 , 25 the differences in the area and size of CC flow voids between the GDM and control groups seemed slight in our study. This might be due to the young age (7 years old) of the offspring in our study, who had not yet developed any organic diseases. In contrast, the study populations in previous studies have been about 40 to 60 years old, and they had already developed diabetes; some of them had even developed microaneurysms in the fundus, the earliest clinical sign of DR. We also found that offspring born to mothers with GDM had increased VD of the DVC in the parafoveal temporal quadrant, but there were no differences between the GDM and control groups in the VD of the DVC in other regions or in the VD of the SVC. Several studies found no significant differences in either the superficial or deep capillary plexuses between diabetic eyes without DR and healthy eyes. 9 , 26 , 27 However, other studies have found increased retinal blood flow in diabetic eyes without DR compared with healthy eyes. 28 – 30 Among them, a study reported that retinal blood velocity and flow were increased in diabetic eyes without DR but decreased in diabetic eyes with mild non-proliferative DR, compared with healthy age-matched controls. 28 This finding suggested an initial increase followed by a progressive decline in retinal blood flow as diabetic eyes progress from having no retinopathy to developing retinopathy, and the initial increase in retinal blood flow might be a compensation for the potential hypoxia in the retina. We also speculate that the increased VD of the DVC in the parafoveal temporal quadrant in GDM offspring found in our study might be a compensation for the decreased blood flow in CC to maintain oxygen supply to the outer retina. Animal studies have demonstrated that, under physiological conditions, choroidal circulation serves as the dominant oxygen source for the outer retina, whereas retinal circulation plays a negligible role; however, under systemic hypoxia, the retinal circulation increases oxygen delivery to the outer retina to compensate for reduced oxygen supply from the choroidal circulation. 31 – 33 In this study, we did not find any differences in macular thickness between the GDM and control groups. However, a previous study found that children from diabetic pregnancies had significantly thinner macular thickness compared with children from nondiabetic pregnancies. 8 This discrepancy might be due to two reasons. First, diabetic pregnancies are not the same as GDM pregnancies, as they also include chronic diabetes complicated by pregnancy, which might have more influence on the ocular health in offspring. Second, the offspring in our study were much younger than those in the previous study (age range, 11.1–14.4 years), so that an impact of GDM on macular thickness in offspring was not shown. The serum glucose from pregnant women is transported to the fetus through the placenta by facilitated diffusion. About half of the serum glucose is used by the placenta and the fetus, and the rest is excreted into the amniotic fluid via direct penetration or fetal urine. Maternal hyperglycemia can affect the glucose transporter expression and glucose transport activity in the placenta, causing an increase in glucose uptake. 34 , 35 The fetus exposed to high glucose levels in the uterus has increased secretion of insulin to compensate for the high glucose, which may cause corresponding metabolic disorders. 5 , 8 Chronic hyperglycemia impacts various pathways and leads to oxidative stresses, further causing mitochondrial dysfunction, dysfunction of inflammatory mediator, and hypoxia. Histopathological studies have observed that metabolic disorders caused by diabetes are related to variable choroidal abnormalities and mainly limited to the CC, including vascular dropout. 36 – 38 Zhang et al. 39 used chick embryos to establish a hyperglycemia model. The expression of Pax6 , Six3 , and Otx2 genes was inhibited in the model, inducing eye malformation. Although this result cannot be directly extrapolated to humans, we need to be vigilant about the possible impact of hyperglycemia on the development of children's eyes. 1 , 6 As far as we know, our research is the first study that evaluates the influence of GDM on microvascular changes in macular retina and CC in offspring using OCTA. We used quantitative parameters to analyze the influence of GDM on ocular microvasculature and minimized the artifacts as much as possible. Our research also has some limitations. First, the prevalence of GDM (6.0%) in our study was much lower than that reported in mainland China (14.8%), 2 which might be due to recall bias, as we collected the history of maternal GDM through a questionnaire. However, the prevalence of GDM varies substantially across different regions of mainland China. In Jiangsu Province, studies have reported a GDM prevalence ranging from 5.9% to 16.5%. 40 , 41 A meta-analysis from China also reported that the prevalence of GDM from 2012 to 2016 was significantly higher than that from 2005 to 2012, which might be related to the improvement of living standards. 42 Thus, the low prevalence of GDM in our study might also be due to the offspring in our study being born between 2011 and 2012. Second, we failed to collect blood test indexes of GDM, such as blood glucose level and glycosylated hemoglobin A1, which makes us unable to further explore the relationship between the severity of GDM and ocular microvascular changes in offspring. Third, as a cross-sectional study, we could not make any causal inference for the associations we found. Fourth, the CC flow voids we measured might be affected by the blood velocity in the CC, which may cause measurement error. Fifth, the FAZ parameters were derived from full-thickness retinal vasculature images, which precluded the separate analysis of the FAZ metrics for the SVC and DVC. Finally, our analysis of multiple retinal regions raises the issue of multiple comparisons, which increases the risk of type I error. However, higher VD in the DVC of the GDM offspring group compared to controls was consistently observed in many retinal regions. Although these differences might not reach statistical significance after adjusting for multiple comparisons, our findings indicate an underlying biological phenomenon worthy of further investigation in larger, confirmatory studies. More research is necessary to clarify the potential effect of GDM on ocular development in offspring.

[conclusion] Conclusions In this cross-sectional population-based study, we found that offspring born to mothers with GDM had larger area and size of CC flow voids, as well as increased VD of the DVC in the parafoveal temporal quadrant, compared with those born to mothers without GDM. These findings suggest that GDM has a potential effect on ocular microvasculature in offspring. Offspring exposed to maternal GDM might be recommended for more comprehensive eye examinations and intensive follow-up observations.

retrieved_papers.title: Longitudinal changes in choroidal thickness in high myopia: Correlation with maculopathy progression and visual outcomes.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] To evaluate longitudinal changes in choroidal thickness (CT) in highly myopic eyes and their correlation with myopic maculopathy progression and visual outcomes.
Retrospective cohort study on 1228 eyes from 781 highly myopic patients with a minimum 5-year follow-up (mean 11.5 ± 3.1 years). Myopic atrophic maculopathy (MAM) was graded according to ATN classification. Subfoveal CT was measured using spectral-domain OCT with enhanced depth imaging. Staphyloma classification followed Curtin's system.
Mean subfoveal CT at baseline was 54.3 ± 32.7 μm, with decreasing values by MAM category: tessellated fundus (172.5 ± 42.6 μm), diffuse chorioretinal atrophy (63.7 ± 27.3 μm), patchy atrophy (39.2 ± 18.3 μm) and macular atrophy (7.2 ± 11.3 μm, often unmeasurable). Different thinning rates were observed: diffuse atrophy (3.1 ± 0.9 μm/year), patchy atrophy (2.1 ± 0.8 μm/year), macular atrophy (1.3 ± 0.7 μm/year) and tessellated fundus (1.2 ± 0.6 μm/year). Lowest CT values were observed in eyes with type IX staphyloma (22.4 ± 12.1 μm). Axial length ≥ 29 mm was associated with lower CT (32.4 ± 18.9 vs. 78.6 ± 36.2 μm, p < 0.001). Baseline CT <40 μm independently predicted macular atrophy development (OR 3.84, p < 0.001) and visual decline (OR 2.76, p < 0.001). Eyes with dome-shaped macula showed thicker CT at dome peak compared with peridome edges (62.3 ± 34.2 vs. 48.4 ± 22.7 μm, p = 0.002). Eyes developing myopic macular neovascularization (mMNV) had lower baseline CT (32.7 ± 17.8 vs. 58.6 ± 33.1 μm, p < 0.001).
The fastest choroidal thinning occurred in eyes with diffuse chorioretinal atrophy, while eyes with severely reduced baseline CT had a higher risk for macular atrophy development and visual impairment. Different staphyloma types showed variable CT patterns.

retrieved_papers.title: Retinal and Choroidal Alterations in Heart Failure: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis of OCT and OCT-A Findings with Emphasis on HFrEF.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] Retina and choroid, given their high metabolic demands, are vulnerable to ischemic injury during the hemodynamic instability associated with heart failure (HF). Despite this, posterior segment ocular changes in HF remain understudied. This systematic review and meta-analysis consolidates optical coherence tomography (OCT) and OCT angiography (OCT-A) findings in HF.
We conducted a comprehensive search of Web of Science, PubMed/MEDLINE, and EMBASE on March 15, 2025. A random-effects meta-analysis of six studies was performed using Hedges' g to estimate effect sizes, with six additional studies included for qualitative synthesis (total n = 1139 eyes). The meta-analysis was limited to studies on heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF), as this was the only heart failure subtype with sufficient data for quantitative analysis.
Key findings revealed significant reductions in parafoveal superficial capillary plexus vessel density (SCP VD) (Hedges' g = - 0.42, 95% CI - 0.78 to - 0.05, p = 0.03) and subfoveal choroidal thickness (Hedges' g = - 0.75, 95% CI - 1.31 to - 0.18, p = 0.01) in patients with HFrEF. Furthermore, severity-dependent thinning of retinal nerve fiber layer and significant correlations between whole macular vessel density and both left ventricular EF and New York Heart Association functional class emphasize the potential of these parameters in stratifying disease severity. Additionally, one study revealed thinning of ganglion cell-inner plexiform layer in patients with HF with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF). Those with acute decompensated HF showed macular VD attenuation in superficial layers and decreased subfoveal choroidal thickness. Pediatric cases demonstrated central macular thinning, selective decreases in SCP VD, and choroidal thinning.
Collectively, OCT and OCT-A metrics provide useful information regarding systemic microvascular dysfunction, suggesting potential for disease detection and improved risk stratification. However, it is important to note that these findings are based on observational studies, which limits the ability to draw definitive causal conclusions. This highlights the need for prospective, longitudinal research to establish temporal relationships and validate these observations.

[Introduction] Introduction Heart failure (HF) is a progressive clinical syndrome characterized by systemic hypoperfusion and multiorgan dysfunction due to impaired cardiac output and/or elevated filling pressures. Among the organs affected by this microcirculatory disturbance, the eye, particularly the retina and choroid, serves as a unique window into systemic vascular health [ 1 , 2 ]. The retinal circulation, supplied by both the central retinal artery and the choroidal vasculature, is exquisitely sensitive to perfusion deficits and endothelial dysfunction [ 3 ]. Given its exceptionally high metabolic demand, the retina is especially vulnerable to ischemic injury during hemodynamic instability in HF [ 4 ]. In HF, systemic fluctuations in blood flow, including to the retina and choroid, can lead to intermittent hypoxia and nutrient deprivation. Subsequent reperfusion, while necessary for oxygen delivery and tissue survival, paradoxically exacerbates damage through ischemia–reperfusion injury, ultimately triggering apoptotic cell death in retinal neurons and vasculature [ 5 , 6 ]. These mechanisms may underlie the structural and microvascular alterations of retina and choroid observed in patients with HF. Advances in optical coherence tomography (OCT) and OCT angiography (OCT-A) have revolutionized in-depth analysis of retinal and choroidal integrity, enabling detailed evaluation of layer thickness, microvascular density, and perfusion patterns [ 7 – 10 ]. Growing evidence suggests that these changes correlate with HF severity and may serve as prognostic biomarkers [ 6 , 11 ]. Notably, both HF with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) and preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF) exhibit ocular microvascular remodeling, though their distinct hemodynamic profiles may differentially affect posterior segment morphology [ 12 , 13 ]. While most studies focus on chronic HF, acute decompensated HF also induces transient but detectable alterations [ 14 ]. Pediatric HF, though less studied, demonstrates similar microvascular perturbations, highlighting the systemic nature of HF-related endothelial dysfunction [ 4 , 5 , 15 ]. Although posterior segment alterations have been well documented in conditions affecting systemic vascular health like coronary artery disease [ 16 – 18 ], diabetes [ 19 ], and hypertension [ 20 ], HF-specific involvement remains understudied. Retinal and choroidal imaging may offer a noninvasive, accessible approach to aid in early HF detection, disease stratification, and progression monitoring. This systematic review synthesizes current evidence on posterior segment structural and microvascular changes in HF, primarily focusing on adults with chronic HFrEF compared to healthy controls. Additionally, we examine findings in HFpEF, acute HF, and pediatric populations, and discuss the potential clinical utility of ocular imaging in HF management.

[introduction] Introduction Heart failure (HF) is a progressive clinical syndrome characterized by systemic hypoperfusion and multiorgan dysfunction due to impaired cardiac output and/or elevated filling pressures. Among the organs affected by this microcirculatory disturbance, the eye, particularly the retina and choroid, serves as a unique window into systemic vascular health [ 1 , 2 ]. The retinal circulation, supplied by both the central retinal artery and the choroidal vasculature, is exquisitely sensitive to perfusion deficits and endothelial dysfunction [ 3 ]. Given its exceptionally high metabolic demand, the retina is especially vulnerable to ischemic injury during hemodynamic instability in HF [ 4 ]. In HF, systemic fluctuations in blood flow, including to the retina and choroid, can lead to intermittent hypoxia and nutrient deprivation. Subsequent reperfusion, while necessary for oxygen delivery and tissue survival, paradoxically exacerbates damage through ischemia–reperfusion injury, ultimately triggering apoptotic cell death in retinal neurons and vasculature [ 5 , 6 ]. These mechanisms may underlie the structural and microvascular alterations of retina and choroid observed in patients with HF. Advances in optical coherence tomography (OCT) and OCT angiography (OCT-A) have revolutionized in-depth analysis of retinal and choroidal integrity, enabling detailed evaluation of layer thickness, microvascular density, and perfusion patterns [ 7 – 10 ]. Growing evidence suggests that these changes correlate with HF severity and may serve as prognostic biomarkers [ 6 , 11 ]. Notably, both HF with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) and preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF) exhibit ocular microvascular remodeling, though their distinct hemodynamic profiles may differentially affect posterior segment morphology [ 12 , 13 ]. While most studies focus on chronic HF, acute decompensated HF also induces transient but detectable alterations [ 14 ]. Pediatric HF, though less studied, demonstrates similar microvascular perturbations, highlighting the systemic nature of HF-related endothelial dysfunction [ 4 , 5 , 15 ]. Although posterior segment alterations have been well documented in conditions affecting systemic vascular health like coronary artery disease [ 16 – 18 ], diabetes [ 19 ], and hypertension [ 20 ], HF-specific involvement remains understudied. Retinal and choroidal imaging may offer a noninvasive, accessible approach to aid in early HF detection, disease stratification, and progression monitoring. This systematic review synthesizes current evidence on posterior segment structural and microvascular changes in HF, primarily focusing on adults with chronic HFrEF compared to healthy controls. Additionally, we examine findings in HFpEF, acute HF, and pediatric populations, and discuss the potential clinical utility of ocular imaging in HF management.

[methods] Methods This systematic review was designed and executed following the established Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) guidelines [ 21 ]. Prior to commencement, the research protocol was registered with International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews (PROSPERO) (Registration ID CRD420251018334). This article is based on previously conducted studies and does not contain any new studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors. Search Strategy A comprehensive electronic search was conducted across three major databases (Web of Science, EMBASE, and PubMed/MEDLINE) on March 15, 2025. An additional search of gray literature was conducted using Google Scholar. The search strategy aimed to explore all relevant studies investigating structural and microvascular changes in the retina and choroid of patients with HF using OCT or OCT-A imaging modalities. The search terms combined “heart failure” with either “optical coherence tomography” or “optical coherence tomography angiography”. The complete search algorithms for each database are available in Supplementary Material. To ensure no potentially relevant studies were missed, we implemented backward and forward citation tracking methods. No limitations were applied regarding publication date or country of origin. Eligible study designs included case–control and cross-sectional studies, while we excluded experimental studies (in vitro and in vivo), editorial correspondence, case reports, case series, conference abstracts, and review articles. All identified records were compiled in EndNote 20, where duplicate removal was performed through both automated functions and manual verification by two independent researchers (KB and PP). Any disagreements were resolved through consensus discussion. Study Selection and Eligibility Criteria Two authors (KB and PP) independently screened the articles to determine their eligibility, with any disagreements resolved through discussion. Studies were included if they (1) were original research published in English; (2) involved human participants; (3) examined patients with HF without coexisting ocular pathology; and (4) included a healthy control group without ocular or systemic disorders. Notably, we did not restrict inclusion on the basis of HF duration or severity. While six studies were excluded from quantitative synthesis, they were retained for qualitative analysis. These comprised investigations of special populations (pediatric HF [ 4 , 5 , 15 ], HFpEF [ 12 ], acute decompensated HF [ 14 ]) and one study reporting inconsistent OCT measurements [ 11 ]. The meta-analysis was limited to studies on HFrEF, as this was the only heart failure subtype with sufficient data for quantitative analysis. The complete study selection process is depicted in the PRISMA flowchart (Fig. 1 ). Fig. 1 PRISMA flowchart Data Extraction Two investigators (KB and PP) independently extracted data using a standardized form that captured (1) study characteristics (first author, publication year, geographic origin, study design); (2) participant demographics (e.g., age, gender), sample size, and eye selection method; (3) clinical parameters [medical history, body mass index, blood pressure, HF etiology, New York Heart Association (NYHA) classification, left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF)]; (4) ophthalmic examination results (visual acuity, axial length, intraocular pressure); (5) manufacturer and model of the OCT/OCT-A device; and (6) quantitative OCT/OCT-A parameters. Any discrepancies in data extraction were settled through joint review and discussion. Risk of Bias Assessment Study quality was assessed using the Newcastle–Ottawa Scale [ 22 ], which evaluates three key domains: selection, comparability, and exposure/outcome. Two reviewers (KB, PP) independently scored each study, with any discrepancies resolved through consensus meetings. All studies received scores of at least 7 (Supplementary Material). Statistical Analysis We conducted a random-effects model meta-analysis using Hedges’ g as the effect size measure, with restricted maximum likelihood estimation for variance components. Heterogeneity was quantified using tau-squared ( τ 2 ) and I -squared ( I 2 ) statistics, with Cochran’s Q test ( p < 0.05) indicating statistical significance. A Galbraith plot was generated to visualize individual study contributions to heterogeneity. Sensitivity analysis was conducted by sequentially excluding each study and observing the impact on pooled estimates. Publication bias was assessed using multiple approaches: Egger’s regression test, Begg’s rank correlation test, and visual inspection of funnel plot symmetry. All analyses were performed using Stata software (version 17), with statistical significance set at p < 0.05.

[results] Results Study Selection A systematic Literature search identified 1320 potential studies (99 from PubMed, 1091 from EMBASE, and 130 from Web of Science). After removal of 181 duplicate records, 1139 articles underwent title and abstract screening, of which 1128 were excluded for irrelevance. Full-text assessment of the remaining 11 articles revealed that six studies could not be included in the meta-analysis because of (1) pediatric populations [ 4 , 5 , 15 ], (2) patients with HFpEF [ 12 ], (3) acutely decompensated HF [ 14 ], and (4) inconsistent measurements [ 11 ]. However, these six studies were retained for qualitative synthesis to ensure a comprehensive evaluation of disease manifestations across distinct patient populations. Additionally, backward citation tracking yielded one eligible study, which was included in the meta-analysis [ 23 ]. The study ultimately incorporated 574 eyes from patients with HF and 565 eyes from healthy controls, totaling 1139 eyes. Figure 1 presents selection process. Characteristics of Studies Among the 12 included studies in this systematic review, four used a case–control [ 11 , 14 , 15 , 24 ] approach and eight employed a cross-sectional design [ 4 – 6 , 12 , 13 , 23 , 25 , 26 ]. The research spanned multiple regions, with contributions from Turkey (five studies) [ 6 , 11 , 23 – 25 ], followed by Poland (three studies) [ 4 , 5 , 15 ], Germany (two studies) [ 14 , 26 ], and single studies from the Netherlands [ 12 ] and Iran [ 13 ]. The primary etiology of HF was dilated cardiomyopathy in five studies [ 4 , 5 , 15 , 23 , 24 ], while three studies included patients with both ischemic and dilated cardiomyopathy [ 13 , 14 , 25 ]; the remaining studies did not specify the etiology. LVEF inclusion criteria varied: < 40% (two studies) [ 13 , 23 ], < 50% (four studies) [ 6 , 11 , 24 , 26 ], ≤ 55% (four studies) [ 4 , 5 , 15 , 25 ]. One study did not specify an LVEF cutoff [ 14 ] and another specifically included patients with HFpEF (LVEF ≥ 50%) [ 12 ]. Seven studies assessed one eye per patient [ 6 , 11 , 14 , 23 – 26 ], four included both eyes [ 4 , 5 , 13 , 15 ], and one used a mixed approach [ 12 ]. Controls were age- and gender-matched in ten studies [ 4 – 6 , 13 – 15 , 23 – 26 ], age-matched only in one [ 11 ], and unmatched in one [ 12 ]. OCT devices varied across studies: six used RTVue XR Avanti Optovue [ 4 , 5 , 13 , 15 , 24 , 26 ], while others employed DRI Triton Topcon [ 11 ], Heidelberg [ 23 , 25 ], Nikon RS-3000 [ 6 ], Zeiss Cirrus [ 14 ], and Zeiss PLEX Elite 9000 [ 12 ]. OCT imaging assessed multiple structural and vascular parameters. Macular thickness assessments covered central [ 4 , 12 – 14 , 23 ], parafoveal [ 4 ], and whole macular thickness [ 4 ]. Vascular evaluations included foveal avascular zone (FAZ) area [ 4 , 12 , 24 , 26 ], perimeter, and circularity index [ 12 ], as well as macular vessel density in the fovea [ 4 , 11 – 14 ], parafovea [ 4 , 11 , 13 , 14 , 24 , 26 ], perifovea [ 13 , 14 ], and whole macula [ 4 , 13 , 14 , 26 ]. Ganglion cell complex (GCC) parameters comprised average, superior, and inferior thickness, ganglion cell inner plexiform layer (GCIPL) thickness [ 12 ], and focal/global GCC volume loss [ 5 ]. Choroidal measurements included general thickness [ 11 ], subfoveal thickness (SFCT) [ 6 , 13 – 15 , 23 , 25 ], and thickness at 1500-μm intervals from the fovea (superior, inferior, temporal, nasal) [ 15 ], along with choroidal vascularity index (CVI) and choriocapillaris flow [ 13 ]. Retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) measurements included average thickness and quadrant-specific (superior, inferior, temporal, nasal) assessments [ 6 ]. Radial peripapillary capillary (RPC) density was evaluated for mean [ 24 , 26 ] and regional distributions (superior/inferior hemi, superotemporal/nasal, nasosuperior/inferior, inferonasal/temporal, temporoinferior/superior) [ 24 ]. Additional parameters included total retinal volume [ 12 ] and superotemporal retinal vein/artery diameters [ 14 ]. A detailed summary of study characteristics is provided in Table 1 . Table 1 Study characteristics Study Country OCT device Eyes (no.) Gender (F/M) Age (years) Case blood pressure (mmHg) LVEF (%) Case Control Case Control Case Control Systolic Diastolic Case Quantitative Khalilipur, 2023 Iran Optovue 52 64 6/20 12/20 53 ± 13 51 ± 15 112 ± 13 76 ± 12 25 ± 13 Alnawaiseh, 2021 Germany Optovue 27 31 – – 54.56 ± 10.64 52.1 ± 12.23 – – 39.56 ± 6.81 Alkan, 2023 Turkey Optovue 48 50 15/33 17/33 43.43 ± 10.24 45.32 ± 10.58 116.97 ± 9.38 75.20 ± 9.72 30.00 ± 7.57 Altinkaynak, 2014 Turkey Heidelberg 56 56 30/26 27/29 53.6 ± 1.49 53.4 ± 2.41 110 ± 11.1 80.1 ± 10.3 37.5 ± 11.50 Kanar, 2020 Turkey Nikon 72 40 – 25/15 63.0 ± 3.1 64.1 ± 2.8 110.7 ± 11.3 82.3 ± 10.3 – Alur, 2019 Turkey Heidelberg 44 44 10/34 10/34 59.3 ± 12.4 59.3 ± 8.5 118.6 ± 19.9 74.3 ± 13.8 – Qualitative Weerts, 2024 Netherlands ZEISS PLEX 22 24 16/6 10/14 74 [68–80] 68 [58–77] 141 [124–151] 81 [66–89] 62 [58–66] Abdin, 2024 Germany Cirrus/Heidelberg 40 40 13/27 14/26 78.9 ± 8.76 77.8 ± 8.7 – – 43 ± 12.8 Rakusiewicz, 2021 Poland Optovue 60 60 14/16 14/16 9.9 ± 3.57 10.16 ± 3.42 – – 49.03 ± 6.63 Rakusiewicz, 2020 Poland Optovue 60 60 14/16 14/16 9.9 ± 3.57 10.08 ± 3.41 – – 49.03 ± 6.63 Rakusiewicz, 2021 Poland Optovue 60 60 14/16 14/16 9.9 ± 3.57 11.27 ± 3.33 – – 49.03 ± 6.63 Topaloglu, 2023 Turkey Triton 33 36 15/18 16/20 73.66 ± 5.37 67.88 ± 3.94 – – – OCT optical coherence tomography, F female, M male, LVEF left ventricular ejection fraction Data Synthesis Adults with Chronic HFrEF Pooled Effect Size Estimates Figure 2 illustrates the comparison of OCT and OCT-A parameters between patients with HF and healthy controls where data pooling was feasible. The analysis demonstrated significantly lower SFCT values in patients with HF compared to controls (Hedges’ g = − 0.75, 95% CI − 1.31, − 0.18, p = 0.01), with high heterogeneity ( I 2 = 86.56%, p < 0.001). Parafoveal superficial capillary plexus (SCP) vessel density (VD) was also significantly reduced (Hedges’ g = − 0.42, 95% CI − 0.78 to − 0.05, p = 0.03), though without significant heterogeneity ( I 2 = 55.97%, p = 0.11). In contrast, parafoveal deep capillary plexus (DCP) VD did not differ significantly between groups (Hedges’ g = − 0.62, 95% CI − 1.68 to 0.43, p = 0.25), despite high heterogeneity ( I 2 = 94.17%, p < 0.001). Fig. 2 Forest plots depicting retinal and choroidal structural and vascular changes in patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction compared to healthy controls. SFCT subfoveal choroidal thickness, SCP superficial capillary plexus, DCP deep capillary plexus Sensitivity Analysis Sensitivity analyses assessed the robustness of these findings. Exclusion of studies by Khalilipur et al. [ 13 ] and Alkan et al. [ 24 ] rendered parafoveal SCP VD differences nonsignificant. Furthermore, after the studies by Khalilipur et al. [ 13 ] and Kanar et al. [ 6 ] were excluded, the observed differences in SFCT were no longer statistically significant. However, for parafoveal DCP VD, no individual study exclusion altered the overall effect sizes. Visual depictions of these analytical findings are presented in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3 Sensitivity analyses of retinal and choroidal structural and vascular changes in patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction compared to healthy controls. SFCT subfoveal choroidal thickness, SCP superficial capillary plexus, DCP deep capillary plexus Heterogeneity We assessed between-study heterogeneity using Galbraith plots, which graphically display variation in effect sizes across included studies. For all OCT and OCT-A parameters, the plots demonstrated low heterogeneity when comparing patients with HF to healthy controls (Fig. 4 ). Fig. 4 Galbraith plots of retinal and choroidal structural and vascular changes in patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction compared to healthy controls. SFCT subfoveal choroidal thickness, SCP superficial capillary plexus, DCP deep capillary plexus Publication Bias To comprehensively evaluate potential publication bias, we employed a multimodal analytical approach. First, funnel plots were systematically examined for visual asymmetry. Second, we conducted formal statistical assessments using Egger’s regression test and Begg’s rank correlation test to quantitatively measure bias. In our comparative analysis of patients with HF versus healthy controls, all three methodologies consistently demonstrated no significant evidence of publication bias (all p values > 0.05) (Fig. 5 and Supplementary Material). Fig. 5 Funnel plots of retinal and choroidal structural and vascular changes in patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction compared to healthy controls. SFCT subfoveal choroidal thickness, SCP superficial capillary plexus, DCP deep capillary plexus Meta-regression To explore potential confounders, we performed a meta-regression, limited by available data to the relationship between systemic blood pressure and SFCT. Systolic blood pressure (SBP) was a significant moderator ( p = 0.022), explaining R 2 = 67.93% of the between-study variance. By contrast, diastolic blood pressure was not significant ( p = 0.197) and accounted for a smaller share of heterogeneity ( R 2 = 22.05%). These results suggest that differences in SBP across studies may contribute to observed variability in SFCT (Table 2 ). Notably, the population in the study by Alur et al. [ 23 ] had the highest SBP, which may explain why it was the only study that did not observe a significant reduction in SFCT among patients with HF. This also likely accounts for the loss of significance in the pooled estimate during sensitivity analyses, as sequential exclusion of other studies increased the relative influence of Alur et al.’s findings. Table 2 Meta-regression of subfoveal choroidal thickness in patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction compared to healthy controls Subfoveal choroidal thickness Systolic blood pressure p value 0.022 β 0.125 R 2 (%) 67.93 Diastolic blood pressure p value 0.197 β − 0.106 R 2 (%) 22.05 Importantly, all studies included in our meta-analysis excluded participants with diabetes mellitus; therefore, diabetes could not have acted as a confounding factor for the observed changes. This exclusion reduces the likelihood that diabetic microvascular disease explains the observed associations. Qualitative Evidence Synthesis Central macular thickness (CMT) was comparable between cases and controls, a result that persisted after age/sex adjustment [ 13 , 23 ]. Whole en face SCP and DCP VD were comparable between groups [ 13 , 26 ], as were foveal and perifoveal SCP/DCP VD [ 13 ]. However, parafoveal DCP VD for superior/inferior hemi and all quadrants (superior, temporal, inferior, and nasal) was lower in HF, while parafoveal SCP VD in these same regions showed no significant difference [ 24 ]. Confirming these trends, Topaloglu et al. reported similar findings: comparable SCP VD but significantly reduced DCP VD in patients with HF. Additionally, they observed lower VD in both SCP and DCP when comparing NYHA class III versus class II patients, suggesting the potential utility of OCTA metrics as objective prognostic indicators for assessing heart failure progression [ 11 ]. Concordantly, macular flow density correlated with NYHA class and LVEF, in another study [ 26 ]. FAZ area findings were inconsistent: one study reported larger FAZ area in HF [ 24 ], while another found no difference in the superficial layer [ 26 ]. FAZ area did not correlate with LVEF [ 26 ]. Topaloglu et al. measured choroidal thickness but did not specify whether their measurements included SFCT; consequently, we excluded their data from our meta-analysis. Nevertheless, their findings align with our meta-analysis results, showing significantly reduced choroidal thickness in NYHA class II and III patients with HF compared to controls. Additionally, they observed no significant difference between NYHA II and III patients [ 11 ]. Two studies demonstrated significant correlations between SFCT and both age and LVEF [ 6 , 25 ], whereas two others observed no correlation between SFCT and LVEF [ 13 , 23 ]. Additionally, these studies found no significant correlations between SFCT and axial length, intraocular pressure, or systolic and diastolic blood pressure [ 13 , 25 ]. Choriocapillaris flow and CVI did not differ between groups, even after age/sex adjustment [ 13 ]. CVI was unrelated to systolic/diastolic blood pressure or LVEF [ 13 ]. RNFL thickness, including average, superior, inferior, nasal, and temporal quadrants, was significantly reduced in patients with HF with LVEF < 30% compared to both patients with HF with LVEF 30–50% and healthy controls. However, no significant difference was observed between patients with HF with LVEF 30–50% and controls. Notably, while RNFL thickness demonstrated a significant correlation with age, it showed no association with LVEF values in the HF cohort [ 6 ]. In the study by Alkan et al., RPC density was significantly lower in patients with HF across all regions except the superotemporal area [ 24 ]. Similarly, Alnawaiseh et al. reported reduced optic nerve head flow density in HF, unrelated to NYHA class [ 26 ]. Notably, Kanar et al. identified SFCT < 250 μm and RNFL < 97 μm as discriminative parameters for HF [ 6 ]. Adults with HFpEF Patients with HFpEF showed reduced GCIPL thickness and total retinal volume, though left eye measurements alone showed no significant difference. Similarly, macular VD was reduced in patients with HFpEF, with this finding remaining unchanged when data were stratified by right and left eyes. In contrast, average central retinal thickness, central retinal perfusion density, and all FAZ metrics (perimeter, area, and circularity index) showed no significant differences between groups in either combined or eye-specific analyses [ 12 ]. Several significant correlations were identified: (1) GCIPL thickness with both central retinal thickness and total retinal volume; (2) macular VD with FAZ perimeter and circulatory index; (3) total retinal volume with markers of diastolic dysfunction; and (4) specifically in right eyes, FAZ perimeter and area with left atrial volume index [ 12 ]. Adults with Acutely Decompensated HF Abdin et al. examined posterior segment structural and vascular changes during both the initial 1–2 days of acute decompensation and the subsequent recompensation phase after diuretic treatment. Compared to controls, patients with HF exhibited significantly reduced SFCT in both phases, while maintaining similar CMT. During acute decompensation, patients demonstrated larger vessel diameters in both the superior temporal retinal vein and artery relative to controls. The investigators also reported significantly reduced both length and area of perfused vasculature per unit area across all measured regions in superficial layers during acute decompensation: the full scan area, foveal center (1 mm), inner ring (3 mm), and outer ring (6 mm) compared to controls [ 14 ]. Children with Chronic HF Rakusiewicz et al. conducted three studies examining pediatric patients with chronic HF secondary to dilated cardiomyopathy [ 4 , 5 , 15 ]. Their findings demonstrated that average GCC thickness and superior/inferior quadrant measurements showed no significant differences between cases and controls. Both focal and global GCC volume loss were also comparable between groups. These measurements were independent of sex, age, eye laterality refractive error, LVEF, and NT-proBNP levels [ 5 ]. In a subsequent study, they observed significant reductions in whole macular, foveal (1 mm), parafoveal (3 mm) thicknesses, in addition to SCP VD across all regions. By contrast, DCP VD, whether measured across the whole macula or specifically in foveal and parafoveal regions, along with FAZ area in SCP showed no case–control differences. Notably, while foveal thickness and foveal SCP VD correlated with age, and FAZ area correlated with foveal thickness, none of these parameters correlated with LVEF or NT-proBNP levels. Furthermore, neither SCP nor DCP VD showed any correlation with sex or refractive error [ 4 ]. Their choroidal thickness evaluation revealed significantly reduced SFCT and choroidal thickness at all 1500-μm locations (superior, inferior, temporal, and nasal to the fovea) in patients with HF compared to controls. The nasal location showed the thinnest measurements while the subfoveal region remained thickest. Although girls exhibited thicker choroidal measurements than boys, choroidal thickness showed no correlation with gender, age, refractive error, NT-proBNP levels, or LVEF [ 15 ]. Table 3 presents a comprehensive overview of retinal and choroidal structural and microvascular alterations, while Fig. 6 depicts macular microvascular changes across HF subtypes and age groups. [discussion] Discussion Patients with HF show ocular microvascular and structural changes, including reduced choroidal thickness and altered retinal vessel density, which may be associated with systemic hemodynamic alterations. These manifestations might reflect interactions between systemic circulation and ocular health and could possibly serve as a tentative diagnostic indicator of cardiac dysfunction, pending further validation. Converging evidence from OCT and OCT-A studies across diverse populations substantiates these mechanistic associations, supporting the utility of these noninvasive imaging modalities as potential surrogate markers for early decompensation detection, disease monitoring, and risk stratification. However, it should be noted that these findings are derived from observational studies, precluding definitive causal or prognostic conclusions. Prospective, longitudinal studies are therefore required to establish temporal relationships. Reduced choroidal thickness emerged as a consistent finding in adults with HFrEF, acutely decompensated HF, and pediatric HF. Supporting this finding, Schuster et al. demonstrated that reduced LVEF, as a cardiovascular risk factor, is significantly associated with choroidal thickness [ 27 ]. Given the choroid’s high metabolic demand [ 28 ], its compensatory capacity to counteract perfusion pressure fluctuations [ 29 – 33 ] appears insufficient to withstand the microvascular changes seen in HF. One proposed mechanism is that low cardiac output in HF reduces systemic blood flow, triggering compensatory vasoconstriction in peripheral vessels, including ocular arteries, to prioritize oxygen delivery to vital organs [ 13 ]. Over time, this may damage choroidal arteries, restrict oxygen and nutrient delivery [ 14 , 15 , 34 ], and induce ischemic changes that promote retinal pigment epithelial atrophy and reduced SFCT [ 25 , 35 – 37 ]. The choroid’s vulnerability stems from its reliance on terminal vascular branches with limited collateral circulation [ 14 ]. Furthermore, elevated central venous pressure may impair venous drainage, causing systemic venous congestion [ 14 ]. This stagnant blood fails to meet oxygen demands and may also exert hydrostatic stress on the choriocapillaris, promoting stromal fluid extravasation and compression of the choroidal stroma. Notably, a single study reported preserved choriocapillaris flow density and CVI in patients with HFrEF, suggesting that both stromal and luminal compartments contribute to HF-related choroidal changes [ 13 ]. While requiring further validation, this observation may underscore HF-specific mechanisms which contrasts with conditions like hypertension or diabetes, where CVI reductions predominate as a result of capillary degeneration [ 38 , 39 ]. One study demonstrated significantly reduced RNFL thickness in patients with severe HFrEF (LVEF < 30%) compared to both healthy controls and moderate HFrEF groups (LVEF 30–50%) [ 6 ]. This reduction in severe cases may result from orbital vessel vasoconstriction secondary to diminished cardiac output and consequent chronic ischemia [ 6 ]. The preserved RNFL observed in moderate HFrEF may reflect compensatory mechanisms which may fail in advanced disease. These findings may indicate potential utility of RNFL measurements as a prognostic marker for HF severity stratification. Regarding VD in the RPC network, both available studies consistently reported decreased values [ 24 , 26 ]. While the underlying pathophysiology remains unclear, some evidence suggests that inadequate blood supply to the optic nerve may contribute to this finding [ 40 , 41 ]. Alternatively, Alkan et al. proposed that technical factors, particularly excessive overlapping of radial capillaries near the optic disc that could have introduced measurement errors, potentially account for this observation [ 24 ]. The observed GCIPL thinning in HFpEF aligns with the microvascular paradigm of its pathophysiology. Retinal hypoxic-ischemic injury can cause retinal ganglion cell (RGC) loss [ 42 – 46 ]. Local renin–angiotensin system (RAS) hyperactivity may impair neurovascular coupling, the process by which light-stimulated retinal neurons release vasoactive signals to dilate nearby vessels and match blood flow to metabolic demand, thereby further contributing to GCIPL thinning [ 47 – 50 ]. Choroidal hypoperfusion may also contribute to photoreceptor degeneration, as it supplies the outer retina [ 51 ]. Preserved GCC thickness in pediatric HF may reflect age-dependent ischemic resilience, as inner retinal layers, though vulnerable to oxidative stress, have stronger adaptive defenses against hypoxia [ 46 , 52 , 53 ]. This resilience may stem from nutrient reserves in the vitreous and retina, which support anaerobic glycolysis in retinal cells to sustain ATP production during hypoxia [ 5 , 54 ]. While CMT remained relatively unchanged in adults with HF across different subtypes, pediatric patients exhibited significant reductions in macular thickness across various regions. This discrepancy may arise from developmental variations in retinal structure or age-dependent differences in vulnerability to hypoxic damage, resulting in an impaired capacity to compensate for hemodynamic fluctuations associated with reduced cardiac output. Compared to healthy controls, the macular microvasculature shows distinct alterations across HF subtypes, potentially influenced by the unique pathophysiological mechanisms and hemodynamic characteristics of each subtype. In HFrEF, reduced cardiac output and tissue hypoperfusion lead to FAZ enlargement and parafoveal SCP VD reduction. These alterations reflect compromised retinal autoregulation, likely due to dysfunctional constriction of terminal retinal vessels [ 4 , 13 ], and ultimately leads to chronic ischemic stress. Peripheral vasoconstriction may further reduce VD [ 55 – 57 ]. Additionally, the sympathetic nervous system and RAS may be implicated [ 58 ]. Supporting this, Almeida-Freitas et al. demonstrated through color Doppler imaging that patients with HF exhibit elevated vascular resistance indices and compromised blood flow in the ophthalmic artery circulation [ 42 ]. A study of adults with HFpEF showed diffuse macular VD reduction without FAZ changes, reinforcing microvascular dysfunction as a central player in HF pathophysiology independent of systolic impairment. These findings substantiate the paradigm of HFpEF as not merely a disorder of myocardial stiffness but rather a systemic vasculopathy. A study of acute decompensation revealed reduced VD in superficial layers secondary to hemodynamic instability and sudden increases in central venous pressure, which disrupts retinal venous outflow and induces vascular congestion [ 14 , 59 ]. Concurrent blood pressure spikes may provoke vessel dilation or structural remodeling [ 14 ]. Notably, Abdin et al. reported a reduction in intraocular pressure following recompensation, attributing this to abrupt variations in systemic venous pressure. These alterations disrupt ocular venous hemodynamics and hinder transscleral drainage of intraocular fluid in decompensated states [ 14 ]. The temporary widening of retinal vessel diameters during acute decompensation may serve as a compensatory mechanism to maintain blood flow amid systemic hypoperfusion; however, this often proves insufficient to prevent tissue hypoxia, as evidenced by persistent VD and perfusion deficits. Furthermore, following recompensation, certain anatomical parameters (e.g., vessel densities) improve in parallel with NT-proBNP normalization. However, functional outcomes (e.g., visual acuity) often fail to recover, suggesting persistent damage following short-term treatment [ 14 , 60 ]. Pediatric HF demonstrates selective SCP involvement with whole, foveal, and parafoveal VD decreases. The DCP remains relatively spared in both HFrEF and pediatric cases. The distinct ischemic patterns observed between the SCP and DCP may stem from their anatomical differences [ 4 ]. The SCP contains medium-to-large arterioles with smooth muscle layers, while the DCP comprises delicate capillaries lined only by endothelial cells, basement membrane, and pericytes [ 61 ]. Notably, the heightened hypoxia sensitivity of pericytes renders the DCP particularly vulnerable to hemodynamic alterations and ischemic damage [ 62 – 64 ]. Collectively, these distinct patterns observed in OCT-A findings highlight the complex, layer-specific responses of retinal vasculature to systemic hemodynamic stress and underscore the importance of segmental retinal and choroidal analysis in HF characterization. The observed reductions in parafoveal SCP VD and choroidal thickness may hold significant clinical relevance. However, our sensitivity analyses attenuated the statistical significance of these parameters, indicating that their utility remains inconclusive and requires further validation through additional studies. If confirmed, these OCT/OCTA-derived metrics could potentially serve as noninvasive surrogate biomarkers of systemic microvascular impairment in HFrEF. Such ocular parameters may enable earlier detection of deteriorating cardiovascular status prior to the manifestation of overt clinical symptoms. Notably, severity-dependent RNFL thinning emerges as a robust biomarker for patient stratification, supported by recent large cohort data linking RNFL attenuation to elevated cardiovascular risk [ 65 ]. Furthermore, the correlation between whole macular VD and both LVEF and NYHA functional class in patients with HFrEF underscores its stratification potential. Together, these OCT/OCTA metrics may provide complementary, clinically accessible markers that reflect systemic HF severity. Their integration could enhance risk stratification, facilitate disease progression monitoring, and ultimately inform personalized therapeutic approaches in HFrEF management. Limitations and Future Directions While our findings offer compelling insights, several limitations should be acknowledged. The small number of available studies limits the generalizability of results for these subgroups. For several key parameters, interpretation was restricted by single-study reporting, underscoring the critical need for more studies with larger and diverse populations, particularly among patients with HFpEF and acute HF. The cross-sectional nature of most included studies limits our ability to determine whether observed ocular microvascular changes precede, coincide with, or result from systemic cardiac dysfunction. Another limitation is that the eligible studies employed different OCT instruments from various manufacturers, each with its own test–retest reliability and segmentation algorithms. While this may limit direct comparison of parameters across studies, the validity of comparisons within studies, where measurements were taken using the same instrument, remains intact. Moreover, the inclusion of multiple instruments enhances the generalizability of this systematic review. Future studies should prioritize longitudinal assessments to determine whether retinal and choroidal parameter changes predict clinically relevant endpoints including disease progression, rehospitalization, and survival outcomes. Notably, retinal and choroidal imaging represents a promising avenue for elucidating the microvascular mechanisms of heart failure-mediated end-organ damage, particularly in highly perfused tissues such as the brain and kidneys, an area that warrants prioritized investigation in future studies.

[conclusion] Conclusion Our systematic review suggests that ocular posterior segment changes, including choroidal thinning and altered retinal vascular metrics, are observed across HF subtypes and may reflect systemic hemodynamic status. The integration of OCT/OCTA into HF management could provide clinicians with actionable insights to support precision medicine in cardiovascular care. However, it is important to note that the current body of literature remains limited, preventing definitive conclusions. Additionally, we observed high heterogeneity in the SFCT meta-analysis, and sensitivity analyses revealed that the differences in SFCT and parafoveal SCP VD were no longer statistically significant after excluding individual studies. Therefore, while ocular biomarkers show potential as accessible tools for HF detection and management, further research, particularly longitudinal studies, is needed to confirm their clinical utility.

retrieved_papers.title: [Fluorescein angiography and optical coherence tomography findings in central fundus of myopic patients].
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2015]
[Abstract] Myopia prevalence grows alike in many countries, including Russia, regardless of geographical and population conditions.
to assess fundus changes in myopic patients at different ocular axial lengths by means of modern diagnostic tools.
The study enrolled 97 patients (194 eyes) aged 45 ± 20.17 years with myopia of different degrees. Besides a standard ophthalmic examination, all patients underwent fundus fluorescein angiography and optical coherence tomography.
The occurrence of retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) atrophy (diffuse or focal) has been shown to increase with increasing ocular axial length. Only 27 eyes (28.1%) appeared intact. As myopia progression implies axial growth of the eye, it is associated with a more severe decrease in choroid, RPE, and photoreceptor layer thicknesses: the longer the anterior-posterior axis, the thinner the above mentioned fundus structures. Age-related changes in the fundus are also likely to be more pronounced in longer axes. Myopic traction maculopathy, which in our case appeared the main cause of increased retinal thickness, was diagnosed in 105 eyes, "outer" macular retinoschisis--in 40 eyes.
Thus, modern diagnostic tools, such as fluorescein angiography and optical coherence tomography, enable objective assessment of the central fundus.
Как во многих странах мира, так и в России темпы роста миопии увеличиваются независимо от географических и популяционных условий. Цель - анализ изменений глазного дна у больных с миопией в зависимости от величины переднезадней оси с использованием современных методов диагностики. Материал и методы. Исследования проведены в выборке из 97 пациентов (194 глаза) (возраст 45±20,17 года) с миопией различной степени. Всем больным, помимо стандартных офтальмологических методов исследования, были проведены флюоресцентная ангиография глазного дна и оптическая когерентная томография. Результаты. При увеличении возрастало число случаев диффузной и очаговой атрофии ретинального пигментного эпителия, 'интактными' оставались лишь 27 (28,1%) глаз. Увеличение степени миопии, анатомическим субстратом которого является осевое нарастание размеров глазного яблока, ожидаемо влияет на толщину хориоидеи, ретинального пигментного эпителия и фоторецепторного слоя: чем больше размеры переднезадней оси глаза, тем существеннее тенденция к истончению вышеперечисленных структур глазного дна. Возрастные изменения хориоидеи, ретинального пигментного эпителия и фоторецепторного слоя при миопии могут усугубляться увеличенными размерами переднезадней оси. Миопическую тракционную макулопатию, которая являлась основной причиной увеличения толщины сетчатки, наблюдали в 105 глазах, а 'наружный' макулярный ретиношизис - в 40. Вывод. Использование таких современных методов исследования, как флюоресцентная ангиография и оптическая когерентная томография, позволяет объективно оценить изменения структур центральной зоны глазного дна.

[Introduction] Introduction Approximately 166 million people around the world have myopic macular degeneration [ 1 ]. During their lifetime, up to 11.3% of these people will develop sight-threatening myopic choroidal neovascularisation (mCNV) [ 2 ]. Due to the progressive natural history of mCNV and time-dependent nature of vision restoration using anti-vascular endothelial growth factor treatments, there is an urgent need to understand how innovative imaging technologies can be used to facilitate early and accurate diagnosis of mCNV [ 2 , 3 ]. Fluorescein angiography (FA) is the current reference standard for diagnosing mCNV [ 4 ]. However, FA is an invasive technique [ 5 ] and causes adverse systemic reactions in 4.8% of patients, with life-threatening anaphylaxis occurring in up to 0.3% of all patients [ 6 , 7 ]. Optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA) and optical coherence tomography (OCT) represent quicker and safer imaging technologies that do not require systemic dye injection, thus avoiding FA’s associated complications [ 4 ]. Whilst multiple comprehensive reviews have described the potential value of using OCTA and/or OCT for the diagnosis of mCNV [ 4 , 5 , 8 , 9 ], we are unaware of any studies that have undertaken a meta-analysis to assess overall pooled diagnostic test accuracy. The primary purpose of this study was to conduct a systematic review and meta-analysis to determine the test accuracy of OCTA and OCT in diagnosing mCNV compared to the reference standard, FA. The primary outcome for meta-analysis was pooled sensitivity and specificity. Pooled likelihood ratios were also estimated, and eligible studies were assessed to determine if direct measures of health consequences (patient-oriented outcomes) could be considered.

[introduction] Introduction Approximately 166 million people around the world have myopic macular degeneration [ 1 ]. During their lifetime, up to 11.3% of these people will develop sight-threatening myopic choroidal neovascularisation (mCNV) [ 2 ]. Due to the progressive natural history of mCNV and time-dependent nature of vision restoration using anti-vascular endothelial growth factor treatments, there is an urgent need to understand how innovative imaging technologies can be used to facilitate early and accurate diagnosis of mCNV [ 2 , 3 ]. Fluorescein angiography (FA) is the current reference standard for diagnosing mCNV [ 4 ]. However, FA is an invasive technique [ 5 ] and causes adverse systemic reactions in 4.8% of patients, with life-threatening anaphylaxis occurring in up to 0.3% of all patients [ 6 , 7 ]. Optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA) and optical coherence tomography (OCT) represent quicker and safer imaging technologies that do not require systemic dye injection, thus avoiding FA’s associated complications [ 4 ]. Whilst multiple comprehensive reviews have described the potential value of using OCTA and/or OCT for the diagnosis of mCNV [ 4 , 5 , 8 , 9 ], we are unaware of any studies that have undertaken a meta-analysis to assess overall pooled diagnostic test accuracy. The primary purpose of this study was to conduct a systematic review and meta-analysis to determine the test accuracy of OCTA and OCT in diagnosing mCNV compared to the reference standard, FA. The primary outcome for meta-analysis was pooled sensitivity and specificity. Pooled likelihood ratios were also estimated, and eligible studies were assessed to determine if direct measures of health consequences (patient-oriented outcomes) could be considered.

[methods] Materials and methods This systematic review and meta-analysis was conducted and reported in compliance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Review and Meta-analysis of Diagnostic Test Accuracy Studies (PRISMA-DTA) [ 10 ] and the Grading of Recommendations, Assessment, Development and Evaluation (GRADE) guidelines [ 11 , 12 ]. The study protocol was published online prior to commencing the literature search [ 13 ]. Study selection, data extraction and risk of bias appraisal were undertaken by at least two authors (SH, GSD). Disagreements were resolved by obtaining the opinion of a third independent author (AL) and majority decisions prevailed. Data sources MEDLINE ( www.PubMed.org ) and EMBASE ( www.Embase.com ) were searched from inception until 30 March 2021, without language restrictions. The query combined appropriate database specific statements using Medical Subject Heading (MeSH) or Emtree terms, and filters for studies of diagnostic accuracy were applied [ 14 – 16 ]. Reference lists of retrieved papers were also hand-searched to identify additional studies. Complete search strategy details are reported in Supplement eTable 1 . Study selection We included all studies evaluating the test accuracy of OCTA and/or OCT against the reference standard FA in diagnosing mCNV. Any combination of OCTA and/or OCT test device assessed against FA was included. Exclusion criteria were case reports, review articles, non-human studies and any article type where primary data to calculate sensitivity and specificity against the reference standard was not completely reported. Endnote software (Clarivate Analytics) was used to manage references. After removal of duplicate studies, articles were screened by title and abstract to identify studies that needed to be retrieved in full text for detailed assessment of eligibility. Data extraction and risk of bias assessment Data regarding study design, population, index test and reference test procedures, and outcomes were extracted from eligible studies. All included studies were appraised for risk of bias using the following domains from the revised Quality Assessment of Diagnostic Accuracy Studies (QUADAS-2) tool: [ 17 ] (1) was consecutive or random sampling used to obtain the patient sample; (2) was a case-control design avoided; (3) were there inappropriate exclusions; (4) were index test results interpreted without knowledge of the reference standard results; (5) were reference standard results interpreted without knowledge of the index test results; (6) was there an appropriate interval between the index test and reference standard; and (7) were all patients included in the analysis. Studies were rated ‘high’, ‘low’ or ‘unclear’ for risk of bias, and results graphed using a ‘traffic light system’ as proposed in the GRADE guidelines [ 11 ]. The ‘unclear’ category was used only when insufficient data was reported to permit a judgement. Data synthesis and analysis Clinical recommendations The GRADE guidelines for assessing certainty of the evidence with respect to study design, risk of bias, indirectness, inconsistency, imprecision and publication bias were used to frame clinical recommendations [ 11 , 12 ]. The GRADE assessment framework is supported by published reporting guidelines including PRISMA-DTA and Cochrane [ 10 , 18 – 20 ]. Outcomes The primary outcome was test accuracy, calculated as the sensitivity and specificity of OCTA or OCT against FA at the initial patient presentation. Downstream consequences of care (management decisions, health outcomes, resource utilisation) [ 21 ] delivered using OCTA or OCT alone vs FA were investigated as secondary outcomes. Statistical analysis Given the limitations of all available statistical models and methods to test for publication bias in test accuracy studies, and lack of a standardised method to register test accuracy studies [ 12 ], we intended to assess publication bias using Deeks’ test [ 22 ] only if 10 or more studies were identified for inclusion. Pooled estimates with 95% confidence intervals (95% CI) of sensitivity and specificity for each index test (OCTA and OCT) were obtained using a bivariate model. Estimates of the positive likelihood ratio, negative likelihood ratio, and positive and negative predictive values were back-calculated from the pooled estimates of sensitivity and specificity. Study differences in patient populations, patient selection, risk of bias, clinical setting, disease severity, scan density, scan quality and retinal thickness were considered as sources of heterogeneity. All statistical analyses were conducted using RevMan 5.4.1 (The Cochrane Collaboration ® , Oxford, England, 2020), and the SAS macro MetaDAS v1.3 [ 23 ].

[results] Results Literature search and study selection The primary literature search identified 410 abstracts of potentially eligible studies. Review of retrieved abstracts and hand-searching of reference lists resulted in 50 articles for full text evaluation, of which five were deemed eligible for inclusion [ 24 – 28 ]. Figure 1 reports the study selection flow. Supplement eTable 2 provides additional details regarding studies deemed ineligible. Fig. 1 Flow diagram of the study selection process. N Number, mCNV Myopic choroidal neovascularisation, FA Fluorescein angiography, Sens Sensitivity, Spec Specificity. Amongst the five studies identified as being on-topic, two evaluated OCTA [ 25 , 28 ], two evaluated spectral domain (SD) OCT [ 24 , 27 ], and one conducted evaluations of OCTA and SD-OCT [ 26 ]. The three studies assessing test accuracy of OCTA reported complete data on a total of 95 participants (101 eyes) [ 25 , 26 , 28 ], whilst the three studies evaluating SD-OCT reported complete data on 130 participants (161 eyes) [ 24 , 26 , 27 ]. Mean (standard deviation) age was 55.90 (7.71) years in the OCTA studies and 53.91 (11.10) years in the SD-OCT studies. Additional details of the studies are reported in Table 1 . Supplement eTable 3 reports detailed descriptions of the index and reference test assessment procedures. Table 1 Key details of included studies. Study Description Major exclusions Participants Sensitivity/Specificity Bagchi et al. 2019 [ 26 ] Retrospective audit of patients who presented to a retinal clinic in the United Kingdom with high myopia (<−6D or AL > 26 mm) and new onset visual disturbance who received FA, OCTA and SD-OCT imaging. Excluded patients who did not receive all three imaging modalities. Excluded patients with poor quality images. Excluded patients with other co-existing major ocular conditions. 27 eyes of 26 patients (18 female, 6 male) Mean age 47.7 ± 19.7 years OCTA vs FA: Sensitivity 19/23, specificity 3/4 SD-OCT vs FA: Sensitivity 23/23, specificity 0/4 Milani et al. 2016 [ 24 ] Retrospective audit of patients seen at a research hospital in Italy with recent vision deterioration, pathologic myopia (<−6D and staphyloma) and suspected mCNV who received near infrared, autofluorescence, FA and SD-OCT imaging at first presentation. Excluded patients who did not receive all four imaging modalities. Excluded patients with poor quality images. Excluded patients who had previous vitreoretinal surgery, diabetes, signs of age-related macular degeneration, or vitreoretinal interface-related pathologies. 65 eyes of 62 patients (44 female, 21 male) Mean age 66.72 years, range 18–89 Mean refraction −9.72D, range −6 to −22 SD-OCT vs FA: Sensitivity 48/49, specificity 16/16 Miyata et al. 2016 [ 25 ] Prospective study of consecutive patients who presented to a university ophthalmology clinic in Japan with pathologic myopia (<−6D or AL > 26 mm, plus chorioretinal abnormalities) and treatment naïve exudative lesions. Patients with OCTA images of insufficient quality were excluded from analysis. 28 eyes of 26 patients (22 female, 4 male) Included in analysis: 21 eyes of 20 patients (17 female, 3 male) Mean age 63.0 ± 13.6 years OCTA vs FA: Sensitivity 16/17, specificity 4/4 Querques et al. 2017 [ 28 ] Retrospective audit of patients who presented to a university hospital’s retinal clinic in Italy with pathologic myopia (<−8D or AL > 26.5 mm, plus characteristic degenerative changes of the sclera/choroid/retina) who were diagnosed with mCNV using FA. a An additional cohort of patients with pathologic myopia and no evidence of mCNV were enrolled as a negative control group. Excluded patients with co-existing retinal conditions, history of ocular inflammation in the study eye, significant media opacities, or large haemorrhage. Patients with OCTA images of insufficient quality or who did not have FA performed on the same day as OCTA were excluded from analysis. Negative control group: Excluded patients with co-existing retinal conditions, or previous ocular treatments in the study eye. 36 eyes of 28 patients (23 female, 5 male) Included in analysis: 21 eyes of 17 patients (14 female, 3 male) Mean age 57.8 ± 14.5 years Negative control group: 32 eyes of 32 patients (27 female, 5 male) Mean age 56.2 ± 14.4 years, range 26–84 OCTA vs FA a : Sensitivity 19/21, specificity 30/32 Su et al. 2014 [ 27 ] Prospective study of patients who presented to a macular service centre in China with high myopia (<−6D and AL > 26.5 mm) and myopic maculopathy. Excluded patients with other retinal or choroidal diseases, or dense cataracts. 69 eyes of 42 patients (23 female, 19 male) Mean age 47.3 ± 17.3 years, range 20–79 SD-OCT vs FA: Sensitivity 16/16, specificity 53/53 D Dioptres, AL Axial length, FA Fluorescein angiography, OCTA Optical coherence tomography angiography, SD-OCT Spectral domain optical coherence tomography, mCNV Myopic choroidal neovascularisation. a Use of FA as reference standard was clarified by direct communication with the corresponding author. Risk of bias assessment All five included studies were found to have at least one major methodological flaw leading to a potential high risk of bias. Two of the five studies were assessed as high risk of bias in five out of seven bias scoring domains [ 24 , 26 ]. One study was assessed as high risk of bias in four out of seven bias scoring domains [ 28 ]. One study was assessed as high risk of bias in two out of seven bias scoring domains [ 27 ], whilst the remaining study was rated high risk of bias in one of the seven bias scoring domains [ 25 ]. See Fig. 2 for complete details of the risk of bias assessment. Fig. 2 Risk of bias assessment of included studies. Low risk of bias, Unclear risk of bias, High risk of bias. Based on a priori defined criteria [ 13 ], publication bias could not be assessed due to the inadequate number of included studies. Primary analysis: test accuracy Studies comparing OCTA with FA Three studies enrolling 95 patients (101 eyes) compared the performance of OCTA against FA [ 25 , 26 , 28 ]. Pooled diagnostic performance showed a sensitivity of 0.89 (95% CI 0.78–0.94) and specificity of 0.93 (95% CI 0.79–0.98). The likelihood ratio of a positive test result was 11.8 (95% CI 3.96–35.25) and the likelihood ratio of a negative test result was 0.12 (95% CI 0.061–0.25). Studies comparing SD-OCT with FA Three studies enrolling 130 patients (161 eyes) compared the performance of SD-OCT against FA [ 24 , 26 , 27 ]. Pooled diagnostic performance showed a sensitivity of 0.99 (95% CI 0.91–1.00). The likelihood ratio of a negative test result was 0.01 (95% CI 0.001–0.095). Pooled specificity and the likelihood ratio of a positive test result were unestimatable. Additional information such as sensitivity and specificity for individual studies, pooled positive predictive value and pooled negative predictive value for each comparison (OCTA vs FA and SD-OCT vs FA) are reported in Table 2 . Table 2 Test accuracy of individual studies and pooled results from meta-analysis. Outcome (95% CI) Outcome (95% CI) OCTA compared to FA Individual studies a TP FP FN TN Sensitivity Specificity Bagchi 2019 19 1 4 3 0.83 (0.61–0.95) 0.75 (0.19–0.99) Miyata 2016 16 0 1 4 0.94 (0.71–1.00) 1.00 (0.40–1.00) Querques 2017 19 2 2 30 0.90 (0.70–0.99) 0.94 (0.79–0.99) Pooled results from meta-analysis b Sensitivity Specificity 0.89 (0.78–0.94) 0.93 (0.79–0.98) LR of a positive test LR of a negative test 11.8 (3.96–35.25) 0.12 (0.061–0.25) Positive PV Negative PV 0.95 (0.79–0.99) 0.85 (0.61–0.94) SD-OCT compared to FA Individual studies a TP FP FN TN Sensitivity Specificity Bagchi 2019 23 4 0 0 1.00 (0.85–1.00) 0.00 (0.00–0.60) Milani 2016 48 0 1 16 0.98 (0.89–1.00) 1.00 (0.79–1.00) Su 2014 16 0 0 53 1.00 (0.79–1.00) 1.00 (0.93–1.00) Pooled results from meta-analysis b Sensitivity Specificity 0.99 (0.91–1.00) unestimatable LR of a positive test LR of a negative test unestimatable 0.01 (0.001–0.095) Positive PV Negative PV unestimatable unestimatable CI confidence interval, OCTA optical coherence tomography angiography, FA fluorescein angiography, TP true positive, FP false positive, FN false negative, TN true negative, LR likelihood ratio, PV predictive value, SD-OCT spectral domain optical coherence tomography. a Calculated using RevMan Ver 5.4.1. b Calculated using SAS macro MetaDAS v1.3. Sources of heterogeneity Studies comparing OCTA with FA With only three studies, a priori identified potential sources of heterogeneity could not be investigated. Studies comparing SD-OCT with FA With only three studies, a priori identified potential sources of heterogeneity could not be investigated. To investigate whether individual study inconsistencies prevented convergence towards a pooled estimate of specificity, meta-analysis was repeated with the study by Bagchi et al. [ 26 ] removed. Reanalysis including results from the studies by Su et al. [ 27 ] and Milani et al. [ 24 ] also failed to converge on a pooled estimate of specificity; see Supplement eTable 4 . Downstream consequences No studies provided a head-to-head comparison of the downstream consequences (e.g. management decisions, health outcomes, resource utilisation) of using OCTA or SD-OCT to diagnose mCNV compared to using the reference standard, FA. Clinical recommendations Given an overall moderate certainty of the evidence, we conditionally recommend the use of OCTA to achieve a diagnosis when mCNV is clinically suspected. With moderate confidence in the consistency of the estimate of sensitivity between studies and moderate confidence in the precision of the pooled estimate of sensitivity, we recommend OCTA as an initial test to rule out mCNV. With moderate confidence in the consistency of the estimate of specificity between studies and moderate confidence in the precision of the pooled estimate of specificity, we also recommend OCTA to rule in the presence of mCNV. However, as indicated by the wide confidence intervals around the likelihood ratios for a positive test result and a negative test result (Table 2 ), OCTA may have high false positive and false negative rates. Given an overall low to moderate certainty of the evidence, we conditionally suggest clinicians may consider the use of SD-OCT to achieve a diagnosis when mCNV is clinically suspected. With high confidence in the consistency of the estimate of sensitivity between studies and high confidence in the precision of the pooled estimate of sensitivity, we recommend SD-OCT as an initial test to rule out mCNV. However, with low confidence in the consistency of the estimate of specificity between studies and an unestimatable pooled specificity, we do not recommend reliance on SD-OCT alone to rule in the presence of mCNV because the false positive rate is unknown. Complete results of the judgements on the certainty of the evidence [ 11 , 12 ] are reported in Supplement eFig. 1 and eTable 5 . Clinical Guidance Recommendations are summarised in Table 3 . Supplement eTable 3 reports complete eligibility criteria used by each study to identify patients who were clinically suspected to have mCNV. Table 3 Key recommendations. Test Recommendation OCTA Conditionally recommend the use of OCTA to achieve a diagnosis when mCNV is clinically suspected. Statement was conditional because all studies excluded patients from analysis due to image quality issues. • Recommend OCTA as an initial screening study to rule out mCNV. • Recommend OCTA to rule in the presence of mCNV. However, due to a possible high false positive rate, a positive diagnosis should be confirmed by FA. SD-OCT Conditionally suggest clinicians may consider the use of SD-OCT to achieve a diagnosis when mCNV is clinically suspected. Statement was conditional because of the inability to estimate a pooled specificity for SD-OCT resulting in an unknown false positive rate. • Recommend SD-OCT as an initial screening study to rule out mCNV. • Do not recommend reliance on SD-OCT alone to rule in the presence of mCNV because the false positive rate of SD-OCT is unknown. A positive diagnosis must be confirmed by FA. OCTA + SD-OCT Clinicians may consider using SD-OCT if an OCTA image of sufficient quality cannot be acquired. • If either OCTA or SD-OCT return a negative result, clinicians may be fairly confident in ruling out mCNV. • If either OCTA or SD-OCT return a positive result, FA should be performed to rule out false positives and confirm the diagnosis. OCTA Optical coherence tomography angiography, mCNV Myopic choroidal neovascularisation, FA Fluorescein angiography, SD-OCT Spectral domain optical coherence tomography.

[discussion] Discussion This systematic review and meta-analysis investigated the test accuracy of OCTA and SD-OCT for the diagnosis of mCNV compared to FA as the reference standard. An extensive literature search identified three eligible OCTA studies and three eligible SD-OCT studies. Although meta-analysis revealed that both OCTA and SD-OCT diagnosed mCNV with high sensitivity, a pooled estimate of specificity for SD-OCT could not be calculated. These findings have implications for the clinical diagnosis of mCNV using either OCTA or SD-OCT. Using the GRADE criteria [ 11 , 12 ] to consider uncertainty, a ‘conditional recommendation’ could be made to support the use of OCTA as an initial test when mCNV is clinically suspected. This recommendation is ‘conditional’ because a large proportion of OCTA images were not of acceptable quality to achieve a positive or negative diagnosis. Studies excluded up to 25% of patients from analysis on account of image quality issues [ 25 , 26 , 28 ]. OCTA is known to be prone to motion and projection artefacts as many patients have poor fixation due to co-existing myopic maculopathy [ 4 ]. Future advances in imaging hardware, software tools and image processing may help overcome these image acquisition limitations [ 29 ]. Meanwhile, a weaker ‘suggestion to consider’ SD-OCT was made because pooled specificity could not be estimated. Reliance on SD-OCT alone to diagnose mCNV may result in an excessively high false positive rate leading to unnecessary over-treatment. Nevertheless, we suggest SD-OCT could be considered when an OCTA image of sufficient quality cannot be acquired.
