[discussion] Discussion The present study constitutes an investigation of menstrual cycle-associated alterations in microvascular and structural characteristics within the macular and peripapillary regions. The investigation was conducted through a comprehensive analysis of OCT parameters such as GCIPL, CMT and CT, as well as OCTA-assessed SCP, DCP, and CC layers. The study analysis demonstrated a significant decrease in macular CT from the follicular to the mid-luteal phase of the menstrual cycle. Peripapillary CT measurements also exhibited a significant reduction in the temporal and inferior quadrants. Furthermore, detailed OCTA analysis revealed significant variations in VD. In the macula, a significant increase was observed exclusively within the inferior quadrant of the CC layer. VD measurements within the peripapillary region revealed significant differences between menstrual cycle phases, particularly in the superior quadrant of the SCP and the inferior quadrant of the DCP. It is noteworthy that significant differences were observed in the CC VD between the different menstrual cycle phases within the peripapillary region, specifically in the temporal and inferior quadrants. Whilst a recent study has examined menstrual cycle-related changes in the microvasculature of the macula and the peripapillary region, the present study is the first to investigate layer-segmented peripapillary vascular changes across menstrual phases using OCTA [ 9 ]. The vasodilatory influence of estrogen on ocular blood flow is exerted via both endothelium-dependent and independent mechanisms [ 3 ]. Elevated estrogen levels have been observed to stimulate the synthesis of nitric oxide while concurrently inhibiting the production of endothelin-1. These counterbalancing effects collectively promote vasodilation and reduce vascular resistance [ 10 ]. In contrast, progesterone functions as an estrogen antagonist, inducing vasoconstriction [ 11 ]. The majority of studies examining the effects of hormones on ocular microvasculature have concentrated on the period of pregnancy. Previous research into the subject of pregnancy has indicated that the observed increase in vascular density within the retina and the optic disc during pregnancy is likely attributable to physiological adaptations necessitated by the gravid state [ 12 ]. Hormonal fluctuations constitute a potential mechanism underlying these ocular vascular changes. Given the established influence of sex hormones on various physiological and pathological processes within ocular tissues, it has been postulated that these hormones may contribute to the pathogenesis of certain ocular diseases [ 13 , 14 ]. A small number of studies have been conducted into the impact of the menstrual cycle on ocular anatomy and vascularization, as evidenced in the literature [ 4 , 5 , 8 ]. Guo et al. demonstrated a significant decrease in VD within the nasal and inferior quadrants of the DCP during the ovulatory phase compared to the early follicular and luteal phases. The authors hypothesise that hormone fluctuations across the menstrual cycle may be responsible for the significant changes in DCP microvasculature observed during the ovulatory phase. This finding contrasts with the stability of DCP VD observed during the early follicular and luteal phases [ 8 ]. In contrast to the aforementioned study, our findings revealed no statistically significant difference in SCP and DCP in the macular area. Nevertheless, a statistically significant alteration in VD was observed in the inferior quadrant of the CC layer. This discrepancy may be attributed to the fact that the choroidal circulation exhibits a comparatively limited capacity for autoregulation in comparison to the retina. The choroid is more directly influenced by systemic conditions and exhibits reduced capacity to maintain constant blood flow during fluctuations in ocular perfusion pressure [ 15 ]. Moreover, retinal arterioles function as the primary resistance vessels within the retinal vasculature, exerting a considerable influence on retinal hemodynamics through the modulation of their luminal diameter. The mechanism of vascular autoregulation in retinal vessels represents a significant area of investigation. Retinal arterioles modulate capillary perfusion by dynamically adjusting their luminal diameter through changes in vascular smooth muscle tone [ 16 ]. Furthermore, the utilisation of different OCTA devices may have contributed to the observed variability in the findings. Device-specific differences in OCTA technology, including segmentation algorithms, can influence the measurement and visualisation of vascular changes and may contribute to the observed discrepancies in VD across studies. In alignment with the established characteristics of the ovulation phase, characterized by elevated estrogen and reduced progesterone levels, the peripapillary region exhibited a notable increase in VD measurements between menstrual cycle phases. This was particularly evident in the superior quadrant of the SCP, as well as in the inferior quadrant of the DCP, and also in the temporal and inferior quadrants of the CC. Post-hoc analysis revealed a pronounced increase in VD during the ovulatory phase relative to both the follicular and mid-luteal phases. Furthermore, our study evaluated alterations in the choroid through OCT. The study demonstrated a significant reduction in macular CT from the follicular to the mid-luteal phase of the menstrual cycle. Also, peripapillary CT measurements demonstrated a significant reduction in the temporal and inferior quadrants. The structural changes observed in the choroid across the menstrual cycle were found to be parallel with the microvascular alterations in the CC. The present findings are consistent with those of recent studies exploring hormonal influences on posterior ocular structures. A recent study reported reduced DCP VD in women using combined oral contraceptives (COCs), suggesting a hormone-mediated modulation of retinal microvasculature detectable by OCTA. In a similar vein, Icoz et al. observed a significant decrease in choroidal vascular index values in individuals who used COC, in comparison to those who did not use COC. This observation is indicative of alterations in the luminal component of the choroid. These findings are complementary to our observation of increased VD during the ovulatory phase, suggesting that the physiological estrogen surge may enhance microvascular perfusion, whereas hormonally suppressed states such as COC use may attenuate it, thereby highlighting the hormonal sensitivity of retinal and choroidal microcirculation [ 17 , 18 ]. The existing literature has demonstrated alterations in CT throughout the menstrual cycle. Ulaş et al. employed spectral-domain OCT to demonstrate a significant reduction in CT during the mid-luteal phase in comparison to earlier stages of the menstrual cycle in a cohort of healthy women. Similarly, Asıkgarip et al. reported a consistent reduction in CT across all regions during the mid-luteal phase. Proposed mechanisms for these changes in CT include vasoconstriction mediated by alpha-adrenergic and/or renin activity, as well as hormonal fluctuations that are characteristic of the mid-luteal phase [ 19 , 20 ]. Although we discussed multiple mechanisms through which estrogen exerts its impact on retinal microvascular structure, there remains a degree of inconsistency in how this effect is represented within the scientific literature. Leung et al. conducted a study to explore the relationship between hormone replacement therapy and retinal arteriolar diameter in an older female population. Contrary to the prevailing hypothesis, their findings indicated no significant vasodilatory effect of hormone replacement therapy on retinal arterioles. In contrast, Wong et al. reported a cross-sectional association between estrogen replacement therapy and narrower retinal vessel diameters in a population-based study, independent of blood pressure and other vascular factors. Both studies employed a standardized methodology, measuring retinal arteriolar and venular diameters within a one-disc diameter radius of the optic disc using computer-assisted image analysis of digitized color fundus photographs [ 21 , 22 ]. Two decades ago, Karadeniz et al. investigated the changes in hemodynamics observed in the ophthalmic, central retinal, and posterior ciliary arteries across the menstrual cycle. Doppler ultrasonography was performed on 23 healthy women across six distinct menstrual cycle phases. No significant variations in pulsatility index, resistance index, or peak systolic velocity were observed among the arterial segments. The authors proposed that these findings indicate that the complex interplay of hormonal influences on systemic hemodynamics, including heart rate, blood pressure, and blood volume, may offset any potential menstrual cycle-related alterations in ocular blood flow [ 7 ]. Recent advancements in ophthalmic technology have shifted the paradigm for assessing intraocular perfusion away from traditional Doppler and pulsatility examinations towards the utilization of OCT and OCTA. In alignment with this technological shift, our study comprehensively employed OCT and OCTA imaging for data acquisition. In a previous OCTA study, Savastano et al. observed a significantly higher VD measurements of CC in patients with hypothalamic amenorrhea compared to controls, both in the whole area and in the fovea area. The authors attributed this finding to potential endothelial dysfunction secondary to estrogen deficiency, characterized by reduced nitric oxide production, elevated oxidative stress, and proinflammatory cytokine levels, which ultimately lead to abnormal vascular remodeling and microvascular constriction. Based on these findings, they hypothesized that the observed increase in VD might reflect compensatory microvascular changes in response to these underlying pathophysiological processes [ 23 ]. Consistent with the findings of Savastano et al., a recent study by Çetinkaya et al. utilized swept-source OCTA to investigate retinal and choroidal microvascular changes in postmenopausal women. The study reported a reduction in VD of CC in postmenopausal participants compared to age-matched controls with ongoing menstruation. Notably, a negative correlation between VD and menopause duration suggested that prolonged hypoestrogenism adversely impacts the microvascular structure of this layer [ 24 ]. A primary limitation of this study was the absence of serum sex hormone level measurements. Without direct quantification of estradiol and progesterone levels, we were unable to correlate retinal and choroidal microvascular changes with precise hormonal fluctuations during the menstrual cycle. Although menstrual phases were carefully timed using well-established cycle days (day 3, 14, and 21) and participants were required to prospectively document the onset of menstruation to confirm regular ovulatory cycles, individual variation in hormonal profiles may still exist. This limitation introduces the potential for misclassification of cycle phases, which could affect the accuracy of interpreting phase-specific vascular changes. The absence of biochemical validation may reduce the granularity of phase assignment. As such, the observed changes in VD and CT should be interpreted with caution, and future studies incorporating serum hormone analysis would offer a more robust framework for confirming the hormonal basis of the vascular alterations observed with OCTA. Additionally, the relatively small sample size may limit the generalizability of the findings and reduce statistical power to detect more subtle microvascular changes across menstrual phases. In conclusion, the present study indicates that the hormonal fluctuations associated with the menstrual cycle exert a substantial impact on the architecture and vasculature of the retina and choroid. A crucial finding is the identification of structural alterations in the choroid across menstrual phases, which may indicate a potential association with concurrent microvascular changes in the CC. Notable variations were observed in both peripapillary CT and CC microvasculature in the inferior and temporal regions. It is therefore recommended that clinicians take the menstrual cycle phase into account when interpreting OCTA data in women of reproductive age, to ensure optimal diagnostic accuracy and treatment planning.

[conclusion] Conclusion Hormonal fluctuations throughout the menstrual cycle appear to influence both retinal and choroidal microvasculature, particularly in the choriocapillaris and peripapillary regions. These findings underscore the importance of considering menstrual phase when interpreting OCTA measurements in women of reproductive age, to improve diagnostic accuracy and consistency in clinical and research settings.

retrieved_papers.title: The effect of the menstrual cycle on the optic nerve head analysis of migrainous women.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2005]
[Abstract] To determine the effect of the menstrual cycle on the optic nerve head topographic analysis of normally menstruating migrainous women.
Randomly selected one eye of 44 migrainous and 49 healthy control women with regular menstrual cycles were included in the study. All subjects underwent complete ocular examination. Optic nervehead topographic analysis were performed using a confocal scanning laser ophthalmoscope, HRT II (Heidelberg Retinal Tomograph II, software version 1.6;Heidelberg Engineering, Heidelberg, Germany). They were repeated for two times during the menstrual cycle: in follicular phase (7th to 10th day of the cycle) and in the luteal phase (days 3 to 4 before the menstrual bleeding). Serum estradiol, progesterone, and luteinizing hormone measurements were repeated at each menstrual phase.
The mean age of migrainous and control subjects were 31.5 + 5.1 years and 33.4 +/- 3.7 years, respectively (P > 0.05). Their mean disc areas were 2.26 +/- 0.46 mm(2) and 1.95 +/- 0.39 mm(2), respectively(P < 0.05). Control subjects did not demonstrate any difference in the disc topography (P > 0.05). The parameter rim volume decreased, while the parameters cup volume and cup shape measure increased significantly in the luteal phase of the migrainous women (all P values <0.05). Mean intraocular pressure of the migrainous women decreased significantly in luteal phase (P < 0.05).
Significant differences exist in the optic rim and cup parameters during the menstrual cycle of the migrainous women. Further clinical trials on ocular blood flow changes during the menstrual cycle of the migrainous women may highlight the role of sex steroids in the optic nerve head of the migrainous women.

[methods] Methods. Blood samples were collected from 1989 to 1990 in the Nurses' Health Study (women) and from 1993 to 1995 in the Health Professionals Follow-up Study (men), and medical-record confirmed incident POAG cases were identified (women: 229 cases and 455 controls; men: 116 cases and 228 controls). Controls were matched on cohort, age, race, ethnicity, cancer status, and date of blood collection. Plasma concentrations of ICAM-1, E-selectin, and soluble TNF receptor 2 (sTNF-R2), a marker related to TNF-α, were measured with ELISA assays. Cohort-specific multivariable conditional logistic regression model results were meta-analyzed.

[results] Results. We observed no associations with ICAM-1 or E-selectin. For sTNF-R2, the mean (SD) plasma levels (pg/mL) in cases and controls were 2888 (997) and 2993 (913), respectively, in women; and 2622 (664) and 2569 (688), respectively, in men. Pooled multivariable results showed no relation between sTNF-R2 levels and POAG. However, compared with the lowest tertile of sTNF-R2, the highest tertile showed a significant decreased risk of POAG in women (multivariable odds ratio [OR] = 0.58, 95% confidence interval [CI] = 0.36–0.93; P trend = 0.03) but not in men ( P trend = 0.21; P for heterogeneity by sex = 0.03). Also, among women, the inverse association with sTNF-R2 was stronger with normal-tension glaucoma (NTG; maximum intraocular pressure <21 mm Hg at diagnosis): highest versus lowest tertile comparison OR = 0.29 (95% CI = 0.12–0.71; P trend = 0.007).

[conclusion] Conclusions. In women, but not in men, higher sTNF-R2 levels at 6 to 8 years before diagnosis were inversely associated with POAG, but more strongly for NTG.

retrieved_papers.title: Comparison of retinochoroidal microvascular circulation in menstrual and postmenopausal periods using swept-source optical coherence tomography angiography.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2023]
[Abstract] We aimed to examine the possible effects of the postmenopausal period on retinal and choroidal microvascular circulation using swept-source optical coherence tomography angiography (SS-OCTA).
This cross-sectional study included 45 eyes of 45 subjects in menstrual group and 40 eyes of 40 subjects in postmenopausal group. SS-OCTA was used for the assessment of vessel density (VD), foveal avascular zone (FAZ), choroidal thickness (CT), choriocapillaris VD, central macular thickness (CMT), nerve fiber layer thickness (RNFL), and ganglion cell layer (GCL) measurements.
The VDs of the superficial capillary plexus (SCP) and deep capillary plexus (DCP) measurements were similar in both group. FAZ area was wider in postmenopausal group (0.305 mm2 (range, 0.212-0.498 mm2)) compared to menstrual group (0.271 mm2 (range, 0.131-0.464 mm2)) (p = 0.013). Choriocapillaris VD was significantly lower in postmenopausal group (p = 0.049). CT was thicker in the postmenopausal group, but with no statistically significant difference (p = 0.066). Central macular thickness, RNFL, and GCL were similar in both groups.
This is the first study in the literature to evaluate the retinochoroidal microcirculation in the menstrual and postmenopausal periods with SS-OCTA. We observed an increase in both superficial and deep FAZ area and a decrease in choroidal blood flow in the postmenopausal period. We think that this enlargement in the FAZ area may be related to the decreased amount of estrogen.

[Introduction] Introduction The menstrual cycle is defined as the regular, cyclical variations in the secretion of female hormones and the accompanying changes in the reproductive organs during a woman’s reproductive years [ 1 ]. The menstrual cycle can be divided into three phases based on the levels of hormones present in the blood. During the follicular phase, low levels of estrogen and progesterone are observed. In contrast, the ovulatory phase is characterized by high estrogen and low progesterone levels. Finally, the luteal phase is typified by high estrogen and progesterone levels [ 2 ]. The eye exhibits sensitivity to sex hormones and functions as a target organ for their effects. Previous research has documented differences in ocular blood flow, tear secretion, and biometric parameters, particularly between premenopausal and postmenopausal women [ 3 , 4 ]. Furthermore, application of optical coherence tomography (OCT) in reproductive-aged women has revealed cyclical variations in choroidal thickness (CT), and optic disc morphology during the menstrual cycle [ 5 , 6 ]. In addition to the aforementioned structural alterations, vascular changes were observed in the ophthalmic and central retinal arteries via Doppler ultrasonography during the menstrual cycle [ 7 ]. Optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA) has emerged as a valuable modality for comprehensively evaluating intraocular blood flow. This non-invasive technique offers quantitative data on the microvascular network within both the macular and peripapillary regions. A recent study identified significant microvascular alterations within the macula during the menstrual cycle [ 8 ]. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to evaluate layer-segmented peripapillary microvascular changes across distinct menstrual phases using OCTA. The objective of this study was to assess both microvascular and structural changes in the macular and optic nerve using OCTA in a cohort of healthy reproductive subjects.

[introduction] Introduction The menstrual cycle is defined as the regular, cyclical variations in the secretion of female hormones and the accompanying changes in the reproductive organs during a woman’s reproductive years [ 1 ]. The menstrual cycle can be divided into three phases based on the levels of hormones present in the blood. During the follicular phase, low levels of estrogen and progesterone are observed. In contrast, the ovulatory phase is characterized by high estrogen and low progesterone levels. Finally, the luteal phase is typified by high estrogen and progesterone levels [ 2 ]. The eye exhibits sensitivity to sex hormones and functions as a target organ for their effects. Previous research has documented differences in ocular blood flow, tear secretion, and biometric parameters, particularly between premenopausal and postmenopausal women [ 3 , 4 ]. Furthermore, application of optical coherence tomography (OCT) in reproductive-aged women has revealed cyclical variations in choroidal thickness (CT), and optic disc morphology during the menstrual cycle [ 5 , 6 ]. In addition to the aforementioned structural alterations, vascular changes were observed in the ophthalmic and central retinal arteries via Doppler ultrasonography during the menstrual cycle [ 7 ]. Optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA) has emerged as a valuable modality for comprehensively evaluating intraocular blood flow. This non-invasive technique offers quantitative data on the microvascular network within both the macular and peripapillary regions. A recent study identified significant microvascular alterations within the macula during the menstrual cycle [ 8 ]. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to evaluate layer-segmented peripapillary microvascular changes across distinct menstrual phases using OCTA. The objective of this study was to assess both microvascular and structural changes in the macular and optic nerve using OCTA in a cohort of healthy reproductive subjects.

[methods] Materials and methods This prospective study was conducted at Beyoglu Eye Training and Research Hospital, University of Health Sciences. The study protocol received ethical approval from the Hamidiye Ethics Committee at the University of Health Sciences and adhered to the tenets of the Declaration of Helsinki (Approval number 20/508). This study included healthy female subjects with a documented history of regular menstrual cycles over the preceding six months. Participants were recruited from among hospital staff volunteers rather than routine ophthalmology outpatients, in order to minimize potential confounding factors related to ocular or systemic pathologies. All participants provided written informed consent after receiving a detailed explanation of the study protocol. The inclusion criteria stipulated that only one eye per participant was eligible for inclusion in the study, provided that both eyes met the requisite criteria. The selection of the included eye was randomized to minimize selection bias. Eumenorrhea was confirmed based on self-reported menstrual cycle history over the preceding two years. The study included women aged 20 to 30 years with self-reported regular ovulatory menstrual cycles ranging from 24 to 35 days. To ensure accurate phase identification, participants were instructed to prospectively track and document the onset of their menstrual cycles. Individuals with irregular or highly variable patterns were excluded. Additional exclusion criteria included a history of pregnancy, childbirth, or breastfeeding within the past year; current or previous use of hormonal contraceptives within the preceding six months; systemic diseases such as hypertension, diabetes mellitus, or thyroid dysfunction; and the use of continuous systemic medications.Ocular exclusion criteria comprised refractive errors exceeding ± 6.00 diopters (spherical equivalent), axial length ≤ 22 mm or ≥ 26 mm, amblyopia, previous intraocular surgery, ocular hypertension, glaucoma, intraocular inflammation, or any media opacity that could compromise imaging. Eyes with poor-quality OCTA scans due to motion artifacts or segmentation errors were also excluded. Given the restricted age range, absence of hormonal medication use, and consistent cycle documentation, follicular and luteal phase durations were considered physiologically comparable among participants. Mean arterial pressure (MAP) was estimated using the following formula: MAP = diastolic blood pressure + 1/3 (systolic blood pressure - diastolic blood pressure). The central corneal thickness was obtained through the utilisation of ultrasound pachymetry (USP). Prior to the measurement, a topical anaesthetic was administered to the eye. The patient was then instructed to fixate on a distant target while the USP probe was positioned perpendicularly to the central corneal surface. Axial length measurements were conducted using the AL-Scan Optical Biometer (NIDEK Co., Ltd., Aichi, Japan) prior to pupillary dilation. For this study, axial length values were obtained as the average of three consecutive high-quality scans. All measurements were performed under standardized room lighting conditions by trained personnel, following the manufacturer’s recommended protocol. All examinations were conducted within a single menstrual cycle for each participant. The examinations were conducted at three specific time points: early follicular phase on cycle day 3, ovulatory phase on cycle day 14, and mid-luteal phase on cycle day 21. To minimize potential circadian variations, all examinations were performed between 11:00 and 12:00 am. Measurements were performed within the same menstrual cycle. An examination was conducted, comprising the evaluation of blood pressure, best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) assessment using a Snellen chart, slit-lamp biomicroscopic examination, intraocular pressure measurement with a Goldmann applanation tonometer, dilated fundus examination, and imaging using OCT and OCTA. OCT and OCTA imaging utilizing the Topcon DRI Triton (Topcon Inc., Japan) swept-source OCT (SS-OCT) with angiography were conducted. Imaging protocol Macular and peripapillary regions were evaluated using a wide-field OCT scan (12 mm x 9 mm) acquired on the device. The instrument’s integrated software was employed to analyze central macular thickness (CMT), ganglion cell inner plexiform layer (GCIPL) thickness, and central CT. Images were analyzed and measured independently by two graders. Retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) thickness and CT were assessed in each of the four peripapillary quadrants. CT was measured manually from the outer border of the hyperreflective retinal pigment epithelium layer to the inner surface of the sclera in the temporal, superior, nasal and inferior segments. Microvascularity in the macula and peripapillary area was assessed using Topcon DRI Triton OCTA. Two scan patterns were used: a 3 × 3 mm volume scan for the macular region and a 4.5 × 4.5 mm volume scan for the peripapillary region. Segmentation of the superficial capillary plexus (SCP), deep capillary plexus (DCP), and choriocapillaris (CC) was performed automatically using the integrated software (IMAGEnet6, v1.23.15008, Basic License 10) (Fig. 1 ). All OCTA scans underwent a meticulous review process to ensure accurate segmentation and optimal image quality. The foveal avascular zone (FAZ) areas in both the SCP and DCP were manually delineated using the built-in measurement tool of the OCTA device by two independent, masked graders. The average of the two observers’ measurements was used for subsequent FAZ area analysis. To assess the reliability of the manual measurements, the interobserver intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC) was calculated based on 30 randomly selected images. Fig. 1 Macular and peripapillary vessel density measurements for the superficial capillary plexus (SCP), deep capillary plexus (DCP), and choriocapillaris (CC) Vessel density (VD) within the SCP, DCP, and CC was evaluated in both the foveal (central) and parafoveal (superior, temporal, inferior, nasal) sectors. This analysis utilized a modified ETDRS grid featuring a 1 mm inner diameter and a 3 mm outer diameter ring to define the measurement regions. Additionally, peripapillary VD was automatically analyzed within each segmented slab across the four peripapillary quadrants (Fig. 1 ). Statistical analysis The analysis of the data was carried out using IBM SPSS Statistics version 23.0 (SPSS Inc. Chicago, IL). Normality of data distribution was evaluated using the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test, in line with the default configuration of the statistical software. Although the Shapiro-Wilk test is generally preferred for smaller samples, the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test remains a valid and widely used method. Quantitative data are presented as means and standard deviations (SD) within the text and tables. A comparison of measurements between the follicular, ovulatory and mid-luteal phases was conducted using repeated measures analysis of variance. The significance level for pairwise comparisons of significant variables was determined using the Bonferroni correction, calculated by dividing 0.05 by the number of sub-hypotheses. The Greenhouse-Geisser correction was used in the lack of sphericity. A p-value < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

[results] Results A total of 33 subjects were initially enrolled in the study; however, two were lost to follow-up, resulting in a final sample size of 31 participants. The mean age of the participants was 27.3 years (SD ± 3.9 years). The mean axial length of the study eyes was 23.4 ± 0.9 mm (range: 22.2–24.8 mm). Notably, all participants maintained BCVA of 20/20 or better throughout the study period, demonstrating no significant changes in BCVA. The baseline ophthalmic examination revealed a mean intraocular pressure of 12.4 ± 2.0 mmHg. All subjects were phakic. There was no history of any previous intraocular surgical procedure. The average menstrual cycle length was 28.1 ± 2.2 days (range: 26 to 32 days). Our analysis revealed no statistically significant differences ( p > 0.05) in MAP, intraocular pressure, refractive error, or central corneal thickness measurements between the three menstrual phases (Table 1 ). Statistical analysis revealed a significant difference ( p = 0.002) in macular CT measurements between the three menstrual phases. Moreover, measurements of peripapillary CT in the temporal and inferior quadrants also showed a significant difference ( p = 0.004 and p = 0.004 respectively). However, peripapillary RNFL thickness measurements in all four quadrants (temporal, nasal, superior, and inferior) did not show statistically significant variations. Tables 3 and 4 present the findings from OCTA measurements of the macular and peripapillary regions, respectively. The results revealed no statistically significant differences in FAZ parameters across the menstrual cycle. However, VD measurements exhibited a significant difference only within the inferior quadrant of the CC in the macula ( p = 0.030). In the peripapillary region, VD measurements demonstrated significant differences during the menstrual cycle, in the superior quadrant of the SCP and the inferior quadrant of the DCP ( p = 0.040 and p = 0.008 respectively). It is notable that there were significant variations observed in the density of CC VD between menstrual cycle phases in the peripapillary region in both the temporal and inferior quadrants ( p = 0.011 and p = 0.007 respectively). In the macular region, CC VD in the inferior quadrant increased significantly from the early follicular phase (54.05 ± 1.76) to ovulation (54.51 ± 2.09, p = 0.001), and further increased in the mid-luteal phase (55.25 ± 1.94, p = 0.001). In the peripapillary region, SCP VD in the superior quadrant rose from 67.53 ± 6.31 to 68.90 ± 5.32 during ovulation ( p < 0.001), then decreased to 66.48 ± 6.24 in the mid-luteal phase ( p = 0.008). DCP VD in the inferior quadrant showed a similar pattern, increasing from 45.14 ± 7.29 to 48.92 ± 7.69 ( p = 0.001), then declining to 47.05 ± 8.20 ( p = 0.007). Peripapillary CC VD also varied significantly across phases. In the temporal quadrant, it rose from 66.34 ± 6.25 to 68.15 ± 5.66 ( p = 0.010) and then declined to 67.11 ± 6.37 ( p < 0.001). In the inferior quadrant, it increased from 57.60 ± 4.85 to 61.40 ± 4.63 ( p < 0.001), followed by a decrease to 59.39 ± 4.91 ( p = 0.001). [discussion] Discussion The present study constitutes an investigation of menstrual cycle-associated alterations in microvascular and structural characteristics within the macular and peripapillary regions. The investigation was conducted through a comprehensive analysis of OCT parameters such as GCIPL, CMT and CT, as well as OCTA-assessed SCP, DCP, and CC layers. The study analysis demonstrated a significant decrease in macular CT from the follicular to the mid-luteal phase of the menstrual cycle. Peripapillary CT measurements also exhibited a significant reduction in the temporal and inferior quadrants. Furthermore, detailed OCTA analysis revealed significant variations in VD. In the macula, a significant increase was observed exclusively within the inferior quadrant of the CC layer. VD measurements within the peripapillary region revealed significant differences between menstrual cycle phases, particularly in the superior quadrant of the SCP and the inferior quadrant of the DCP. It is noteworthy that significant differences were observed in the CC VD between the different menstrual cycle phases within the peripapillary region, specifically in the temporal and inferior quadrants. Whilst a recent study has examined menstrual cycle-related changes in the microvasculature of the macula and the peripapillary region, the present study is the first to investigate layer-segmented peripapillary vascular changes across menstrual phases using OCTA [ 9 ]. The vasodilatory influence of estrogen on ocular blood flow is exerted via both endothelium-dependent and independent mechanisms [ 3 ]. Elevated estrogen levels have been observed to stimulate the synthesis of nitric oxide while concurrently inhibiting the production of endothelin-1. These counterbalancing effects collectively promote vasodilation and reduce vascular resistance [ 10 ]. In contrast, progesterone functions as an estrogen antagonist, inducing vasoconstriction [ 11 ]. The majority of studies examining the effects of hormones on ocular microvasculature have concentrated on the period of pregnancy. Previous research into the subject of pregnancy has indicated that the observed increase in vascular density within the retina and the optic disc during pregnancy is likely attributable to physiological adaptations necessitated by the gravid state [ 12 ]. Hormonal fluctuations constitute a potential mechanism underlying these ocular vascular changes. Given the established influence of sex hormones on various physiological and pathological processes within ocular tissues, it has been postulated that these hormones may contribute to the pathogenesis of certain ocular diseases [ 13 , 14 ]. A small number of studies have been conducted into the impact of the menstrual cycle on ocular anatomy and vascularization, as evidenced in the literature [ 4 , 5 , 8 ]. Guo et al. demonstrated a significant decrease in VD within the nasal and inferior quadrants of the DCP during the ovulatory phase compared to the early follicular and luteal phases. The authors hypothesise that hormone fluctuations across the menstrual cycle may be responsible for the significant changes in DCP microvasculature observed during the ovulatory phase. This finding contrasts with the stability of DCP VD observed during the early follicular and luteal phases [ 8 ]. In contrast to the aforementioned study, our findings revealed no statistically significant difference in SCP and DCP in the macular area. Nevertheless, a statistically significant alteration in VD was observed in the inferior quadrant of the CC layer. This discrepancy may be attributed to the fact that the choroidal circulation exhibits a comparatively limited capacity for autoregulation in comparison to the retina. The choroid is more directly influenced by systemic conditions and exhibits reduced capacity to maintain constant blood flow during fluctuations in ocular perfusion pressure [ 15 ]. Moreover, retinal arterioles function as the primary resistance vessels within the retinal vasculature, exerting a considerable influence on retinal hemodynamics through the modulation of their luminal diameter. The mechanism of vascular autoregulation in retinal vessels represents a significant area of investigation. Retinal arterioles modulate capillary perfusion by dynamically adjusting their luminal diameter through changes in vascular smooth muscle tone [ 16 ]. Furthermore, the utilisation of different OCTA devices may have contributed to the observed variability in the findings. Device-specific differences in OCTA technology, including segmentation algorithms, can influence the measurement and visualisation of vascular changes and may contribute to the observed discrepancies in VD across studies. In alignment with the established characteristics of the ovulation phase, characterized by elevated estrogen and reduced progesterone levels, the peripapillary region exhibited a notable increase in VD measurements between menstrual cycle phases. This was particularly evident in the superior quadrant of the SCP, as well as in the inferior quadrant of the DCP, and also in the temporal and inferior quadrants of the CC. Post-hoc analysis revealed a pronounced increase in VD during the ovulatory phase relative to both the follicular and mid-luteal phases. Furthermore, our study evaluated alterations in the choroid through OCT. The study demonstrated a significant reduction in macular CT from the follicular to the mid-luteal phase of the menstrual cycle. Also, peripapillary CT measurements demonstrated a significant reduction in the temporal and inferior quadrants. The structural changes observed in the choroid across the menstrual cycle were found to be parallel with the microvascular alterations in the CC. The present findings are consistent with those of recent studies exploring hormonal influences on posterior ocular structures. A recent study reported reduced DCP VD in women using combined oral contraceptives (COCs), suggesting a hormone-mediated modulation of retinal microvasculature detectable by OCTA. In a similar vein, Icoz et al. observed a significant decrease in choroidal vascular index values in individuals who used COC, in comparison to those who did not use COC. This observation is indicative of alterations in the luminal component of the choroid. These findings are complementary to our observation of increased VD during the ovulatory phase, suggesting that the physiological estrogen surge may enhance microvascular perfusion, whereas hormonally suppressed states such as COC use may attenuate it, thereby highlighting the hormonal sensitivity of retinal and choroidal microcirculation [ 17 , 18 ]. The existing literature has demonstrated alterations in CT throughout the menstrual cycle. Ulaş et al. employed spectral-domain OCT to demonstrate a significant reduction in CT during the mid-luteal phase in comparison to earlier stages of the menstrual cycle in a cohort of healthy women. Similarly, Asıkgarip et al. reported a consistent reduction in CT across all regions during the mid-luteal phase. Proposed mechanisms for these changes in CT include vasoconstriction mediated by alpha-adrenergic and/or renin activity, as well as hormonal fluctuations that are characteristic of the mid-luteal phase [ 19 , 20 ]. Although we discussed multiple mechanisms through which estrogen exerts its impact on retinal microvascular structure, there remains a degree of inconsistency in how this effect is represented within the scientific literature. Leung et al. conducted a study to explore the relationship between hormone replacement therapy and retinal arteriolar diameter in an older female population. Contrary to the prevailing hypothesis, their findings indicated no significant vasodilatory effect of hormone replacement therapy on retinal arterioles. In contrast, Wong et al. reported a cross-sectional association between estrogen replacement therapy and narrower retinal vessel diameters in a population-based study, independent of blood pressure and other vascular factors. Both studies employed a standardized methodology, measuring retinal arteriolar and venular diameters within a one-disc diameter radius of the optic disc using computer-assisted image analysis of digitized color fundus photographs [ 21 , 22 ]. Two decades ago, Karadeniz et al. investigated the changes in hemodynamics observed in the ophthalmic, central retinal, and posterior ciliary arteries across the menstrual cycle. Doppler ultrasonography was performed on 23 healthy women across six distinct menstrual cycle phases. No significant variations in pulsatility index, resistance index, or peak systolic velocity were observed among the arterial segments. The authors proposed that these findings indicate that the complex interplay of hormonal influences on systemic hemodynamics, including heart rate, blood pressure, and blood volume, may offset any potential menstrual cycle-related alterations in ocular blood flow [ 7 ]. Recent advancements in ophthalmic technology have shifted the paradigm for assessing intraocular perfusion away from traditional Doppler and pulsatility examinations towards the utilization of OCT and OCTA. In alignment with this technological shift, our study comprehensively employed OCT and OCTA imaging for data acquisition. In a previous OCTA study, Savastano et al. observed a significantly higher VD measurements of CC in patients with hypothalamic amenorrhea compared to controls, both in the whole area and in the fovea area. The authors attributed this finding to potential endothelial dysfunction secondary to estrogen deficiency, characterized by reduced nitric oxide production, elevated oxidative stress, and proinflammatory cytokine levels, which ultimately lead to abnormal vascular remodeling and microvascular constriction. Based on these findings, they hypothesized that the observed increase in VD might reflect compensatory microvascular changes in response to these underlying pathophysiological processes [ 23 ]. Consistent with the findings of Savastano et al., a recent study by Çetinkaya et al. utilized swept-source OCTA to investigate retinal and choroidal microvascular changes in postmenopausal women. The study reported a reduction in VD of CC in postmenopausal participants compared to age-matched controls with ongoing menstruation. Notably, a negative correlation between VD and menopause duration suggested that prolonged hypoestrogenism adversely impacts the microvascular structure of this layer [ 24 ]. A primary limitation of this study was the absence of serum sex hormone level measurements. Without direct quantification of estradiol and progesterone levels, we were unable to correlate retinal and choroidal microvascular changes with precise hormonal fluctuations during the menstrual cycle. Although menstrual phases were carefully timed using well-established cycle days (day 3, 14, and 21) and participants were required to prospectively document the onset of menstruation to confirm regular ovulatory cycles, individual variation in hormonal profiles may still exist. This limitation introduces the potential for misclassification of cycle phases, which could affect the accuracy of interpreting phase-specific vascular changes. The absence of biochemical validation may reduce the granularity of phase assignment. As such, the observed changes in VD and CT should be interpreted with caution, and future studies incorporating serum hormone analysis would offer a more robust framework for confirming the hormonal basis of the vascular alterations observed with OCTA. Additionally, the relatively small sample size may limit the generalizability of the findings and reduce statistical power to detect more subtle microvascular changes across menstrual phases. In conclusion, the present study indicates that the hormonal fluctuations associated with the menstrual cycle exert a substantial impact on the architecture and vasculature of the retina and choroid. A crucial finding is the identification of structural alterations in the choroid across menstrual phases, which may indicate a potential association with concurrent microvascular changes in the CC. Notable variations were observed in both peripapillary CT and CC microvasculature in the inferior and temporal regions. It is therefore recommended that clinicians take the menstrual cycle phase into account when interpreting OCTA data in women of reproductive age, to ensure optimal diagnostic accuracy and treatment planning.

[conclusion] Conclusion Hormonal fluctuations throughout the menstrual cycle appear to influence both retinal and choroidal microvasculature, particularly in the choriocapillaris and peripapillary regions. These findings underscore the importance of considering menstrual phase when interpreting OCTA measurements in women of reproductive age, to improve diagnostic accuracy and consistency in clinical and research settings.

retrieved_papers.title: The effect of oral contraceptive pills on the macula, the retinal nerve fiber layer, the ganglion cell layer and the choroidal thickness.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2019]
[Abstract] To evaluate the effect of oral contraceptive pills (OCP) on the macula, the retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL), the ganglion cell layer (GCL), and the choroidal thickness (CT).
In this prospective observational cross-sectional study, 60 eyes of 30 healthy women taking monophasic OCP (0.03 mg ethinylestradiol and 0.15 mg levonorgestrel) for contraception for at least 1 year were compared with 60 eyes of a control group of 30 healthy women who were not taking any OCP. Spectral-Domain Optical Coherence Tomography (SD-OCT) was used to evaluate the macula, the RNFL, the GCL, and the CT. Measurements were taken in the follicular phase (day 3) of the last menstrual cycle in all women. The body mass index (BMI) scores of all participants were also recorded.
No disparity in terms of age and BMI between both groups was observed (p = 0.444, p = 0.074, respectively). All the macular parameters measurements were considerably lower in the OCP group compared to the control group (p < 0.001). Also, the RNFL thickness, the GCL thickness, and the CT were all significantly thinner in the OCP group (p < 0.001).
The use of OCP can cause significant changes in the retina and choroid thickness over 1 year period. The women who are using OCP for a longer duration could have some eye problems. OCT should be routinely done for follow up. Further long term studies are required, using different preparations of OCP. It is important to find out when this thickness alterations can be clinically significant or symptomatic and if these changes are reversible or not.

[Introduction] Background Oral contraceptive pills (OCP) are commonly used by women all over the world during their reproductive life. Several studies reported the occurrence of a variety of ocular disorders in women using OCP [ 1 ]. Estrogen and progesterone receptors have been observed in multiple ocular tissues, such as the choroid, retina, lens, conjunctiva, cornea, and the Meibomian gland [ 2 ]. Sex hormones proved to affect the retina and the choroid and a correlation between sex hormones and retinal disorders have been described [ 2 – 4 ]. The macula is the central part of the retina and any degenerative changes in this area could affect the central vision. The macula, the (RNFL), the ganglion cell layer (GCL), and the choroidal thickness (CT) may decrease with age, degenerative diseases, ischemia, chronic inflammatory processes [ 5 ]. These changes may also be due to genetic, environmental, and hormonal factors [ 6 ]. Since oral contraceptive pills (OCP) usually contain estrogen and progesterone, the retina and choroid are at risk. In literature, there is very little data concerning the effect of OCP on the thickness of the macula, the RNFL, the GCL, and the CT [ 7 ]. In this study, we aim to evaluate the effect of OCP on the macula, the NFL, GCL, and the CT, to address any reported complications or side effects to people who use OCP for contraception or for other reasons other than contraception and to consider regular ocular examination and follow up while using these pills.

[introduction] Background Oral contraceptive pills (OCP) are commonly used by women all over the world during their reproductive life. Several studies reported the occurrence of a variety of ocular disorders in women using OCP [ 1 ]. Estrogen and progesterone receptors have been observed in multiple ocular tissues, such as the choroid, retina, lens, conjunctiva, cornea, and the Meibomian gland [ 2 ]. Sex hormones proved to affect the retina and the choroid and a correlation between sex hormones and retinal disorders have been described [ 2 – 4 ]. The macula is the central part of the retina and any degenerative changes in this area could affect the central vision. The macula, the (RNFL), the ganglion cell layer (GCL), and the choroidal thickness (CT) may decrease with age, degenerative diseases, ischemia, chronic inflammatory processes [ 5 ]. These changes may also be due to genetic, environmental, and hormonal factors [ 6 ]. Since oral contraceptive pills (OCP) usually contain estrogen and progesterone, the retina and choroid are at risk. In literature, there is very little data concerning the effect of OCP on the thickness of the macula, the RNFL, the GCL, and the CT [ 7 ]. In this study, we aim to evaluate the effect of OCP on the macula, the NFL, GCL, and the CT, to address any reported complications or side effects to people who use OCP for contraception or for other reasons other than contraception and to consider regular ocular examination and follow up while using these pills.

[methods] Methods Aim Is to evaluate the thickness of the macula, the RNFL, the GCL, and the choroid using SD-OCT in women using the same OCP and compare them with those who do not use any OCP. Sixty eyes of 30 healthy women who had received OCP for more than 1 year (the OCP group) were compared with 60 eyes of healthy women who were not using any OCP (the control group). All participants were Egyptian women, in their reproductive age period of 23–36 years. Those who do not use OCP have a regular natural menstrual cycle of 28–30 days. All participants were evaluated in the follicular phase (day3) of their last menstrual cycle. Design An observational comparative cross-sectional study. Setting The study was performed in The Eye Subspecialty Center (ESC), Heliopolis, Cairo, Egypt. The cases were recruited from patients visiting the Outpatient Clinic, and the OCT was performed in the Investigation Unit. All procedures in this study adhered to the Declaration of Helsinki’s ethical principles for medical research involving human subjects and were approved by the Ethics Committee Of The Eye Subspecialty Center (ESC), 18 El Khalifa El Maamoun Street, Heliopolis, 11,402 Cairo, Egypt. Verbal informed consent was obtained from all participants. All candidates had a complete ophthalmologic examination including medical history, visual acuity, cycloplegic refraction, Goldmann applanation tonometry, slit-lamp bio microscopy, and fundus examination. Those with the best-corrected visual acuity of ≥20/25, spherical refraction within ±3.00 D, and cylinder refraction within ±1.50 D, and had normal ocular examination results were included in the study. Body mass index (BMI) of all candidates was also measured and compared in both groups based on the formula: BMI = (weight/height 2 [kg/m 2 ]). Exclusion criteria Those who had previous ocular surgery, ocular trauma, uveitis, glaucoma, cataract, macular degeneration, and cystoid macular edema were excluded from the study. Pregnancy, diabetes mellitus, hypertension, cardiovascular events, thyroid disorders, Cushing disease, thromboembolic disease, cancer, chronic liver disease, kidney disease, pancreatitis, congenital adrenal hyperplasia, psychotic disorders, and those who use antidepressants, steroidal hormone, mood stabilizers, caffeine or tobacco were also excluded from the study. The used OCP is a commonly prescribed type of combined oral contraceptive (COC) birth control in Egypt. It is monophasic pills having estrogen (0.03 mg ethinylestradiol) and progestin (0.15 mg levonorgestrel). Monophasic pills contain equal amounts of the hormones estrogen and progestin for an entire monthly cycle. Spectral-Domain Optical Coherence Tomography (SD-OCT), Cirrus HD-OCT model 5000 (Carl Zeiss Meditec, Germany) was used to measure the thickness of macula, the RNFL, the GCL, and the CT. The procedure was done by the same person using the same OCT unit. The Scan Protocol was done as follows: Macular Cube Scan 512 × 128 was used for macular thickness, cube volume, cube average thickness, and ganglion cell layer thickness. The Optic Disc Cube 200 × 200 SCAN was used for RNFL thickness analysis. The Enhanced Depth Image Scan was used for choroidal thickness, in which three points were measured, subfoveal, 3 mm point nasal to the fovea and 3 mm point temporal to the fovea. The study was done in the period between January 2017 and October 2018. Statistics Quantitative data were presented as mean and standard deviation. Independent samples t-test was used to test the statistical significance of the difference of means of the two groups. P values ≤0.05 were considered significant. The Statistical Package for the Social Sciences SPSS version 20 for Windows (IBM Corp., Armonk, NY) was used for the statistical analysis.

[results] Results There was no disparity in terms of age and BMI between both the studied groups. The OCP group had a mean age of 30.48 ± 3.4 years while the control group had a mean age of 29.97 ± 3.87 years (t = 0.777, p = 0.444). The mean BMI scores was 23.3 + 2.2 kg/m 2 for the OCP group and 22.3 + 2.3 kg/m 2 for the control group (t = 1.818, P = 0.074). BMI of 18.5–24.9 kg/m 2 is considered as normal . The difference between the two groups was statistically insignificant (Table 1 ). The OCP used in this study had a significant thinning effect on all the parameters of all parts of the macula ( p < 0.001), all parts of RNFL ( p < 0.001), all parts of GCL ( p < 0.001), subfoveal choroid, temporal and nasal choroid 3 mm points nasal and temporal to the fovea ( p < 0.001) (Tables 2 , 3 , 4 ). Table 1 Demographic data for oral contraceptive pills group and the control group Parameter OCP Group ( n = 60) Control Group ( n = 60) t p Age, years 30.48 ± 3.4 29.97 ± 3.87 0.777 0.444 Gender female female Number of subjects 30 30 Ethnicity Egyptians Egyptians Study location Cairo, Egypt Cairo, Egypt BMI. kg/m 2 23.3 + 2.2 22.3 + 2.3 1.818 0.074 OCP Oral contraceptive pills, BMI Body Mass Index, Values are expressed as mean ± SD. Values are expressed as mean ± SD. P values ≤0.05 were considered significant

[discussion] Discussion Estrogen and progesterone receptors have been observed in different ocular tissues, such as the cornea, the conjunctiva, the Meibomian glands, the lens, the choroid, and the retina. The expression of estrogen and progesterone receptors in the eye is responsible for their ocular effects and various studies published in the literature about these receptors and their role in changing eye structures [ 2 ]. Oral Contraceptive Pills are widely used for contraception, but they are also indicated in menorrhagia, endometriosis, acne and hirsutism, fibroid uterus and premenstrual syndrome [ 8 ]. Reviewing the impact of combined oral contraceptives on ocular tissues showed that the OCP has multiple adverse effects on ocular tissues [ 1 ], which include dry eye symptoms related to decreased lipid production [ 9 ], asymptomatic corneal edema, contact lenses discomfort and intolerance [ 10 ]. Significant increase in the central corneal thickness values was also reported in patients using OCP [ 11 ] . There are also retinal neuro-ophthalmologic complications involving the 6th cranial nerve paralysis, parietal syndrome, hemianopsia, papillary edema, and retrobulbar neuritis [ 12 ]. The vascular complications of OCP include central retinal artery or vein occlusion, intraocular hemorrhages, aneurysms, macular or disc edema, and acute ischemic optic neuropathy [ 13 ]. Also, they have been associated with high rates of cardiovascular events, venous thromboembolic disease, ischemic strokes, and breast cancer [ 14 , 15 ]. There are also some studies concerning the effect of sex hormone fluctuations during the menstrual cycle on the eye. Some researchers have shown that these hormone fluctuations exhibited correlations with changes in ocular tissue. Tear production, stability, dryness, and inflammation were significantly related to hormonal fluctuations in the menstrual cycle. Impairment of these functions appeared to be related to the estrogen peak during the follicular phase, especially in patients with a dry eye [ 16 ]. During the menstrual cycle, the corneal thickness is thinner at the beginning of the cycle and thicker at the end [ 17 ]. Evaluation of the effects of the menstrual cycle on the choroidal thickness of healthy women of reproductive age showed a significant decrease in thickness in the mid-luteal phase of the menstrual cycle [ 18 ]. Also, the choroidal thickness measured by SD-OCT was reported to be significantly thinner in postmenopausal women than healthy reproductive-age women, which speculated to be secondary to menopausal estrogen deficiency [ 19 ]. In this study, we were evaluating the thickness of the macula, the RNFL, GCL, and CT in women who received OCP (0.03 mg ethinylestradiol and 0.15 mg levonorgestrel) regularly for at least 1 year. We observed that the thickness of all the macular parameters, the cube macular volume, the RNFL, the GCL, and the CT decreased significantly in the OCP group compared to the control group. These thinning effect of the OCP are similar to age-related hormonal changes in which thinning of the macula is due to macular atrophy or RNFL atrophy [ 20 ]. In this study, these findings were not considered related to age since the average age of the women was 30.48 ± 3.4 years. Apart from the study done by Madendag et al. [ 7 ] in 2017, concerning the effect of OCP on the thickness of the macula, the RNFL and the choroid using OCT, there are no other available data. Madendag et al. used OCP containing 0.03 mg ethinylestradiol and 3 mg drospirenone. They found that all OCT measurements of macular parameters were considerably decreased in the OCP group compared to the control group except the foveal center thickness which remained unchanged. They speculated that the absence of blood vessels in the fovea could be the cause of unaffected fovea. They also found that only the average of the RNFL, the nasal-inferior, and the temporal-inferior parts of the RNFL was considerably slimmer in the OCP group but the other parts of the RNFL remained unchanged. They also reported unchanged CT. They speculated that using a combined oral contraceptive could be the cause of unaffected CT since the estrogen is opposed by the progesterone. In accordance with our study is that all macular variable values were considerably lower in the OCP group compared to the control. The same was applied to the cube macular volume which decreased in both studies. Controversial with our results is reporting thinning of only parts of the peripapillary RNFL and not all the parts. They also reported unaffected choroid. We reported significant thinning of all parts of RNFL, all parts, of GCL and the choroid. We also compared our data of the control group regarding the MT, CT, and NFL thickness (Tables 2 and 3 ) with the normative data of other studies using SD-OCT. Liu et al. [ 21 ], using Cirrus HD-OCT reported CST of 262.4 ± 22.8 μm, macular thickness (MT) of 281.3 ± 14.5 μm, and macular volume (MV) of 10.1 ± 0.6 mm 3 , respectively. Manjunath et al. [ 22 ], reported mean subfoveal (CT) of 272 ± 81 μm, showing a thin choroid nasally, thickest choroid sub-foveally, and thinner choroid temporally. Using Spectralis SD-OCT, Bindu et al. [ 23 ], provided the normative data of MT and RNFL thickness in Indians. They reported normal central foveal thickness of 260.1 ± 18.19 um. The nasal inner quadrant showed maximum retinal thickness (338.88 ± 18.17 um). The mean RNFL thickness was 101.43 ± 8.63 um with maximum thickness in the inferior quadrant. El-Hifnawy et al. [ 24 , 25 ] collected normative MT and RNFL thickness data in the Egyptian population, the mean CST was 262.70 ± 19.64 μm and the mean NFLT was 101.74 ± 10.05 μm. The mean MT values were significantly greater in men than in women and were found to be less than those seen in the studies published previously on Caucasians using Spectralis SD-OCT. Although the values in our study are in the normative range of other studies, SD-OCT measurements cannot be compared between different machines because each technology’s normative database is derived from a different population of different racial or ethnic groups. Sex and age also have an influential effect.

[conclusion] Conclusions We conclude that women who are using OCP for more than 1 year could develop some eye problems which could involve the central vision. We also think that using OCP with different preparations could give different results. Further long term studies are required using different preparations of combined oral contraceptive pills (COC) or progestin-only contraceptive pills (POC) to find out what are the consequences of these changes when these thickness alterations can be clinically significant or symptomatic and if these changes are reversible or not. OCT should be routinely done for follow up these cases. According to these findings, physicians should take into consideration patients ocular history, before selecting the method of contraception and before prescribing OCP for reasons other than contraception.

retrieved_papers.title: Chorioretinal biomarkers in hypothalamic amenorrhea.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2024]
[Abstract] The aim of our study was to evaluate changes in the retinal and choriocapillaris circulations in patients with hypothalamic amenorrhea.
Prospective, cross-sectional observational study on 25 patients (50 eyes) diagnosed with hypothalamic amenorrhea and 25 age-matched healthy women. Optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA) was used to evaluate the vessel density (VD) of superficial capillary plexus (SCP), deep capillary plexus (DCP), and choriocapillaris VD layers in whole 6.4 × 6.4-mm image and in fovea grid-based image. In patients' group, systemic parameters were collected: body mass index (BMI), endometrial rhyme thickness, follicle stimulating hormone (FSH), luteinizing hormone (LH), prolactin, insulin, and cortisol.
SCP and DCP did not show any statistical difference when comparing patients and controls (all p > 0.05). Differently, choriocapillaris VD in the whole region showed a non-significant tendency toward higher values in the patients group in both eyes (p = 0.038 for right eye [RE], p = 0.044 for left eye [LE]). Foveal choriocapillaris VD was higher in hypothalamic amenorrhea women vs. healthy controls (66.0 ± 2.4 vs. 63.7 ± 6.6%, p = 0.136 for RE; 65.0 ± 2.4 vs. 61.6 ± 7.0%, p = 0.005 for LE). Focusing on correlation with systemic parameters, SCP and DCP foveal density had a medium/high effect size with endometrial rhyme, along with DCP in the fovea area vs. cortisol and SCP in the whole area vs. FSH.
When comparing hypothalamic amenorrhea patients to healthy subjects, OCTA detected changes in the choriocapillaris layer, showing increased VD in the early stage of the systemic pathology, suggesting that microvascular "compaction" could be a first phase of hypoestrogenism adaptation.

[Introduction] Introduction Amenorrhea is defined as absence or abnormal cessation of the menses, and it can be differentiated as primary or secondary entity. Primary amenorrhea consists in the absence of menses since birth. For this reason, patients do not reach menarche by the age of 15 or up to 3 years after thelarche. Causes can be represented by chromosomal irregularities (e.g., Turner syndrome) or anatomic abnormalities (e.g., Müllerian agenesis) [ 1 ]. On the other hand, secondary amenorrhea is defined as the interruption of regular menses for 3 months or the cessation of irregular menses for 6 months, excluding pregnancy. The most frequent causes of secondary amenorrhea are polycystic ovary syndrome, hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis dysfunction, hyperprolactinemia, or primary ovarian insufficiency [ 2 ]. An exhaustive anamnesis in secondary amenorrhea is crucial, comprehending eating and exercise habits, presence of psychosocial stress, body weight changes, medication use, and chronic illness [ 3 ]. In most cases, amenorrhea is accompanied by severe hypoestrogenism. Low levels of estrogen manifest in multiple ways, such as low bone mineral density, vaginal and breast atrophy, infertility, and dyspareunia [ 4 ]. Moreover, in cases of hypoestrogenism, the beneficial effects on the circulatory system of these hormones should be considered. Particularly, estrogens show vasodilatory properties, modifying the production of endothelial derived substances and leading to vasodilatation through endothelial-dependent events, such as increasing nitric oxide (NO) and prostacyclin (PGI2) production and reducing endothelin (ET-1) plasma levels [ 5 , 6 ]. Estrogen-mediated vasodilatation is as well caused by endothelial independent events, which seem to be calcium mediated [ 7 ]. These responses lead to a reduction in vascular resistance, which has been observed in various vascular beds such as carotid, cerebral arteries [ 8 ], and central retinal artery [ 9 ]. Several studies have investigated the features of the ocular circulation, comparing women and men, as well as fertile women and those in menopause, particularly with Doppler and pulsatility exams [ 10 – 15 ]. Ustymowicz et al. assessed the differences in retrobulbar blood velocities in women and men and found lower values for the short posterior ciliary artery in men compared to women [ 10 ]. Kavroulaki and colleagues, investigating choroidal blood flow in female patients of different ages, found significantly higher values in women younger than 40 years compared to women older than 55 years [ 11 ]. Further research analyzed the pulsatile ocular blood flow and pulse amplitude, which resulted significantly higher in pre-menopausal women compared to age-matched males and postmenopausal women not under hormone therapy (HT) [ 12 ]. Moreover, it has been reported that pulsatile ocular blood flow in women decreases after menopause, and suspension of estrogen replacement therapy in postmenopausal women causes reduction in ocular blood flow [ 13 ], while pre-clinical studies showed that HT increases retinal perfusion and protects retinal nerve fiber layer [ 16 ]. Consistent with these results, Toker et al. highlighted a direct relationship between serum estradiol and peak systolic velocity (PSV) and end-diastolic velocity (EDV) in the ophthalmic and central retinal arteries, measured by color Doppler imaging, suggesting a beneficial effect of estrogens on ocular hemodynamics [ 17 ]. Moreover, several works showed an increased resistivity index in both ophthalmic and short posterior ciliary arteries in postmenopausal women [ 18 , 19 ]. On the other side, Wong et al. discovered that estrogen replacement therapy (ERT) seemed to exert vasoconstrictive effects in smaller retinal blood vessels, in contrast to the vasodilating effects of estrogen in bigger arteries elsewhere [ 20 ]. However, in our acknowledgment, no previous studies had investigated chorioretinal involvement in women suffering from hypothalamic amenorrhea. The recent introduction of optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA) has allowed to deeply investigate these circulations and to obtain a post-processing quantitative analysis of vascular parameters [ 21 ]. Few researchers have used the OCTA to analyze eventual vascular changes in gynecological patients [ 22 , 23 ]. Thus, the aim of our study was to evaluate eventual changes in the retinal and choriocapillaris circulations, evaluated with the OCTA, in female patients with previous diagnosis of hypothalamic amenorrhea in comparison with age- and sex-matched healthy controls. Moreover, as secondary outcome, we evaluated the correlation of systemic parameters and hormone profile obtained by blood test, with ocular biomarkers.

[introduction] Introduction Amenorrhea is defined as absence or abnormal cessation of the menses, and it can be differentiated as primary or secondary entity. Primary amenorrhea consists in the absence of menses since birth. For this reason, patients do not reach menarche by the age of 15 or up to 3 years after thelarche. Causes can be represented by chromosomal irregularities (e.g., Turner syndrome) or anatomic abnormalities (e.g., Müllerian agenesis) [ 1 ]. On the other hand, secondary amenorrhea is defined as the interruption of regular menses for 3 months or the cessation of irregular menses for 6 months, excluding pregnancy. The most frequent causes of secondary amenorrhea are polycystic ovary syndrome, hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis dysfunction, hyperprolactinemia, or primary ovarian insufficiency [ 2 ]. An exhaustive anamnesis in secondary amenorrhea is crucial, comprehending eating and exercise habits, presence of psychosocial stress, body weight changes, medication use, and chronic illness [ 3 ]. In most cases, amenorrhea is accompanied by severe hypoestrogenism. Low levels of estrogen manifest in multiple ways, such as low bone mineral density, vaginal and breast atrophy, infertility, and dyspareunia [ 4 ].
