[discussion] Discussion The present study constitutes an investigation of menstrual cycle-associated alterations in microvascular and structural characteristics within the macular and peripapillary regions. The investigation was conducted through a comprehensive analysis of OCT parameters such as GCIPL, CMT and CT, as well as OCTA-assessed SCP, DCP, and CC layers. The study analysis demonstrated a significant decrease in macular CT from the follicular to the mid-luteal phase of the menstrual cycle. Peripapillary CT measurements also exhibited a significant reduction in the temporal and inferior quadrants. Furthermore, detailed OCTA analysis revealed significant variations in VD. In the macula, a significant increase was observed exclusively within the inferior quadrant of the CC layer. VD measurements within the peripapillary region revealed significant differences between menstrual cycle phases, particularly in the superior quadrant of the SCP and the inferior quadrant of the DCP. It is noteworthy that significant differences were observed in the CC VD between the different menstrual cycle phases within the peripapillary region, specifically in the temporal and inferior quadrants. Whilst a recent study has examined menstrual cycle-related changes in the microvasculature of the macula and the peripapillary region, the present study is the first to investigate layer-segmented peripapillary vascular changes across menstrual phases using OCTA [ 9 ]. The vasodilatory influence of estrogen on ocular blood flow is exerted via both endothelium-dependent and independent mechanisms [ 3 ]. Elevated estrogen levels have been observed to stimulate the synthesis of nitric oxide while concurrently inhibiting the production of endothelin-1. These counterbalancing effects collectively promote vasodilation and reduce vascular resistance [ 10 ]. In contrast, progesterone functions as an estrogen antagonist, inducing vasoconstriction [ 11 ]. The majority of studies examining the effects of hormones on ocular microvasculature have concentrated on the period of pregnancy. Previous research into the subject of pregnancy has indicated that the observed increase in vascular density within the retina and the optic disc during pregnancy is likely attributable to physiological adaptations necessitated by the gravid state [ 12 ]. Hormonal fluctuations constitute a potential mechanism underlying these ocular vascular changes. Given the established influence of sex hormones on various physiological and pathological processes within ocular tissues, it has been postulated that these hormones may contribute to the pathogenesis of certain ocular diseases [ 13 , 14 ]. A small number of studies have been conducted into the impact of the menstrual cycle on ocular anatomy and vascularization, as evidenced in the literature [ 4 , 5 , 8 ]. Guo et al. demonstrated a significant decrease in VD within the nasal and inferior quadrants of the DCP during the ovulatory phase compared to the early follicular and luteal phases. The authors hypothesise that hormone fluctuations across the menstrual cycle may be responsible for the significant changes in DCP microvasculature observed during the ovulatory phase. This finding contrasts with the stability of DCP VD observed during the early follicular and luteal phases [ 8 ]. In contrast to the aforementioned study, our findings revealed no statistically significant difference in SCP and DCP in the macular area. Nevertheless, a statistically significant alteration in VD was observed in the inferior quadrant of the CC layer. This discrepancy may be attributed to the fact that the choroidal circulation exhibits a comparatively limited capacity for autoregulation in comparison to the retina. The choroid is more directly influenced by systemic conditions and exhibits reduced capacity to maintain constant blood flow during fluctuations in ocular perfusion pressure [ 15 ]. Moreover, retinal arterioles function as the primary resistance vessels within the retinal vasculature, exerting a considerable influence on retinal hemodynamics through the modulation of their luminal diameter. The mechanism of vascular autoregulation in retinal vessels represents a significant area of investigation. Retinal arterioles modulate capillary perfusion by dynamically adjusting their luminal diameter through changes in vascular smooth muscle tone [ 16 ]. Furthermore, the utilisation of different OCTA devices may have contributed to the observed variability in the findings. Device-specific differences in OCTA technology, including segmentation algorithms, can influence the measurement and visualisation of vascular changes and may contribute to the observed discrepancies in VD across studies. In alignment with the established characteristics of the ovulation phase, characterized by elevated estrogen and reduced progesterone levels, the peripapillary region exhibited a notable increase in VD measurements between menstrual cycle phases. This was particularly evident in the superior quadrant of the SCP, as well as in the inferior quadrant of the DCP, and also in the temporal and inferior quadrants of the CC. Post-hoc analysis revealed a pronounced increase in VD during the ovulatory phase relative to both the follicular and mid-luteal phases. Furthermore, our study evaluated alterations in the choroid through OCT. The study demonstrated a significant reduction in macular CT from the follicular to the mid-luteal phase of the menstrual cycle. Also, peripapillary CT measurements demonstrated a significant reduction in the temporal and inferior quadrants. The structural changes observed in the choroid across the menstrual cycle were found to be parallel with the microvascular alterations in the CC. The present findings are consistent with those of recent studies exploring hormonal influences on posterior ocular structures. A recent study reported reduced DCP VD in women using combined oral contraceptives (COCs), suggesting a hormone-mediated modulation of retinal microvasculature detectable by OCTA. In a similar vein, Icoz et al. observed a significant decrease in choroidal vascular index values in individuals who used COC, in comparison to those who did not use COC. This observation is indicative of alterations in the luminal component of the choroid. These findings are complementary to our observation of increased VD during the ovulatory phase, suggesting that the physiological estrogen surge may enhance microvascular perfusion, whereas hormonally suppressed states such as COC use may attenuate it, thereby highlighting the hormonal sensitivity of retinal and choroidal microcirculation [ 17 , 18 ]. The existing literature has demonstrated alterations in CT throughout the menstrual cycle. Ulaş et al. employed spectral-domain OCT to demonstrate a significant reduction in CT during the mid-luteal phase in comparison to earlier stages of the menstrual cycle in a cohort of healthy women. Similarly, Asıkgarip et al. reported a consistent reduction in CT across all regions during the mid-luteal phase. Proposed mechanisms for these changes in CT include vasoconstriction mediated by alpha-adrenergic and/or renin activity, as well as hormonal fluctuations that are characteristic of the mid-luteal phase [ 19 , 20 ]. Although we discussed multiple mechanisms through which estrogen exerts its impact on retinal microvascular structure, there remains a degree of inconsistency in how this effect is represented within the scientific literature. Leung et al. conducted a study to explore the relationship between hormone replacement therapy and retinal arteriolar diameter in an older female population. Contrary to the prevailing hypothesis, their findings indicated no significant vasodilatory effect of hormone replacement therapy on retinal arterioles. In contrast, Wong et al. reported a cross-sectional association between estrogen replacement therapy and narrower retinal vessel diameters in a population-based study, independent of blood pressure and other vascular factors. Both studies employed a standardized methodology, measuring retinal arteriolar and venular diameters within a one-disc diameter radius of the optic disc using computer-assisted image analysis of digitized color fundus photographs [ 21 , 22 ]. Two decades ago, Karadeniz et al. investigated the changes in hemodynamics observed in the ophthalmic, central retinal, and posterior ciliary arteries across the menstrual cycle. Doppler ultrasonography was performed on 23 healthy women across six distinct menstrual cycle phases. No significant variations in pulsatility index, resistance index, or peak systolic velocity were observed among the arterial segments. The authors proposed that these findings indicate that the complex interplay of hormonal influences on systemic hemodynamics, including heart rate, blood pressure, and blood volume, may offset any potential menstrual cycle-related alterations in ocular blood flow [ 7 ]. Recent advancements in ophthalmic technology have shifted the paradigm for assessing intraocular perfusion away from traditional Doppler and pulsatility examinations towards the utilization of OCT and OCTA. In alignment with this technological shift, our study comprehensively employed OCT and OCTA imaging for data acquisition. In a previous OCTA study, Savastano et al. observed a significantly higher VD measurements of CC in patients with hypothalamic amenorrhea compared to controls, both in the whole area and in the fovea area. The authors attributed this finding to potential endothelial dysfunction secondary to estrogen deficiency, characterized by reduced nitric oxide production, elevated oxidative stress, and proinflammatory cytokine levels, which ultimately lead to abnormal vascular remodeling and microvascular constriction. Based on these findings, they hypothesized that the observed increase in VD might reflect compensatory microvascular changes in response to these underlying pathophysiological processes [ 23 ]. Consistent with the findings of Savastano et al., a recent study by Çetinkaya et al. utilized swept-source OCTA to investigate retinal and choroidal microvascular changes in postmenopausal women. The study reported a reduction in VD of CC in postmenopausal participants compared to age-matched controls with ongoing menstruation. Notably, a negative correlation between VD and menopause duration suggested that prolonged hypoestrogenism adversely impacts the microvascular structure of this layer [ 24 ]. A primary limitation of this study was the absence of serum sex hormone level measurements. Without direct quantification of estradiol and progesterone levels, we were unable to correlate retinal and choroidal microvascular changes with precise hormonal fluctuations during the menstrual cycle. Although menstrual phases were carefully timed using well-established cycle days (day 3, 14, and 21) and participants were required to prospectively document the onset of menstruation to confirm regular ovulatory cycles, individual variation in hormonal profiles may still exist. This limitation introduces the potential for misclassification of cycle phases, which could affect the accuracy of interpreting phase-specific vascular changes. The absence of biochemical validation may reduce the granularity of phase assignment. As such, the observed changes in VD and CT should be interpreted with caution, and future studies incorporating serum hormone analysis would offer a more robust framework for confirming the hormonal basis of the vascular alterations observed with OCTA. Additionally, the relatively small sample size may limit the generalizability of the findings and reduce statistical power to detect more subtle microvascular changes across menstrual phases. In conclusion, the present study indicates that the hormonal fluctuations associated with the menstrual cycle exert a substantial impact on the architecture and vasculature of the retina and choroid. A crucial finding is the identification of structural alterations in the choroid across menstrual phases, which may indicate a potential association with concurrent microvascular changes in the CC. Notable variations were observed in both peripapillary CT and CC microvasculature in the inferior and temporal regions. It is therefore recommended that clinicians take the menstrual cycle phase into account when interpreting OCTA data in women of reproductive age, to ensure optimal diagnostic accuracy and treatment planning.

[conclusion] Conclusion Hormonal fluctuations throughout the menstrual cycle appear to influence both retinal and choroidal microvasculature, particularly in the choriocapillaris and peripapillary regions. These findings underscore the importance of considering menstrual phase when interpreting OCTA measurements in women of reproductive age, to improve diagnostic accuracy and consistency in clinical and research settings.

retrieved_papers.title: The effect of postmenopausal hormonal drop on optic nerve head and peripapillary perfusion using optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA).
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2022]
[Abstract] We studied the effect of menopause with subsequent estrogen drop on optic nerve head structure and peripapillary vasculature. This cross-sectional analytic study was carried out on 100 eyes of 100 patients; patients were divided into a premenopausal group (50 eyes) and a postmenopausal group (50 eyes). Optical coherence tomography was done to evaluate retinal nerve fiber layer thickness (RNFLT) and optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA) to assess the peripapillary capillary vessel density. RNFLT as well as the peripapillary vessel density (VD) were significantly lower in the postmenopausal group (P value < 0.001) with increasing age, hormonal drop, and higher intraocular pressure (IOP), specifically in the inferior quadrant. However, the negative correlation between IOP and VD (r = - 0.541) was stronger than its negative correlation with RNFLT (r = - 0.318). Postmenopausal hormonal changes lead to a significant rise in IOP-although still not glaucomatous- and a decrease in the RNFLT and perfusion of the optic nerve. This confirms the relation between hormonal drop and glaucoma in postmenopausal women. Changes in peripapillary vascular density were more evident than RNFL in correlation with IOP and age changes. So, OCTA can be used to detect early optic nerve affection.

[Introduction] Introduction Despite the global increase in life expectancy, the age of menopause remains fixed. As the proportion of older women increases, an increasing prevalence of menopause and postmenopausal diseases may be expected to be seen 1 . Postmenopausal hormonal changes, especially the decline in estrogen level, are thought to play an important role in increasing the incidence of ocular symptoms and ocular diseases 2 . Estrogen is a known vasodilator and neuroprotective hormone. Its postmenopausal withdrawal can affect the retina because of the presence of estrogen receptors in the retina and its vasculature 3 . In addition, it can increase the likelihood of glaucomatous optic nerve damage 4 , because of the effect of estrogen on lowering the intraocular pressure (IOP) 5 . Currently, there is accumulating evidence that suggests a decrease in RNFLT in postmenopausal women 6 . However, no studies investigated the peripapillary vasculature in postmenopausal women to support similar changes. So, in this study, we investigated the effect of postmenopausal hormonal change on ONH and peripapillary perfusion.

[introduction] Introduction Despite the global increase in life expectancy, the age of menopause remains fixed. As the proportion of older women increases, an increasing prevalence of menopause and postmenopausal diseases may be expected to be seen 1 . Postmenopausal hormonal changes, especially the decline in estrogen level, are thought to play an important role in increasing the incidence of ocular symptoms and ocular diseases 2 . Estrogen is a known vasodilator and neuroprotective hormone. Its postmenopausal withdrawal can affect the retina because of the presence of estrogen receptors in the retina and its vasculature 3 . In addition, it can increase the likelihood of glaucomatous optic nerve damage 4 , because of the effect of estrogen on lowering the intraocular pressure (IOP) 5 . Currently, there is accumulating evidence that suggests a decrease in RNFLT in postmenopausal women 6 . However, no studies investigated the peripapillary vasculature in postmenopausal women to support similar changes. So, in this study, we investigated the effect of postmenopausal hormonal change on ONH and peripapillary perfusion.

[methods] Materials and methods This cross-sectional analytic study was carried out on 100 patients selected from the general Outpatient Clinics of Kasr Alainy school of medicine, Cairo University Hospitals. Informed consent was obtained from all participants prior to any study procedure. Patients’ selection Premenopausal and postmenopausal healthy women aged 40–60 years with a best corrected visual acuity (BCVA) ≥ 0.8 were recruited. Pregnant and lactating women, or women who were currently or previously using oral contraceptives or hormone replacement therapy (within 6 months prior to enrollment) were excluded. We also excluded subjects with a history of systemic or local disease that could affect ONH or retinal perfusion, such as diabetes mellitus, hypertension, cardiac disease, glaucoma patients, or suspects (either open or closed angle), anemia, or any neurological disease. We also excluded candidates with a history of previous ocular surgery or refractive myopia greater than − 6 diopters or hyperopia greater than + 4 diopters. In addition, those with any media opacity not allowing good fundus visualization and OCTA imaging were excluded. Patients were divided into two groups: Group 1 (Premenopausal): 50 eyes of 50 patients while group 2 (Postmenopausal): 50 eyes of 50 patients. The eyes of all participants were evaluated for BCVA using Snellen’s chart (in decimals), slit-lamp examination, gonioscopy, IOP measurement using Goldman applanation tonometry, dilated fundus examination by binocular indirect slit-lamp biomicroscopy, detection of serum estrogen level (in the mid-follicular phase of the cycle in the premenopausal group) and finally SD-OCT and OCTA (Optovue Inc., Fremont, CA, USA), using split-spectrum amplitude-decorrelation algorithm. Each image set comprised two raster volumetric patterns (one with vertical priority and one with horizontal priority) that scanned an area of 4.5 × 4.5 mm centered on ONH to assess RNFLT and VD in the peripapillary area. Each volume comprised 216 line-scan locations at which five consecutive B-scans were obtained. Each B-scan contained 216 A-scans, which compared the consecutive B-scans at the same location to detect flow based on motion contrast. An En-face angiogram was obtained using the maximum flow (decorrelation value) projection. The OCTA images were co-registered with OCT B-scans that were obtained concurrently to enable visualization of both the vasculature and structure in tandem. Analysis of OCT A and OCTA Images The built-in Angio Analytics software was used to evaluate VD and RNFLT. The software defines the peripapillary region as a 1.0 mm wide round annulus extending from the optic disc boundary. The disc margin is automatically detected based on Bruch’s Membrane Opening (BMO), and both cup and rim are measured within the BMO plane. The ONH map protocol was used to obtain RNFLT measurements which were calculated in a 10 pixel-wide band along a circle of 3.45 mm in diameter centered on the ONH. The overall average RNFLT as well as that in the superior, inferior, nasal, and temporal quadrants were used. Peripapillary VD was defined as the percentage of the area occupied by the vessels in the peripapillary region. The software calculated the whole image vessel density (wiVD) in the entire 4.5 × 4.5 mm 2 image. Overall peripapillary VD, as well as the VD measurements of the 4 quadrants, were calculated. Color maps were also used to show the VD. All OCTA scans were evaluated for image quality and segmentation errors. Quality index lower than 6 or images with persistent motion artifacts as doubled vessel pictures and artifact lines were excluded. If both eyes were eligible, a random blinded selection was done. In case of high refractive error or media opacity hindering the OCT scanning (e.g., dense cataract, nebulous cornea), the other eye was chosen. Statistical analysis Data was coded and entered using the statistical package for the Social Sciences (SPSS) version 26 (IBM Corp., Armonk, NY, USA). Data was summarized using mean and standard deviation. Comparisons between groups were done using the unpaired t test 7 . Correlations between quantitative variables were done using the Pearson correlation coefficient. Multivariate stepwise linear regression analysis was done to detect if age, estrogen and IOP act as independent predictors of densities 8 . P -value less than 0.05 was considered statistically significant . Ethical approval This report was approved by the ethical committee of at Cairo University and followed the tenets of the Declaration of Helsinki. Consent to participate All participants received oral and written consents as mentioned in methodology.

[results] Results Demographic and clinical characteristics One hundred eyes of one hundred patients were enrolled in the study divided into fifty eyes into each of the premenopausal and postmenopausal groups. The Patients’ age ranged from 41 to 48 years with a mean age of 43.22 ± 2.18 years in the premenopausal group and from 52 to 60 years with a mean age of 54.56 ± 3.80 years in the postmenopausal group. The estrogen level was significantly lower in the postmenopausal group. Postmenopausal women also had significantly lower BCVA and higher IOP than the premenopausal ones ( P value < 0.001). Table 1 summarizes the clinical characteristics of the study population. Table 1 Demographic and clinical characteristics of the study population. Premenopausal group Postmenopausal group P value Mean Standard deviation Mean Standard deviation Age (Years) 43.22 2.18 54.56 3.80 < 0.001 BCVA (Decimals) 0.94 0.06 0.81 0.07 < 0.001 CCT (um) 577.92 27.22 506.46 14.27 < 0.001 SE (diopters) 0.21 1.05 − 0.01 1.54 0.203 IOP (mmHg) 13.54 1.18 18.18 1.66 < 0.001 VCDR 0.31 0.11 0.32 0.09 0.351 Estrogen (pg/ml) 232.98 8.30 17.24 2.99 < 0.001 BCVA Best corrected visual acuity, CCT central corneal thickness, SE spherical equivalent, IOP Intraocular pressure, VCDR vertical cup to disc ratio. Retinal nerve fiber layer thickness The RNFLT was analyzed in the four quadrants (superior, inferior, nasal, and temporal). The mean RNFLT was significantly thinner in the postmenopausal women (Fig. 1 A) in all quadrants compared to the premenopausal ones (Fig. 1 B) ( P value < 0.001) as summarized in Table 2 . Figure 1 Example of RNFLT maps in a postmenopausal and a premenopausal participant. Table 2 Comparison between C in both groups. Premenopausal group Postmenopausal group P value Mean Standard deviation Mean Standard deviation Superior NFLT (um) 128.28 12.90 113.58 5.20 < 0.001 Inferior NFLT (um) 129.12 13.21 110.78 11.85 < 0.001 Nasal NFLT (um) 85.66 7.08 73.22 9.49 < 0.001 Temporal NFLT (um) 76.58 7.42 66.44 7.80 < 0.001 RNFLT Retinal nerve fiber layer thickness. Peripapillary vessel density (VD) The average of all peripapillary VD measurements was significantly lower in the postmenopausal group (Fig. 2 A) (wiVD, inside disc VD, peripapillary VD, superior and inferior hemi) and in the 4 quadrants (superior, inferior, nasal, and temporal) compared to the premenopausal group (Fig. 2 B) ( P value < 0.001) as summarized in Table 3 . Figure 2 Example of a peripapillary OCTA scan 4.5 × 4.5 mm of ONH at Vitreous/Retinal level, RPC level, and Choroidal level, RPC density and thickness in a postmenopausal and a premenopausal participant. Table 3 Comparison between vessel denisty in both groups. Premenopausal group Postmenopausal group P value VD (%) Mean Standard deviation Mean Standard deviation Wi VD (%) 51.55 1.98 48.80 2.72 < 0.001 Inside disc VD (%) 50.88 3.44 46.10 4.77 < 0.001 Peripapillary VD (%) 53.74 2.62 50.36 2.58 < 0.001 Superior hemi VD (%) 53.52 2.85 50.48 3.62 < 0.001 Inferior hemi VD (%) 53.84 2.86 51.21 2.26 < 0.001 Superior quadrant VD (%) 54.02 3.42 50.38 4.57 < 0.001 Inferior quadrant VD (%) 56.12 3.71 52.48 3.52 < 0.001 Nasal quadrant VD (%) 52.14 3.76 48.26 4.21 < 0.001 Temporal quadrant VD (%) 55.14 2.44 51.34 3.19 < 0.001 VD Vessel density, Wi Whole image. Pearson correlation coefficients of peripapillary VD with RNFLT showed positive correlations in the vascular and structural changes. The correlation was statistically significant between RNFLT and VD in the inferior ( r = 0.516, P < 0.001), nasal ( r = 0.446, P = 0.001) and temporal quadrants ( r = 0.577, P < 0.001) as shown in Table 4 . Table 4 Correlation between RNFLT and VD in the postmenopausal group. Postmenopausal group Superior RNFLT Inferior RNFLT Nasal RNFLT Temporal RNFLT Wi VD (%) r 0.031 0.302 0.425 0.495 P value 0.832 0.033 0.002 < 0.001 Inside disc VD (%) r -0.257- -0.038- 0.227 0.240 P value 0.072 0.796 0.114 0.094 Peripapillary VD (%) r 0.016 0.094 0.516 0.510 P value 0.912 0.515 < 0.001 < 0.001 Superior quadrant VD (%) r -0.099- 0.006 0.470 0.537 P value 0.495 0.967 0.001 < 0.001 Inferior quadrant VD (%) r 0.259 0.516 0.038 0.104 P value 0.070 < 0.001 0.792 0.474 Nasal quadrant VD (%) r 0.018 0.035 0.446 0.361 P value 0.901 0.807 0.001 0.010 Temporal quadrant VD (%) r -0.097- 0.168 0.426 0.577 P value 0.502 0.242 0.002 < 0.001 Significant values are in [bold]. RNFLT Retinal nerve fiber layer thickness, Wi VD Whole image vessel density, VD Vessel density, r Linear correlation coefficient. Correlation between IOP, estrogen and RNFLT and VD We studied the relation of IOP and estrogen with all parameters in the post-menopausal group. Analysis of Pearson correlation coefficients of the RNFLT with IOP showed a statistically significant negative correlation between IOP and RNFLT in the inferior quadrant only (r = − 0.318, P value 0.024) (Fig. 3 ). Figure 3 Correlation of IOP with inferior RNFLT and inferior VD in the postmenopausal group. Similarly, Pearson correlation coefficients of the VD with IOP revealed a significant negative correlation between IOP and VD in the inferior quadrant (r = − 0.541, P value < 0.001) (Fig. 3 ). Analysis of the relation between the estrogen level and RNFLT and VD showed that there was a statistically significant positive correlation with nasal (r = 0.286, P value 0.044) and temporal RNFLT (r = 0.287, P value 0.043) and with peripapillary VD (r = 0.257, P value 0.029) and superior VD (r = 0.340, P value 0.016). Multivariate stepwise linear regression analysis was done, with age, estrogen level and IOP (as independent predictors) and vessel density (as dependent variable). Results in Table 5 showed that estrogen level is an independent predictor for whole image, peripapillary superior and temporal VD affection. IOP has been shown to be a predictor of inferior VD affection. Table 5 Multivariate stepwise linear regression analysis with age, estrogen and IOP as independent predictors and vessel density as dependent variable. Model Unstandardized coefficients Standardized coefficients t P value 95.0% Confidence interval for B B Std. error Beta Lower bound Upper bound Whole image Estrogen 0.013 0.002 0.508 5.832 < 0.001 0.008 0.017 Inside the disc vessel density Age -0.445- 0.124 -0.603- -3.579- 0.001 -0.692- -0.198- IOP 0.933 0.269 0.534 3.468 0.001 0.399 1.468 Estrogen 0.019 0.009 0.430 2.230 0.028 0.002 0.036 Peripapillary vessel density Estrogen 0.016 0.002 0.551 6.542 < 0.001 0.011 0.020 Superior vessel density Estrogen 0.017 0.004 0.423 4.620 < 0.001 0.010 0.025 Inferior Vessel density IOP -0.758- 0.128 -0.514- -5.934- < 0.001 -1.012- -0.505- Nasal vessel density Age -0.322- 0.061 -0.471- -5.279- < 0.001 -0.442- -0.201- Temporal vessel density Estrogen 0.018 0.003 0.562 6.720 < 0.001 0.012 0.023 IOP intraocular pressure.

[discussion] Discussion Postmenopausal drop in estrogen level is thought to play an important role in increasing the incidence of ocular symptoms and ocular diseases 2 . In this study, we evaluated the structural and vascular changes of the ONH in postmenopausal women in comparison to premenopausal women. We found that the difference in the mean IOP was statistically significant between both groups with higher IOP in the postmenopausal group. This agreed well with the results of a study conducted by Siuw et al. 9 who found a significant higher IOP in the postmenopausal group, and estradiol was shown to be a protective factor in reducing IOP among these women . Regarding the optic nerve changes, older studies support the hypothesis that estrogen deficiency is involved in the pathophysiology of optic nerve aging and glaucomatous neurodegeneration through several mechanisms 6 , 9 . In this study, we found that the difference in RNFLT in the 4 quadrants was statistically significant between pre and postmenopausal groups. And by correlating the IOP with RNFLT in the different quadrants in the postmenopausal group, we found a statistically significant negative correlation between IOP and RNFLT in the inferior quadrant. Deschênes et al. in 2010 found that the measures of ONH topography indicated a significantly thicker RNFL in menopausal women on Hormonal replacement therapy (HRT) compared to women who never used HRT 3 . Comparably, Açmaz et al. 6 in 2014 evaluated the RNFLT and choroidal thickness in premenopausal and postmenopausal women where no significant difference was found between the postmenopausal study and control groups regarding all the peripapillary RNFLT thickness parameters . We found that all the average measurements of VDs are significantly lower in the postmenopausal group with a negative correlation between IOP and inferior hemi, and inferior quadrant only. No previous studies reported using OCTA to evaluate the peripapillary vasculature to compare with. Yet, others used different methods to predict ocular blood flow. As an example, Centofanti et al. in 2002 studied the pulsatile ocular blood flow (POBF) and have revealed gender and hormonal status-related alterations in the choroidal circulation 10 , 11 . We found a statistically positive correlation between the loss of VDs and RNFLT in 3 out of the 4 quadrants. This agreed with previous studies stating that the radial peripapillary capillary densities correlated significantly with the RNFL thickness 12 , 13 . We then compared the changes of RNFLT and VD with IOP. We found that with hormonal drop, increasing age and higher IOP, there was a decline in both RNFLT and peripapillary VD, especially in the inferior quadrant; but VD showed more significant negative correlation than RNFLT with IOP. So, according to this study, we can consider the assessment of VD by OCTA to be more sensitive in the early detection of peripapillary changes if IOP rises with menopause. This agreed with Lee et al. 14 who reported that the inferior VD showed better diagnostic ability than most of the other OCT measurements including peripapillary RNFLT and macular ganglion cell inner plexiform layer (GCIPL) thickness in glaucomatous highly myopic eyes. To our knowledge, this is the first study to elaborate the peripapillary vascular changes in menopausal women and correlate it with RNFLT and IOP. The limitations of this study are the lack of a group diagnosed with glaucoma to compare the changes with them. Axial length measurement would have been of additional asset to exclude changes caused by over stretching; but that could be forgiven since we excluded women with high refractive errors from the start. Also, it would have been of great value if we could follow up the premenopausal women to detect the changes that occur to them after menopause; but this needs a longer study duration.

retrieved_papers.title: Comparison of subfoveal choroidal thickness in pre-eclamptic, healthy pregnant, and non-pregnant women.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] The purpose of the study was to compare the choroidal thickness (CT) in pre-eclamptic (PE), healthy pregnant, and age-matched healthy non-pregnant females by spectral-domain optical coherence tomography (SD-OCT) and to investigate any possible correlation between subfoveal CT (SFCT) and mean arterial pressure (MAP) in PE.
This was a hospital-based, comparative study. A total of 90 females (30 in each group) aged 20-40 years were recruited prospectively over 1 year. Females with refractive error beyond ±3 D, intraocular pressure >21 mmHg, prior ocular trauma or surgeries, systemic diseases, pregnancy-related complications (except PE), and poor OCT scans were excluded. Following comprehensive ophthalmic evaluation, CT was measured bilaterally using SD-OCT (EDI) at the subfoveal region and at 1500 µm nasal and temporal to fovea. Systolic and diastolic blood pressures were recorded in all.
The PE group had significantly thinner choroid (SFCT: 337.5 ± 49.8 OD, 333.0 ± 50.8 OS) as compared to the healthy non-pregnant group (SFCT: 351.6 ± 34.4 OD, 365.3 ± 33.6 OS) and healthy pregnant group (SFCT: 374.2 ± 44.1 OD, 377.2 ± 49.5 OS) ( P < 0.05) in all locations (nasal, temporal, and subfoveal) in both eyes (except for OD nasal CT). However, there was no significant correlation between SFCT and MAP in the PE group.
Significantly lower CT in PE without any direct correlation between SFCT and MAP may implicate that choroidal thinning in PE is governed by multifactorial choroidal vasospasm and endothelial dysfunction, instead of MAP solely.

[methods] Methods This hospital-based, comparative cross-sectional study was conducted in the Ophthalmology and Obstetrics-Gynecology Departments at a tertiary care center. The study adhered to the tenets of the Declaration of Helsinki and was approved by the institutional ethics committee. All participants received oral and written information about the study, and each participant provided informed consent. PE was diagnosed according to the definition by the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists as the new onset of hypertension and proteinuria or the new onset of hypertension and significant end-organ dysfunction with or without proteinuria after 20 weeks of gestation or postpartum in a previously normotensive woman.[ 2 ] The study involved women aged between 20 and 40 years divided into three groups: PE (n = 30), healthy pregnant (n = 30), and healthy non-pregnant (n = 30) women. The pregnant study participants (in the PE group and normotensive healthy pregnancy group) were above 20 weeks of pregnancy and had singleton pregnancies. Gestational week and estimated fetal weight (EFW) were confirmed with transabdominal ultrasound scans by experienced sonographers. These patients were recruited from the antenatal clinic and Obstetrics and Gynecology OPD. The age-matched healthy non-pregnant group was selected from the hospital staff or accompanying attendants of patients visiting ophthalmology OPD, who met the eligibility criteria. Detailed obstetric history, including gravidity and parity and details of first, second, and third trimesters, were noted in all pregnant women. The duration of PE and associated medications were recorded. Ocular history was taken to rule out recent or past significant ocular diseases. Refractive error more than ±3D, a best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) worse than 6/9, intraocular pressure (IOP) readings >21 mmHg, significant ocular disease, glaucoma, uveitis, retinal disease, ocular trauma, dense media opacities, history of previous intraocular surgery, or participants with prior history of any medical or obstetrical problems (except PE) were excluded from the study. All patients underwent a detailed ophthalmic evaluation, including assessment of distant BCVA by Snellen’s chart, refractive error by an automated kerato-refractometer (KR-9 LENSit, India), IOP, and central corneal thickness (CCT) by non-contact tonometer with pachymeter (NT-530P; Nidek Co. Ltd., Japan), and axial length (AL) by using optical biometer (Lenstar LS900, Haag-Streit, USA). The anterior segment and dilated fundus were evaluated as per standard protocol. The EDI mode of spectral domain optical coherence tomography (SD-OCT; CirrusTM, Carl Zeiss Meditec, Dublin, CA) was used to measure the CT in all the patients. One single operator well-versed with the technique captured all the scans. Adequate signal strength ( ≥ 7) in each of the scanned images was ensured. HD-21 scan with EDI mode was used to measure the CT in both eyes. The SFCT was measured using a built-in caliper as close as possible to the fovea as the perpendicular distance between the hyperreflective outer border of the retinal pigment epithelial–Bruch’s membrane layer (RPE-BM) and the sclero-choroidal interface. The CT was also measured at 1500 µm nasal and 1500 µm temporal to the fovea in a similar way. Fig. 1 a–c shows a representative image showing the measurement of CT in all three groups. The SD-OCT tests were performed between 12 pm and 3 pm in all patients to avoid any diurnal variation of CT. Both eyes of each participant were included in the study analysis. All measurements were done by one experienced observer blinded to the study groups. Figure 1 Representative image showing measurement of CT at the subfoveal (SF) location, 1500 µm nasal to fovea (N), and 1500 µm temporal to fovea (T) in a pre-eclamptic (a), healthy pregnant (b), and healthy non-pregnant women (c) The height and weight of every patient were recorded, and body mass index (BMI) was calculated. Systolic blood pressure (SBP) and diastolic blood pressure (DBP) were recorded in the upper arm of each participant with appropriate adult cuff size after a resting period of at least 10 minutes. An average of two blood pressure measurements was recorded as final. Mean arterial pressure (MAP) was calculated using the formula: Mean Arterial Pressure = 2/3 DBP + 1/3 SBP. Additional laboratory investigations and 24-hour urinary protein were documented in PE patients, if available. Statistical analysis Data were tabulated in an Excel sheet and were analyzed with the help of SPSS software version: 23.0 (IBM Corp. Armonk, NY, US). Descriptive statistics for quantitative variables were summarized as mean ± standard deviation (SD) and/or median [interquartile range (IQR)]. Percentages were reported for categorical variables. Group comparisons for continuously distributed data were made using the independent sample t -test when comparing two groups and one-way ANOVA when comparing more than two groups. If data were found to be non-normally distributed, appropriate non-parametric tests were used. Correlation analysis was performed by Pearson’s coefficient or Spearman’s coefficient as appropriate. A P value less than 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

[results] Results Table 1 summarizes the ocular, obstetric, and systemic features of all the participants. No statistically significant differences were detected in terms of mean gestational age, gravida status, and EFW in the healthy pregnant and PE group. There was no statistical difference in AL, CCT, or IOP between all three groups. Among the three groups, there was a significant difference in BMI (kg/m 2 ) ( P < 0.001). The PE group had the highest median BMI. In terms of MAP (mmHg), there was a significant difference between the three groups ( P < 0.001), with the PE group having the highest mean MAP. Table 1 Comparison of obstetric, ocular, and systemic parameters between three groups Parameters Group P Pre-eclampsia Healthy Pregnant Healthy Non-Pregnant G.A (weeks) 0.30 # Mean±SD 34.0 (3.7) 32.9 (4.5) — Range 20.1–39 20–38.5 Distribution of GA 0.60 ^ 20–27+6 Weeks 2 (6.7%) 3 (10.0%) — 28–36+6 Weeks 25 (83.3%) 22 (73.3%) ≥37 Weeks 3 (10.0%) 5 (16.7%) Gravida Status 0.40** Primi 13 (43.3%) 8 (26.7%) — Second 9 (30.0%) 12 (40.0%) ≥Third 8 (26.7%) 10 (33.3%) EFW (g) 0.70 ^^ Mean±SD 1725.8 (641.6) 1657.0 (573.7) — Range 400–3164 174–2564 IOP (mmHg) OD 14.2 (3.5) 13.6 (2.6) 14.6 (2.7) 0.427 ┴ OS 14.1 (2.2) 13.6 (2.8) 15.3 (3.4) 0.157* Axial Length (mm) OD 22.4 (0.8) 22.8 (0.7) 22.7 (0.7) 0.074* OS 22.5 (0.7) 22.8 (0.7) 22.7 (0.6) 0.493 ┴ CCT OD 536.0 (35.9) 545.0 (28.4) 518.6 (89.9) 0.199* OS 532.2 (28.6) 544.8 (26.8) 534.4 (19.1) 0.121 ┴ BMI (Kg/m 2 ) Mean±SD 27.6 (2.8) 25.7 (3.2) 23.35 (3.1) <0.001* Range 23-34.1 20-31.6 17.7–33.8 MAP (mmHg)* Mean±SD 107.9 (8.3) 89.3 (5.9) 89.4 (8.0) <0.001 ┴ Range 83–131 79–100 77–106 *Kruskal-Wallis test, ┴ One-way ANOVA # Wilcoxon-Mann-Whitney U test, ^Fisher’s exact test, **Chi-squared test ^^ t -test Table 2 compares the CT between the three groups. The healthy pregnant group had the highest mean SFCT (µm) in both eyes (OD: 374.2, OS: 377.2) followed by the group of healthy non-pregnant women (OD: 351.6, OS: 365.3). The PE group had the thinnest SFCT in both eyes (OD: 337.5, OS: 333.0). Similarly, the nasal CT was highest in the healthy pregnant group (OD: 306.8, OS: 320.6) followed by the healthy non-pregnant group (OD: 296.1, OS: 314.5) and the PE group (OD: 279.7, OS: 281.6). An identical pattern of CT was observed in the temporal area also, with highest being the healthy pregnant group (OD: 351.2, OS: 343.5) followed by the healthy non-pregnant group (OD: 336.8, OS: 338.6) and the PE group (OD: 308.8, OS: 307.5). The difference in CT in the three groups was statistically significant ( P < 0.05) for nasal, temporal, subfoveal, and average CT in both eyes except for nasal CT in the right eye. Table 2 Comparison of choroidal thickness (CT) measurements between three groups Parameters Group P Pre-eclampsia Healthy Pregnant Healthy Non-Pregnant Subfoveal CT μm) OD 0.006 Mean±SD 337.5 (49.8) 374.2 (44.1) 351.6 (34.4) Range 218–421 289–450 283–429 OS 0.001 Mean±SD 333.0 (50.8) 377.2 (49.5) 365.3 (33.6) Range 240–440 241–463 286–454 Nasal CT μm) OD Mean±SD 279.7 (52.8) 306.8 (47.6) 296.1 (41.5) 0.089 Range 167–366 170–387 200–382 OS 0.007 Mean±SD 281.6 (65.6) 320.6 (47.5) 314.5 (30.2) Range 167–410 200–408 225–381 Temporal CT μm) OD 0.002 Mean±SD 308.8 (53.4) 351.2 (46.4) 336.8 (37.0) Range 189–387 261–425 265–420 OS 0.013 Mean±SD 307.5 (56.1) 343.5 (56.5) 338.6 (32.9) Range 204–413 218–451 274–440 Average CT (μm) OD 0.003 Mean±SD 308.6 (48.0) 347.4 (41.8) 328.1 (35.3) Range 195.3–387.7 240–413.7 252.3–397 OS 0.002 Mean±SD 307.4 (55.7) 346.99 (47.4) 339.5 (28.1) Range 211.7–421 223–439 261.7–403.7 One-way ANOVA Table 3 demonstrates the correlation between MAP and CT (subfoveal and average). There was a statistically significant negative correlation between CT (subfoveal or average) and MAP if all groups were combined but was insignificant for any single group isolated. Fig. 2 a–d depicts the correlation between SFCT (OD) and MAP. Except for nasal and average CT in the left eye, there was no statistically significant association between 24-hour urine protein and CT in the PE patients. In the PE group, the mean SFCT (µm) in the urine albumin dipstick: 1+ group (n = 19) was OD 337.3, OS 319.7; in the urine albumin dipstick: 2+ group (n = 8) was OD 349.7, OS 356.7; and in the urine albumin dipstick: 3+ group (n = 3) was OD 306.3, OS 353.7. There was no significant difference in SFCT among different grades of urine albumin dipstick ( P = 0.508) in the PE group. Figure 2 The above scatterplot depicts the correlation between subfoveal CT (µm) (OD) and MAP (mmHg). Individual points represent individual cases. The blue trendline represents the general trend of correlation between the two variables. The shaded grey area represents the 95% confidence interval of this trendline. [a: All groups combined; b: Pre-eclampsia group; c: Healthy Pregnant group, d: Healthy non-pregnant group] Table 3 Correlation analysis between the CT (average and subfoveal) and the MAP in all three groups Correlation between Average CT (µm) & MAP (mmHg) Pearson’s Correlation Coefficient P Pre-eclampsia OD 0.1 0.769 OS 0.1 0.450 Healthy Pregnant OD −0.2 0.342 OS −0.1 0.792 Healthy Non-Pregnant OD 0.2 0.329 OS −0.1 0.799 All groups OD −0.2 0.039 OS −0.2 0.019 Correlation between Subfoveal CT (µm) & MAP (mmHg) Pearson’s Correlation Coefficient P Pre-eclampsia OD 0.0 0.989 OS 0.1 0.757 Healthy Pregnant OD −0.2 0.239 OS 0.1 0.791 Healthy Non-Pregnant OD 0.1 0.463 OS 0.0 0.950 All groups OD −0.2 0.042 OS −0.3 0.006

[discussion] Discussion PE is a complex pregnancy disorder characterized by the onset of hypertension, proteinuria, or organ dysfunction after 20 weeks of gestation.[ 2 ] During pregnancy, the mother’s circulatory system undergoes adaptations that result in a slight decrease in blood pressure and increased kidney function. However, in PE, this transformation is impaired. In PE, the placenta produces a substance called soluble fms-like tyrosine kinase-1, which disrupts normal blood vessel relaxation and leads to increased arterial pressure and vascular resistance.[ 15 ] In normal placentation, trophoblast cells infiltrate and modify the maternal uterine spiral arteries, creating wider vessels with improved blood flow.[ 16 ] The transformation of maternal uterine spiral arteries by trophoblast cells, which usually improves blood flow, is impaired in PE. This results in the release of placental factors that negatively affect the mother’s endothelial function, causing systemic vascular dysfunction.[ 17 ] The exact cause of abnormal placentation in PE is still unknown, but genetics, immune response, and environmental factors are thought to play a role.[ 18 19 ] The choroid is a highly vascular structure with a crucial role in maintaining normal eye function and can be affected by various pathological processes.[ 20 ] Changes in hemodynamics during pregnancy are also likely to affect the CT. Choroidal circulation is more susceptible to PE than retinal circulation as choroidal vasculature is devoid of an autoregulatory mechanism and is controlled by the sympathetic nervous system. Choroidal arteries have fewer branches, and they also directly transmit the brunt of high BP in PE to the choriocapillaris due to the shorter right-angled course. Hence, studying the choroidal parameters in PE patients could provide a unique opportunity and valuable insight into understanding disease pathogenesis as it is possible to directly analyze the cross-sectional images of the choroid in situ and in real time by OCT. Commercial SD-OCT has an EDI mode, which moves the objective lens closer to the eye, bringing the light backscattered from the choroid closer to zero delays, thus increasing sensitivity.[ 21 22 ] Therefore, this modality produces deeper choroid images, which makes it possible to visualize the choroid-sclera interface and measure CT accurately and quantitatively, especially in the absence of other advanced modalities such as swept-source OCT. CT is known to be affected by AL, SE, IOP, CCT, and diurnal variation.[ 23 24 25 26 27 ] Choroid is found to be thinner in high myopes,[ 23 ] and CT generally decreases with an increase in the AL.[ 24 ] CT has an inverse relation with IOP.[ 25 ] Diurnal variation has also been demonstrated in the choroids of healthy eyes.[ 26 27 ] All of our participants in each group had BCVA of 6/9 or better with maximum refractive error in the range of ±3 D, thus almost eliminating any potential effect of SE on CT. In addition, there was no significant difference in AL, IOP, and CCT between the three groups. Moreover, all the OCT scans in all groups were performed in a similar fixed time frame to avoid any possible diurnal variation. These factors ensured that we could precisely compare the CT between PE, healthy pregnancy, and age-matched non-pregnant healthy controls in an identical background. Choroidal thinning in PE was previously demonstrated by Ataş et al . and Sayin et al .[ 12 13 ] They found that in comparison to mild PE and healthy non-pregnant women, healthy pregnant women had significantly higher CT. However, in the PE group, the CT rise was relatively less. Duru et al .[ 14 ] conducted a longitudinal study in PE and healthy pregnant women to study SFCT during pregnancy and 3 months post-partum. They found that SFCT significantly decreased in PE during pregnancy. There was no difference in SFCT values in the post-partum period. Our results were consistent with these studies which included PE without severe systemic features or any retinopathy changes. We found that the SFCT was significantly thinner in the PE group ( P < 0.05). The previous authors hypothesized that this change in the CT in PE is possibly due to marked systemic vascular resistance encountered in PE. Few studies have shown contrasting results.[ 6 7 8 9 10 ] Kim et al .[ 6 ] found that CT was significantly greater in PE patients than in healthy pregnant women. They included patients with only severe PE. Sharudin et al .[ 7 ] found the SFCT to be higher in PE as compared to healthy pregnancy and age-matched controls. They also demonstrated a positive correlation between the severity of PE with the amount of proteinuria and thus concluded that the SFCT increases with increasing severity PE. Garg et al .[ 8 ] included severe PE and found SFCT to be thicker in severe PE compared to normotensive pregnant women and age-matched healthy women. Evcimen et al. [ 9 ] studied the eyes of PE women; among those with positive qualitative findings on OCT such as neurosensory detachments, the SFCT was significantly higher. Benfica et al .[ 10 ] demonstrated that the severity of PE may affect the CT, with severe cases showing higher CT increase than mild cases. These divergent findings among studies may stem from various other factors also such as differences in sample size, inclusion criteria, methodologies, population demographics, and ethnic backgrounds. Moreover, the complex nature of PE and its multifactorial pathogenesis could contribute to the discrepancies observed in the results. It emphasizes the need for further research to better understand the potential relationship between CT and PE. Studies have demonstrated a decrease in CT in essential hypertensive patients without renal dysfunction possibly due to vasoconstriction secondary to aggravated sympathetic stimulus.[ 28 ] On the contrary, choroidal thinning in chronic kidney disease (CKD) patients has been associated with lower estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) and higher proteinuria but not BP.[ 29 ] In patients with CKD, proteinuria, inflammatory markers, and eGFR have been positively correlated with chorioretinal thinning.[ 30 ] These facts suggest that alteration of CT in renal dysfunction probably occurs as a result of the combination of arterial stiffness, inflammation, and endothelial tissue dysfunction, rather than the effect of high blood pressure alone. Considering that endothelial dysfunction contributes to the emergence of multi-organ failure related to PE also, it is possible that the observed decrease in SFCT in individuals with PE in our study cohort could occur through a similar mechanism, regardless of blood pressure levels. Endothelial dysfunction in PE can lead to widespread vascular abnormalities, affecting not only the systemic circulation but also the ocular vasculature, including the choroidal vessels. This could potentially lead to a decrease in CT as the compromised endothelial function may impair choroidal blood flow and contribute to choroidal thinning. It is important to note that this mechanism may be independent of blood pressure levels as we could not find any significant correlation between MAP and CT in PE patients. Shim et al .[ 31 ] demonstrated the correlation of urinary protein creatinine ratio (PCR) with CT in PE patients. They found that patients with greater urine PCR had significantly decreased CT. However, in our study, 24-hour urinary protein had no significant correlation with SFCT. Notable strengths of our study include the prospective inclusion of the participants with a comparative design. We independently assessed both eyes and measured CT at multiple topographical points bilaterally, including subfoveal, nasal, and temporal to fovea. Validating our data in both eyes at multiple choroidal areas enabled us to draw inferences on widespread choroidal changes with more accuracy (unlike most of the previous studies measuring only unilateral subfoveal CT). In our study, we also excluded severe PE with concomitant retinopathy with the aim of studying CT parameters solely. Our study has a few limitations too. Lack of swept-source OCT, manual in-built caliper-based measurement of CT, and non-availability of longitudinal follow-up in the post-partum phase are the prime limitations in the current study. As our study was conducted in a relatively smaller homogenous population of identical demographic backgrounds, these data may not be extrapolated to other ethnic groups.

[conclusion] Conclusion The current study showed significant choroidal thinning in PE patients as compared to healthy pregnant and non-pregnant women, suggesting increased choroidal vasospasm in PE. However, no significant correlation was found between CT and MAP in PE patients. This possibly implies that choroidal thinning in PE is governed by multifactorial choroidal vasospasm and endothelial dysfunction, not solely attributed to blood pressure. Hence, OCT-derived CT can possibly be used as a biomarker in PE patients to measure vascular injury in the eye. Future prospective comparative studies with longitudinal follow-up in antepartum as well as postpartum period and larger sample size are required to understand whether CT can be used as a surrogate marker of global endothelial dysfunction in PE. Ethical statement Institutional Ethics Committee approval was obtained for this work. (AIIMS/IEC/22/201). Conflicts of interest: There are no conflicts of interest.

retrieved_papers.title: Evaluation of the macula, retinal nerve fiber layer and choroid thickness in postmenopausal women and reproductive-age women using spectral-domain optical coherence tomography.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2014]
[Abstract] Menopause is a physiological life period that potentially affects various organs and systems. Therefore, a wide spectrum of signs and symptoms of eyes may be clinically observed in the postmenopausal period.
To evaluate the macular, retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) and choroidal thickness alterations by using spectral-domain optical coherence tomography (SD-OCT) in postmenopausal women and compare with healthy reproductive-age women controls.
The study population included a healthy reproductive-age control group (n = 72) and postmenopausal study group (n = 72). Retinal thickness parameters were measured by SD-OCT. Peripapillary RNFL thickness parameters, macular thickness and choroidal thickness were evaluated.
Superior inner macula, temporal inner macula, inferior inner macula, nasal inner macula, inferior outer macula and choroid thickness were significantly thinner in the postmenopausal study group than the healthy reproductive-age control group (p = 0.007, p = 0.037, p = 0.027, p = 0.006, p = 0.016, p < 0.001, respectively). After adjusting for age, only choroid thickness was significantly thinner in the postmenopausal study group than controls (p (†) = 0.005).
This study revealed that choroidal thickness measured by SD-OCT was significantly thinner in postmenopausal women than healthy reproductive-age women. We can speculate that the decrease in choroidal thickness in postmenopausal women may indicate a reduced estrogen-dependent vasodilatory effect in ophthalmic artery secondary to menopausal estrogen deficiency.

[Introduction] Introduction Simultaneously with success in postponing death, the ratio of the elderly population is rapidly increasing. This increase in the elderly population is also increasing the rate of postmenopausal women in society. Despite a great increase in the life expectancy of women, the age at menopause has remained remarkably constant. As the proportion of older females in the population increases, an increasing prevalence of menopause and age-related disease may be expected to be seen [ 1 ]. Menopause is also associated with ocular changes. An increase in the incidence of ocular pathologies and ocular symptoms have been observed during the postmenopausal period. Postmenopausal hormonal status also seems to play a role in these ocular pathologies [ 2 ]. For decades, conventional imaging methods with low resolution and low accuracy rate have been used to evaluate the ocular changes in postmenopausal women. Moreover, interobserver conflicts often exist for previous ocular imaging techniques. Optical coherence tomography (OCT) is a new diagnostic imaging modality that provides high-resolution, cross-sectional images of ocular structures with a longitudinal resolution of 3-10 μm [ 3 ]. This provides high-resolution ocular images in vivo when compared with other available imaging techniques. Optical coherence tomography makes it possible to obtain noninvasive, rapid, objective, high-resolution, cross-sectional imaging of the retina and also permits direct, real-time imaging of ocular pathology that previously could not be visualized using traditional methods. There is no recent comprehensive study using OCT which investigates the influence of menopause on the retinal structure. To our knowledge, the present study in which macular, RNFL and choroidal thickness measured together using OCT is the first such research reported in the literature. The aim of the present study was to evaluate the macular, choroid and peripapillary nerve fiber layer thickness in postmenopausal women by means of OCT and to compare these findings in healthy reproductive-age controls.

[introduction] Introduction Simultaneously with success in postponing death, the ratio of the elderly population is rapidly increasing. This increase in the elderly population is also increasing the rate of postmenopausal women in society. Despite a great increase in the life expectancy of women, the age at menopause has remained remarkably constant. As the proportion of older females in the population increases, an increasing prevalence of menopause and age-related disease may be expected to be seen [ 1 ]. Menopause is also associated with ocular changes. An increase in the incidence of ocular pathologies and ocular symptoms have been observed during the postmenopausal period. Postmenopausal hormonal status also seems to play a role in these ocular pathologies [ 2 ]. For decades, conventional imaging methods with low resolution and low accuracy rate have been used to evaluate the ocular changes in postmenopausal women. Moreover, interobserver conflicts often exist for previous ocular imaging techniques. Optical coherence tomography (OCT) is a new diagnostic imaging modality that provides high-resolution, cross-sectional images of ocular structures with a longitudinal resolution of 3-10 μm [ 3 ]. This provides high-resolution ocular images in vivo when compared with other available imaging techniques. Optical coherence tomography makes it possible to obtain noninvasive, rapid, objective, high-resolution, cross-sectional imaging of the retina and also permits direct, real-time imaging of ocular pathology that previously could not be visualized using traditional methods. There is no recent comprehensive study using OCT which investigates the influence of menopause on the retinal structure. To our knowledge, the present study in which macular, RNFL and choroidal thickness measured together using OCT is the first such research reported in the literature. The aim of the present study was to evaluate the macular, choroid and peripapillary nerve fiber layer thickness in postmenopausal women by means of OCT and to compare these findings in healthy reproductive-age controls.

[methods] Material and methods The study was approved by the institutional ethics committee (no. 2013/60) and all participants signed an informed consent form regarding participation in the present study. This prospective study was performed at the Kayseri Education and Training Hospital of Medicine, a tertiary referral centre in Turkey between January 2013 and May 2013. The study was carried out with two groups. The study group comprised 72 postmenopausal women and the control group comprised 72 healthy women volunteers at the reproductive age. Postmenopausal women were at least 1 year after spontaneous menopause in association with elevated FSH serum levels (> 40 IU/L). None of the women in the control group was pregnant. Women who reported histories of any medical problems, taking hormone replacement therapy (especially six months prior to the study or current use of hormone replacement therapy [HRT]), acute or chronic liver diseases, kidney and pulmonary diseases were excluded from the study. Subjects were also excluded if they used caffeine or tobacco. Furthermore, ocular surgery, ocular trauma or any ocular disease at the time of OCT measurement were also excluded from the study. Each subject underwent a comprehensive ophthalmologic examination. Following this detailed ophthalmologic examination, the third-generation Spectralis OCT device (software version 5.6.3.0; Spectralis OCT Heidelberg Engineering, Dossenheim, Germany) was used for the assessment. The OCT assessments of subjects involved in the study were performed by the same experienced technician. The procedure was achieved without pupillary dilatation and under the same intensity of dim room lighting. Macular thickness was determined automatically and was analyzed by OCT software. Fast Macular Thickness Map option was utilized for the macular measurements. During the assessments, macular thickness and volume analysis were used. The results obtained from the macular scan were classified by region as shown in Figure 1 . We selected the macular map analysis protocol ( Fig. 2 ) on the Spectralis to display numeric averages of the measurements for each of 9 subfields as defined by the Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS) [ 4 ]. Fig. 1 Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study subfield template used to derive macular thickness measurements from different regions of a high-density volume scan of the macula. Circles are 1, 3, and 6 mm in diameter Fig. 2 Sample RNFL report provided by SD-OCT. Numbers reflect individual mean RNFL thickness. Numbers in parentheses are the mean thickness of adults in the Spectralis normative database The peripapillary RNFL thickness parameters that were automatically calculated by the fast RNFL mode and divided into regions included temporal quadrant thickness, temporal superior quadrant thickness, nasal superior quadrant thickness, nasal quadrant thickness, nasal inferior quadrant thickness, temporal inferior quadrant thickness, and average thickness. The method of obtaining enhanced depth imaging (EDI) OCT images has been reported previously [ 5 ]. The resultant images were viewed and measured with the contained Heidelberg Eye Explorer software (version 5.6.3.0; Heidelberg Engineering). Choroid imaging was obtained by activating the EDI button. The choroid was manually measured from the outer border of the hyper-reflective line corresponding to the retinal pigment epithelium to the inner surface of the sclera ( Fig. 3 ). The measurements of subfoveal choroidal thickness were done by one of the authors in a masked fashion without knowledge of subject information. Fig. 3 Measurement of subfoveal choroidal thickness with EDI with Spectralis OCT Statistical analysis Statistical analysis was conducted using R 3.0.0 software. Shapiro-Wilk's test was used, histogram and q-q plot were examined to assess the data normality. Levene test was used to assess the variance homogeneity. A two-sided independent samples t test was applied to compare the differences of variables between groups. One-way analysis of covariance (ANCOVA) was also applied to compare the differences of variables between groups by adjusting the age variable. Values are expressed as mean±standard deviation. p < 0.05 value was considered as statistically significant.

[results] Results The study group included 72 eyes of 72 postmenopausal women, control group included 72 eyes of 72 healthy reproductive-age women. Mean age was 56.89 ±4.63 and 39.57 ±8.41 in study and control groups, respectively. There was a statistical difference for age between the study group and healthy reproductive-age controls ( p < 0.001). None of the patients had retinopathy, retinal hemorrhages, optical papilledema, retinal or macular edema at the time of examination. The macular and choroidal thickness analysis, macular volume analysis, and peripapillary RNFL thickness analysis obtained by the SD-OCT were displayed in Table I , Table II , and Table III , respectively.
