[discussion] Discussion In this study, we found that offspring born to mothers with GDM had larger area and size of CC flow voids at 7 years of age, indicating that GDM might influence the choroidal microvasculature in offspring from early life on. Furthermore, offspring born to mothers with GDM had increased VD of the DVC in the parafoveal temporal quadrant compared with those born to mothers without GDM, which might be a compensation for the defected blood flow in the CC. Diabetic retinopathy (DR) is a common microvascular complication of diabetes. Previous studies using OCTA have found that diabetes will affect both retinal and choroidal vessels, resulting in significant changes in retinal and choroidal blood flow. 9 – 12 , 20 Because the choroid is the vascular layer that supplies the outer retina, choroidal blood flow has attracted more and more attention. Recent studies have found that the CC blood flow of DR patients is generally reduced compared with healthy people. In diabetic patients without DR, the decrease in CC blood flow is also obvious. 9 , 21 Therefore, these studies suggest that CC microvascular changes occur early in diabetes and may play a role in the pathogenesis of DR. It has been reported that the microvascular ischemia originated in the choroidal layer and extended inward, affecting the deep and superficial layers of retina. 11 CC flow voids have been used to assess CC blood perfusion, reflecting areas in the CC with low perfusion or without perfusion. The quantitative analysis of CC flow voids used in our study has shown good repeatability in previous studies. 19 , 22 – 24 Offspring born to mothers with GDM have been found to have a high risk of developing diabetes. 5 Our study found that the area and size of CC flow voids both increased in offspring exposed to maternal GDM, suggesting that these offspring might have already had reduced CC blood perfusion before they developed diabetes or DR. The reduced CC perfusion might serve as an early screening biomarker of diabetes or DR in GDM offspring. Moreover, maternal diabetes during pregnancy has been reported to be associated with an increased risk of myopia in offspring. 7 This association might be partially mediated by reduced CC perfusion in these offspring, which might impair scleral oxygen supply and subsequently trigger hypoxia-induced remodeling of the scleral extracellular matrix. Therefore, the reduced CC perfusion might also be a sign of developing myopia in GDM offspring. However, compared with previous studies, 9 , 25 the differences in the area and size of CC flow voids between the GDM and control groups seemed slight in our study. This might be due to the young age (7 years old) of the offspring in our study, who had not yet developed any organic diseases. In contrast, the study populations in previous studies have been about 40 to 60 years old, and they had already developed diabetes; some of them had even developed microaneurysms in the fundus, the earliest clinical sign of DR. We also found that offspring born to mothers with GDM had increased VD of the DVC in the parafoveal temporal quadrant, but there were no differences between the GDM and control groups in the VD of the DVC in other regions or in the VD of the SVC. Several studies found no significant differences in either the superficial or deep capillary plexuses between diabetic eyes without DR and healthy eyes. 9 , 26 , 27 However, other studies have found increased retinal blood flow in diabetic eyes without DR compared with healthy eyes. 28 – 30 Among them, a study reported that retinal blood velocity and flow were increased in diabetic eyes without DR but decreased in diabetic eyes with mild non-proliferative DR, compared with healthy age-matched controls. 28 This finding suggested an initial increase followed by a progressive decline in retinal blood flow as diabetic eyes progress from having no retinopathy to developing retinopathy, and the initial increase in retinal blood flow might be a compensation for the potential hypoxia in the retina. We also speculate that the increased VD of the DVC in the parafoveal temporal quadrant in GDM offspring found in our study might be a compensation for the decreased blood flow in CC to maintain oxygen supply to the outer retina. Animal studies have demonstrated that, under physiological conditions, choroidal circulation serves as the dominant oxygen source for the outer retina, whereas retinal circulation plays a negligible role; however, under systemic hypoxia, the retinal circulation increases oxygen delivery to the outer retina to compensate for reduced oxygen supply from the choroidal circulation. 31 – 33 In this study, we did not find any differences in macular thickness between the GDM and control groups. However, a previous study found that children from diabetic pregnancies had significantly thinner macular thickness compared with children from nondiabetic pregnancies. 8 This discrepancy might be due to two reasons. First, diabetic pregnancies are not the same as GDM pregnancies, as they also include chronic diabetes complicated by pregnancy, which might have more influence on the ocular health in offspring. Second, the offspring in our study were much younger than those in the previous study (age range, 11.1–14.4 years), so that an impact of GDM on macular thickness in offspring was not shown. The serum glucose from pregnant women is transported to the fetus through the placenta by facilitated diffusion. About half of the serum glucose is used by the placenta and the fetus, and the rest is excreted into the amniotic fluid via direct penetration or fetal urine. Maternal hyperglycemia can affect the glucose transporter expression and glucose transport activity in the placenta, causing an increase in glucose uptake. 34 , 35 The fetus exposed to high glucose levels in the uterus has increased secretion of insulin to compensate for the high glucose, which may cause corresponding metabolic disorders. 5 , 8 Chronic hyperglycemia impacts various pathways and leads to oxidative stresses, further causing mitochondrial dysfunction, dysfunction of inflammatory mediator, and hypoxia. Histopathological studies have observed that metabolic disorders caused by diabetes are related to variable choroidal abnormalities and mainly limited to the CC, including vascular dropout. 36 – 38 Zhang et al. 39 used chick embryos to establish a hyperglycemia model. The expression of Pax6 , Six3 , and Otx2 genes was inhibited in the model, inducing eye malformation. Although this result cannot be directly extrapolated to humans, we need to be vigilant about the possible impact of hyperglycemia on the development of children's eyes. 1 , 6 As far as we know, our research is the first study that evaluates the influence of GDM on microvascular changes in macular retina and CC in offspring using OCTA. We used quantitative parameters to analyze the influence of GDM on ocular microvasculature and minimized the artifacts as much as possible. Our research also has some limitations. First, the prevalence of GDM (6.0%) in our study was much lower than that reported in mainland China (14.8%), 2 which might be due to recall bias, as we collected the history of maternal GDM through a questionnaire. However, the prevalence of GDM varies substantially across different regions of mainland China. In Jiangsu Province, studies have reported a GDM prevalence ranging from 5.9% to 16.5%. 40 , 41 A meta-analysis from China also reported that the prevalence of GDM from 2012 to 2016 was significantly higher than that from 2005 to 2012, which might be related to the improvement of living standards. 42 Thus, the low prevalence of GDM in our study might also be due to the offspring in our study being born between 2011 and 2012. Second, we failed to collect blood test indexes of GDM, such as blood glucose level and glycosylated hemoglobin A1, which makes us unable to further explore the relationship between the severity of GDM and ocular microvascular changes in offspring. Third, as a cross-sectional study, we could not make any causal inference for the associations we found. Fourth, the CC flow voids we measured might be affected by the blood velocity in the CC, which may cause measurement error. Fifth, the FAZ parameters were derived from full-thickness retinal vasculature images, which precluded the separate analysis of the FAZ metrics for the SVC and DVC. Finally, our analysis of multiple retinal regions raises the issue of multiple comparisons, which increases the risk of type I error. However, higher VD in the DVC of the GDM offspring group compared to controls was consistently observed in many retinal regions. Although these differences might not reach statistical significance after adjusting for multiple comparisons, our findings indicate an underlying biological phenomenon worthy of further investigation in larger, confirmatory studies. More research is necessary to clarify the potential effect of GDM on ocular development in offspring.

[conclusion] Conclusions In this cross-sectional population-based study, we found that offspring born to mothers with GDM had larger area and size of CC flow voids, as well as increased VD of the DVC in the parafoveal temporal quadrant, compared with those born to mothers without GDM. These findings suggest that GDM has a potential effect on ocular microvasculature in offspring. Offspring exposed to maternal GDM might be recommended for more comprehensive eye examinations and intensive follow-up observations.

retrieved_papers.title: Swept-Source OCT Angiography-Derived Regional Normative Data of Peripapillary Vessel Density in Healthy Populations.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] The purpose of this study was to investigate the distribution of peripapillary vessel density (VD) across different regions of retinal and choroidal layers and analyze its influencing factors after axial length (AL) correction.
This study included 337 eyes without significant fundus abnormalities or systemic conditions affecting blood flow. Swept-source optical coherence tomography angiography (SS-OCTA) was utilized to measure peripapillary VD and associated ocular parameters.
VD in all regions of the choroidal layer decreased significantly with increasing AL among AL groups. In non-highly myopic eyes, VD followed the pattern temporal (T) > superior nasal (SN) > inferior nasal (IN) in the inner retina and superficial vascular complex (SVC), whereas in the deep vascular complex (DVC), it was inferior (I) > superior (S). In the choroid, VD ranked as nasal (N) > T. Highly myopic eyes showed higher temporal but lower nasal VD in the inner retina and SVC, whereas DVC exhibited the opposite trend. Choroidal VD decreased significantly across all regions, most prominently in the T hemisphere. Multivariable analysis identified age, signal strength, and retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) thickness as key determinants of inner retinal VD, whereas AL, signal strength, vertical cup-to-disc ratio (C/D), Bruch's membrane opening (BMO), and optic disc-fovea distance (DFD) significantly influenced choroidal VD.
Inner retinal VD followed T > SN > IN, primarily contributed by the SVC, whereas choroidal VD followed N > T. With increasing AL, choroidal VD declined across all regions, most prominently in the T hemisphere, whereas the inner retinal VD trends varied by region and layer.
The findings of this study may contribute to the early warning of the disease and provide an important theoretical basis for the study of myopia-related microcirculatory alteration mechanisms.

[Introduction] Introduction Blood flow impairment to the optic nerve head (ONH) is one of the major causes of blindness or impaired vision, especially in blinding diseases such as glaucoma and ischemic optic neuropathy (ION). 1 Early detection of ONH microcirculatory abnormalities is clinically crucial for disease monitoring and intervention. However, traditional assessment methods have significant limitations: fundus photograph-based observation of optic disc color is highly subjective and unquantifiable; color Doppler ultrasound can measure blood flow velocity but is difficult to analyze the microvascular network; and fluorescein angiography, although it can dynamically observe blood flow, is invasive and potentially allergenic, limiting its clinical application. With advancements in tomographic imaging techniques, particularly optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA), stratified, noninvasive imaging enables the quantitative assessment of microvascular density, facilitating a deeper understanding of the relationship between various diseases and optic disc vessel density (VD). It has been shown that a reduction in VD is strongly associated with glaucoma severity, with retinal microvascular changes potentially preceding detectable visual field defects. 2 , 3 OCTA pinpoints areas of perfusion loss in ION, and its distribution is highly coincident with areas of retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) loss and visual field loss. 4 Notably, OCTA has also revealed significant structural remodeling and microcirculatory abnormalities in the ONH of highly myopic eyes, even in the absence of pathological changes. 5 Tang et al. 6 demonstrated that peripapillary microcirculatory parameters were more predictive of myopic progression than the macula, suggesting the potential importance of VD monitoring in the ONH region in myopia. However, large-scale baseline studies on peripapillary VD in healthy populations remain scarce, particularly regarding subregional analysis, leading to a lack of reliable reference standards for disease localization. Existing studies have predominantly focused on the retinal layer, and it has been well established that reduced choroidal perfusion is associated with myopia progression. 7 , 8 However, most of these studies have concentrated primarily on the macular region. Tang et al. 9 used ultra-widefield OCTA to observe the whole choroidal blood flow in patients with myopia and divided it into nine regions for their study, demonstrating for the first time that choroidal vessels in the optic disc grid were significantly thinner in the early stages of myopia. However, choroidal perfusion studies have largely overlooked peripapillary choroidal blood flow characteristics around the ONH, and research on ONH VD across different regions remains limited. The traditional quadrant partitioning method (superior/inferior/nasal/temporal) does not accurately correspond to capillary anatomic distribution and may obscure early pathological changes. Several factors influence OCTA-based VD measurements, including gender, age, axial length (AL), signal intensity, systemic diseases, and so on. 10 Understanding these influencing factors is essential for accurately interpreting OCTA imaging results and ensuring comparability across individuals. However, most prior studies focused primarily on the retinal layer, with inadequate control for confounding variables, such as the lack of AL correction. The primary aim of this study was to investigate the regional distribution of VD across different layers in a population with normal fundus structures. Additionally, we collected a broad range of ocular and physiological parameters to evaluate their influence on VD within different layers.

[introduction] Introduction Blood flow impairment to the optic nerve head (ONH) is one of the major causes of blindness or impaired vision, especially in blinding diseases such as glaucoma and ischemic optic neuropathy (ION). 1 Early detection of ONH microcirculatory abnormalities is clinically crucial for disease monitoring and intervention. However, traditional assessment methods have significant limitations: fundus photograph-based observation of optic disc color is highly subjective and unquantifiable; color Doppler ultrasound can measure blood flow velocity but is difficult to analyze the microvascular network; and fluorescein angiography, although it can dynamically observe blood flow, is invasive and potentially allergenic, limiting its clinical application. With advancements in tomographic imaging techniques, particularly optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA), stratified, noninvasive imaging enables the quantitative assessment of microvascular density, facilitating a deeper understanding of the relationship between various diseases and optic disc vessel density (VD). It has been shown that a reduction in VD is strongly associated with glaucoma severity, with retinal microvascular changes potentially preceding detectable visual field defects. 2 , 3 OCTA pinpoints areas of perfusion loss in ION, and its distribution is highly coincident with areas of retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) loss and visual field loss. 4 Notably, OCTA has also revealed significant structural remodeling and microcirculatory abnormalities in the ONH of highly myopic eyes, even in the absence of pathological changes. 5 Tang et al. 6 demonstrated that peripapillary microcirculatory parameters were more predictive of myopic progression than the macula, suggesting the potential importance of VD monitoring in the ONH region in myopia. However, large-scale baseline studies on peripapillary VD in healthy populations remain scarce, particularly regarding subregional analysis, leading to a lack of reliable reference standards for disease localization. Existing studies have predominantly focused on the retinal layer, and it has been well established that reduced choroidal perfusion is associated with myopia progression. 7 , 8 However, most of these studies have concentrated primarily on the macular region. Tang et al. 9 used ultra-widefield OCTA to observe the whole choroidal blood flow in patients with myopia and divided it into nine regions for their study, demonstrating for the first time that choroidal vessels in the optic disc grid were significantly thinner in the early stages of myopia. However, choroidal perfusion studies have largely overlooked peripapillary choroidal blood flow characteristics around the ONH, and research on ONH VD across different regions remains limited. The traditional quadrant partitioning method (superior/inferior/nasal/temporal) does not accurately correspond to capillary anatomic distribution and may obscure early pathological changes. Several factors influence OCTA-based VD measurements, including gender, age, axial length (AL), signal intensity, systemic diseases, and so on. 10 Understanding these influencing factors is essential for accurately interpreting OCTA imaging results and ensuring comparability across individuals. However, most prior studies focused primarily on the retinal layer, with inadequate control for confounding variables, such as the lack of AL correction. The primary aim of this study was to investigate the regional distribution of VD across different layers in a population with normal fundus structures. Additionally, we collected a broad range of ocular and physiological parameters to evaluate their influence on VD within different layers.

[methods] Methods Ethics This cross-sectional study enrolled patients attending the Second Affiliated Hospital of Xi'an Jiaotong University. Details of this study were reviewed and approved by the Ethics Committee of the Second Affiliated Hospital of Xi'an Jiaotong University (pid: 248264) and were performed in adherence to the tenets of the Declaration of Helsinki. Participants A total of 337 eyes were included in our study. All subjects underwent swept-source optical coherence tomography angiography (SS-OCTA, VG200; SVision Imaging, Ltd., Luoyang, China) examinations and fundus photography (Zeiss, Clarus 500) first to evaluate whether their retinal structures were normal. Patients who met the following criteria were enrolled in the study: (1) subjects with no obvious abnormalities in fundus structures; (2) subjects capable of cooperating with all required examinations; and (3) subjects with clear refractive media and image signal quality greater than 6. Exclusion criteria included previous ocular trauma, poor general health, and diseases affecting the fundus structure and blood flow, such as hypertension, diabetes mellitus, coronary artery disease, heart failure, cerebrovascular accidents, and others. Image Acquirement All participants underwent SS-OCTA examinations with a scanning speed of 200,000 A-scans per second. Each participant underwent both ONH angiography and biological measurement modes. ONH angiography covers a 6 × 6 mm 2 area centered on the ONH, including both vascular and structural information. It provides vessel information for the inner retina layer, superficial vascular complex (SVC), deep vascular complex (DVC), and choroidal layer. The inner retinal layer comprises the SVC and DVC. The SVC is defined as the region extending from the internal limiting membrane (ILM) to the inner third of the inner plexiform layer (IPL), encompassing both the radial peripapillary capillary plexus (RPCP) and the superficial vascular plexus (SVP). The DVC spans from the lower third of the IPL to the outer plexiform layer (OPL), and includes the intermediate vascular plexus (IVP) and the deep vascular plexus (DVP). The choroidal layer is delineated from Bruch's membrane to the outer boundary of the choroid, covering both the choriocapillaris and large choroidal vessels. Additionally, RNFL thickness, average cup-disc ratio (C/D), vertical C/D, Bruch's membrane opening (BMO), optic disc-fovea distance (DFD), and optic disc-fovea angle (DFA) were measured. In biological measurement mode, we can collect AL, central corneal thickness (CCT), aqueous depth (AQD), anterior chamber depth (ACD), lens thickness (LT), and choroidal thickness (CT). Image Processing VDs for the different layers were defined as the percentage of the area occupied by the vessels and was calculated automatically using the OCTA built-in software within a 2 to 4 mm circle centered on the optic disk. BMO, DFD, and DFA were measured manually using B-scan and en face images ( Figs. 1 E, 1 F) It is worth noting that all the indices from the ONH angiography mode should be corrected for AL using Bennett's formula, including RNFL thickness, BMO, and DFD. 11 , 12 VD was corrected by adjusting the diameters of each circle based on the formula. Figures 1 A to 1 D presents detailed VD in 5 regions of the inner retinal and choroidal layers before and after correction of the AL. These regions include superior temporal (ST), inferior temporal (IT), inferior nasal (IN), superior nasal (SN), and disc (D). Figure 1. Vessel density of inner retinal layer ( A , B ) and choroidal layer ( C, D ) before ( A , C ) and after correction ( B , D ) in five regions, including the superior temporal (ST), inferior temporal (IT), inferior nasal (IN), superior nasal (SN), and disc. BMO ( E ), DFD, and DFA ( F ) manual measurements. Statistical Analysis The statistical analyses were performed using SPSS Statistics 27.0 (IBM Corp.). The normality of the data was assessed using the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test. Data were expressed as means ± standard deviations (SDs) for normally distributed variables and as median (Q1 and Q3) for non-normally distributed variables. Differences in continuous variables were compared using the 1-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) test for normally distributed data and Kruskal-Wallis test for non-normally distributed data. Univariate analyses were conducted prior to multivariate analyses to screen for relevant variables. The variables with statistically significant associations in univariate analyses were included in the multivariate models. To mitigate multicollinearity, variables with a variance inflation factor (VIF) greater than 5 were excluded. Differences were considered statistically significant when P < 0.05.

[results] Results Characteristics of Subjects Finally, 337 eyes of 208 participants (70 male subjects and 138 female subjects) were included in this study. Owing to suboptimal image quality or participants’ inability to complete bilateral examinations, the contralateral eyes of 79 subjects were excluded from the analysis. Table 1 shows the demographic and clinical characteristics of the participants. Table 1. Clinical Characteristics of the Study Population Characteristics N = 337 Eyes of 208 Patients Number of subjects’ eyes, n 337 Female eyes, n (%) 226 (67.06) Age, y, M (Q1, Q3) 27.0 (15.5, 46.0) AL, mm, M (Q1, Q3) 24.29 (23.35, 25.32) CCT, µm, mean ± SD 562.93 ± 32.59 AQD, mm, M (Q1, Q3) 2.90 (2.62, 3.13) ACD, mm, mean ± SD 3.43 ± 0.40 LT, mm, M (Q1, Q3) 3.72 (3.44, 4.23) CT, µm, M (Q1, Q3) 289.16 (238.75, 348.05) Average C/D, M (Q1, Q3) 0.51 (0.38, 0.61) RNFL thickness, mm, mean ± SD 108.39 ± 9.62 Vertical C/D, M (Q1, Q3) 0.46 (0.34, 0.54) BMO, mm, M (Q1, Q3) 1803.62 (1693.73, 1902.01) DFD, mm, M (Q1, Q3) 4724.48 (4513.17, 5023.04) DFA, degrees, mean ± SD 6.64 ± 3.39 Inner retinal layer VD (%), M (Q1, Q3) 70.35 (66.05, 74.32) SVC-VD (%), mean ± SD 64.73 ± 5.48 DVC-VD (%), M (Q1, Q3) 19.61 (15.90, 23.58) Choroidal layer VD (%), M (Q1, Q3) 99.98 (96.68, 99.75) ACD, anterior chamber depth; AL, axial length; AQD, aqueous depth; BMO, Bruch's membrane opening; CCT, central corneal thickness; CT, choroidal thickness; C/D, cup-disc ratio; DFA, optic disc-fovea angle; DFD, optic disc-fovea distance; DVC, deep vascular complex; LT, lens thickness; SVC, superficial vascular complex; VD, vessel density. Characteristics of Each Parameter Grouped by Age and Gender All eyes were classified into 4 age groups: age 1 (≤20 years), age 2 (21–40 years), age 3 (41–60 years), and age 4 (≥61 years). ACD and AQD decreased progressively with age ( P < 0.001), whereas LT increased significantly ( P < 0.001), and intergroup comparisons revealed significant differences across all age categories ( P max = 0.005, α B = 0.008). RNFL thickness also showed a significant age-related decline ( P = 0.024), with the >60-year group exhibiting significantly thinner RNFL compared to the 1 to 40-year groups (p max = 0.005, α B = 0.008). The change of VD stratified with age and gender are shown in Figure 2 . In all layers, VD in the female population was higher than that in the male population in most age groups. In the inner retinal and SVC layers, VD exhibited similar trends across age groups, with a trend of decreasing VD with age in the >20 years group. In the choroidal layer, female participants exhibited significantly higher VD than male participants in both the 21 to 40-year and the >60-year age groups ( P >21–40 = 0.044; P >60 = 0.030). Across age groups, VD differed significantly in all layers except the DVC ( P < 0.001). Notably, only the inner retinal layer and SVC demonstrated consistent age-related trends, whereas other layers showed variable patterns of change with age. Figure 2. The change of vessel density stratified with age and gender in different layers. ( A ) The change of vessel density stratified with age and gender in the inner retinal layer. ( B ) The change of vessel density stratified with age and gender in superficial vascular complex (SVC). ( C ) The change of vessel density stratified with age and gender in deep vascular complex (DVC). ( D ) The change of vessel density stratified with age and gender in the choroidal layer. Statistical differences between gender groups are indicated by * ( P < 0.05). Characteristics of Each Parameter Grouped by Axial Length All eyes were divided into 3 groups based on AL: AL1 (≤24.00 mm); AL2 (24.01–26 mm); and AL3 (>26 mm). As the AL increased, parameters including AQD, ACD, DFD, and BMO diameter significantly increased (all P < 0.001), along with RNFL thickness ( P = 0.031). In contrast, LT ( P < 0.001), CT ( P < 0.001), and vertical C/D ( P = 0.002) showed a significant overall decrease. Furthermore, average VD in both the DVC ( P = 0.008) and choroidal layer ( P < 0.001) decreased progressively with increasing AL. No significant difference was found in the inner retinal layer and SVC average VD in the three groups. The distribution of average VD in the five regions among three groups is shown in Figure 3 . In the SVC and DVC layers, VD in the four regions surrounding the ONH exhibited opposite trends with AL, whereas the inner retinal layer followed a pattern consistent with the SVC. Among all layers, only the choroidal layer exhibited a consistent and significant reduction in VD across all regions as AL increased among the three AL groups ( P max < 0.001, α B = 0.017). Moreover, the choroidal layer displayed greater stability of data in terms of error bars. Figure 3. The average vessel density in five regions (disk [D], superior temporal [ST], inferior temporal [IT], inferior nasal [IN], and superior nasal [SN]) among the three groups (AL1 = <24.00 mm; AL2 = 24.01–26 mm; and AL3 = >26 mm). ( A ) The average vessel density in five regions in the inner retinal layer. ( B ) The average vessel density in five regions in the inner retinal layer in the superficial vascular complex (SVC). ( C ) The average vessel density in five regions in the inner retinal layer in the deep vascular complex (DVC). ( D ) The average vessel density in five regions in the inner retinal layer in the choroidal layer. Statistical differences between groups are indicated by * ( P < 0.05). Distribution of Vessel Density Between the Axial Length Less Than or Equal to and More Than the 26 mm Groups Eyes with an AL of less than or equal to 26 mm were included in the non-highly myopia group, and the remaining eyes were in the highly myopia group. The non-highly myopic group consisted of 282 eyes, wheres the highly myopic group included 55 eyes. The distribution of VD in the non-highly myopia group is shown in Figure 4 . In the non-highly myopic group, VD followed a consistent ranking pattern in the inner retina and SVC: temporal (T) > SN > IN, whereas in the DVC, it was ranked as inferior (I) > superior (S), and in the choroidal layer, it followed nasal (N) > T. When comparing the differences in VD between the superior-inferior and nasal-temporal hemispheres, consistent results were observed in the inner retinal layer and SVC, with the order S > I ( P inner = 0.004, P SVC < 0.001) and T > N ( P < 0.001). In the DVC, the order was I > S ( P < 0.001) and N > T ( P = 0.026). In the choroidal layer, N > T ( P < 0.001), no statistically significant differences were found in the S and I hemispheres. Figure 4. Vessel density distribution (the superior temporal [ST] on the top left , the inferior temporal [IT] region on the bottom left , the inferior nasal [IN] region on the bottom right , and the superior nasal [SN] region on the top right ) in different layers among the non-highly myopia group. ( A ) Vessel density distribution in the inner retinal layer. ( B ) Vessel density distribution in the superficial vascular complex. ( C ) Vessel density distribution in the deep vascular complex. ( D ) Vessel density distribution in the choroidal layer. In the same pie chart, different colored regions represent significant differences between regions ( P < 0.05). High saturation colors represent higher vessel density. As shown in Figure 5 , in the inner retina and SVC, the VD was higher in the temporal hemisphere and lower in the nasal hemisphere in the highly myopic group. In contrast, VD changes in the DVC exhibited an opposite trend to those observed in the temporal region of the SVC. In the choroidal layer, VD was significantly lower in all regions of the highly myopia group, with the most pronounced reduction occurring in the temporal hemisphere, but the ordering of VD was consistent between the two groups, as was the ordering in the inner retinal layer. A significant difference between the two groups was observed at all layers, except for the SN region in the DVC. However, the direction and magnitude of these differences varied across different layers and regions. Across different vascular layers, VD followed the pattern: choroidal layer > inner retinal layer > SVC > DVC. Figure 5. Vessel density distribution (the superior temporal [ST], the inferior temporal [IT], the inferior nasal [IN], and the superior nasal [SN]) in different layers among the non-highly myopia and highly myopia groups. ( A ) Vessel density distribution in the inner retinal layer. ( B ) Vessel density distribution in the superficial vascular complex. ( C ) Vessel density distribution in the deep vascular complex. ( D ) Vessel density distribution in the choroidal layer. Statistical differences between gender groups are indicated by * ( P < 0.05). High saturation colors represent higher vessel density. The bars indicate the vascular density percentage (VD%) across different regions. Blue denotes the non-highly myopia group, whereas orange represents the highly myopia group. Analysis of Factors Influencing Vessel Density at Different Layers Tables 2 and 3 summarized factors contributing on the VD in the inner retinal and choroidal layers. In univariable models, ACD, LT, and signal strength plays important roles in both models. However, gender, CCT, AQD, time, and DFA were not significantly associated with VD in either the inner retinal or choroidal layers. LT and average C/D were excluded, respectively, from the multivariable model 1 and 2 due to their VIF being greater than 5, which could lead to inaccurate model results. In multivariable models, ACD was no longer significantly associated with VD in either layer. LT and CT also became not significantly associated with VD in the choroidal layer. Finally, age, signal strength, and RNFL thickness were included in multivariable model for the inner retinal layer, whereas AL, signal strength, vertical C/D, BMO, and DFD were included in the multivariable model for the choroidal layer. Table 2. Factors Influencing Vessel Density in Inner Retinal Layers Univariable Model 1 Inner Retinal Layer Multivariable Model 1 Inner Retinal Layer Variables Coefficient, 95% CI P Value Coefficient, 95% CI P Value Gender 1.32 (−0.08 to 2.72) 0.064 Age 0.06 (0.02 to 0.10) 0.002 0.09 (0.05 to 0.13) <0.001 AL −0.18 (−0.64 to 0.27) 0.427 CCT −0.02 (−0.04 to 0.00) 0.093 AQD 0.02 (−0.02 to 0.07) 0.323 ACD −2.06 (−3.70 to −0.43) 0.013 0.40 (−1.41 to 2.21) 0.663 LT 1.70 (0.38 to 3.04) 0.012 CT 0.01 (0.00 to 0.02) 0.051 Time −0.65 (−1.97 to 0.67) 0.333 Signal strength 2.36 (1.80 to 2.92) <0.001 2.21 (1.70 to 2.73) <0.001 Average C/D 2.08 (−2.20 to 6.36) 0.339 RNFL thickness 0.23 (0.17 to 0.30) <0.001 0.22 (0.16 to 0.28) <0.001 Vertical C/D 2.82 (−1.64 to 7.29) 0.215 BMO 0.00 (−0.00 to 0.01) 0.147 DFD −0.00 (−0.00 to 0.00) 0.058 DFA 0.12 (−0.08 to 0.31) 0.235 Differences were considered statistically significant when P < 0.05. The P values in bold represent statistical significance. Table 3. Factors Influencing Vessel Density in Choroidal Layers Univariable Model 2 Choroidal Layer Multivariable Model 2 Choroidal Layer Variables Coefficient, 95% CI P Value Coefficient, 95% CI P Value Gender 0.23 (−0.43 to 0.88) 0.497 Age 0.01 (−0.01 to 0.03) 0.190 AL −1.39 (−1.54 to −1.24) <0.001 −1.11 (−1.32 to −0.89) <0.001 CCT 0.00 (−0.01 to 0.01) 0.988 AQD 0.01 (−0.02 to 0.03) 0.606 ACD −1.49 (−2.24 to −0.74) <0.001 0.47 (−0.33 to 1.27) 0.249 LT 0.89 (0.27 to 1.50) 0.005 −0.28 (−0.89 to 0.34) 0.378 CT 0.01 (0.01 to 0.01) <0.001 0.00 (−0.00 to 0.00) 0.204 Time 0.04 (−0.58 to 0.65) 0.901 Signal strength 0.83 (0.55 to 1.10) <0.001 0.41 (0.22 to 0.61) <0.001 Average C/D 2.70 (0.73 to 4.67) 0.007 RNFL thickness 0.02 (−0.01 to 0.05) 0.215 Vertical C/D 3.50 (1.46 to 5.54) <0.001 2.74 (1.16 to 4.32) <0.001 BMO −0.01 (−0.01 to 0.00) <0.001 −0.00 (−0.00 to −0.00) 0.007 DFD −0.00 (−0.00 to 0.00) <0.001 −0.00 (−0.00 to −0.00) 0.044 DFA 0.03 (−0.06 to 0.12) 0.563 Differences were considered statistically significant when P < 0.05. The P values in bold represent statistical significance.

[discussion] Discussion In this study, we analyzed the distribution characteristics of ONH VD across different regions and layers, as well as its influencing factors, in highly myopic and non-highly myopic populations using an OCTA imaging system. Our findings demonstrated that the correlation between age and VD exhibited significant tissue-layer specificity: a trend of decreasing VD with age in adults was observed in the SVC and inner retina, consistent with previous findings in the radial peripapillary retinal capillary plexus (RPC). 13 In contrast, the DVC and the choroidal layer did not show a significant correlation with age. We believe the age-related changes in the SVC and inner retinal layers may be associated with degenerative vascular conditions such as atherosclerosis. With aging, arterial walls progressively thicken and narrow, resulting in slower blood flow and reduced VD. 14 As atherosclerosis mainly affects large and medium-sized arteries, this could explain why the SVC is more prominently affected than the DVC. It is noteworthy that gender did not have a significant effect on VD in the three retinal layers. However, statistical significance was observed in the choroidal layer of individuals aged 21 to 40 years and those aged ≥60 years, probably indicating a potential hormonal influence that requires further validation through biochemical and molecular analyses. The effect of AL on VD demonstrated regional and layer specificity. In the different AL subgroups, we observed that VD in all regions of the choroidal layer significantly decreased with increasing AL. However, VD in the inner retina showed no significant change, likely due to the opposing trends observed in the SVC and DVC. Chen et al. 15 investigated the VD in RPC, SVC, and DVC. They reported significant differences in VD in the I, S, and N regions for RPC and SVC, with a decreasing trend associated with increasing AL in regions with group differences. However, no significant differences were found in the DVD across all four peripapillary regions. The influence of AL on VD varied by region within the same retinal layer, with the SVC demonstrating the most sensitive response, making it a key focus for analysis. Inter-regional analysis is particularly important to avoid misleading conclusions, as averaging VD across regions may obscure significant variations in VD trends. The effect of different ALs on retinal VD remains controversial in the literature. Cheng et al. 16 found that the VD in the RPC decreased with increasing AL in all five regions (I, S, N, T, and D). One study indicated that the VD in the D region of the RPC did not differ significantly between AL groups. 17 However, another study showed that average VD of the peripapillary regions in the RPC decreased with AL, whereas the VD in the D region increased. 18 Notably, in our study, the central region of the D region showed paradoxically elevated VD at all layers. This may be due to the fact that the D region is primarily supplied by the Zinn-Haller arterial circle, which has abundant collateral circulation, whereas the other four regions are supplied by the central arteries of the retina. 19 The difference between studies may be attributed to the different partitioning methods and the fact that the studies mentioned did not correct for AL, which standardizes the range of VD observations across different eyes, leading to more objective and accurate results. Additionally, differences in the layers of the VDs observed, the ages of the study populations, and the subgroups of AL may also account for the discrepancies. Consistent with previous studies, 9 the peripapillary choroidal layer VD may serve as a predictor of myopia. A tendency for VD to decrease with AL elongation was observed across all regions. Additionally, the SVC VD may also act as a sensitive predictor of myopia. However, this requires regional analysis due to varying trends in different regions, which still needs to be further validated by additional studies. We further stratified subjects into highly myopic and non-highly myopic groups to conduct a quantitative analysis of VD distribution and regional ordering. In the non-highly myopic group, the anatomic ordering of the VD in the inner retina mirrored the previously reported sequence of retinal artery and vein diameters. 20 The VD ordering in the inner retinal layers and the SVC followed a consistent pattern across the groups, likely due to the large retinal vessels traveling within the SVC, suggesting that perfusion in these two layers is predominantly governed by major retinal vessels. Interestingly, the VD distribution pattern in the DVC appears to be associated with that of the SVC. The IN region, which had the lowest VD in the SVC, showed a relatively higher rank in the DVC, whereas the ST region, which had the highest SVC VD, showed a reduced rank in the DVC. We hypothesize that this may reflect a compensatory vascular mechanism. VD changes may be influenced by transmural pressure, which varies across different vascular layers. Downstream vessels might undergo morphological changes to compensate for upstream flow alterations. 21 However, this speculation requires further investigation. Between the highly myopic and non-highly myopic groups, significant VD alterations were observed in the temporal hemisphere of the inner retinal layers, with an increase in VD in the SVC and a decrease in the DVC, whereas the opposite trend was observed in the nasal hemisphere of the layer, which may result in the two groups of VD being ordered differently in the SVC and the DVC but being ordered consistently in the inner retina due to the counteracting effect. Mo et al. 22 reported significant reductions in retinal VD in the N and T regions of the ONH in highly myopic eyes. These discrepancies may stem from the lack of correction in their analysis and inconsistencies in the segmentation of regions. As the AL increases, the central vascular trunk in the inner retinal layer is dragged nasally. 23 Jonas et al. 24 found that a reduction in the angle between the superior and inferior temporal vascular arches of the ONH may explain the observed increase in the temporal VD in the inner retinal layer. This may be attributed to biomechanical stretching, which could enhance perfusion efficiency in the temporal arcuate fiber region by altering vascular geometry, thereby preserving visual function. 17 The ordering of the choroidal layers showed a significantly higher VD on the nasal side than on the temporal side, with the possible explanation being that the watershed area located between the temporal artery and the area supplied by the posterior nasociliary artery mostly involves the temporal side and rarely the nasal side. 25 Most studies examining the correlation between choroidal blood flow and myopia have focused on the macular region. Changes in choroidal blood flow, which affect CT, may serve as a predictor for myopia progression, as demonstrated in studies on guinea pigs’ optic nerves. 26 Our results also showed that choroidal VD decreases with increasing AL across all five regions, with the most significant reduction in the T hemisphere region especially in the IT region, but there was no change in the ordering of VD, probably due to the absence of significant regional damage to the fundus structures. In addition, choroidal layer VD showed the most stable performance with the smallest standard deviation among all layers. This suggests that choroidal VD may serve as a sensitive indicator for early disease prediction, particularly in the T hemisphere region. Excessive axial elongation of the eyeball leads to biomechanical stretching of the retina and choroid, 27 , 28 which may result in the straightening and narrowing of vessels in highly myopic eyes and a reduction in vessel branching, which in turn leads to lower VD. 29 The reasons for the more pronounced reduction in the VD of the T region of the choroidal layer in highly myopic eyes remain to be further explored. Our study focused on the VD in a population with normal fundus structures and analyzed its influencing factors. Signal strength, a key parameter of OCTA imaging quality, was found to have a significant impact on VD measurements across all layers. Whether signal strength mediates the relationship between AL and choroidal VD is a noteworthy concern. To explore this, we conducted a mediation analysis. The results showed a direct effect of AL on choroidal VD of −0.6673 and an indirect effect of −0.0358 mediated by signal strength, leading to a total effect of −0.7031. The mediating role of signal strength accounted for approximately 5% of the total effect, indicating a limited influence. Therefore, we did not include signal strength as a covariate in the final analysis. After correction according to the AL, we observed no significant association between AL and inner retinal VD, which aligns with the findings of Lv et al., who reported a similar trend in the SVP VD. 30 In our model 1, we identified a positive correlation between RNFL thickness and inner retinal VD, which is consistent with a previous study demonstrating a similar positive correlation between RNFL thickness and SVC VD. Together, these findings reinforce the structural-functional relationship between retinal circulation and neural integrity. 31 CT thinning due to increased AL has been recognized as a strong correlate of decreased choroidal VD in several studies, 32 – 34 and this was confirmed in our univariable model. However, CT was excluded from the multivariable model. Both CT and choroidal VD are influenced by factors such as age and AL, and their physiological link requires further investigation. 35 This aligns with the results of our multivariable model, suggesting that CT may not be a sufficient indicator of choroidal circulation. Smaller LT and deeper ACD may reflect a compensatory mechanism to counteract the optical effects of axial elongation during myopic progression. 36 – 38 However, due to the confounding effects of LT and ACD with age, AL, and other factors, these variables were similarly excluded from the multivariable models 1 and 2. The effect of vertical C/D on the choroidal blood flow has not been previously reported, but a possible explanation is that vertical C/D is negatively correlated with AL, 39 and the exact mechanism needs to be further investigated. DFD and DFA are important parameters of the posterior fundus that collectively characterize the position of the ONH relative to the macula. DFD increased with axial elongation, but DFA did not appear to be significantly correlated with AL. The axial elongation-related increase in DFD was attributed to the enlargement of the parapapillary β / γ zone, which was defined as the parapapillary zone with visible sclera and visible large choroidal vessels and was associated with capillary occlusion. 40 , 41 This may explain the inclusion of DFD in the model 2. In moderately myopic eyes, BMO movement typically occurred, which may lead to passive exposure of the choroid, thereby forming the γ zone. In highly myopic eyes, the enlargement of BMO may directly stretch the choroidal capillaries leading to a decrease in branch density. 42 Therefore, BMO was also included in our multivariate model. Our study further confirmed that CT, ACD, AQD, and DFD all increased with increasing AL across the different AL subgroups, and these findings highlighted the need for further investigation to elucidate the exact mechanisms. Previous studies have suggested that the retinal and choroidal blood flow is influenced by circadian rhythms. 43 , 44 In our study, when we categorized time into ante meridiem and post meridiem as dichotomous variables, we did not find a correlation between time and VD. For the first time, we comprehensively examined the choroidal VD surrounding the optic disc across different regions and analyzed its influencing factors. Additionally, we experimentally collected and sequenced VD data from patients with glaucoma and those with glaucoma combined with high myopia, revealing distinct regional VD ordering patterns among diseased and healthy individuals. This finding highlights the importance of regional hemodynamic studies in disease characterization. Moreover, our partitioning method more accurately reflects vascular alignment and anatomic structures, an approach that has been rarely explored in previous studies. Furthermore, our study corrected for AL-related imaging distortions in OCTA, collected a broad range of ocular parameters, and accounted for the effects of physiological factors such as age, gender, and examination time. Our findings may contribute to the early detection of disease-related microvascular alterations, provide a theoretical foundation for understanding the mechanisms of myopia-associated microcirculatory changes, elucidate the dynamic evolution of VD with axial elongation, and establish a baseline database of VD across different ONH regions and layers in healthy populations. However, several limitations should be acknowledged: First, we used AL instead of diopters as a variable to assess its effect on VD. Second, the highly myopic group had a relatively small sample size of only 55 eyes, representing 16.3% of the total sample, which may limit the generalizability of the findings. Future studies should incorporate larger highly myopic cohorts to enable a more comprehensive assessment of VD alterations. Last, due to the exclusion of patients with hypertension and diabetes, recruiting elderly subjects was challenging. Nonetheless, we accounted for age-related effects when assessing VD. In conclusion, we examined the distribution of retinal and choroidal VD across different regions after correcting for AL and analyzed the factors influencing VD at both layers. Our findings demonstrate that choroidal VD remains relatively stable, exhibiting a progressive decline in all regions with increasing AL, with the most pronounced reduction observed in the temporal hemisphere, but the ordering of the different regions did not change. However, not all retinal regions exhibited similar reductions, expanding our understanding of blood flow dynamics around the optic disc.

[conclusion] Conclusions Inner retinal VD followed T > SN > IN, primarily contributed by the SVC, whereas choroidal VD followed N > T. With increasing AL, choroidal VD declined across all regions, most prominently in the T hemisphere, whereas the inner retinal VD trends varied by region and layer.

retrieved_papers.title: The Effects of Adjuvant Tamoxifen Use on Macula Pigment Epithelium Optical Density, Visual Acuity and Retinal Thickness in Patients with Breast Cancer.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2020]
[Abstract] Purpose: We aimed to compare best corrected visual acuity, macular pigment optical density and macular thickness in patients with breast cancer, who received oral adjuvant hormone therapy.Materials and Methods: We enrolled consecutive eligible patients with breast cancer who were receiving regular medical tamoxifen treatment. The participants were divided into two groups as cases and controls. Best-corrected visual acuity and retinal thickness were examined. Macular pigment optical density was measured by fundus reflectometry using the one-wavelength reflection method. The output parameters included max optical density, mean optical density, volume and area of the right eye.Results: A total of 104 eyes, cases (n: 50) and controls (n: 54) were included in the study. Mean age in cases was 49.95 ± 9.2 years and 50.21 ± 9.3 years in controls (p = .151). The mean foveal optical density and the maximum optical density differed between cases (0.13 ± 0.03 density units (DU)/0.35 ± 0.07 DU) and controls (0.18 ± 0.04 DU/0.41 ± 0.06 DU) (p = .002/p = .009). Macular pigment optical density volume was 8102.84 ± 2412.67 in cases versus 8280.18 ± 2904.56 in controls (p = .034), and mean MPOD area was 59567.79 ± 11538.06 in cases versus 61748.14 ± 10591.19 in controls (p = .023). The best corrected visual acuity and retinal thickness were similar in both groups (p > .05).Conclusions: Patients in care of oral tamoxifen therapy were found to have significantly reduced macular pigment optical density. In addition, higher drug use duration correlated significantly with reduced macular pigment optical density, suggesting that the poor long-term effects may play a role in macular pigment absorption and incorporation in the retinal tissue.

[Introduction] Introduction Tamoxifen, a selective estrogen receptor modulator, has long been a cornerstone in the treatment of hormone receptor-positive breast cancer 1 , 2 . Its efficacy in reducing the risk of recurrence and mortality in breast cancer patients is well established 3 . Guidelines from reputable organizations, such as the National Comprehensive Cancer Network (NCCN) and the American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) provide recommendations regarding tamoxifen use in breast cancer treatment 4 , 5 . These guidelines suggest tamoxifen therapy in premenopausal women with a recommended daily dose of 20 mg. The therapy may last from 5 to 10 years depending on the patient's menopausal status and individual risk factors 6 , 7 . Furthermore, tamoxifen is used in metastatic breast cancer treatment, at recommended dosages ranging from 20 to 40 mg daily 8 . Recent advancements in breast cancer management have led to the development of tailored treatment approaches, as evidenced by the Pan-Asian adapted ESMO Clinical Practice Guidelines 8 . These guidelines advocate for the inclusion of ovarian function suppression or ovarian ablation in conjunction with endocrine-based therapies for pre- and peri-menopausal women in Asian countries. Despite the well-established efficacy of tamoxifen and evidence-based recommendations or guidelines, the incorporation of these recommendations into national breast cancer guidelines varies across regions 7 , 9 . In Korea, however, the guidelines have limited specific directives on tamoxifen use for breast cancer treatment and prevention 9 . However, the therapeutic advantages of tamoxifen may come with potential ocular complications, especially those impacting the macula 1 , 2 , 10 – 13 .The ocular side effects of tamoxifen, specifically tamoxifen-related retinopathy (maculopathy), have raised concerns among prescribing physicians and ophthalmologists. Alterations in the retina are reported to manifest in approximately 12% of individuals who have been using a daily dose of 20 mg of tamoxifen for a period exceeding two years 14 , corresponding to a cumulative dose of approximately 15 g. These alterations include the presence of crystalline deposits, telangiectasia, hyperreflective deposits in the inner retinal layers, pseudocystic foveal cavitation, and cystoid macular edema 1 , 2 . Understanding the demographic and clinical characteristics of individuals at a higher risk of developing maculopathy is crucial for identifying preventive measures and optimizing patient care. Only a few studies have focused on the risk and associated factors of maculopathy, mandating a comprehensive investigation into the risk factors and incidence rates of ocular complications associated with this widely used drug 1 , 14 . In the present study, we investigated the risk of diverse macular conditions potentially associated with tamoxifen use in a nationwide cohort of tamoxifen users. By examining clinical characteristics, such as age, sex, and systemic diseases that potentially affect drug metabolism or retinal diseases, we explored the risk factors for macular conditions by focusing on age, drug exposure, and other systemic diseases. This study aimed to delineate the association between these factors and the occurrence of several types of maculopathies, providing valuable insights into the dose–response relationship between macular diseases and drug exposure and aiding in the formulation of evidence-based guidelines for tamoxifen maculopathy screening.

Its efficacy in reducing the risk of recurrence and mortality in breast cancer patients is well established 3 . Guidelines from reputable organizations, such as the National Comprehensive Cancer Network (NCCN) and the American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) provide recommendations regarding tamoxifen use in breast cancer treatment 4 , 5 . These guidelines suggest tamoxifen therapy in premenopausal women with a recommended daily dose of 20 mg. The therapy may last from 5 to 10 years depending on the patient's menopausal status and individual risk factors 6 , 7 . Furthermore, tamoxifen is used in metastatic breast cancer treatment, at recommended dosages ranging from 20 to 40 mg daily 8 . Recent advancements in breast cancer management have led to the development of tailored treatment approaches, as evidenced by the Pan-Asian adapted ESMO Clinical Practice Guidelines 8 . These guidelines advocate for the inclusion of ovarian function suppression or ovarian ablation in conjunction with endocrine-based therapies for pre- and peri-menopausal women in Asian countries. Despite the well-established efficacy of tamoxifen and evidence-based recommendations or guidelines, the incorporation of these recommendations into national breast cancer guidelines varies across regions 7 , 9 . In Korea, however, the guidelines have limited specific directives on tamoxifen use for breast cancer treatment and prevention 9 . However, the therapeutic advantages of tamoxifen may come with potential ocular complications, especially those impacting the macula 1 , 2 , 10 – 13 .The ocular side effects of tamoxifen, specifically tamoxifen-related retinopathy (maculopathy), have raised concerns among prescribing physicians and ophthalmologists. Alterations in the retina are reported to manifest in approximately 12% of individuals who have been using a daily dose of 20 mg of tamoxifen for a period exceeding two years 14 , corresponding to a cumulative dose of approximately 15 g. These alterations include the presence of crystalline deposits, telangiectasia, hyperreflective deposits in the inner retinal layers, pseudocystic foveal cavitation, and cystoid macular edema 1 , 2 . Understanding the demographic and clinical characteristics of individuals at a higher risk of developing maculopathy is crucial for identifying preventive measures and optimizing patient care. Only a few studies have focused on the risk and associated factors of maculopathy, mandating a comprehensive investigation into the risk factors and incidence rates of ocular complications associated with this widely used drug 1 , 14 . In the present study, we investigated the risk of diverse macular conditions potentially associated with tamoxifen use in a nationwide cohort of tamoxifen users. By examining clinical characteristics, such as age, sex, and systemic diseases that potentially affect drug metabolism or retinal diseases, we explored the risk factors for macular conditions by focusing on age, drug exposure, and other systemic diseases. This study aimed to delineate the association between these factors and the occurrence of several types of maculopathies, providing valuable insights into the dose–response relationship between macular diseases and drug exposure and aiding in the formulation of evidence-based guidelines for tamoxifen maculopathy screening.

[methods] Methods Study population This study used the Health Insurance Review and Assessment (HIRA) database, a comprehensive repository encompassing health claims data of approximately 50 million individuals in South Korea 15 , 16 . This database, previously employed in a similar cohort study, contains information on diagnoses, procedures, prescriptions, visit dates, and demographic characteristics, utilizing codes from the Korean Standard Classification of Diseases, 7th and 8th Revision, with modifications from the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, Tenth Revision (ICD-10). Tamoxifen users were identified within the database, focusing on individuals who initiated tamoxifen therapy between January 1, 2013, and December 31, 2021. To ensure accurate assessment of treatment duration, patients using tamoxifen before January 1, 2015, were excluded. Individuals with macular diseases before initiating tamoxifen treatment were also excluded aiming to include only patients with macular conditions occurring after tamoxifen initiation. The detailed inclusion/exclusion criteria and the number of subjects included/excluded are presented in Fig. 1 . The Institutional Review Board of Hanyang University Hospital approved the study (IRB File No. 2023-01-003), which was conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki. The need for informed consent was waived by the Institutional Review Board of Hanyang University Hospital because of the retrospective nature of the study and the use of deidentified data. The reporting adhered to the Strengthening the Reporting of Observational Studies in Epidemiology (STROBE) guidelines. Figure 1 Flowchart illustrating the inclusion and exclusion criteria employed in this study, along with the populations after applying these criteria. Definitions and evaluations The identification and classification of macular conditions were based on specific KCD-8 codes within the dataset. Overall macular diseases were defined as the presence of H31.0 (macular scar), H35.3 (degeneration of the macula and posterior pole; including from H35.30 [nonexudative age-related macular degeneration] to H35.39 [unspecified macular degeneration]), or H35.80 (macular edema). Maculopathy excluding common macular diseases was delineated by the code H35.37 (toxic maculopathy) and H35.39 (unspecified macular degeneration; category for the macular diseases after exclusion of common macular diseases such as age-related macular degeneration and epiretinal membrane), while macular edema was specifically identified by the code H35.80 (macular edema). The cumulative incidence of and risk factors for maculopathies among tamoxifen users were investigated. Among the patients receiving sufficient cumulative tamoxifen exposure, specifically 15 g in total (conventional daily dose of 20 mg for two years), the cumulative incidences of overall macular diseases, maculopathy excluding common macular diseases, and macular edema during the study period were evaluated. Although a consensus on the “at risk” population, targeted for toxicity screening, for tamoxifen maculopathy has not been reached, a common recommendation is to commence regular screenings including optical coherence tomography (OCT) for patients who have been on a tamoxifen dose of 20 mg/day for at least two years 2 , which is equivalent to a cumulative dose of 14.6 g. Therefore, we defined patients at risk as those with a cumulative tamoxifen dose ≥ 15 g. We calculated the incidences of several macular conditions according to age and cumulative dose groups among the patients at risk. These were stratified by cumulative dose and age categories into three cumulative dose groups (15 – < 30 g, 30 – < 45 g, and ≥ 45 g) and five age groups (below 40, 40–49, 50–59, 60–69, and ≥ 70). Logistic regression analysis was performed to identify risk factors for diverse macular conditions among tamoxifen users. The analysis included demographic variables, such as sex and age, monitoring period, cumulative dose group, and systemic diseases affecting drug metabolism and the vascular system. We confirmed that variance inflation factors (VIF) were less than 10 and tolerance values were greater than 0.1 for all independent variables used for multivariate analyses, suggesting that multicollinearity was not a significant issue for multivariate analyses performed in this study. Thus, no further adjustment for statistical modeling was necessary 17 – 19 . Odds ratios (ORs) and their corresponding 95% confidence intervals (CI) are reported. Univariate and multivariate analyses were performed per macular condition to ascertain the individual impact of the identified risk factors while controlling for potential confounders. To identify predictive factors among the baseline clinical characteristics, Cox proportional hazard model regression was performed for demographic factors and systemic diseases, and hazard ratios (HR) were calculated for predictive factors. Using the health claims data from the HIRA database, we assessed the tests performed for macular evaluation, including fundoscopy/fundus photography, OCT, and fluorescein angiography. The percentage of patients undergoing each test was assessed and presented as demographic and clinical characteristics. Data analysis Descriptive statistics summarized the findings, presenting categorical variables as frequencies and percentages and continuous variables as mean (standard deviation) or median (interquartile range). Chi-square or Fisher’s exact tests were used to compare categorical variables between the groups. In our study, we used the Cochran–Mantel–Haenszel test, a statistical method for analyzing stratified or matched categorical data, to assess the association between a characteristic and a binary outcome, such as maculopathy status, while accounting for the effects of stratification and potential confounding factors. The cumulative incidence of macular conditions was evaluated with Kaplan–Meier curves. Univariate and multivariate Cox proportional hazard models were utilized to identify predictive factors while controlling for potential confounding variables, including age, sex, and systemic diseases like diabetes mellitus, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, ischemic heart disease, and stroke. Statistical significance was set at P < 0.05. SAS Enterprise Guide version 7.1 conducted all analyses.

[results] Results Demographic and clinical characteristics of study population Table 1 provides a comprehensive overview of the demographic and clinical characteristics of the tamoxifen users enrolled in this study, revealing the diverse profiles of individuals undergoing tamoxifen therapy. The cohort, consisting of 14,267 users, exhibited a notable sex distribution, with 79.9% female and 20.1% male. The mean age of the population was 54.0 years, with a range from 4 to 94 years. The most common age group among tamoxifen users included in this study was 40–49 years old, comprising 37.6% of the cohort. Underlying diseases including diabetes (30.8%), hypertension (37.7%), dyslipidemia (61.1%), kidney disease (16.1%), and liver disease (41.1%) were prevalent in the cohort. The primary indications for tamoxifen use varied, with breast cancer being the dominant indication, accounting for 58.7% of the cases. Additionally, the table presents the mean duration of tamoxifen use (33.1 ± 23.4 months) and the distribution across different duration categories. The cumulative daily dose of tamoxifen, an indicator of drug exposure, was delineated, with 40.5% of the users falling within the < 15 g category. The cumulative doses for the other patients ranged as follows; 15 to 30 g, 27.6% (n = 3931); 30 to 45 g, 29.9% (n = 4269); and > 45 g, 2.0% (n = 284). Cumulative incidences of diverse macular conditions in overall patients and subgroups according to drug exposure level or age groups Figure 2 shows the overall cumulative incidence of macular diseases (26.4%; n = 3,764), maculopathy excluding common macular diseases (11.4%; n = 1,632), and macular edema (6.5%; n = 925) in the entire patient cohort during the study period. However, common macular diseases such as age-related macular degeneration, epiretinal membrane, and macular hole were observed in 8.5% of the included patients over the study period. Figure 2 Cumulative incidences of macular conditions potentially affected by tamoxifen in the users. Table 2 presents the cumulative incidence of overall macular diseases, other maculopathy excluding common macular diseases, and macular edema among the different risk groups of tamoxifen users during the study period, stratified by cumulative dose and age categories. For overall macular diseases, the cumulative incidence showed variations across both age and cumulative dose groups. Notably, a trend of increasing cumulative incidence was observed with higher age groups and cumulative tamoxifen doses ( P < 0.001 by the Cochran–Mantel–Haenszel test), which was statistically significant. This pattern is consistent with maculopathy excluding common macular diseases and macular edema, highlighting the potential influence of age and cumulative tamoxifen dosage on the development of these macular conditions. The presented P -values (< 0.001 for both maculopathy excluding common macular diseases and macular edema) indicated statistically significant differences among the risk groups. These findings suggest the importance of considering both age and cumulative tamoxifen dosage when assessing the risk of overall macular diseases, maculopathy excluding common macular diseases, and macular edema in tamoxifen users. Risk factors and baseline predictive factors for maculopathy in tamoxifen users Table 3 depicts the outcomes of the logistic regression analyses exploring the risk factors associated with various macular conditions among tamoxifen users. In the univariate analysis, several demographic and clinical characteristics were significantly associated with macular diseases. In multivariate analysis, after adjusting for potential confounders, advancing age was significantly associated with overall macular diseases and maculopathy excluding common macular diseases (both P < 0.001). Moreover, specific systemic diseases such as liver diseases emerged as significant risk factors across different macular conditions. The cumulative dose group was significantly associated with all three macular conditions (all P < 0.001). The group with a cumulative dose exceeding 45 g exhibited the highest OR for overall macular diseases (2.31, 95% CI 1.73–3.10) and maculopathy excluding common macular diseases (1.78, 95% CI 1.18–2.69). Additionally, liver disease was associated with overall macular diseases and maculopathy excluding common macular diseases, with ORs of 1.24 (95% CI 1.14–1.35) and 1.18 (95% CI 1.05–1.32), respectively.
