FIGURE 1 Bioaterial-based interventions for ocular diseases induced by cardiovascular risk factors. This infographic illustrates various nanomedical strategies for addressing ocular diseases. It is divided into four sections: PLGA nanoparticles for neuroprotective drug delivery, liposomes for siRNA targeting the MMP-9 gene, exosomes delivering anti-inflammatory miRNAs, and microneedle patches for insulin delivery. Additional solutions include smart contact lenses for intraocular pressure monitoring, zwitterionic coatings for lipoprotein reduction, hydrogels for uric acid-lowering drug delivery, and peptide-based systems for VEGF neutralization. Each section highlights specific conditions like glaucoma, diabetic retinopathy, and hyperlipidemia, with diagrams showing processes like inflammation and oxidative stress.

[methods] 7.2 Application of materials science in NAION Although the application of materials science in the treatment of non-arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (NAION) remains in the exploratory phase, early advances show promising potential. Notably, curcumin-polydopamine nanocomposite hydrogels (Cur@PDA@GelCA) have demonstrated significant neuroprotective effects in optic nerve injury models. These materials act by inhibiting reactive oxygen species (ROS)-mediated oxidative stress, indicating potential utility for acute-phase intervention in NAION. In addition to their therapeutic efficacy, the hydrogel’s strong tissue adhesion and excellent biocompatibility highlight its suitability for localized drug delivery and targeted treatment ( Liu et al., 2024 ). These findings underscore the broader feasibility of integrating materials science into NAION therapy. Future research should aim to further elucidate the molecular mechanisms underlying NAION and leverage multi-omics technologies alongside biomaterials innovation to develop personalized treatment strategies that can improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_papers.title: Age and gender-related changes in choroidal thickness: Insights from deep learning analysis of swept-source OCT images.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] The choroid is a vital vascular layer of the eye, essential for maintaining ocular health. Understanding its structural variations, particularly choroidal thickness (CT), is crucial for the early detection of diseases, such as age-related macular degeneration (AMD), high myopia (HM), and diabetes mellitus (DM). Recent advancements in deep learning have significantly improved the segmentation and measurement of choroidal layers.
This study aims to investigate age- and gender-related changes in CT and its components through deep learning analysis of swept-source optical coherence tomography (SS-OCT) images.
A total of 262 participants (136 females and 126 males) were recruited from Peking University International Hospital. Exclusion criteria included ocular pathologies and systemic conditions. SS-OCT was utilized for CT, Sattler layer-choriocapillaris complex thickness (SLCCT), and Haller layer thickness (HLT) measurements. auto-measurement method, based on deep learning algorithms, ensured accuracy. Ethics approval and informed consent were obtained from all participants.
Significant thinning of CT and SLCCT was observed after the age of 60, with HLT declining after the age of 30. Females exhibited marked thinning between the ages of 40 and 50, while males began to show thinning at age 60.
This research highlights age-related changes in choroidal thickness, with a particular emphasis on gender differences. The findings suggest that females experience earlier thinning, potentially attributable to hormonal changes. Additionally, the study validates the efficiency of deep learning algorithms in measuring choroidal thickness, thereby enhancing the reliability of clinical practice.

retrieved_papers.title: Assessment of Choriocapillaris in Healthy and Diseased Eyes Using 3-Dimensional Topographic Maps Based on OCT Angiography.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] To achieve clear in vivo imaging and systematic quantification of choriocapillaris (CC), enabling the exploration of age-related and disease-related changes of CC within a large sample size.
Cross-sectional study.
A total of 1050 eyes from 727 individuals, comprising 633 healthy eyes, 277 eyes with age-related macular degeneration (AMD), and 140 eyes with high myopia.
The 3-dimensional (3D) topographic maps of CC were obtained by performing image enhancement, depth reconstruction, and 3D rendering on a single OCT angiography enface image. Based on this map, we targeted key quantitative parameters to analyze vessel thickness, vascular complexity, vascular distribution uniformity, and blood perfusion of CC. We statistically analyzed the correlation between CC metrics and age in healthy population, as well as differences in CC metrics between healthy and diseased populations. To further explore the association between changes in CC and choroidal large vessels, we defined a parameter called "arterial zone (AZ) to venous zone (VZ) ratio" to assess the choroidal large-vessel perfusion.
The 3D topographic map of CC, 8 parameters of CC, and AZ/VZ.
The 3D topographic map effectively displayed the morphology of CC while maintaining high image accuracy. Statistical analysis revealed that with aging, the vessel thickness of CC in healthy eyes became thicker, and there was a decrease in vascular complexity, distribution uniformity, and perfusion. Additionally, AZ/VZ exhibited a trend of initial decline followed by an increase. In eyes with increasing severity of AMD, the vessel thickness of CC showed bidirectional changes followed by significant thickening. Complexity and distribution uniformity initially increased, followed by a marked decrease, whereas blood perfusion decreased initially before increasing. However, no significant intergroup differences in AZ/VZ were observed. In highly myopic eyes, compared to healthy eyes, the CC exhibited vessel thickening, reduced complexity and distribution uniformity, decreased perfusion, and a significant reduction in AZ/VZ.
This study explores age-related and disease-related CC changes via clear in vivo imaging and quantification. It aids understanding of CC states and may contribute to early disease diagnosis and prognosis.
Proprietary or commercial disclosure may be found in the Footnotes and Disclosures at the end of this article.

[methods] Methods Study Population This cross-sectional study involved the collection of OCTA images from the Second Affiliated Hospital of Zhejiang University and the Second Affiliated Hospital of Xi’an Jiaotong University between June 2021 and March 2024. Images were captured using a swept-source OCTA device (VG200, SVision Imaging. The device utilized a swept-source laser with a central wavelength of approximately 1050 nm and a scanning rate of 200 000 A-scans per second. It scanned areas of 3 × 3 mm or 15 × 12 mm centered on the fovea (each with a raster scan protocol of 512 B-scans) and could automatically calculate the regional thickness of the choroid. For this study, CC layer enface images of each eye were collected within a 3 × 3 mm area centered on the macula for the construction of 3D topographic maps. Additionally, information on the thickness of different regions of the choroid within a 15 × 12 mm area centered on the macula was gathered for the quantitative analysis of choroidal large vessels. A total of 1050 OCTA images from 727 individuals were collected, categorized as follows, and the inclusion criteria were listed in Supplement 1 (available at www.ophthalmologyscience.org ): 1. Healthy eyes: 633 eyes from 402 individuals with a mean age of 43. 2. Age-related macular degeneration eyes: 277 eyes from 231 patients diagnosed with AMD. 3. High myopia eyes: 140 eyes from 94 patients diagnosed with high myopia. In addition, to better demonstrate the differences between our images and the traditional ones, as well as the value of our method in longitudinal studies, we included an eye affected by polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy, which had both OCTA and indocyanine green angiography images and a complete follow-up record. This study was conducted in compliance with the Declaration of Helsinki. The research protocols were approved by the Medical Ethics Committee of the Second Affiliated Hospital, Zhejiang University (NO Y2023-1073). Written informed consent was obtained from all participants prior to the collection of clinical data. For minors, the informed consent forms were signed by their guardians, in accordance with the ethical standards of the institutional review board.

[results] Results Verification of the CC Layer 3D Topographic Map As shown in Figure 1 D, the CC 3D topographic map exhibits a high degree of overlap with the original OCTA enface image. When overlaid with the 3D reconstruction image of the CC volumetric data, the stereoscopic information from the 3D topographic map aligns closely with the former. The extra green represents vascular details added by the interpolation algorithm, which connects with areas of signal loss in the 3D reconstruction image. There are 2 reasons for the regions where red areas fail to overlap. First, the resolution of the 3D reconstruction map is lower, making it difficult to distinguish the voids between vessels, which all appear red, leading to larger red areas. Besides, the boundaries of the CC layer in the B-scan sequences of the 3D reconstruction map were manually segmented, which may result in deviations from the automatic segmentation in the enface images. Statistical Analysis Summarized data for CC layer parameters and the choroidal large-vessel parameter are shown in Table 1 . The age distribution of the included population is presented in Figure S3 (available at www.ophthalmologyscience.org ). Table 1 Summary of Demographic Characteristics, CC Parameters, and Choroidal Large Vessel Parameter of the Study Population Healthy Healthy (Elderly) Dry AMD Wet AMD High Myopia Early AMD Intermediate AMD Advanced AMD Number of subjects (eyes) 402 (633) 163 (249) 60 (90) 55 (59) 52 (55) 64 (73) 94 (140) Age (mean) 5–92 (43) 50–92 (68) 50–92 (66) 50–86 (71) 51–88 (75) 50–92 (73) 5–83 (40) Male. No (%) 213 (53%) 79 (48%) 27 (45%) 28 (51%) 28 (54%) 34 (53%) 45 (48%) BCVA (LogMAR) 0.0 0.0 0.03 ± 0.06 0.34 ± 0.21 0.57 ± 0.46 0.54 ± 0.43 0.21 ± 0.37 SE (diopter) - - - - - - 9.97 ± 3.07 AL (mm) - - - - - - 27.80 ± 1.68 VDI (μm) 6.54 ± 0.29 6.64 ± 0.37 6.30 ± 0.44 6.52 ± 0.72 6.95 ± 1.30 7.44 ± 1.62 6.80 ± 0.54 BPI (μmˆ2) 90.10 ± 14.59 96.91 ± 13.92 115.79 ± 15.35 139.86 ± 44.76 167.61 ± 70.17 175.74 ± 110.57 113.86 ± 34.49 VLD 4.32 ± 0.29 4.12 ± 0.32 4.44 ± 0.41 4.16 ± 0.55 4.08 ± 0.86 3.83 ± 0.59 4.25 ± 0.28 VFD 1.91 ± 0.01 1.90 ± 0.01 1.91 ± 0.01 1.89 ± 0.02 1.89 ± 0.04 1.89 ± 0.02 1.90 ± 0.01 D-VLD 0.49 ± 0.27 0.64 ± 0.33 0.54 ± 0.18 0.75 ± 0.31 1.27 ± 0.94 1.48 ± 0.78 0.96 ± 0.84 D-VFD 0.003 ± 0.001 0.004 ± 0.002 0.003 ± 0.001 0.004 ± 0.002 0.007 ± 0.005 0.009 ± 0.005 0.005 ± 0.004 VD (%) 39.75 ± 3.09 37.59 ± 2.97 30.46 ± 4.23 29.20 ± 5.26 31.13 ± 8.41 38.91 ± 8.93 40.14 ± 4.69 FD (%) 34.61 ± 6.74 39.33 ± 5.84 44.24 ± 7.34 49.69 ± 8.44 48.87 ± 8.84 46.12 ± 9.02 38.30 ± 8.48 AZ/VZ 1.21 ± 0.22 1.33 ± 0.29 1.28 ± 0.33 1.31 ± 0.25 1.23 ± 0.36 1.27 ± 0.32 0.88 ± 0.26 AL = axial length; AMD = age-related macular degeneration; AZ = arterial zone; BCVA = best-corrected visual acuity; BPI = bifurcation point index; CC = choriocapillaris; D-VFD, distribution of VFD; D-VLD, distribution of VLD; LogMAR = logarithm of the minimum angle of resolution; SE = spherical equivalent; VD = vascular density; VDI = vessel diameter index; VFD = vascular fractal dimension; VLD = vascular length density; VZ = venous zone. Age-Related Changes Age-related changes in the CC are depicted in Figure 4 A. Changes include thickening of blood vessels, decreased vascular complexity, reduced uniformity of vascular distribution, and diminished blood perfusion. The changes did not correlate linearly with age for choroidal large vessels. However, when age was categorized into 4 groups (children, young adults, middle-aged adults, and older adults), the mean + standard deviation of these 4 groups were 1.242 ± 0.1574, 1.140 ± 0.2020, 1.247 ± 0.2444, and 1.243 ± 0.2552, respectively. This reflects that the AZ/VZ tended to decrease and then increase with age, with an accompanying increase in the degree of dispersion. The correlation between CC parameters and AZ/VZ of healthy eyes is shown in Figure S5 (available at www.ophthalmologyscience.org ), and there was no significant linear correlation between CC and the choroidal large vessels except for vessel thickness. The alterations of CC in different situations can also be verified in Figure 6 . The first row of the figure represents the state of a healthy CC layer; however, with age and disease, the CC layer shows changes such as thickening of blood vessels, loss of vascular distribution uniformity, and loss of vascular complexity. Figure 4 Statistical results of CC and choroidal large-vessel parameters. A, Correlation of CC and choroidal large-vessel parameters with age in healthy eyes. B, Comparative analysis of CC and choroidal large-vessel parameters among healthy eyes, dry AMD eyes, and wet AMD eyes. C, Comparative analysis of CC and choroidal large-vessel parameters between healthy eyes and high myopic eyes. AMD = age-related macular degeneration; AZ = arterial zone; BPI = bifurcation point index; CC = choriocapillaris; D-VFD = distribution of VFD; D-VLD = distribution of VLD; ns = not significant; VD = vascular density; VDI = vessel diameter index; VFD = vascular fractal dimension; VLD = vascular length density; VZ = venous zone; ∗ P < 0.05; ∗∗ P < 0.01; ∗∗∗ P < 0.001; ∗∗∗∗ P < 0.0001. Figure 6 Morphology and schematic representation of choroidal vasculature in eyes of different ages and diseases. The yellow circle in the second column shows the 1 × 1 mm area centered on the fovea, and the areas shown in the third and fourth columns are the enlargement of the area of the yellow circle. AMD = age-related macular degeneration. Changes in Patients with AMD Figure 4 B demonstrates the statistical differences in CC and choroidal large-vessel parameters among healthy eyes, eyes with dry AMD of different severity, and eyes with wet AMD within a similar age group, which is the group that was classified as the elderly healthy population group in Table 1 . For the 2 parameters reflecting vessel thickness, the VDI shows a trend of first decreasing and then increasing as AMD progresses, whereas the bifurcation point index exhibits a continuously increasing trend. This difference is due to the threshold setting of the bifurcation point index, which causes it to overlook finer bifurcation points and vessels. These 2 parameters suggest a bidirectional distribution of CC vessel diameters in dry AMD, especially in the early stage. Regarding vascular complexity and distribution uniformity, dry AMD was not much different from healthy eyes and even exhibited higher complexity and better distribution uniformity in the early dry AMD group. In contrast, wet AMD displayed clear pathological changes with lower complexity and distribution uniformity. As for vascular perfusion, dry AMD showed lower perfusion than healthy eyes, whereas wet AMD exhibited higher perfusion. However, this high perfusion in wet AMD is primarily due to macular neovascularization (MNV) in the CC layer enface image. For choroidal large vessels, there was no significant difference between groups. As illustrated in Figure S7 (available at www.ophthalmologyscience.org ), CC of eyes with AMD did not show a significant correlation with AZ/VZ except for vessel thickness. The morphology of the CC in healthy eyes changes in a unidirectional trend with age, whereas, in patients with AMD, these changes are diverse and do not show a direct unidirectional correlation with disease severity, as illustrated in Figure 8 . On the one hand, age might obscure differences in disease severity. On the other hand, when AMD progresses to a state of significant ischemia, VEGF may drive compensatory proliferation of the remaining vasculature. Figure 8 Comparison of CC morphology between (A) healthy eyes of different ages and eyes with ( B ) AMD of different severity. A1 and B1, B-scan images of the corresponding eyes passing through the fovea; ( A2 ) and ( B2 ) are the original OCTA enface images of the CC layer; ( A3 ) and ( B3 ) are 3D topographic maps constructed based on the OCTA enface images, in which the yellow dots represents vascular bifurcation points, and the blue area represents areas with drusen and geographic atrophy, the yellow box is the macular area with a size of 0.5 × 0.5 mm; ( A4 ) and ( B4 ) are the enlargements of the yellow box areas in ( A3 ) and ( B3 ), respectively. AMD = age-related macular degeneration; BCVA = best-corrected visual acuity; CC = choriocapillaris; OCTA = OCT angiography. Changes in Patients with High Myopia Figure 4 C illustrates the differences in CC and choroidal large-vessel parameters between highly myopic eyes and healthy eyes. Compared to healthy eyes, the CC in highly myopic eyes had thicker blood vessels, reduced complexity, decreased distribution uniformity, and no significant differences in blood perfusion. Additionally, highly myopic eyes had significantly lower AZ/VZ in the choroidal large vessels compared to healthy subjects, and AZ/VZ of high myopic eyes is also statistically correlated with CC parameters, as shown in Figure S9 (available at www.ophthalmologyscience.org ). To further explore the correlation between the severity of high myopia and choroidal pathological changes, we analyzed the associations between spherical equivalent (SE), axial length (AL), and the indicators of CC and choroidal large vessels. The results are shown in Figure S10 (available at www.ophthalmologyscience.org ). We found that in the partial correlation analysis controlling for AL and age, SE was significantly negatively correlated with the vessel diameter of the CC layer, and significantly positively correlated with vascular complexity and vascular distribution uniformity. In the partial correlation analysis controlling for SE and age, AL was significantly negatively correlated with AZ/VZ. Case Analysis of the Patient with Polypoidal Choroidal Vasculopathy The CC changes in the patient with polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy before and after anti-VEGF treatment were demonstrated in Figure 11 . In the indocyanine green angiography images, the polyp subsided after anti-VEGF treatment, but the associated vascular morphology was blurred and difficult to discern. In contrast, the vascular morphology of the CC layer was clearly visualized in the 3D topographic map. By superimposing the RPE-detached layer with the CC layer, it was evident that the MNV within the RPE-detached layer (in red) and the vessels in the CC layer (in green) were connected. The branching vascular network within the RPE-detached layer was part of the abnormally dilated dendritic vessels in the CC layer. The polyp (yellow arrowhead) located at the tip of the branching vascular network exhibited fine vessel-rich vascular tangles at baseline and after the first anti-VEGF treatment, which transformed into a dendritic shape as the vessels within the CC layer remodeled after 3 anti-VEGF treatments. Figure 11 Indocyanine green angiography and OCTA images of a patient with PCV at the baseline, after anti-VEGF treatment once and 3 times. The yellow boxes are all in the same area, and the yellow arrows are all pointing to the same location, which is the location of the polyp at the baseline. A1 and B1, Enlargements of the yellow boxed areas in ( A ) and ( B ), respectively, and ( C3 – E3 ) are the superimposed images of the 3D topographic maps of the yellow boxed areas of ( C1 – E1 ) and ( C2 – E2 ), respectively. BCVA = best-corrected visual acuity; ICGA = indocyanine green angiography; OCTA = OCT angiography; PCV = polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy).

[discussion] Discussion In this study, we propose a new optimization strategy for OCTA CC layer images, which can clearly present the grid-like morphology of the CC using only a single enface image. This method fully utilizes the information in a single OCTA image and compensates for the limitations in lateral resolution inherent in OCTA. While ensuring the accuracy of CC morphology, our method is simple, fast (taking <1 second), and highly applicable in clinical scenarios. This approach enables rapid statistical analysis of large sample sizes and allows for the assessment of disease severity and prognosis through clear vascular images. We observed in intermediate and advanced AMD, there was a notable increase in vessel thickness and length, and a trend toward multilayered growth in areas without drusen or geographic atrophy ( Fig 8 ). This may represent a compensatory proliferation of blood vessels driven by elevated VEGF levels to prevent the development of geographic atrophy. 32 , 33 Alternatively, it may indicate the state of MNV before it penetrates Bruch membrane, which aligns with CC changes and VEGF levels detected by Lutty et al 34 and Sohn et al 35 in donor specimens from patients with dry AMD. This observation supports the hypothesis that protective MNV secondary to geographic atrophy originates as proposed by previous investigators. Moreover, the clear CC images generated by this method are also well-suited for longitudinal follow-up in clinical treatment ( Fig 11 ). According to the theories of angiogenesis and arteriogenesis, dense capillary proliferation is driven by VEGF. In contrast, thick branch-like vessel growth is driven by platelet-derived growth factor. 32 , 36 In this case, the polyp subsided after anti-VEGF treatment, and the branching vascular network evolved to become more continuous and elongated, resembling the formation of collateral circulation. Continuous image observation reveals the process and details of vascular maturation, helping to identify the precise target and effects of anti-VEGF therapy. This aids ophthalmologists in making treatment decisions and evaluating prognosis. The health of the CC is closely related to the arteriovenous perfusion of the choroidal large vessels. 37 , 38 In this study, we proposed the AZ/VZ, a parameter based on the anatomical arteriovenous distribution pattern of the choroidal large vessels. Regarding the statistical results of AZ/VZ in healthy eyes, we speculate that the sclera is soft and elastic in children. As age progresses, scleral stiffness gradually increases. Since the sclera is thickest at the posterior pole, 39 it is most affected by scleral sclerosis, causing the arteries entering from this area to be thinner due to compression and leading to decreasing AZ/VZ. However, as age advances, these changes in scleral stiffness spread to the periphery, 40 , 41 causing an increase in AZ/VZ in middle age compared to youth. In older age, although the arterial capacity to supply blood gradually decreases, the concomitant increase in venous stasis causes only a slight, statistically insignificant decrease in AZ/VZ. We speculate that there may be a link between age-related changes in the CC and venous stasis or insufficient arterial supply of the choroidal large vessels. This speculation, however, requires more accurate quantification based on choroidal arteriovenous segmentation in future studies. There was no statistically significant difference in the AZ/VZ of AMD eyes compared to healthy eyes of the same age group. This seems to support previous studies suggesting that the pathological changes in the CC in AMD originate from the aging of the RPE layer. 4 However, as shown in Figure 6 , the large arteries in the macula of some patients with wet AMD were significantly thinner. Therefore, we believe that, although the aging of the RPE might play a crucial role, the alteration of choroidal large vessels should not be overlooked. In highly myopic patients, AZ/VZ showed a statistically significant difference. Combined with image observations, we concluded that choroidal large arteries in highly myopic eyes were significantly undersupplied, leading to insufficient perfusion of the CC layer. In this study, we proposed a new image optimization strategy and an evaluation system for choroidal capillaries, validating the analysis in both healthy and diseased eyes. However, several shortcomings remain. First, the final CC images we obtained exhibited thinner blood vessel diameters due to the influence of 3D rendering and binarization. Although this method is suitable for subsequent quantification, it is essential to note that only the statistical results derived under the same image processing strategy are reliable. Second, this study introduced only a preliminary quantitative parameter for choroidal large vessels, and its assessment of choroidal arteriovenous perfusion was quite limited. We will conduct more detailed studies on choroidal large-vessel perfusion in the future.

[conclusion] Conclusions This study explores age-related and disease-related CC changes via clear in vivo imaging and quantification. It aids understanding of CC states and may contribute to early disease diagnosis and prognosis.

retrieved_papers.title: Evaluation of retinal nerve fibre layer, ganglion cell layer and choroidal thickness with optical coherence tomography in migraine patients: a case-control study.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2018]
[Abstract] Evaluation of retinal nerve fibre layer (RNFL), ganglion cell layer (GCL) and choroidal thickness (CT) with optical coherence tomography (OCT) in chronic migraine patients, to compare with healthy controls.
Ninety-four eyes of 47 chronic migraine patients (Group 1) and 68 eyes of 34 healthy individuals (Group 2) were included in this prospective case-control study. The right and left eyes were separately evaluated. Mean peripapillary RNFL thicknesses, mean GCL measured from superior and inferior quadrants, and mean CT were measured at three different regions (central, 500 μm nasal and temporal region of the fovea).
There was no statistically significant differences in RNFL between the two groups (p > 0.05), while CT values were significantly higher and GCL values were significantly lower in chronic migraine groups (p < 0.05). There were no statistically significant differences between migraine duration, frequency and length of attacks, presence of aura, relation to menstrual cycle, white matter lesions in cranial magnetic resonance imaging and RNFL, GCL and CT (p > 0.05).
In this study, we observed chronic migraine disease does not have any effect on peripapillary RNFL thickness; however, increases in CT and decreases in GCL thickness were observed in migraine patients.

[methods] Methods This study is a hospital-based comparative, cross-sectional study conducted in the departments of ophthalmology and neurology of a tertiary care hospital in eastern India from June 2023 to July 2024. Institutional ethical committee clearance was obtained before starting the study (IEC/IMS.SH/SOA/2023/553 dated June 27, 2023). The study adhered to the basic tenets of the Helsinki Declaration. Written informed consent was obtained from each patient before enrolling them in the study. All patients aged between 18 and 50 years diagnosed with migraine as per the International Classification of Headache Disorders, third edition (ICHD-3) classification were included in the study.[ 6 7 ] Healthy age- and sex-matched individuals without any ocular and systemic abnormalities constituted the control group. Patients with the following were excluded from the study: best corrected visual acuity <20/20, N6, spherical or cylindrical errors >±2 D, history of systemic disorders like diabetes, hypertension, anemia, neurologic diseases like multiple sclerosis, Parkinsonism, Alzheimer’s, and ocular diseases like glaucoma, uveitis, significant cataract, retinal and optic nerve disorders. Patients with a past or present history of smoking, history of any previous ocular trauma or surgery, and intake of any systemic medications like isotretinoin, hydroxychloroquine, thioridazine, etc., which could affect the study parameters, were also excluded from the study. After obtaining informed written consent, a detailed history including the patient’s name, age, sex, occupation, address, presenting symptom, progression, and associated symptoms was recorded. A detailed history of the type of headache, duration of the headache, duration of each episode, and laterality was obtained from each patient. Detailed information about the severity of migraine was obtained using the headache impact test-6 (HIT-6) questionnaire.[ 14 ] Best corrected visual acuity was measured using Snellen’s chart. Ishihara’s chart was used to assess color vision. A thorough evaluation of the anterior segment was done in each case using the slit lamp. Intraocular pressure was measured using Goldmann’s applanation tonometer. Pupils of both eyes were dilated using a combination of 0.8% tropicamide and 5% phenylephrine. Posterior segment evaluation was done using an indirect ophthalmoscope and a 20-D lens. Measurement of pRNFL thickness, ONH, and macular parameters was done using the 3D disc, 3D macula, and 3D macula (V) modes of the spectral domain OCT machine (3D OCT-1 Topcon Maestro version 8.3; Topcon, Tokyo, Japan). RNFL and ONH details were obtained using the 3D disc mode having a scan length of 6.0 × 6.0 mm and a resolution of 512 × 128. Macular parameters were studied using the 3D macula (V) and 3D macula modes having a scan length of 7.0 × 7.0 and 6.0 × 6.0 mm, respectively. The resolution of both the above macular scans was 512 × 128. The RNFL parameters studied were average, superior, inferior, nasal, and temporal thicknesses. The ONH parameters included were disc area, cup area, rim area, vertical cup disc ratio, linear cup disc ratio, and rim volume. Central macular thickness (CMT), superior macular RNFL, inferior macular RNFL, superior GCL, and inferior GCL thicknesses were the various macular parameters studied. The thickness calculated in each cell was compared with a normative database that had already been fed into the machine. Values within the normal range were represented as green, values in the borderline as yellow, and values outside the normal limits were shown in red. In our study, migraine patients were divided into episodic and chronic as per the ICHD-3 criteria.[ 6 7 ] According to the HIT-6 score,[ 14 ] the severity of migraine is classified as mild (little-to-no) impact: HIT-6 score: 36–49; moderate impact: HIT-6 score: 50–59; and severe impact: HIT-6 score: 60–78. Statistical analysis All analyses were done using Statistical Package for the Social Sciences software, version 24.0. The analysis included profiling patients using different demographic and clinical parameters. For all statistical purposes, values of the right eye of both study and control groups were used. Descriptive analysis of quantitative parameters was expressed as means and standard deviation. Categorical data was expressed as absolute numbers and percentages. Independent Student’s t -test was used for testing the mean between independent groups, whereas paired Student’s t -test and analysis of variance were used to calculate statistical significance between two or more groups. Cross tables were generated, and the Chi-squared test was used to test associations. P value <0.05 was considered statistically significant.

[results] Results The present study enrolled 200 subjects (100 migraine patients and 100 healthy subjects). The mean age in migraine and control groups was 31.01 ± 8.56 and 30.80 ± 8.65 years, respectively. Most subjects were females (84% in migraine group and 82% in the control group). Of the 100 migraine patients, only three patients had migraine with aura. In 43%, 32%, and 25% of cases, mild, moderate, and severe migraine were seen, respectively. Episodic migraine was seen in 71% of cases, with chronic migraine making up the rest. The mean duration of migraine in study subjects was 6.56 ± 5.08 years. The mean duration of individual attacks was 9.33 ± 13.41 h, and the mean number of migraine attacks each month was 9.31 ± 6.57. Salient demographic details are presented in Table 1 . Table 1 Salient demographic characteristics Variable Migraine ( n =100) Control ( n =100) Age (years) 31.01±8.56 30.80±8.65 Sex Male (16), female (84) Male (18), female (82) Intraocular pressure (mmHg) 14.10±2.61 14.14±2.49 Aura Present (3), absent (97) - Severity of migraine Mild- 43 Moderate- 32 Severe- 25 - Type of migraine Episodic- 71 Chronic- 29 - Headache duration (years) 6.56±5.08 - Individual attack duration (h) 9.33±13.41 - Attack frequency (months) 9.31±6.57 - Analysis of RNFL thickness The average pRNFL thickness in migraine patients and controls was 91.62 ± 15.70 and 106.19 ± 10.46 µm, respectively [ Table 2 ]. Migraine patients had statistically significant ( P < 0.05) thinner pRNFL (average and all quadrants) compared to age- and gender-matched healthy controls. Compared to patients with episodic migraine, chronic migraine patients exhibited statistically significant ( P < 0.05) thinning in pRNFL (average and all quadrants). pRNFL (average and all quadrants) was found to be statistically thinner ( P < 0.05) in severe migraine patients compared to those with mild and moderate migraine. Compared to migraine patients with a disease duration of less than 5 years, patients with a disease duration of more than 5 years had statistically significant thinning in pRNFL (average and all quadrants). Table 2 Comparison of various study parameters between migraine patients and healthy controls Parameter Migraine Controls P Average RNFL thickness (μm) 91.62±15.70 106.19±10.46 <0.001 Superior RNFL thickness (μm) 112.22±28.08 134.30±14.82 <0.001 Inferior RNFL thickness (μm) 114.26±23.34 135.45±16.97 <0.001 Nasal RNFL thickness (μm) 73.66±19.05 81.98±15.43 0.001 Temporal RNFL thickness (μm) 65.96±11.26 73.10±10.61 <0.001 Disc area (mm 2 ) 2.45±0.98 2.14±0.46 0.004 Cup area (mm 2 ) 0.86±0.61 0.70±0.45 0.029 Rim area (mm 2 ) 1.59±0.81 1.44±0.46 0.103 Linear cup disc ratio 0.56±0.15 0.54±0.17 0.258 Vertical cup disc ratio 0.57±0.15 0.52±0.16 0.033 Rim volume (mm 3 ) 0.45±0.30 0.27±0.16 <0.001 Central macular thickness (μm) 207.67±16.92 228.09±13.58 <0.001 Macular superior RNFL (μm) 31.25±3.56 35.85±2.94 <0.001 Macular inferior RNFL (μm) 32.53±4.06 37.82±3.95 <0.001 GCL superior (μm) 67.88±5.08 68.81±3.93 0.149 GCL inferior (μm) 66.95±5.25 68.87±3.88 0.004 GCL=ganglion cell layer, RNFL=retinal nerve fiber layer Analysis of ONH parameters Disc area was 2.45 ± 0.98 mm 2 in migraine patients and 2.14 ± 0.46 mm 2 in controls. Optic disc area, cup area, vertical cup disc ratio, and rim volume were more in migraine patients compared to healthy controls, and this difference was statistically significant ( P < 0.05). All the ONH parameters, that is, optic disc area, cup area, rim area, linear and vertical cup disc ratio, and rim volume, were more in chronic migraine patients than in episodic migraine patients. However, this difference was statistically insignificant ( P > 0.05). Optic disc area, cup area, rim area, linear and vertical cup disc ratio, and rim volume were more in severe migraine patients than in mild and moderate migraine patients. However, this difference was statistically significant ( P < 0.05) only in optic disc area and rim volume. In patients with migraine for more than 5 years, there was an increase in optic disc area, cup area, rim area, linear and vertical cup disc ratio, and rim volume. However, this difference was found to be statistically insignificant ( P > 0.05). Analysis of macular parameters Mean CMT was 207.67 ± 16.92 µm min migraine patients and 228.09 ± 13.58 µm in healthy controls. This difference was found to be statistically significant ( P < 0.05). Migraine patients had statistically significant ( P < 0.05) thinner macular parameters, including superior and inferior macular RNFL thicknesses, CMT, and inferior GCL thickness, compared to age- and gender-matched healthy controls. Chronic migraine patients had statistically significant ( P < 0.05) thinning in all macular parameters compared to episodic migraine patients. Compared to patients with mild and moderate migraine, those with severe migraine exhibited statistically significant ( P < 0.05) thinner macular parameters. Similarly, all macular parameters were found to be significantly ( P < 0.05) thinner in migraine patients with a longer duration of the disease (>5 years). Tables 3 - 5 compare the various study parameters based on type, severity, and duration of migraine, respectively. [discussion] Discussion The present study was a hospital-based comparative, cross-sectional study that included 100 migraine patients and 100 healthy subjects. In our study, the mean age in the migraine group was 31.0 ± 8.56 years and in the control group was 30.80 ± 8.65 years ( P > 0.05), which was comparable to the findings reported by Simsek[ 15 ] (study group: 35.7 ± 9.5, control group: 40.9 ± 12.7 years), Gunes et al .[ 13 ] (mean age: 33.05 ± 8.83 years), and Tunç et al .[ 16 ] (migraine group: 30.1 ± 7.36 years). Female preponderance was seen in both groups. Female preponderance has been reported in most of the studies.[ 15 17 ] Females suffer from migraines at about twice the rate of men. Although both biological and psychosocial factors contribute to the sex differences in migraine, it seems that sex hormones (estrogen) are the most important. Migraine attacks are associated with fluctuating hormonal levels. High estrogen levels, high estrogen fluctuations, and hormonal replacement therapy are associated with the worst migraine outcomes.[ 18 ] Compared to the studies performed by Simsek[ 15 ] on 40 migraine patients (18- migraine with aura and 22- migraine without aura) and Kanar et al .[ 19 ] on 77 migraine patients (37- migraine with aura and 40- migraine without aura), our study had a very contrasting feature: only three patients out of 100 had migraine with aura and the rest had migraine without aura. There was no explanation for the discrepancy in the results between our study and other studies, which is a limitation of our study. Due to its simplicity and ease of use, the HIT-6 questionnaire was used to determine the severity of migraine in our study, in contrast to the Migraine disability assessment test (MIDAS) score used in the studies of Simsek[ 15 ] and Kanar et al .[ 19 ] Out of 100 migraine cases, 43% had mild, 32% had moderate, and 25% had severe migraine. Moreover, 71% of the study subjects had episodic migraine. In comparison, 29% had chronic migraine, in contrast to Reggio et al .,[ 12 ] who reported episodic and chronic migraine in 43% and 57% of their cases, respectively. Certain factors like stress, menstrual cycle changes, sleep disturbances, and alcohol trigger migraine attacks. Most likely, most of our patients had an intermittent presence of these predisposing factors, leading to a higher incidence of episodic migraine compared to chronic migraine. The mean duration of migraine in study subjects was comparable to the values reported in the studies of Simsek[ 15 ] (6.2 ± 5.6 years) and Kanar et al .[ 19 ] (8.05 ± 4.7 years). Our study’s mean duration of individual migraine attacks was 9.33 ± 13.41 h. Studies by Reggio et al .[ 12 ] (28.1 ± 22.8 h) and Sorkhabi et al .[ 20 ] (25.2 ± 31.2 h) have reported considerably higher durations of individual migraine attacks. Probable reasons for the shorter duration of the individual migraine attacks in our study can be patients presenting earlier to the neurologist, a correct diagnosis, and early institution of treatment. The higher number of patients with episodic migraine in the present study could be the reason for the lesser mean number of migraine attacks per month (9.31 ± 6.57/month). This is similar to that reported in the study of Demircan et al .[ 21 ] (8.05 ± 3.16/month), but differs from that reported by Sorkhabi et al .[ 20 ] (13.6 ± 10.2/month). Migraine is a neurovascular and progressive disorder. During a migraine attack, there is vasoconstriction of the retrobulbar artery, leading to optic nerve hypoperfusion and, eventually, ganglionic cell death. Kara et al .[ 22 ] used color Doppler imaging to demonstrate that migraine patients had greater vascular resistance in the central retinal and posterior ciliary arteries during both headache and headache-free periods. Supporting this hypothesis, our study showed statistically significant ( P < 0.05) thinner pRNFL (average and all quadrants) in migraine patients compared to age- and sex-matched healthy controls. Martinez et al .[ 11 ] found thinning in the temporal quadrant, Simsek[ 15 ] and Sorkhabi et al .[ 20 ] in the nasal quadrant, Reggio et al .[ 12 ] in the nasal, temporal, and inferior quadrants, while Gunes et al .[ 13 ] and Yülek et al .[ 23 ] found no differences. This discrepancy could be attributed to the use of different OCT machines, racial variations, and the absence of standardization in the inclusion and exclusion criteria of the participants. This study examined ONH parameters since recurring constriction of the retinal and ciliary arteries can cause ischemic damage and changes to the optic disc. We found that optic disc area, cup area, vertical cup disc ratio, and rim volume were higher in migraine patients compared to healthy controls. This difference was statistically significant ( P < 0.05). The results of our study contradicted the findings of Michael et al .,[ 17 ] Martinez et al .,[ 11 ] and Yener and Korucu.[ 24 ] They reported no difference in ONH parameters. This disparity might have arisen from including individuals with supranormal values in the migraine group, which would have affected intergroup comparisons. It can also be attributed to differences in the OCT machines used in different studies. Changes in central retinal artery circulation can alter the inner retinal layers, which may be reflected in macular thickness. In the macular region, ganglionic cells form stratified multicellular layers. Thus, loss of axons due to ischemia will lead to GCL thinning. Macular parameters like superior and inferior RNFL thicknesses, CMT, and inferior GCL thickness were lesser in migraine patients ( P < 0.05) compared to healthy controls. In contrast to the present study, Demircan et al .[ 21 ] and Hamurcu et al .[ 25 ] reported no difference in the mean macular thickness between the migraine and healthy groups. Kanar et al .[ 19 ] found superior and inferior GCL thickness reduction, while Tunç et al .[ 16 ] reported no difference in GCL measurements. CMT in our study group was comparable to that reported in the study by Cankaya and Tecellioglu[ 26 ] (211.07 ± 7.36 µm). Hamurcu et al .[ 25 ] reported no difference in RNFL thickness, CMT, or optic disc area, although the optic disc rim area was larger in the migraine group. In chronic migraine, due to more frequent attacks and resultant episodes of vasospasm and hypoperfusion, there are ischemic changes in the retina and optic nerve. These changes correlate with the number of headache episodes.[ 27 28 ] Our research findings showed that individuals with chronic migraine had a significant ( P < 0.05) decrease in pRNFL thickness (average and all quadrants) and macular parameters, including CMT, superior and inferior RNFL thicknesses, as well as superior and inferior GCL thicknesses, when compared to individuals with episodic migraine. Our findings differ from those of Gunes et al .,[ 13 ] who found no differences in RNFL values, but thinner GCL values. Like the present study, Reggio et al .[ 12 ] have reported thinner RNFL, especially in the superior and inferior sectors, and GCL in chronic migraine patients compared to patients with episodic migraine. In contrast to our findings, a study by Kirbas et al .[ 29 ] reported thinning in superior quadrant RNFL thickness only (no difference in other quadrants RNFL and macular parameters) in patients with chronic migraine (CM). Since migraine is a chronic condition with varying severity, the duration and intensity of the disease may have a degrading effect on RNFL and GCL. The study’s findings revealed that migraine patients with a duration of disease >5 years exhibited thinning in pRNFL and macular parameters. Specifically, all quadrants and average values of pRNFL, as well as superior and inferior GCL thicknesses, superior and inferior macular RNFL thicknesses, and CMT showed statistically significant differences between patients with duration <5 years and >5 years ( P < 0.05). However, the ONH parameters did not show significant changes. The study suggests that as the duration of migraine increases, there is a greater extent of vasoconstriction, impacting blood flow to the choroid and retina. Several studies have reported similar results. Gunes et al .[ 13 ] found an inverse correlation between headache duration and superior and temporal RNFL, Yülek et al .[ 23 ] found an inverse correlation between duration and average RNFL thickness, and Abdellatif and Fouad[ 30 ] found an inverse correlation of GCL thickness with duration. However, the findings of Gipponi et al .,[ 31 ] Kirbas et al .[ 29 ] (no correlation with RNFL), and Cankaya and Tecellioglu[ 26 ] (no correlation with CMT) contradicted our study. Our study found an inverse correlation of severity with pRNFL thickness (average and all quadrants) and macular parameters (CMT, superior and inferior GCL, and superior and inferior RNFL). Similar to our study, Kanar et al .[ 19 ] and Abdellatif and Fouad[ 30 ] found that severity (MIDAS score) was inversely correlated with superior and inferior GCL; the former found a significant inverse correlation with average, superior, and nasal RNFL thicknesses and the latter found a significant inverse correlation with inferior and temporal RNFL thicknesses only. The present study has a few limitations. The study was limited to a specific geographic area, and the results cannot be generalized to migraine patients from other areas. Since the study was conducted in an urban setting at a tertiary institute, the study population might not reflect true representatives of the general population of rural areas. OCT angiography could have helped in supporting our hypothesis of chronic ischemia in migraine being responsible for changes in retinal and ONH parameters. However, choroidal thickness and assessment of retinal vascular plexuses could not be done because of the absence of the requisite technology in our center. We also did not perform visual field analysis in our subjects, the addition of which could have further increased the value of the study. The study is cross sectional in nature; the effect of disease progression on the retinal parameters could not be assessed.

[conclusion] Conclusion The present study reports a significant variation in pRNFL, ONH, and macular parameters in migraine patients compared to age- and gender-matched healthy individuals. Patients with a history of longer duration (>5 years) of migraine had significantly thinner pRNFL. Based on our findings, we recommend careful examination of retinal and ONH parameters in migraine patients at regular intervals to avoid any future complications. Conflicts of interest There are no conflicts of interest.

retrieved_papers.title: Influence of sex, menstrual cycle, and oral contraceptives on cerebrovascular resistance and cardiorespiratory function during Valsalva or standing.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2017]
[Abstract] Women experience orthostatic intolerance more than men, and they experience faintness more in the early follicular [i.e., low-hormone (LH)] than luteal [i.e., high-hormone (HH)] phase of the menstrual cycle. Men (n = 13, 25.8 ± 1.8 yr old) and women in the LH (days 2-5; placebo) and HH (days 18-24; high dose) phases of the menstrual cycle with (OC; n = 14, 22.0 ± 0.8 yr old) or without (NOC; n = 12, 21.8 ± 0.5 yr old) oral contraceptive (OC) use underwent the Valsalva maneuver and a supine-sit-stand protocol. Blood pressure, normalized stroke volume [stroke volume index (SVi)], cardiac output index, heart rate, end-tidal CO2, and middle cerebral artery (MCA) blood flow velocity were measured. When subjected to the Valsalva maneuver, all women had a greater increase in diastolic and mean MCA blood flow velocity than men (P ≤ 0.065), with no significant effect of menstrual cycle phase or OC use. When subjected to the supine-sit-stand protocol, men had lower MCA blood flow velocity (P < 0.038) than all women, and SVi was higher in men than in the NOC group in all postures (P < 0.011) and in the OC group in the LH phase of the menstrual cycle during standing (P = 0.010). Only men experienced higher resistance index (P < 0.001) and pulsatility index (P < 0.001) with standing. The OC group had lower end-tidal CO2 (P = 0.002) than the NOC group (P = 0.030) and men (P ≤ 0.067). SVi (P = 0.004) and cardiac output index (P = 0.008) were higher in the OC than NOC group. A tendency toward a lower mean MCA blood flow velocity (P = 0.058) and higher SVi (P = 0.059) and pulsatility index (P = 0.058) was noted in the HH than LH phase. Mean arterial pressure was higher in the OC than NOC group in the LH phase (P = 0.049) and lower in the HH than LH phase (P = 0.014). Our results indicate that cycling estrogens/progestins can influence ventilatory, cardiovascular, and/or cerebrovascular physiology.NEW & NOTEWORTHY We have found sex differences in the cerebrovascular response to the Valsalva maneuver and standing. Men have greater cerebral vasoconstriction (or women have greater cerebral vasodilation) during late phase II of the Valsalva maneuver, and the cerebrovascular resistance index increases in men, but not in women, during standing. Furthermore, our findings indicate that both the menstrual cycle phase and oral contraceptive use can influence cardiovascular function both at rest and during active standing.

retrieved_papers.title: Assessment of optic disc morphological characteristics and related factors of highly myopic eyes in Chinese school-aged children.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2024]
[Abstract] The morphological characteristics of the optic nerve head (ONH) in myopic eyes are a clinically significant issue, especially for high myopia in school-aged children, and this can be monitored using optical coherence tomography.
The purpose of this study is to investigate the morphological characteristics of ONH, and the factors associated with peripapillary choroidal thickness in Chinese school-aged high myopia children.
A total of 48 patients, possessing 48 high myopia eyes and 48 contralateral low myopia eyes were enrolled. The ONH characteristic parameters, including peripapillary retinal nerve fibre layer thickness, peripapillary choroidal thickness, peripapillary choroidal blood flow density, Bruch's membrane opening (BMO) characteristic parameters were measured on optical coherence tomography scans.
Eyes with high myopia had a larger disc size, higher peripapillary atrophy area proportion, larger peripapillary atrophy area, larger BMO minimum rim width, lower peripapillary choroidal thickness compared with those contralateral low myopia eyes (all P < 0.001). The BMO distance and border length were longer, and border tissue angle was smaller in the high myopia eyes. The multivariate regression analysis revealed that border length, axial length, and border tissue angle were independently associated with peripapillary choroidal thickness (all P < 0.05); axial length was associated with peripapillary retinal nerve fibre layer thickness (P = 0.007).
The peripapillary atrophy area, BMO area, border length, BMO distance, and BMO minimum rim width increased, but peripapillary choroidal thickness, retinal nerve fibre layer thickness decreased with axial elongation of the globe in young myopia children. Longer axial length and border length were positively correlated with lower peripapillary choroidal thickness, and a smaller border tissue angle was positively correlated with lower peripapillary choroidal thickness were found in this study. Monitoring of border length and border tissue angle is essential in the early stages of myopia in children.

retrieved_papers.title: OCTA-based evaluation of the correlation between macular retinal and choroidal structural characteristics and refractive status in patients with retinopathy of prematurity.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] To investigate the correlation between macular retinal and choroidal structural characteristics and refractive status in preschool-aged children with retinopathy of prematurity (ROP), based on optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA), and to analyze the relationship between serum vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) levels and microcirculatory alterations.
310 preterm children with a history of ROP who were followed up at our hospital between January 2022 and June 2024 were included. According to the International Classification of Retinopathy of Prematurity (ICROP), participants were categorized into mild (n = 150, stage 1-2 ROP not meeting treatment criteria), moderate (n = 150, pre-threshold stage 3 ROP requiring treatment), and severe (n = 10, stage 3 threshold or above, or cases with retinal detachment) groups. Foveal avascular zone (FAZ) area, vessel densities of the superficial capillary plexus (SCP) and deep capillary plexus (DCP), choroidal thickness, number of vascular branches, vascular tortuosity, and foveal thickness were evaluated. Refractive parameters and VEGF levels were measured. The correlation between OCTA parameters, refractive parameters, and serum VEGF levels was analyzed.
The myopia rate in the severe group (80.0%) was higher than that in the moderate (52.0%) and mild (18.7%) groups (χ² = 68.45, P < 0.001). The spherical equivalent (SE) was (-2.35 ± 2.75) D, (-0.85 ± 2.15) D, and (+0.75 ± 1.25) D in the severe, moderate, and mild groups, respectively (F = 45.32, P < 0.001). The FAZ area was negatively correlated with the SE (r = -0.659, P < 0.001) and positively correlated with the axial length (r = 0.653, P < 0.001). The SCP vessel density was positively correlated with the SE (r = 0.618, P < 0.001). The choroidal thickness was positively correlated with the SE (r = 0.655, P < 0.001). Multivariable logistic regression analysis showed that a history of severe ROP (OR = 3.85, 95% CI: 2.52-5.86), SCP vessel density <40% (OR = 2.58, 95% CI: 1.78-3.76), FAZ area >0.35 mm² (OR = 2.24, 95% CI: 1.55-3.25), and choroidal thickness <250 μm (OR = 2.32, 95% CI: 1.62-3.36) were associated with the development of myopia. Serum VEGF level was positively correlated with FAZ area (r = 0.375, P < 0.001) and negatively correlated with SCP vessel density (r = -0.419, P < 0.001).
Increased FAZ area, decreased SCP vessel density, and decreased choroidal thickness were associated with the development of myopia. Serum VEGF levels were correlated with macular microcirculatory parameters.

retrieved_papers.title: Pregnancy and the Eye.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2015]
[Abstract] Pregnancy causes significant changes in all systems of the body. Although most of them are physiological, they may also lead to pathological consequences. The resulting pathological changes may occur for the first time or existing diseases affected by pregnancy can become more serious or change course. Diseases specific only to pregnancy may arise. Like all systems of the body, the visual system is also affected by pregnancy, developing a wide range of physiological and pathological changes. Knowing the ocular physiological changes and diagnosing eye diseases that may develop during pregnancy, and preventing and treating these diseases is crucial to ensure the baby's healthy development. Therefore, we have reviewed the conditions that an ophthalmologist should recognize, follow-up, and pay attention to during treatment and summarized them under the topic "pregnancy and the eye".

[Introduction] INTRODUCTION Pregnancy causes major changes in all systems of the body. Physiological changes protect the fetus, support development, and also prepare the mother for birth. These changes affect the cardiovascular, renal, pulmonary, endocrine, metabolic, hematologic and visual systems. 1 For example, in early pregnancy cardiac output and blood volume increase by 30-50%. Decreased fibrinolytic activity and increases in plasminogen, fibrinogen and factors I, V, VII, IX and X result in a predisposition toward coagulation. Toward the end of pregnancy, extracellular fluid increases by up to two liters. Cellular immunity decreases, but there are no changes in immunoglobins. 2 Ocular changes during pregnancy are categorized as physiological or pathological. Pregnancy-related pathological changes may present as new ocular developments, changes in existing ocular pathology, and ocular complications of systemic diseases. 3 , 4 This article examines pregnancy-related physiological and pathological changes in the eye and visual system; the diagnosis, monitoring and treatment of these changes; and issues to be considered.

[introduction] INTRODUCTION Pregnancy causes major changes in all systems of the body. Physiological changes protect the fetus, support development, and also prepare the mother for birth. These changes affect the cardiovascular, renal, pulmonary, endocrine, metabolic, hematologic and visual systems. 1 For example, in early pregnancy cardiac output and blood volume increase by 30-50%. Decreased fibrinolytic activity and increases in plasminogen, fibrinogen and factors I, V, VII, IX and X result in a predisposition toward coagulation. Toward the end of pregnancy, extracellular fluid increases by up to two liters. Cellular immunity decreases, but there are no changes in immunoglobins. 2 Ocular changes during pregnancy are categorized as physiological or pathological. Pregnancy-related pathological changes may present as new ocular developments, changes in existing ocular pathology, and ocular complications of systemic diseases. 3 , 4 This article examines pregnancy-related physiological and pathological changes in the eye and visual system; the diagnosis, monitoring and treatment of these changes; and issues to be considered.

[conclusion] CONCLUSION Visual complaints are a common occurrence in pregnant women. It is extremely important that ophthalmologists are aware of the various physiological and pathological conditions that may arise or be altered during pregnancy, and are knowledgeable about their differential diagnosis, treatment and monitoring. Especially rare and serious complications may include visual symptoms and pregnant patients may initially be seen by an ophthalmologist. Responding carefully, rapidly and strategically to patients with these complaints can prevent possible risks to mother and baby.

retrieved_papers.title: Oral contraceptive and hormone replacement therapy in women with cerebral aneurysms.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2011]
[Abstract] It is well known that cerebral aneurysms occur more frequently in women, with numerous studies suggesting a role for hormones in aneurysm pathogenesis. Estrogen promotes normal physiologic vascular endothelial function but also fluctuates during the menstrual cycle and drops significantly at menopause.
A retrospective, case control study was conducted to determine if exogenous estrogen use, which stabilizes estradiol levels, had any association with the presence of cerebral aneurysms. 60 women with intradural cerebral aneurysms were interviewed about their basic medical and female reproductive health histories, including oral contraceptive pill and hormone replacement therapy use and duration of use. This information was compared with the same data collected from women in the general public, as represented by 4682 women contacted through random digit phone dialing in the National Institute of Child Health and Human Development sponsored Contraceptive and Reproductive Experiences Study, published in 2002.
Multivariate logistic regression showed a significant association between a lower rate of oral contraceptive (OR 2.1, CI 1.17 to 3.81; p=0.01) and hormone replacement therapy (OR 3.09, CI 1.54 to 6.22; p=0.002) use and the presence of a cerebral aneurysm.
These data suggest that exposure to exogenous estrogen agents in women is associated with a lower frequency of cerebral aneurysms.

[Introduction] Introduction Approximately 3% of individuals in the general population have an unruptured intracranial aneurysm (UIA) [ 1 ]. Rupture of a UIA causes aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage (aSAH), a devastating subtype of stroke, with approximately one-third of patients dying and one-third remaining dependent on help of others [ 2 ]. Women are overrepresented in both the UIA and aSAH populations, with an overall two-thirds of patients being female [ 1 , 3 ]. The reason women are more prone to develop UIA and aSAH is unknown. However, understanding this predisposition is of critical importance for improving preventative and treatment strategies. While research on risk factors for the formation of UIAs is less extensive than for aSAH, a positive familial history of UIA, hypertension, cigarette smoking, and alcohol consumption have been associated with UIAs [ 3 ]. Sex differences in the strength of risk factor associations with UIA might contribute to the higher burden of UIA in women. Indeed, sex differences in risk factor associations have been reported for aSAH, where the association between smoking and aSAH is stronger in women than in men [ 4 ]. We confirmed this finding in a recent systematic review and meta-analysis of risk factors for aSAH, which we conducted concurrently with the present study on UIA [ 5 ]. It is unknown whether similar sex differences exist for UIAs. Therefore, we performed a systematic review of clinical risk factors for UIAs, with a specific aim to assess whether these risk factor associations are sex-dependent.

[introduction] Introduction Approximately 3% of individuals in the general population have an unruptured intracranial aneurysm (UIA) [ 1 ]. Rupture of a UIA causes aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage (aSAH), a devastating subtype of stroke, with approximately one-third of patients dying and one-third remaining dependent on help of others [ 2 ]. Women are overrepresented in both the UIA and aSAH populations, with an overall two-thirds of patients being female [ 1 , 3 ]. The reason women are more prone to develop UIA and aSAH is unknown. However, understanding this predisposition is of critical importance for improving preventative and treatment strategies. While research on risk factors for the formation of UIAs is less extensive than for aSAH, a positive familial history of UIA, hypertension, cigarette smoking, and alcohol consumption have been associated with UIAs [ 3 ]. Sex differences in the strength of risk factor associations with UIA might contribute to the higher burden of UIA in women. Indeed, sex differences in risk factor associations have been reported for aSAH, where the association between smoking and aSAH is stronger in women than in men [ 4 ]. We confirmed this finding in a recent systematic review and meta-analysis of risk factors for aSAH, which we conducted concurrently with the present study on UIA [ 5 ]. It is unknown whether similar sex differences exist for UIAs. Therefore, we performed a systematic review of clinical risk factors for UIAs, with a specific aim to assess whether these risk factor associations are sex-dependent.

[methods] Methods We systematically searched the electronic medical databases of PubMed, Embase, Emcare, Web of Science, and the Cochrane Library to identify all relevant literature from inception to March 2024. Since we conducted the systematic reviews of UIA and aSAH simultaneously, we combined search terms for both into one search string. Cohort or case-control studies were included if they reported on risk factors associated with the formation of saccular UIAs in an adult population. Selected risk factors included smoking, alcohol use, hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, physical activity, diabetes, and BMI and were preselected based on a previous 2005 meta-analysis [ 6 ]. UIA diagnosis had to be confirmed by neuroimaging, autopsy or International Classification of Diseases (ICD) codes. Two review authors independently assessed study eligibility and quality using an adapted version of the Newcastle-Ottawa Scale. Any disagreements were resolved by a third review author.

[results] Results A total of 21 studies met the inclusion criteria. A summary of study characteristics is shown in Table 1 . Eighteen of these studies included both women and men. Overall, a total of 347,907 participants and 8,698 UIA cases were included, of whom 178,417 (51%) participants were women and 5,773 (66%) of UIAs occurred in women. The overall quality of the included studies varied: two out of 21 were rated as low risk of bias across all domains, two had high or possible risk in one domain, and the remaining 17 had high or possible risk in two or more of the four domains. Among the 18 studies that included both sexes, 44% reported findings adjusted for sex in multivariable analyses. However, only one study (<6%) stratified its results by sex, making sex-stratified analyses not possible (shown in Fig. 1 ) [ 7 ]. This one study found similar associations of hypertension and diabetes with UIA in women and men. Table 1. Characteristics of included studies Author, year of publication and country Study design Size of the study population, n (% women) UIA cases, n (% women) Sex-adjusted a Sex-stratified b Horikoshi, Japan [ 8 ] (2002) Case-control 381 (unclear) 127 (66%) Yes No Inagawa, Japan [ 7 ] (2010) Case-control 1,064 (65%) 266 (65%) No Yes Chen, USA [ 9 ] (2011) Case-control 459 (100%) 39 (100%) No No Vlak, The Netherlands [ 10 ] (2013) Case-control 780 (69%) 206 (67%) No No Li, China [ 11 ] (2013) Cross-sectional 4,813 (51%) 366 (56%) No No Li, China [ 12 ] (2014) Cross-sectional 3,993 (46%) 350 (57%) No No Matsukawa, China [ 13 ] (2014) Case-control 132 (67%) 66 (67%) No No Zhang, China [ 14 ] (2015) Case-control 294 (46%) 37 (49%) No No Kang, South-Korea [ 15 ] (2015) Cross-sectional 18,954 (39%) 367 (51%) Yes No Kim, Korea [ 16 ] (2016) Prospective cohort study 131,999 (41%) 491 (59%) No No Atchaneeyasakul, USA [ 17 ] (2018) Case-control 486 (53%) 243 (71%) Yes No Imaizumi, Japan [ 18 ] (2018) Cross-sectional 4,070 (42%) 176 (60%) Yes No Yoon, South-Korea [ 19 ] (2019) Case-control 238 (32%) 25 (36%) Yes No Müller, Norway [ 20 ] (2019) Prospective cohort study 83,710 (53%) 92 (75%) Yes No Cras, The Netherlands [ 21 ] (2020) Cross-sectional 5,841 (55%) 134 (68%) Yes No Ogilvy, USA (single center) [ 22 ] (2020) Case-control 194 (100%) 64 (100%) No No Ogilvy, USA and Canada (multicenter) [ 23 ] (2020) Case-control 226 (100%) 113 (100%) No No Kim, Korea [ 24 ] (2021) Cross-sectional 2,118 (44%) 80 (55%) No No Igase, Japan [ 25 ] (2021) Cross-sectional 1,376 (59%) 79 (70%) Yes No Räisänen, Finland [ 26 ] (2022) Retrospective cohort study 1,419 (50%) 42 (50%) No No Park, Korea [ 27 ] (2023) Case-control 85,360 (68%) 5,335 (68%) No No UIA, unruptured intracranial aneurysm. a Whether the study adjusted for sex in multivariable analyses. b Whether the study provided their findings stratified by sex. Fig. 1. Pie charts displaying the availability of sex-stratified data for number of studies ( a ), number of patients ( b ), and number of UIA cases ( c ). UIA, unruptured intracranial aneurysm.

[discussion] Discussion The importance of considering sex in health and medical research has been increasingly recognized [ 28 ]. Yet, as our review shows, many studies still fail to report their results by sex, even when the data are available. Stratification by sex in less than 6% of studies on risk factors for UIA is exceptionally low compared to other fields of cardiovascular medicine, where sex-stratified data are reported in up to 36% of studies [ 29 , 30 ]. This underreporting may reflect a persisting lack of awareness or prioritization of incorporating sex as a relevant variable in reporting research results. Another contributing factor may be that many scientific journals still do not require sex-specific analyses in their publication guidelines, which could reinforce this underrecognition. Additionally, a commonly expressed concern is that sex-stratified analyses might be underpowered to yield meaningful results. While a single study might indeed not be sufficient to reach definitive conclusions, the publication of sex-stratified results would still facilitate future meta-analyses, such as the one we intended to perform here. Collectively, such studies could help increase our understanding of sex differences in UIA and unveil reasons for the female predominance. To address these challenges, funders and editorial policies should actively encourage the incorporation of sex-specific results. Additionally, special issues and themed editions, such as the Go Red for Women edition in Stroke , can provide positive reinforcement and raise awareness of this topic. In summary, we highlight the need for the reporting of sex-stratified results in the field of UIA research.

[conclusion] Conclusion Our findings illustrate the scarcity of sex-stratified results in studies on risk factors for UIAs. Since knowledge on potential sex differences in UIA could help understand the female predominance, we highlight a need for more sex-stratified research in this field.

retrieved_papers.title: Assessing outer retinal and choroidal changes in scd: potential for high-risk screening using SS-OCTA.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] Subjective cognitive decline (SCD) serves as an initial symptom of preclinical Alzheimer's disease (AD). The accumulation of amyloid-beta (Aβ) is acknowledged as a critical risk factor for the eventual progression to mild cognitive impairment or dementia in individuals with SCD, highlighting the necessity for early detection and intervention. Previous studies have identified the retina and choriocapillaris as potential biomarkers for AD; however, these investigations have not thoroughly examined large and medium-sized choroidal vessels. Ultra-wide swept-source optical coherence tomography angiography (SS-OCTA), an innovative noninvasive imaging modality, facilitates rapid and precise quantitative assessment of retinal and choroidal boundaries and vasculature through dynamic scanning, encompassing large and medium-sized choroidal vessels. This study aims to characterize the outer retinal and choroidal vasculature and structure in individuals with SCD, examine the correlation between altered choroidal vasculature parameters and amyloid burden, and the presence of the apolipoprotein E (APOE) ε4 allele in SCD participants, to identify potential ocular biomarkers for high-risk SCD screening. In this study, 57 individuals with SCD and 45 matched normal controls were enrolled. Ultra-wide SS-OCTA was employed to assess the thickness of the outer retina and choroid and the blood flow within the choriocapillaris and large, medium-sized choroidal vessels. 18F-Florbetapir positron emission tomography scans were performed to classify amyloid-positive-SCD (Aβ + SCD) and amyloid-negative-SCD (Aβ-SCD) groups. Plasma Aβ42/40 and APOE ε4 genotypes were also measured. Compared with normal controls, individuals with SCD exhibited a significant increase in the choroidal vessel index and a reduction in outer retinal thickness. The Aβ + SCD group demonstrated an elevated choriocapillaris flow area relative to the Aβ- SCD group. Moreover, a negative correlation was observed between the choriocapillaris flow area and plasma Aβ42/40 levels in the SCD cohort. Among SCD participants, APOE ε4 carriers displayed increased choriocapillaris flow area and choroidal vessel volume compared to APOE ε4 non-carriers. Our findings provide intriguing insights into the relationship between amyloid pathology and changes in the choriocapillaris flow area. The choroid may serve as a potential biomarker for screening Aβ + SCD.

retrieved_papers.title: Impaired brachial flow-mediated dilation may predict choroidal and retinal nerve fibre layer thickness changes in people with obesity.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] Choroidal thickness and peripapillary retinal nerve fibre layer (RNFL) changes are known to occur in obesity. Endothelial dysfunction and systemic atherosclerosis may play a role in the pathophysiology of these differences.
Flow-mediated dilation (FMD) is reduced in patients with endothelial dysfunction, and the ankle-brachial index is a surrogate marker for atherosclerosis. This study was conducted to examine the relationship between systemic vascular parameters (FMD, and ankle-brachial index), subfoveal choroidal thickness, and peripapillary RNFL thickness in obese individuals.
This observational, cross-sectional study involved 108 total participants who were divided into two groups. One group consisted of 54 obese subjects who each had a body mass index of 30 kg/m2 or more. The other control group contained 54 participants who each had a body mass index of 25 kg/m2 or less but higher than 20 kg/m2 . For each participant, only one eye was examined in this study. Subfoveal choroidal thickness, RNFL thickness, ankle-brachial index, and ultrasound measurement of the brachial artery FMD were performed. FMD was categorised according to receiver operating characteristic analysis, and endothelial dysfunction was defined as an FMD ≤ 7.29%.
Subfoveal choroidal and RNFL thicknesses in the temporal quadrant were significantly lower in the obese group (p < 0.05). Lower mean values of subfoveal choroidal and RNFL thicknesses in the temporal and inferior quadrants were observed in subjects with an FMD ≤ 7.29% (p < 0.05). In people with obesity, FMD was positively correlated with subfoveal choroidal thickness (r = 0.322, p = 0.001), inferior RNFL thickness (r = 0.259, p = 0.007), and temporal RNFL thickness (r = 0.297, p = 0.002). However, the ankle-brachial index was not correlated with obesity.
Impaired FMD was associated with reduced subfoveal choroidal and RNFL thicknesses in the temporal and inferior quadrants of people with obesity.

retrieved_papers.title: Retinal vascular alterations as assessment indicators of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease risk in dyslipidemia patients.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] Atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) remains the leading cause of global mortality, particularly among individuals with dyslipidemia. Traditionally, the retina has been considered a key site for examining microvascular changes. Recent evidence, however, indicates that retinal alterations may also reflect macrovascular changes. This study proposes a hypothesis in which Optical Coherence Tomography Angiography (OCTA) is utilized to evaluate retinal vascular changes as a potential biomarker for ASCVD risk assessment in dyslipidemia patients.
In this cross-sectional study, 261 dyslipidemia patients were recruited and classified into non-ASCVD and ASCVD groups. OCTA was performed on all patients, with the macula and optic disc being the primary areas of assessment. The following parameters were measured: retinal vessel density (VD), retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) thickness, retinal thickness, foveal avascular zone (FAZ) area, FAZ perimeter, and VD within a 300 μm width ring surrounding the FAZ (FD). Ultimately, data from 231 eyes were analyzed. Comparisons of OCTA-derived metrics between groups were made, and receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve analysis was conducted to assess the discriminatory power of these metrics for identifying ASCVD in dyslipidemia patients. The DeLong test was used to compare areas under the ROC curve for these indicators. All statistical tests were two-tailed, with significance set at P < 0.05.
In the ASCVD group, RNFL thickness, superficial capillary plexus (SCP) parafoveal VD, SCP perifoveal VD, macular parafoveal thickness, and FD were significantly lower compared to the non-ASCVD group. ROC curve analysis confirmed the predictive value of these indicators for ASCVD identification in dyslipidemia patients. SCP parafoveal VD, SCP perifoveal VD, and FD correspond to the macular superficial capillary plexus vessel densities. When combined, these indicators formed a new composite measure, macular superficial vessel density (MSVD). The ROC curve further validated MSVD's predictive utility for ASCVD in dyslipidemia patients, with the optimal threshold identified at 143.22% using the Youden index.
OCTA-derived indicators, particularly MSVD, demonstrate significant potential as novel biomarkers for ASCVD risk assessment in dyslipidemia patients.

[Introduction] 1 Introduction Atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD), including coronary atherosclerotic heart disease (CHD), ischemic stroke, and peripheral arterial disease, remains the leading global cause of mortality ( 1 – 3 ). CHD can result in myocardial infarction and sudden cardiac death, ischemic stroke can lead to aphasia, prolonged immobility, and secondary cerebral hemorrhage, and peripheral arterial disease in the lower extremities may cause impaired mobility or necessitate amputation ( 4 – 7 ). These conditions severely impair patients’ quality of life, impose significant economic burdens, and increase mortality rates. Accurate risk stratification and early identification of individuals at high risk are essential to prevent disease initiation, mitigate progression, and reduce associated mortality. The pathophysiological hallmark of ASCVD is the formation and advancement of atherosclerotic plaques, with dyslipidemia, particularly elevated low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C), recognized as a principal risk factor ( 1 – 3 ). Evaluating ASCVD risk in individuals with dyslipidemia, predominantly characterized by elevated LDL-C levels, is crucial for implementing targeted interventions and enhancing clinical outcomes. Previous ASCVD risk assessment tools have been developed ( 8 – 11 ), but they do not specifically cater to the needs of high-risk individuals with dyslipidemia. The predictive variables used, such as blood pressure, lipid levels, and waist circumference, exhibit considerable fluctuation. This highlights the importance of identifying stable, reliable biomarkers for risk evaluation. The retina, recognized as a window into microvascular health, has gained attention as a potential indicator for coronary artery alterations. Recent studies reveal significant differences in retinal vascular features between CHD patients and controls, with these differences correlating with the extent of coronary artery stenosis ( 12 , 13 ). This evidence suggests an association between retinal microvascular changes and macrovascular conditions. Thus, the current study investigates the feasibility of assessing ASCVD risk in individuals with dyslipidemia by analyzing retinal vascular characteristics. Optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA) represents an advanced, non-invasive imaging technique that enables rapid and quantitative evaluation of retinal vascularity ( 14 ). Compared to traditional methods, such as color fundus photography and fundus fluorescein angiography, OCTA offers clear advantages. The former lacks quantitative capabilities, while the latter necessitates the injection of a contrast agent, making it invasive.
