[methods] 2 Materials and Methods 2.1 Participants A priori sample size calculations conducted using G*Power 3.1 (Faul et al. 2009 ) determined that a total sample size of 34 participants is required to achieve a power of 0.8 at a significance level of α = 0.05 for a repeated‐measures analysis of variance (ANOVA) design with two groups, two measurements (LFP and PMP), given medium effect sizes of f = 0.25. Hence, 65 women with normal menstrual cycles, including 36 MRM patients and 29 healthy controls (HCs), were recruited between April 2019 and August 2022. The flowchart of participant recruitment in this study was illustrated in Figure 1 . The inclusion criteria were as follows: (1) age between 18 and 50 years; (2) not and not having become pregnant; (3) self‐reported regular menstrual cycles, that is, ranging from 21 to 35 days, without self‐report of irregular cycles; and (4) being right‐handed. The exclusion criteria were: (1) history of neurological/psychiatric diseases or severe head trauma and/or drug abuse; (2) abnormal anatomical MRI data; and (3) time intervals between two fMRI acquisitions exceeding 2 months. FIGURE 1 Flowchart of participant recruitment, retention, and quality control procedures. A total of 36 MRM patients, who had not received preventive treatment for migraine, were initially recruited from the Department of Neurology at the First Affiliated Hospital of Henan University of Science and Technology (HAUST). The diagnosis of MRM was based on the self‐reported data, according to the ICHD‐3 (Olesen 2018 ), included (i) fulfilling the criteria for migraine without aura; (ii) the attacks occur on 1 ± 2 days of the menstrual phase (within the PMP) in at least two out of three menstrual cycles, attacks additionally occur during other time intervals of the menstrual cycle. One MRM patient was excluded due to a history of head trauma identified. Eight patients were excluded for not completing both the fMRI sessions during the PMP and the LFP. Among the 27 MRM patients who completed both MRI sessions, five were further excluded due to extended time intervals (> 2 months) between the two fMRI scans. This resulted in a sample of 22 participants in the MRM group. In the HC group, 29 age‐match female participants were recruited. Two were excluded for not completing both fMRI sessions, and one was excluded due to excessive head motion during fMRI scanning, resulting in a final sample of 26 participants. All participants provided written informed consent, and the study was approved by the Ethics Committee of the First Affiliated Hospital and College of Clinical Medicine of HAUST (Approve No. K‐2025‐0122). 2.2 General Experimental Procedure As illustrated in Figure 2A , each participant was invited to visit the Imaging Center of the First Affiliated Hospital of HAUST between 7:00 pm and 8:00 pm for two separate sessions: one is LFP session (approximately 14 ± 1–2 days prior to the next menstruation, characterized by elevated sex hormone levels) and the other is the PMP session (approximately 1 ± 2 days of the menstruation, indicating low sex hormone levels). The PMP was chosen as the study period due to the fact that migraine attacks are most likely to occur within this time window for patients with MRM (Olesen 2018 ). The LFP was selected for testing because it coincides with the peak of estradiol levels that can be measured in this phase (Borsook et al. 2014 ). FIGURE 2 General experiment procedure and hormone fluctuations during the menstrual cycle. (A) Each participant was invited to collect the blood samples and MRI images in the LFP and the PMP. (B) Changes in progesterone levels between the LFP and PMP. (C) Estradiol levels were lower in the PMP than in the LFP, and (D) the decrease in estradiol levels was significantly related to disease duration in the MRM group. Dots indicate individual data for each group. HC = healthy controls; LFP = late‐follicular phase; MRM = menstrual‐related migraine; PMP = perimenstrual phase. For the MRM group, participants were instructed to complete the PMP session on the same day that they reported a migraine attack. This session was intended to capture around the ictal phase. MRM patients rated their present pain intensity (PPI) index on a 0–5 intensity scale, with 0 = none, 1 = mild, 2 = discomforting, 3 = distressing, 4 = horrible, and 5 = excruciating (Dworkin et al. 2009 ). The LFP session was scheduled only after at least three consecutive days without any self‐reported migraine attacks, corresponding to the interictal phase. Venous blood samples were also collected during both the LFP and PMP sessions. Serum progesterone and estradiol concentrations were measured using the IMMULITE 2000 XPi Immunoassay system (Siemens Healthcare Diagnostics Inc., Germany). Subsequently, the participants underwent an fMRI scan. 2.3 MRI Data Acquisition During the LFP and PMP sessions, MRI data were acquired using a 20‐channel head coil Siemens 3 T MRI scanner. Resting‐state fMRI data with a total of 197 volumes were acquired using a T2*‐weighted echo‐planar imaging (EPI) sequence with the following parameters: repetition time (TR) = 2000 ms, echo time (TE) = 30 ms, flip angle = 90°, field of view (FOV) = 220 × 220 mm 2 , matrix = 64 × 64, slice thickness = 3.5 mm, and number of slices = 37. Individual T1‐weighted structural MRI data were also acquired with the following parameters: TR = 2300 ms, TE = 2.27 ms, flip angle = 9°, FOV = 250 × 250 mm 2 , matrix = 256 × 256, slice thickness = 1 mm, and number of slices = 192. 2.4 MRI Preprocessing The fMRI data were preprocessed using SPM12 ( https://www.fil.ion.ucl.ac.uk/spm/ ) running in MATLAB 2023a. For each participant, the first seven images collected in each fMRI session were discarded to allow MRI equilibration. The remaining 190 images from each session were first slice‐time corrected and then realigned to the average image across two sessions. The individual structural image was subsequently co‐registered to the mean of the time‐ and head motion‐corrected images and segmented into grey matter, white matter, and cerebrospinal fluid. A customized template was created using the Diffeomorphic Anatomical Registration Through Exponentiated Lie Algebra (DARTEL) toolbox (Ashburner 2007 ). Finally, the functional data were spatially normalized to Montreal Neurological Institute (MNI) space using the parameters obtained from DARTEL, resampled to 3 mm × 3 mm × 3 mm voxels, and spatially smoothed with a 6 mm Gaussian blurring kernel. Additionally, an independent component analysis based strategy for automatic removal of motion artifacts (ICA‐AROMA) (Pruim et al. 2015 ) was employed to eliminate any remaining head motion‐related artefacts, and the average signals of the white matter and cerebrospinal fluid were further regressed out after applying the ICA‐AROMA approach. 2.5 Voxel‐Wise HRF Estimation The HRF shape represents the relationship between underlying neural activity and the BOLD response measured using fMRI, and can be used to model neurovascular coupling across the brain (Wu et al. 2019 ). With the recently proposed spontaneous HRF, we can estimate the voxel‐wise HRF on the basis of resting‐state BOLD fMRI data and subsequently evaluate local neurovascular coupling throughout the whole brain (Wu et al. 2019 , 2021 ). Given the variations in the shape of the HRF across individuals, brain regions, and menstrual phases, a data‐driven approach implemented in the rsHRF toolbox (Wu et al. 2021 ) was adopted to estimate the voxel‐wise HRF from each session of preprocessed resting‐state fMRI data for each participant. Specifically, the preprocessed data were detrended and band‐pass filtered, and the signal peaks with relatively large amplitudes (larger than 1.5 times the standard deviation over the mean of each voxel) were considered spontaneous neural events. The HRF for each voxel across the entire brain was estimated by fitting a general linear model to a double‐gamma function with the corresponding temporal derivative (Wu et al. 2013 ). Three parameters, namely the time to peak, response height, and full width at half maximum (FWHM), were estimated for each voxel‐wise HRF to characterize the HRF shape across the whole brain (Gao et al. 2023 ; Wu et al. 2019 ). The time‐to‐peak of the HRF shape has been considered to measure the latency of neuronal activity. The response height of the HRF has been used to interpret the magnitude of coupling between neural activity and vascular responses (Wu et al. 2019 ). The FWHM of the HRF shape has been used to indicate the duration of the BOLD response (Gao et al. 2023 ). 2.6 Insula Subdivision‐To‐The‐Whole‐Brain Phase Synchronization For the i ‐th voxel, the BOLD signal x i t can be modelled as the response of a neural activity n i t via a linear time‐invariant system with a voxel‐specific HRF denoted as h i t , that is, x i t = n i t ⨂ h i t . The convolution between n i t and h i t leads to bias in the phase of the neural activity. That is, the phase information encoded in x i t is a biased version of the phase encoded in n i t . To eliminate this bias and recover the phase of the neural activity, a temporally reversed HRF convolution was conducted, that is, x i t ⨂ h i − t = n i t ⨂ h i t ⨂ h i − t (Lou et al. 2024 ). In the frequency domain, the phase information of n i t is the same as x i t ⨂ h i − t . Therefore, the HRF for each voxel was temporally reversed and convolved with the unfiltered BOLD signal. The instantaneous phase was estimated as the circular mean of the phases derived from a set of equivalent band‐pass filtered (0.01–0.08 Hz) signals followed by a Hilbert transform. As in our previous study (Lou et al. 2024 ), four types of zero‐phase lag filters were employed: ten Butterworth filters with orders from 2 to 20, eight Chebyshev Type I filters with orders from 6 to 20, seven Chebyshev Type II filters with orders from 8 to 20, and seven Elliptic filters with orders from 6 to 18. The first and last 10 decoded instantaneous phases were discarded to prevent the edge effects inherent to the Hilbert transform (Ponce‐Alvarez et al. 2015 ; Wang et al. 2019 ), leaving 170 time points per session for each participant. The estimated time course of the instantaneous phase was considered to reflect the phase information of the ongoing neural activity. Subsequently, the seed‐to‐whole‐brain phase synchronization between subregions of the insula and the whole brain was estimated to investigate functional connectivity. In the present study, six subregions of the insula identified by clustering the segregated functional connectivity (Deen et al. 2011 ) were adopted. These regions have also been used in previous pain‐related functional connectivity studies (Wei et al. 2022 ; Zamorano et al. 2019 ). Six 6‐mm radius spheres designated as the left and right sides of the dorsal anterior insula (dAI), ventral anterior insula (vAI), and posterior insula (PI) were defined as seeds. The center coordinates of these regions of interest (ROIs) were defined in MNI space, that is, left dAI (−38, 6, 2), right dAI (35, 7, 3), left vAI (−33, 13, −7), right vAI (32, 10, −6), left PI (−38, −6, 5), and right PI (35, −11, 6). For each ROI, the instantaneous phase difference between the ROI and the k ‐th voxel of the whole brain at time point t , denoted as Δ ϕ k t , was determined as the circular mean of the phase difference between the k ‐th voxel and all the voxels within that ROI. The seed‐to‐voxel phase synchronization was quantified via the phase locking value (PLV) (Lachaux et al. 1999 ; Ponce‐Alvarez et al. 2015 ). The PLV is calculated as PLV k = 1 T ∑ t = 1 T e i Δ ϕ k t where T is the total number of time points and i = − 1 . The PLV ranges from 0 to 1 and represents the consistency of the phase difference between the ROI and the k ‐th voxel across the time interval T . If the phase difference is consistent across T , the PLV is equal to 1. A PLV of 0 indicates a random phase difference across T . 2.7 Statistical Analysis The statistical analyses of the demographic and clinical data were performed via IBM SPSS Statistics 28. The demographic data of the MRM and HC groups were compared using the Mann–Whitney test because the data were not normally distributed. A two‐way repeated‐measures ANOVA with a between‐subjects factor of group (MRM vs. HC) and a within‐subjects factor of menstrual phase (LFP vs. PMP) was conducted to analyze the progesterone and estradiol levels separately. The statistical analyses of the neuroimaging data were carried out using the statistical module in SPM12 and a toolbox for Data Processing & Analysis for Brain Imaging (DPABI) (Yan et al. 2016 ). For each of the HRF parameter maps, a two‐way repeated‐measures ANOVA was conducted, with age, years of education, and average head motion framewise displacement as covariates. To correct for multiple comparisons, the Gaussian random field (GRF) implemented in DPABI was used ( p < 0.001 at the voxel level and p < 0.05 at the cluster level). Furthermore, to account for the six subregions of the insula in seed‐to‐whole‐brain phase synchronization maps, the cluster level p was set to 0.0083 (i.e., 0.05/6) based on Bonferroni correction. Post hoc comparisons were conducted whenever a significant main effect or interaction effect was observed. A general linear model was employed to examine the associations between decreases in hormone levels and neurovascular/phase synchronization changes. Specifically, each brain region with significant main or interaction effects for HRF parameters or phase synchronization was modeled as the dependent variable; decreases in progesterone and estradiol levels were modeled as independent variables, while age and years of education were modeled as nuisance covariates. Additionally, the relationships between the identified neurovascular and phase synchronization alterations and relevant clinical features, including attack frequency, disease duration, and PPI scores during the PMP session, were examined using a general linear model in the MRM group.

[results] 3 Results 3.1 Changes in Pain Intensity and Hormonal Profiles During the Menstrual Cycle As presented in Table 1 , age, education level, and the time intervals between the LFP and PMP did not differ between the MRM and HC groups ( p > 0.05). TABLE 1 Demographic data for the MRM and HC groups. MRM ( N = 22) HC ( N = 26) Statistic p‐ value Age (years) 33.86 ± 6.23 33.00 ± 6.83 0.663 Education level (years) 15.77 ± 3.31 16.92 ± 6.15 0.299 Time interval between LFP and PMP (days) 16.45 ± 9.59 17.08 ± 8.51 0.377 Disease duration (years) 8.32 ± 5.57 NA NA Attack frequency (days/month) 4.43 ± 3.00 NA NA Note: Data are expressed as the mean ± standard deviation. Statistical p values were obtained via the Mann–Whitney test between the MRM and HC groups. Furthermore, fluctuations in progesterone and estradiol levels between the LFP and the PMP are shown in Figure 2B,C . Analysis of the progesterone levels revealed a significant main effect of menstrual phase ( F = 6.200, p = 0.016). Progesterone levels were significantly higher in the LFP compared to the PMP. Analysis of the estradiol levels also revealed a significant main effect of the menstrual phase ( F = 65.227, p < 0.001), with significantly lower estradiol levels in the PMP than in the LFP. There was no significant main effect of group or interaction effect between group and menstrual phase on the estradiol and progesterone levels. Additionally, we compared the changes in hormone levels between the PMP and LFP (i.e., PMP−LFP). However, there were no significant between‐group differences in the decreased progesterone levels ( p = 0.495) or the decreased estradiol levels ( p = 0.396). For the MRM group, the association of hormone changes with the relevant clinical variables, including disease duration, attack frequency, and PPI scores in the PMP, was evaluated using a general linear model. Only the decreased estradiol levels were observed to have a significant relationship with the disease duration ( t = −2.719, p = 0.015) (Figure 2D ). These findings suggest that there are no significant differences in the fluctuation of estradiol and progesterone levels between the MRM and HC groups; however, the longer the disease duration of MRM is, the greater the decrease in estradiol levels between the PMP and the LFP. 3.2 Changes in Hemodynamic Response Function Characteristics During the Menstrual Cycle Two‐way repeated‐measures ANOVA of the HRF parameters revealed that the response height and FWHM of the HRF are modulated by the menstrual cycle. A significant main effect of the menstrual phase on the response height of the estimated HRF was found in the left superior temporal gyrus (STG)/insula, the left inferior occipital lobe, and the right STG (Figure 3A ). A decreased response height was observed in the PMP compared to that in the LFP. Moreover, a significant interaction effect between group and menstrual phase on the FWHM was observed in the anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) and the left STG (Figure 3B ). Specifically, the FWHM values of the HRFs in the ACC and the left STG of the MRM group were significantly decreased in the PMP. Conversely, the FWHM values of the HRFs in the ACC and the left STG were greater in the MRM group than the HC group in the LFP. These results suggest altered neurovascular coupling during the menstrual cycle and migraine. FIGURE 3 The response height and FWHM of the HRF are modulated by the menstrual phase. (A) The response heights of the HRF in the left STG/insula, the left occipital lobe, and the right STG significantly decreased in the PMP across the groups. (B) The FWHM of the HRF in the MRM group decreased in the PMP but increased in the LFP compared to the HC group in the ACC and left STG. (C) Decreased estradiol levels between the PMP and LFP are positively associated with changes in the response height of the HRF in the bilateral STG. (D) Decreases in progesterone levels tended to be positively correlated with FWHM changes in the left STG in the MRM group. ACC = anterior cingulate cortex; HC = healthy control; LFP = late‐follicular phase; MRM = menstrual‐related migraine; PMP = perimenstrual phase; STG = superior temporal gyrus. * p < 0.05; ** p < 0.01; *** p < 0.001. 3.3 Neurovascular Function Changes With the Decreased Hormone Levels in MRM As illustrated in Figure 3 , the associations between changes in hormone levels, that is, the difference between the PMP and the LFP hormone levels, showed a positive correlation with the altered HRF parameters across these two menstrual phases in the MRM group. The reductions in estradiol levels from LFP to PMP in the MRM group were significantly associated with the reduced HRF response height in the right STG ( t = 3.606, p = 0.002) and the left STG/insula ( t = 2.668, p = 0.016) (Figure 3C ). Additionally, a marginally significant relationship ( t = 1.946, p = 0.068) was observed between decreased progesterone levels from LFP to PMP and FWHM reductions in the left STG in the MRM group (Figure 3D ). No significant associations between hormone level changes and the HRF parameter alterations were observed in the HC group. Therefore, these decreases in hormone levels may modulate the neurovascular coupling in the STG and insula in patients with MRM. Furthermore, the neurovascular function alterations observed between the PMP and LFP in the MRM group were not significantly correlated with clinical variables, including attack frequency, disease duration, or PPI scores around the ictal phase. 3.4 Alterations in Anterior Insular Phase Synchronization After accounting for the hemodynamic changes caused by the menstrual cycle and migraine, the corrected instantaneous phase information was used to estimate the PLVs between insular subregions and the whole brain. Two‐way repeated‐measures ANOVA of the PLVs between the left dAI and the whole brain revealed a significant interaction effect between group and menstrual phase in the middle cingulate cortex (MCC), the ACC, and the thalamus (Figure 4A ). Post hoc comparisons revealed that the PLVs between the left dAI and the MCC as well as the thalamus were increased in the LFP but decreased in the PMP in the MRM group (Figure 4B ). Furthermore, the PLVs between the left dAI and the aforementioned regions were significantly weaker in the PMP than in the LFP in the MRM group. Conversely, these regions exhibited stronger PLVs in the PMP than the LFP in the HC group. FIGURE 4 Interaction effect of group and menstrual phase on the dAI‐to‐the‐whole‐brain PLVs. (A) F ‐statistic maps of the interaction effect of group and menstrual phase identified by two‐way repeated‐measures ANOVA for the PLVs between the left dAI and whole brain. (B) The PLVs between the left dAI and the thalamus, ACC, and MCC decreased in the MRM group and increased in the HC group in the PMP compared with those in the LFP. (C) F ‐statistic maps of the interaction effect of group and menstrual phase identified by two‐way repeated‐measures ANOVA for the PLVs between the right dAI and the whole brain. (D) The PLVs between the right dAI and the thalamus, SMA, and right posterior insula in the MRM group were stronger than those in the HC group in the LFP and reduced in the PMP. ACC = anterior cingulate cortex; dAI = dorsal anterior insula; HC = healthy control; LFP = late‐follicular phase; MCC = middle cingulate cortex; MRM = menstrual‐related migraine; PLV = phase locking value; PMP = perimenstrual phase; SMA = supplementary motor area. * p < 0.05; ** p < 0.01; *** p < 0.001. For the right dAI, an interaction effect between group and menstrual phase was also observed for the PLVs between the right dAI and the thalamus, SMA, and right PI (Figure 4C ). Compared to those in the HC group, the PLVs between the right dAI and the thalamus, SMA, and right PI were significantly increased in the LFP and decreased in the PMP in the MRM group, as revealed by post hoc analysis (Figure 4D ). For the vAI, significant interaction effects between group and menstrual phase were found between the left vAI and left PI (Figure 5A ), and between the right vAI and the left paracentral lobule (Figure 5B ). The PLVs between those brain regions and the vAI were significantly stronger in the LFP and weaker in the PMP in the MRM group compared with that in the HC group (Figure 5C,D ). However, no significant main effects of group or menstrual phase were observed. We also investigated the relationship between changes in PLVs and hormone level reductions from the LFP to the PMP, but no significant effects were found in either the MRM or the HC groups. Additionally, for the MRM patients, the PPI scores during the PMP session showed a significant negative correlation with the reductions of PLVs from the LFP to the PMP between the right vAI and the left paracentral lobule ( t = −2.285, p = 0.035). FIGURE 5 Interaction effect between group and menstrual phase for the vAI‐to‐the‐whole‐brain PLVs. (A) F ‐statistic maps of the interaction effect of group and menstrual phase were identified by two‐way repeated‐measures ANOVA for the PLVs between the left vAI and the whole brain. (B) F ‐statistic maps of the interaction effect of group and menstrual phase were identified by two‐way repeated‐measures ANOVA for the PLVs between the right vAI and the whole brain. (C) For the left vAI, a significantly increased PLV with the posterior insula was found in the LFP, and the PLV in the PMP was decreased in the MRM group. (D) For the MRM group, the PLV between the right vAI and left paracentral lobule was significantly increased in the LFP and decreased in the PMP. HC = healthy control; LFP = late‐follicular phase; MRM = menstrual‐related migraine; PLV = phase locking value; PMP = perimenstrual phase; vAI = ventral anterior insula. * p < 0.05; ** p < 0.01; *** p < 0.001. 3.5 Changes in Posterior Insular Phase Synchronization A significant interaction effect between group and menstrual phase was observed for the PLVs between the left PI and the thalamus, left amygdala, right fusiform/parahippocampal gyrus, and inferior occipital gyrus (IOG) (Figure 6A ). Additionally, a significant interaction effect between group and menstrual phase was identified for the PLV between the right PI and the thalamus (Figure 6B ). A post hoc analysis revealed that the PLVs between the left PI and the thalamus, left amygdala, right fusiform/parahippocampal gyrus, and IOG were significantly increased in the LFP and decreased in the PMP in the MRM group compared to the HC group (Figure 6C ). FIGURE 6 Changes in the PI‐to‐the‐whole‐brain PLVs. (A) F ‐statistic maps of the interaction effect between group and menstrual phase, as well as the main effect of the menstrual phase, were identified using two‐way repeated‐measures ANOVA for the PLVs between the left PI and the whole brain. (B) F ‐statistic maps of the significant interaction effects between group and menstrual phase for the right PI‐to‐the‐whole‐brain PLVs revealed by two‐way repeated‐measures ANOVA. (C) In the MRM group, the PLVs between the left PI and the thalamus, amygdala, fusiform gyrus, and IOG were stronger in the LFP and weaker in the PMP compared to the HC group. (D) The PLV between the right PI and the thalamus was increased in the LFP and decreased in the PMP in MRM patients compared to HCs. (E) The PLVs between the left PI and the hippocampus and IOG were weaker in the PMP compared to the LFP across both groups. HC = healthy control; IOG = inferior occipital gyrus; LFP = late‐follicular phase; MRM = menstrual‐related migraine; PI = posterior insula; PLV = phase locking value; PMP = perimenstrual phase. * p < 0.05; ** p < 0.01; *** p < 0.001. For the right PI‐thalamus phases synchronization, a significantly decreased PLV in the PMP and increased PLV in the LFP were found in the MRM group compared with those in the HC group (Figure 6D ). In the MRM group, a reduction in right PI‐thalamus PLV from the LFP to the PMP was significantly associated with disease duration ( t = 3.089, p = 0.006) and age ( t = −2.310, p = 0.034), as revealed by a general linear model. However, when the interaction between age and disease duration was included in the model, the association between disease duration and reduced right PI‐thalamus PLV was no longer significant ( t = −0.795, p = 0.437). In addition, a main effect of menstrual phase was found for the PLVs between the left PI and the right hippocampus and right IOG (Figure 6A ). The PLVs between the left PI and the right hippocampus as well as the right IOG were significantly lower in the PMP compared to the LFP across the two groups (Figure 6E ). No significant correlations were found between changes in PI‐to‐the‐whole‐brain PLVs and decreases in hormone levels in either the MRM or HC groups.

[discussion] 4 Discussion In the present study, we investigated the modulation of neurovascular coupling and insular‐subdivisions‐to‐the‐whole‐brain functional connectivity by the menstrual cycle in patients with MRM. The principal findings of this study can be summarized as follows: (i) Neither progesterone nor estradiol levels nor fluctuations in these hormone levels differed between the MRM and HC groups. However, estradiol fluctuations were significantly correlated with the disease duration in patients with MRM. (ii) Alterations in neurovascular coupling, particularly in the insula, were evident from the altered HRF shapes observed in MRM patients during different menstrual phases. Additionally, hormone fluctuations in patients with MRM were positively correlated with changes in HRF shapes in the STG and insula. (iii) The activity of the insula in patients with MRM exhibited a strong coupling with brain regions involved in pain processing during the LFP as revealed by PLVs. Conversely, these phase locking patterns were reversed during the PMP. Decreases in estrogens and progesterone levels during the premenstrual phase have been hypothesized to trigger migraine attacks and modulate pain in migraine patients (Raffaelli et al. 2023 ; Somerville 1971 ). Ibrahimi et al. ( 2015 ) reported that MRM patients had similar estradiol and progesterone levels during the menstrual phase but reduced estradiol levels during the luteal phase. However, Rustichelli et al. ( 2020 , 2021 ) reported comparable estradiol and progesterone levels between MRM patients and HCs during the follicular phase. In the present study, progesterone and estradiol levels were examined in both the PMP and the LFP. The results confirmed that serum levels of progesterone and estradiol were comparable between MRM patients and HCs in both the LFP and the PMP. It is unlikely that the levels of estradiol and progesterone in the PMP and the LFP, or their fluctuations, directly contribute to MRM. However, decreases in estradiol levels are significantly associated with the disease duration of migraine. This suggests that the longer the disease duration, the greater the decrease in estradiol levels in the PMP observed in patients with MRM. It is possible that the fluctuations in estradiol levels between the PMP and LFP may indirectly link to migraine pathophysiology (Raffaelli et al. 2023 ). The HRF shape has been reported to be linked to a set of neurovascular factors. Specifically, a lower response height indicates less brain activation, while a smaller FWHM indicates a shorter duration of neuronal activity. In this study, we observed reduced neurovascular coupling responses in the bilateral STG/insula and the left occipital lobe in the PMP compared with that in the LFP. These findings suggest that changes in the serum levels of progesterone and estradiol from the LFP to the PMP would modulate the neurovascular response, particularly in the MRM group. To further explore this relationship, we examined the associations between changes in hormone levels and changes in HRF response height across the two groups using a linear mixed‐effects model. As shown in the Table S1 , reductions in HRF response height were significantly or marginally positively associated with decreases in serum levels from the LFP to the PMP across both groups. Additionally, an interaction effect in FWHM revealed distinct patterns of neurovascular function change between the MRM and the HC groups. It is likely that the reduced duration of neural activity in the ACC and left STG contributes to the altered neurovascular function observed in patients with MRM. We found the differences in BOLD response duration between the PMP and LFP, as indicated by the FWHM of the HRF in the ACC and the left STG. The durations of the BOLD responses in these brain regions are longer during the LFP but shorter during the PMP for MRM patients. The alterations in neurovascular coupling in the STG have been previously demonstrated in patients with episodic migraine using cerebral blood flow (CBF) (Z. Y. Chen et al. 2018 ). The ACC is believed to be the origin of altered laser‐evoked potentials during the menstrual cycle in migraine patients (de Tommaso et al. 2009 ) and has recently been identified as a crucial region in pain management (Xiao et al. 2021 ). The altered FWHM of the HRF shapes in patients with MRM can be attributed to abnormal neuronal excitability in the ACC and left STG. The decrease in hormone levels during menstruation appears to be associated with a reduced duration of neuronal activity in the STG in patients with MRM. The present study provides evidence of altered neurovascular coupling in patients with MRM. Moreover, these hormone‐induced changes in neurovascular coupling could be indicated by the HRF estimated from resting‐state fMRI data. To ensure unbiased phase synchronization analysis, voxel‐wise HRF variations were corrected to eliminate phase distortion for the phase synchronization between insular subregions and the whole brain. In the MRM patients, significantly increased synchronization between the anterior insula and brain regions involved in somatosensory, emotional, and cognitive processes was found in the LFP. This enhanced synchronization significantly decreased in the PMP. The anterior insula is believed to integrate sensory information with the cognitive and emotional processes of pain (Kong et al. 2006 ; Labrakakis 2023 ; Roy et al. 2009 ). Electrophysiology studies have demonstrated that information flows from the PI to the anterior insula during pain processing (Bastuji et al. 2018 ; Frot et al. 2014 ; Gehrlach et al. 2020 ). This coincides with the abnormal phase synchronization observed between the anterior insula and PI in the MRM patients. The SMA and paracentral lobule are key regions of the sensorimotor network and constitute parts of pain intensity pathways (Qin et al. 2020 ). The negative correlation between the reduction in right vAI‐paracentral lobule PLV and PPI scores during the PMP session suggests that the greater decreases in PLV from the LFP to the PMP in MRM are associated with higher pain intensity during migraine. The ACC and MCC are involved in the emotional processing of pain (Houde et al. 2020 ; Vogt 2005 ). Additionally, the thalamus is implicated in pain modulation in migraine patients (Younis et al. 2019 ). Notably, the functional connectivity between the anterior insula and thalamus was preferentially enhanced by painful stimuli (Tu et al. 2024 ). Therefore, the aberrant phase synchronization between the anterior insula and the brain regions identified in the present study suggests that patients with MRM exhibit abnormal integration of sensory and affective/emotional information in both the LFP and PMP. Furthermore, patients with MRM exhibited disrupted posterior insular PLVs in both the LFP and PMP. The PI has been previously reported to exhibit the highest CBF levels during migraine attacks (Stankewitz et al. 2021 ). The looped interactions between the PI and amygdala, thalamus in the limbic system have been demonstrated to play crucial roles in affective pain processing (Labrakakis 2023 ; Shi and Cassell 1998 ). Additionally, these regions are likely specific to females who experience migraine (Maleki et al. 2012 ). Consequently, the disrupted posterior insular phase synchronization suggests that pain‐modulating circuits are disturbed in patients with MRM in both the PMP and LFP. It is noteworthy that both the anterior and posterior insular phase synchronizations were increased in the LFP but reduced in the PMP in MRM patients. Accumulating evidence suggests increased functional connectivity of the anterior insula in interictal migraine patients with (Gollion et al. 2022 ) and without aura (Tso et al. 2015 ), as well as in chronic pain patients (Ferraro et al. 2022 ). In a recent study, enhanced connectivity between the right insula and the middle and superior temporal gyri was reported in the interictal period of MRM compared with that in non‐menstrual migraine (non‐MRM) patients (Huang et al. 2024 ). Notably, the increased functional connectivity decreased significantly during the PMP in the present study, which is consistent with the results of recent longitudinal studies. Reduced hypothalamic‐limbic connectivity has also been observed during the migraine attack phase when assessed across the migraine cycle (Stankewitz et al. 2021 ). Considering that migraine attacks most likely occur in the PMP, the reduced phase synchronization in patients with MRM during the PMP may be linked to the pathophysiology of MRM, which supports the pathophysiological model of the disconnectome in patients with migraine (Silvestro et al. 2021 ). Several limitations should be considered when interpreting the findings in this study. First, the relatively small sample size may have limited the generalizability of our findings and reduced the sensitivity to detect subtle changes in the HRF and phase synchronization in patients with MRM. The findings of this study should be validated in future studies with larger MRM cohorts. Second, the migraine attacks and menstrual cycle data were self‐reported, potentially introducing reporting bias. Regular headache diaries and menstruation calendars prior to data collection could enhance the reliability of the results (Verhagen et al. 2022 ). Third, the present longitudinal study primarily focused on the LFP (i.e., with the lowest risk of migraine attacks) and the PMP (i.e., with the highest risk of migraine attacks in patients with MRM), which may have limited the scope of the investigation. Further research involving more menstrual phases during the menstrual cycle is warranted to reveal the modulatory effects of the menstrual cycle on migraine attacks. Additionally, this study did not include a non‐MRM control group. Including such a group in future research would help distinguish the effects of hormonal fluctuations from general migraine‐related brain alterations. Fourth, in addition to estradiol and progesterone, other types of hormones, such as pregnanolone and pregnenolone sulfate (Rustichelli et al. 2021 ), should be considered in future studies. Finally, the neurovascular coupling abnormalities in patients with MRM were examined on the basis of the HRF shapes derived from resting‐state fMRI in the present study. Future studies evaluating the associations between CBF and functional connectivity might provide detailed evidence of abnormal trigeminal neurovascular coupling in migraine patients (Z. W. Chen et al. 2024 ).

[conclusion] 5 Conclusion Our findings provide evidence that both dysregulated neurovascular coupling and the dysfunctional brain connectome contribute to the neuropathology of MRM. The increased insular phase synchronization during the LFP and reduced synchronization during the PMP could indicate that the menstrual cycle modulates disruptions in the integration of sensory and affective information in patients with MRM. Furthermore, the findings suggest that hormone fluctuations are likely associated with changes in neurovascular function only.

retrieved_papers.title: Longitudinal changes in mammographic density and reproductive hormones during the menopausal transition in middle-aged Korean women: effect modification by obesity.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] Breast cancer, strongly associated with female hormones, peaks around perimenopause in Asia, versus post-menopause in Western countries. This study aims to investigate longitudinal changes in reproductive hormones and mammographic density (MD) through the menopausal transition (MT), as MD serves as both a strong breast cancer risk factor and a proxy marker of cumulative hormone exposure.
This longitudinal study included 4,737 Korean women who were in the premenopausal stage at baseline. MD was measured using digital mammography and analyzed with LIBRA software. Mean of craniocaudal views from both breasts were used for density assessments. A subset of 255 women with samples collected at four visits was assayed for hormone measurements across menopausal stages. Linear mixed-effects models were used to analyze longitudinal changes in reproductive hormones, including follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), estradiol (E2), and anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH), and MD to evaluate potential interaction by obesity status.
For MD analyses, the mean age of participants was 41.0 (± 3.3) years, with a median follow-up of 7.0 years (IQR 4.1-9.1). For hormone analyses, the mean age was 45.9 (± 2.4) years, with a median follow-up of 6.1 years (IQR 5.8-6.9). As menopausal stage advanced, FSH increased, whereas AMH and E2 decreased. The association between E2 and menopausal stages differed by obesity (P for interaction = 0.016); in early transition, E2 increased in the non-obese group (14.53 [95% CI: -15.68, 44.75]) but decreased in the obese group (- 86.02 [95% CI: -151.05, - 21.00]). Similarly, MD changes across the MT varied by BMI category (P for interaction < 0.001); overall, mean MD decreased with advancing stages, but an increasing MD pattern was observed during early transition in the underweight group (1.69 [95% CI: -0.08, 3.46]).
In Korean women undergoing MT, transient increases in MD paralleled E2 elevations, particularly in non-obese women. These findings align with the peak breast cancer incidence in Korean women in their 40s, preceding menopause, and the importance of considering hormonal and MD dynamics, as well as BMI, in breast cancer risk assessment and screening strategies for Asian women.

[Introduction] Background Breast cancer is the most prevalent malignancy among women worldwide with increasing incidence, underscoring the need to identify modifiable risk factors and develop targeted prevention strategies across populations [ 1 , 2 ]. In Asian countries, including Korea, breast cancer incidence peaks earlier—typically during the perimenopausal period—compared to that in Western populations, which peaks predominantly after menopause [ 3 ]. Endogenous estrogen levels and mammographic density (MD) are well-established risk factors for breast cancer [ 4 , 5 ]. Although both decline significantly after menopause, the perimenopausal period (also known as the menopausal transition [MT]), is characterized by dynamic hormonal fluctuations [ 6 ]. and a non-linear decline in breast density [ 7 , 8 ]. Notably, breast cancer risk does not necessarily decline during the MT, particularly among Asian women, highlighting a potentially vulnerable period that warrants further investigation into the interplay between hormonal changes, breast tissue composition, and cancer risk. The MT begins with irregular menstruation due to declining ovarian follicle numbers [ 6 ]. The Study of Women’s Health Across the Nation (SWAN) challenged the traditional view of a uniform estrogen decline during the MT, showing that non-obese women may experience transient elevations in estradiol (E2) levels before their final menstrual period (FMP) [ 9 ]. Elevated E2 levels are associated with increased risk of breast cancer development, particularly estrogen receptor (ER)-positive subtypes [ 10 ]; this transient hormonal increase may influence breast cancer risk. Moreover, body mass index (BMI) shows a strong inverse association with hormone receptor-positive subtypes [ 11 ], and menopausal status modifies this relationship as follows: in post-menopausal women, obesity increases breast cancer risk, but may reduce the risk in premenopausal women [ 12 ]. A large multicenter study of premenopausal women reported a 12–23% reduction in breast cancer risk per 5 kg/m² increase in BMI among premenopausal women including Asians [ 11 ], a finding confirmed in Korean populations [ 13 ]. Accordingly, the interaction between hormonal fluctuations and BMI could potentially influence breast cancer risk in perimenopausal women. We hypothesized that transient E2 level elevations occurring during the MT may be accompanied by temporary increases in MD, a well-established risk factor for breast cancer, and that these patterns differ by BMI. To test this hypothesis, we conducted a longitudinal analysis of changes in E2, follicle-stimulating hormone [FSH], and anti-Müllerian hormone [AMH] levels, and MD, across four MT stages (pre-menopause, early transition, late transition, and post-menopause), while accounting for the interaction of this association with BMI.

[introduction] Background Breast cancer is the most prevalent malignancy among women worldwide with increasing incidence, underscoring the need to identify modifiable risk factors and develop targeted prevention strategies across populations [ 1 , 2 ]. In Asian countries, including Korea, breast cancer incidence peaks earlier—typically during the perimenopausal period—compared to that in Western populations, which peaks predominantly after menopause [ 3 ]. Endogenous estrogen levels and mammographic density (MD) are well-established risk factors for breast cancer [ 4 , 5 ]. Although both decline significantly after menopause, the perimenopausal period (also known as the menopausal transition [MT]), is characterized by dynamic hormonal fluctuations [ 6 ]. and a non-linear decline in breast density [ 7 , 8 ]. Notably, breast cancer risk does not necessarily decline during the MT, particularly among Asian women, highlighting a potentially vulnerable period that warrants further investigation into the interplay between hormonal changes, breast tissue composition, and cancer risk. The MT begins with irregular menstruation due to declining ovarian follicle numbers [ 6 ]. The Study of Women’s Health Across the Nation (SWAN) challenged the traditional view of a uniform estrogen decline during the MT, showing that non-obese women may experience transient elevations in estradiol (E2) levels before their final menstrual period (FMP) [ 9 ]. Elevated E2 levels are associated with increased risk of breast cancer development, particularly estrogen receptor (ER)-positive subtypes [ 10 ]; this transient hormonal increase may influence breast cancer risk. Moreover, body mass index (BMI) shows a strong inverse association with hormone receptor-positive subtypes [ 11 ], and menopausal status modifies this relationship as follows: in post-menopausal women, obesity increases breast cancer risk, but may reduce the risk in premenopausal women [ 12 ]. A large multicenter study of premenopausal women reported a 12–23% reduction in breast cancer risk per 5 kg/m² increase in BMI among premenopausal women including Asians [ 11 ], a finding confirmed in Korean populations [ 13 ]. Accordingly, the interaction between hormonal fluctuations and BMI could potentially influence breast cancer risk in perimenopausal women. We hypothesized that transient E2 level elevations occurring during the MT may be accompanied by temporary increases in MD, a well-established risk factor for breast cancer, and that these patterns differ by BMI. To test this hypothesis, we conducted a longitudinal analysis of changes in E2, follicle-stimulating hormone [FSH], and anti-Müllerian hormone [AMH] levels, and MD, across four MT stages (pre-menopause, early transition, late transition, and post-menopause), while accounting for the interaction of this association with BMI.

[methods] Methods Study population This longitudinal observational study included women aged 42 to 52 years, who were either premenopausal or in the early MT, between 2014 and 2018, with follow-up through 2023. Participants underwent annual or biennial health examinations at the Total Healthcare Center of Kangbuk Samsung Hospital in Seoul and Suwon, South Korea [ 14 , 15 ]. Women were eligible for the MD analysis if digital mammography images were available and suitable for density quantification. Although recruitment for the prospective cohort began in 2014, participants had undergone digital mammography at our center before enrollment; these pre-enrollment images were retrospectively retrieved and included in the analysis after obtaining explicit participant consent for research use. The MD dataset comprised quantitative measurements from digital mammograms obtained between 2009—when digital mammography was first implemented at our center—and 2020, when mammographic data became available for de-identification and analysis. Of the 5,016 initially enrolled participants, we excluded 230 individuals who withdrew from the study, along with those with missing breast density data or a history of hormone therapy at baseline. This resulted in 4,737 participants included in the final mammographic density analysis (Fig. 1 ). For the hormonal analysis, we included women from the same prospective cohort who met the following criteria: (a) experienced natural menopause during follow-up (2014–2023), (b) attended at least one follow-up visit after menopause, and (c) had stored serum samples from at least four time points, including one premenopausal and one postmenopausal sample obtained within 1 year of the final menstrual period (FMP). From all eligible women meeting these criteria, we selected 255 participants using systematic sampling prioritized by proximity of sample collection timing to the FMP, ensuring optimal capture of hormonal changes in E2, FSH, and AMH levels across the MT. This approach yielded approximately 1,020 serum samples for hormonal analysis (4 samples per participant). This selection process yielded 255 women, of whom 238 (93.3%) also had mammography data and were included in analyses examining both hormone levels and MD. The remaining 17 women (6.7%) did not have suitable mammographic data. For participants who initiated hormone therapy during follow-up, only data collected prior to therapy initiation were included, and these individuals were censored at the start of hormone therapy owing to the small number of cases (Fig. 1 ). This study was approved by the Institutional Review Board of Kangbuk Samsung Hospital (IRB No. KBSMC 2025-03-008), and the need for informed consent was waived owing to the use of de-identified retrospective data collected during the health screening process. Data collection Demographic, behavioral, reproductive, and medical history data were collected through standardized, self-administered questionnaires. Participants were classified as ever (≥ 100 lifetime cigarettes) or never smokers [ 16 ]. Alcohol consumption was categorized as ≥ 10 or < 10 g/day [ 17 ]. Physical activity was evaluated using the validated Korean version of the short-form International Physical Activity Questionnaire [ 18 ]. Trained nurses measured body height and weight with participants barefoot and in a hospital gown. Body mass index (BMI) was then classified according to Asian-specific criteria [ 19 ]: underweight (< 18.5 kg/m 2 ), normal weight (18.5–23 kg/m 2 ), overweight (23–25 kg/m 2 ), and obese (≥ 25 kg/m 2 ). Marital status was classified as single, married/cohabiting, or divorced/separated/widowed. Education level was classified as college or higher and high school or lower. Parity was defined as having one or more births (live or stillbirths) or none. Age at menarche was categorized as < 14, 14–16, and >17 years. Medical history included hypertension, diabetes, medication use for dyslipidemia or hormone therapy. Quantification of mammographic breast density measurements All MD measurements were obtained at the time of participants’ routine health examination visits, not according to a predetermined schedule corresponding to specific MT stages. Mammographic data, including Breast Imaging Reporting and Data System’s (BI-RADS) final assessment categories and MD, were obtained from the original radiology reports of routine breast cancer screening. Participants underwent a standard four-view digital mammography examination, capturing bilateral craniocaudal (CC) and mediolateral oblique (MLO) views, using a full-field digital mammography (FFDM) system (Sonograph 2000D/DMR/DS, GE Healthcare, Chicago, IL, USA), at the Suwon and Seoul Total Healthcare Centers. Breast density was quantified using Laboratory for Individualized Breast Radiodensity Assessment (LIBRA), an open-source, fully automated software that analyzes both raw and processed FFDM images to obtain area-based measurements of MD [ 20 ]. Laboratory for Individualized Breast Radiodensity Assessment (LIBRA) estimates the total absolute dense area, while the percentage of breast density is calculated by normalizing the absolute dense area against the total breast area. The density assessments performed using LIBRA showed a strong correlation with the widely used Volpara ® methods ( r = 0.89, P < 0.001) [ 21 ]. In this study, LIBRA was used to determine the percentage of breast density (absolute dense area divided by total breast area × 100) from images stored in Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM) format, with density measurements averaged across left and right mammographic views for bilateral CC views [ 22 ]. The CC views were selected because pectoral muscle removal is more reliable and standardized in CC projections compared to MLO views, reducing potential measurement artifacts [ 23 , 24 ]. While our primary analysis used bilateral averaged MD measurements, we also conducted single-side analysis as a sensitivity analysis to assess the robustness of our findings. This approach addresses potential concerns where unilateral breast pathology, asymmetric breast composition, or differential imaging quality between sides might influence bilateral density measurements [ 25 ]. Percent dense area was selected as the primary MD metric because it is the most widely used and clinically relevant measure in breast cancer risk assessment and epidemiological research. Unlike absolute dense area, percent dense area accounts for overall breast size, making it more comparable across women with different breast volumes and body sizes [ 26 ]. This standardization is particularly important when studying hormonal influences on MD across the MT, as breast composition and total breast volume change significantly during this period [ 27 ]. Serum hormone assays Blood samples were obtained from the antecubital vein after at least a 10-h fast on the same day as the health examination, including mammography. Three hormones were analyzed in this study, including E2 and FSH serum levels quantified using electrochemiluminescence immunoassay (ECLIA) on a Roche Cobas e801 analyzer; and AMH, measured using an Elecsys AMH Plus reagent on the Cobas e801 immunoassay analyzer (Roche Diagnostics, Tokyo, Japan). Defining the menopausal stages and menstrual cycle phase Menopausal stage at each MD measurement was classified retrospectively as pre-menopause, early transition, late transition, or post-menopause according to the STRAW + 10 criteria [ 28 ], based on menstrual history information from the structured self-administered questionnaire at each visit: (1) Pre-menopause — does not meet any of the diagnostic criteria for the subsequent stages; (2) Early menopausal transition — a difference of ≥ 7 days in menstrual cycle length observed on at least two occasions within a 1-year period; (3) Late menopausal transition — amenorrhea persisting for ≥ 60 days; and (4) Post-menopause — amenorrhea persisting for ≥ 12 months. At each visit, the date of last menstrual period, regularity of menstrual cycles, and usual cycle length (if cycles were regular) were also assessed. For hormone measurements obtained during pre-menopause and early transition stages, menstrual cycle phase at the time of blood draw was determined based on days since last menstrual period and estimated cycle length. Cycle phases were classified as: early follicular (≤ 7 days since menses onset), late follicular/peri-ovulatory (8–14 days since onset), mid-luteal (7–14 days before next expected menses), late luteal (< 7 days before next expected menses), or irregular cycles (when specific phase could not be determined due to cycle irregularity). Statistical analyses Baseline characteristics are presented using descriptive statistics, including the mean (standard deviation [SD]) and percentage, as appropriate. We defined confounding variables using the following criteria: Each variable is (1) causally associated with the outcome (MD and Hormones), (2) non-causally or causally associated with the exposure (MT), but (3) is not an intermediate variable in the causal pathway between exposure (MT) and the outcome (MD and Hormones). We examined correlations among the three hormones (E2, FSH, and AMH) using Pearson correlation analysis. Mutual adjustment for the three hormones was performed using their original values to evaluate interaction terms between each hormone and MT status. Among the 238 participants with data on MD and all three hormones, we extended the main model by including each of the three log-transformed hormones (E2, FSH, AMH) as explanatory variables to estimate their associations with MD. To investigate the MD changes along with hormonal changes by MT stage over time, a linear mixed model with a random intercept was applied using maximum likelihood estimation, which was considered a within-participant correlation for the study participants. Despite the uneven distribution of MD measurements across MT, we used linear mixed-effects models to appropriately analyze the longitudinal data, which can handle unbalanced repeated measures and varying follow-up intervals among participants [ 29 , 30 ]. When participants had multiple mammograms within the same menopausal stage, all measurements were included in analyses using linear mixed-effects models with random intercepts, which appropriately handle unequal numbers of observations and varying follow-up intervals among participants. A linear mixed model with a random intercept for identifiers was applied, adjusted for age, smoking status, age at menarche, parity, marital status, and education level. An interaction term between MT status and BMI categories was included. All covariates were treated as time-varying except for age at menarche. The normality of residuals from MD model was assessed by visual inspection of their distribution within each menopausal stage, although fixed effects in linear mixed-effects models are robust to violations of residual normality [ 31 ]. For anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) values, which had a lower detection limit of 0.001 ng/mL, a sensitivity analysis was performed using log-transformed AMH values to address potential distributional concerns. We used the mean value of the CC view MD from both the breasts. We compared changes in breast density across time-variant menopausal stages, stratified by BMI categories (underweight, normal, overweight, obese). For hormonal changes, BMI was dichotomized (non-obese: BMI < 25 kg/m², obese: BMI ≥ 25 kg/m²) for detailed subgroup analysis, owing to limited sample size. In additional analyses, changes in breast density were also evaluated across time-varying menopausal stages, stratified by body fat percentage categories: <25%, 25–29.9%, 30.0–34.9%, and ≥ 35%. The analysis was conducted using Stata version 17.0 (College Station, Texas, USA), with statistical significance set at 0.05.

[results] Results Table 1 shows baseline characteristics: for MD, mean age 41.0 (± 3.3) years and BMI 22.0 (± 2.9) kg/m²; for hormones, mean age 45.9 (± 2.4) years and BMI 22.7 (± 3.1) kg/m². The mean baseline age of 41 years reflects the age when participants’ first digital MD measurements became available (from 2009 onwards), while the 42–52 year age range represents their age at formal enrollment into the prospective study. The median right and left breast densities were 40.3% (interquartile range [IQR]: 23.5–57.7) and 39.1% (IQR: 21.3–57.0), respectively. The median baseline E2, FSH, and AMH levels were 118.0 pg/mL (IQR: 68.4–183.0); 7.9 mIU/mL (IQR: 4.9–13.7); and 0.17 ng/mL (IQR: 0.07–0.36), respectively. The distributions of MD across MT stages are presented in the supplementary figures (Additional file 2, Figure S1 -1, 1–2, 1–3, and 1–4). For MD and hormone analyses, participants were followed for a median of 7.0 years (IQR 4.1–9.1) and 6.1 years (IQR 5.8–6.9), respectively. The median interval between observations was 1.0 year (IQR 0.9–1.4) for MD and 2.0 years (IQR 1.2–2.8) for hormones. Among the 255 women in the hormonal analysis, 238 (93.3%) also had mammography data, enabling combined hormone–density analyses; 17 (6.7%) did not have available mammograms. The smaller hormonal sample reflects stringent eligibility criteria and the requirement for multiple biospecimens spanning the MT. Age-adjusted baseline comparisons showed differences in smoking status, education level, and age at menarche, while BMI was comparable between groups (Additional file 1, Table S1 ). Longitudinal hormonal changes across MT Table 2 presents the longitudinal changes in E2, FSH, and AMH levels across the MT. Age was negatively associated with E2 and AMH levels, but positively associated with FSH levels. Hormonal patterns varied by menopausal stage as follows: FSH increased and AMH and E2 decreased with advancing menopausal stage, without interaction terms, after adjusting for age and other confounders. E2 levels significantly decreased during the late transition (− 55.82 [95% CI: −77.37, − 34.28]) and post-menopause (− 94.83 [95% CI: −121.35, − 68.32]) stages, whereas a non-significant change was observed in the early transition (− 2.33 [95% CI: −30.00, 25.34]). FSH levels showed a non-significant increase during the early transition (1.74 [95% CI: −4.78, 8.26]), followed by significant increases during the late transition (27.39 [95% CI: 22.23, 32.56]) and post-menopause (38.55 [95% CI: 32.08, 45.02]) stages. AMH levels significantly declined from the early transition (− 0.11 [95% CI: −0.16, − 0.06]) and continued to decrease during the late transition (− 0.13 [95% CI: −0.17, − 0.09]) and post-menopause (− 0.07 [95% CI: −0.13, − 0.02]) stages. Changes in E2 and AMH levels across the MT significantly differed by BMI category (obese vs. non-obese), whereas FSH levels did not (Table 2 ; Fig. 2 - 1 – 3 ). Notably, during the early transition, a transient increase in E2 levels was observed only among women with BMI < 25.0 kg/m² (14.53 [95% CI: -15.68, 44.75]), whereas a significant decline was observed among women with BMI ≥ 25.0 kg/m² (-86.02 [95% CI: -151.05, -21.00]), with a significant interaction by obesity status ( P for interaction = 0.016). In later stages, E2 levels declined further in both groups, with greater reductions observed among obese women. Similarly, AMH levels declined drastically across all MT stages in women with BMI ≥ 25.0 kg/m² than those with BMI < 25.0 kg/m², with a significant interaction by obesity status ( P for interaction = 0.038). In our dataset, the lowest AMH value was 0.001 ng/mL, corresponding to the assay’s lower detection limit, with postmenopausal women exhibiting the expected near-zero values. The sensitivity analysis using log-transformed AMH values yielded results consistent with the main analysis (Additional file 3, Figure S2 ). Among the 1,020 hormone measurements from 255 women, the distribution of menstrual cycle phases at the time of blood draw was as follows: early follicular phase in 64 measurements (6.3%), late follicular/peri-ovulatory phase in 91 measurements (8.9%), mid-luteal phase in 118 measurements (11.6%), late luteal phase in 81 measurements (7.9%), postmenopausal status in 276 measurements (27.1%), and irregular menstrual cycles in 390 measurements (38.2%). The high proportion of irregular cycles reflected the natural increase in menstrual irregularity characteristic of the MT period. Additionally, in sensitivity analyses adjusting for menstrual cycle phase, the patterns of estradiol, follicle-stimulating hormone, and AMH changes across MT stages remained consistent with the main analysis. Specifically, the significant interaction between BMI status and estradiol levels across menopausal stages persisted ( P for interaction = 0.030) (Additional file 4, Figure S3 ). Longitudinal MD changes across MT All 4,737 women with at least once measurement of mammogram were included in the analysis. Among these, 4,378 (92.5%) had at least two MD measurements and the number of mammograms per person was median 6 (interquartile range 3–8, maximum 12) over the study period. All participants were premenopausal at baseline. By the end of follow-up, MD data were available for 1,483 (31.3%) in early transition, 1,039 (21.9%) in late transition, and 633 (13.4%) in post-menopause. Table 3 presents changes in MD across the MT. After adjusting for age and other confounders without interaction, MD showed a non-significant decrease during the early transition (− 0.28 [95% CI: −0.71, 0.16]) compared to the pre-menopausal stage, followed by significant decreases during the late transition (− 2.22 [95% CI: −2.75, − 1.69]) and post-menopause (− 3.62 [95% CI: −4.30, − 2.93]). Age was negatively associated with MD (− 0.88 [95% CI: −0.93, − 0.83]). The distributions of MD residuals across MT stages are presented in the supplementary figures (Additional file 5, Figure S4 -1, S4-2, S4-3, and 4–4). The residuals showed approximately normal distributions of MD across menopausal stages, satisfying the assumptions for linear mixed-effects models without requiring transformation (Additional file 5, Figure S4 -1, S4-2, S4-3, and 4–4). Figure 3 illustrates that changes in MD across the MT significantly differed by BMI category (underweight, normal weight, overweight, and obese) after adjusting for the same confounders. Notably, during the early transition, MD showed an increasing pattern (1.69 [95% CI: -0.08, 3.46]) in the underweight group, whereas only modest changes were observed in the normal weight (-0.79 [95% CI: -1.32, -0.25]), overweight (-0.03 [95% CI: -0.91, 0.86]), and obese (0.66 [95% CI: -0.27, 1.60]) groups (Table 3 ). As an additional analysis to assess potential residual confounding, we evaluated the interaction between continuous BMI and MT stages, with MD as the outcome. The interaction terms were statistically significant (overall P for interaction < 0.001), consistent with the results obtained when BMI was modeled as a categorical variable. Separate analyses of the right- and left-sided MD across the MT, including interaction terms for BMI categories, demonstrated patterns consistent with those observed when using the mean of both the breasts (Additional file 1, Table S2 ). During the early transition, single-sided MD showed an increasing pattern in the underweight group for both breasts (Additional file 6, Figure S5 -1, S5-2). In additional analyses, bilateral mean MD also showed a slight increase in the lowest body fat percentage group (< 25%) during the early transition (Additional file 7, Figure S6 ), aligning with patterns observed in BMI-based categories. Among participants, oral contraceptive use was low: 1.1% (50/4,737) in the MD analysis and 1.6% (4/255) in the hormonal analysis. In the sensitivity analysis, adjustment for oral contraceptive use did not affect the results (Additional file 8, Figure S7-1 and 7 -2). Correlations among hormones and their associations with MD E2 was negatively correlated with FSH ( r = − 0.50, p < 0.001) and positively correlated with AMH ( r = 0.22, p < 0.001), while FSH was negatively correlated with AMH ( r = − 0.33, p < 0.001) (Additional file 1, Table S3). After mutual adjustment, the interaction term between E2 and MT remained significant without appreciable change. The interaction term between AMH and MT attenuated from significant to marginally significant, whereas the interaction term between FSH and MT was not statistically significant irrespective of mutual adjustment (Additional file 9, Figure S8). We further performed sensitivity analysis among 238 participants with complete hormone and MD data. When added individually, log-transformed E2 showed a positive association with MD (1.08 [95% CI: 0.35, 1.82]), while log-transformed FSH showed an inverse association (–1.54 [95% CI: − 2.59, − 0.49]). However, when E2, FSH, and AMH were included simultaneously, variance inflation factor (VIF) values remained below 2, and none of the hormones were significantly associated with MD (Additional file 1, Table S4).

[discussion] Discussion We observed the following distinct hormonal patterns across MT stages: FSH levels increased and AMH levels decreased progressively, whereas E2 showed a transient increase during the early transition, followed by a marked decline. Notably, this transient increase in E2 was observed only among non-obese women, consistent to the SWAN findings. A corresponding increase in MD during the early transition was particularly evident in underweight women, potentially reflecting the temporal E2 elevation during this period. These patterns may represent a previously underrecognized feature of early MT and could help explain the incidence of breast cancer among Korean women, a relatively lean population. Our findings align with those of the SWAN, which showed a transient E2 increase in non-obese women during MT, suggesting BMI-hormonal interactions [ 9 ]. A previous review [ 32 ] and small population-based studies [ 33 , 34 ] have shown that perimenopause is marked by complex, extreme, hormonal fluctuations, particularly of estrogen. The transient increase in E2 levels during early MT possibly results from declining ovarian follicles, leading to reduced negative feedback and elevated FSH levels [ 33 ]. Elevated FSH levels may stimulate estrogen production in the subsequent cycle and likely initiates folliculogenesis prematurely [ 35 , 36 ]. Additionally, reduced hypothalamic-pituitary sensitivity to estrogen may cause E2 elevation before the FMP [ 37 ]. In underweight women, with lower ovarian reserve, this FSH-driven feedback response may be exaggerated. Limited follicle availability may prompt increased pituitary FSH output to recruit the remaining follicles, intensifying transient estrogen surges during early MT. This may ultimately shorten the length of the follicular phase and the overall cycle, thereby accelerating the approach to menopause [ 38 ]. In contrast, obese women show attenuated changes in E2 and FSH during MT. Obesity appears to suppress gonadotropin secretion, the FSH increase after FMP, and altering E2 patterns—showing lower pre-FMP but higher post-FMP E2 levels—resulting in lower overall FSH and E2 levels in overweight and obese women [ 39 , 40 ]. Unlike previous studies, we assessed AMH changes throughout the MT alongside E2 and FSH. AMH is a well-established marker of ovarian reserve and a predictor of time to menopause, with potentially greater accuracy than FSH [ 41 , 42 ]. Our findings show that AMH levels steadily decreased throughout MT without significant fluctuations, consistent with prior studies highlighting its stability [ 43 , 44 ]. We observed that obese women maintained higher mean AMH levels during MT compared to non-obese women, a finding that contrasts with some earlier studies [ 45 , 46 ]. However, a recent meta-analysis reported inconclusive results, attributed to high heterogeneity in AMH levels, small sample sizes, cross-sectional designs, and inadequate representation across the entire adiposity spectrum in previous studies [ 47 ]. Given our relatively lean premenopausal population, further studies are needed to clarify the relationship between obesity and AMH levels, considering obesity subtypes and fat mass measures. Research on longitudinal changes in MD to estimate breast cancer risk across detailed MT stages remains limited, particularly in large populations with regular screening data. A study based on the San Francisco Mammography Registry (2007–2013) using automated software (Volpara) to measure breast density, found that women with higher premenopausal density experienced more significant declines in dense breast volume during the transition [ 48 ]. Another study from the Melbourne Women’s Midlife Health Project reported decreased mean percentage of mammographically dense tissue throughout the MT [ 49 ]. A longitudinal analysis revealed that women with stable MD (≤ 10% annual change) had higher breast cancer risk than those with >10% annual decline [ 50 ]. Although these studies examined density changes over time, unlike our study, they did not specifically categorize distinct MT stages, or assessed interaction with BMI, or evaluate concurrent hormonal changes, limiting understanding of the underlying hormonal mechanisms. Interestingly, we observed similar pattern of changes in MD corresponding to the changes in E2 levels across MT, particularly in underweight women. The association between MD and cyclic variations in E2 levels has been studied in both premenopausal [ 51 ] and post-menopausal women [ 52 , 53 ]. In premenopausal women, higher levels of estrogen during the menstrual cycle have been associated with increased MD, highlighting a hormonal influence on breast tissue composition [ 51 ]. Similarly, a meta-analysis examining the association between premenopausal endogenous estrogen levels and breast cancer risk found a positive correlation, with elevated circulating E2 levels associated with a higher risk of breast cancer [ 4 ]. Given that estrogen, a key determinant of breast density in premenopausal women, shows a positive association with MD, transient MD elevation along with E2 elevation appears compatible. To our knowledge, this is the first study examining longitudinal MD changes in relation to reproductive hormone fluctuations across distinct MT stages. We analyzed changes in MD as a proxy for breast cancer risk across MT. MD is a well-established risk factor in both Western [ 24 , 26 , 54 ] and Asian populations, including Koreans [ 13 , 55 – 57 ], regardless of menopausal status. MD serves as an important predictor of future breast cancer risk [ 13 , 55 , 56 ]. Studies indicate that women with increasing or persistently high MD trajectories are at higher breast cancer risk [ 58 ]. Thus, MD may be a tissue-specific marker of the biological processes underlying the rate of breast tissue aging and, thereby, shape the breast cancer incidence–age curve [ 7 , 59 , 60 ]. However, further cohort studies are needed to investigate long-term MD trajectories in relation to obesity phenotypes—such as specific adiposity measures—and their interaction with hormonal changes. These investigations should also incorporate incident breast cancer data to validate and extend current findings. Single-side analysis serves as a sensitivity analysis to validate our main findings, given the high bilateral correlation ( r = 0.85–0.95) consistently reported in MD studies [ 61 ]. Single-side analysis ensures that our observed associations between hormonal changes and MD across the MT are not dependent on bilateral averaging methodology and remain valid even when only unilateral measurements are available or reliable. For the hormonal analysis, 255 women who had ≥ 4 serum samples across menopausal transition were selected Fig. 2 Trends in E2, FSH, and AMH across the menopausal transition stratified by obesity status ( n = 255): (2 − 1) estradiol (E2), (2–2) follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), and (2–3) anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) ( n = 255). Data shows trends in hormones across the menopausal transition stratified by BMI categories in underweight, normal weight, overweight, and obese groups. Analysis was conducted using a linear mixed-effects model adjusted for age and other covariates, including an interaction term between menopausal stage and BMI category Fig. 3 Trends in mean mammographic density of both breasts across the menopausal transition stratified by BMI categories ( n = 4737). Data show mean MD trends in underweight, normal weight, overweight, and obese groups. Analysis was conducted using a linear mixed-effects model adjusted for age and other covariates, including an interaction term between menopausal stage and BMI category At each health examination visit, oral contraceptive use was assessed using the structured self-administered questionnaire. During the observation period, among the 4,737 participants in the MD analysis, 50 (1.1%) reported oral contraceptive use, and among the 255 participants in the hormonal analysis, 4 (1.6%) reported use. The low oral contraceptive use rate in our cohort was expected and consistent with our study design. First, any hormone use including oral contraceptives was an exclusion criterion at enrollment. Second, our cohort focused on women aged 42–52 years during the MT, when oral contraceptive use typically declines. For context, a recent Korean population study reported oral contraceptive usage rates of 2–8% among women aged 20–59 years [ 62 , 63 ], with lower rates in older age groups, making our observed rates plausible for this demographic.
